text: Appendix). This enabled us to also consider the type of document when analysing the findings from the coding. Such reflections are included in our discussion below. We adopted a critical qualitative discourse analysis approach. Methodologically, dis- course analysis is useful in the analysis of institutional records since it entails the repro- duction of a discourse through the empirical analysis of its realisation in practices’ (Laffey and Weldes 2004, 28). Methods of discourse analysis provide analytical tools to apprehend discursive production and distribution of power and struggles over interests, identity and social relations (Aydın-Düzgit 2014; Laffey and Weldes 2004). Discourse analysis techniques have been widely used in analyses of institutional records and docu- mentation. Illustratively, Penny Griffin has undertaken an extensive analysis of policy documents to explore gendered discourses at the World Bank (2009) , while Laura J Shep- herd has examined UN documents on the Women, Peace and Security agenda (2008, 2021) and with Paul Kirby (2021, 2023), and Charlotte Epstein has examined the discursive contestation in the rise of anti-whaling attitudes and policy (2008). The 35 documents were analysed in Dedoose, a secure cloud-based data analysis appli- cation, functionally like NVivo but chosen for its accessibility for both authors. The docu- ment dataset for this article came to 857 pages of material (446,583 words). Coding was both inductive and deductive, with an initial guiding code list established collaboratively through discussion of the aims of the research and key questions (as per Auerbach and Silverstein 2003). This initial coding looked for descriptors of youth inclusion (seeking alternative terms such as participation and phrases such as ‘to address exclusion’), for the context in which that inclusion is discussed (around certain thematic foci such as CVE, education, COVID, gender-oriented initiatives etc), and how youth are described (negative, positive, etc.). This broad initial inductive coding list was refined and expanded through a preliminary coding of a selection of documents by each author. We then reviewed each other’s coding to establish interpretive convergence (Saldaña 2016, 37) and we discussed preliminary impressions and findings, before settling a final code list to guide the process. The two perspectives enabled different views of the documents, which supported the ‘creation of shared interpretation and understanding of the phenomenon being studied’ (Weston et al. 2001, 382). Codes were broad and descriptive, with the aim of allowing the researchers to make evaluations of the context and position- ing of the statements as is good practice with qualitative discourse analysis approaches. The resulting four thematic framings discussed below emerged from consideration of the coding and discussion between the authors. These thematic framings best captured the dominant ways in which youth themselves, and their inclusion were discussed in the documents. Analysing formal UN documents in this way can only reveal so much, and while being attuned to the context and broader political machinations at play in a forum like the UN, we do not suggest we can reveal intent of the political statements by Member States, representatives of UN bodies, or briefers beyond the words they bring to the forum in which they speak. This is not a weakness. As Shepherd has demonstrated with her own 8 H. BERENTS AND R. FOSU analysis of the plurality of narratives in the WPS agenda (2008, 2021), narratives have a ‘constitutive effect’ on how agendas are known and enacted (2021). Conditionality of youth inclusion Youth inclusion is a fraught political space. On one hand, the inclusion of young people is seen as an easy social and political project to support. The swift and unanimous adoption of the first YPS resolution by the UNSC attests to

text: beyond the words they bring to the forum in which they speak. This is not a weakness. As Shepherd has demonstrated with her own 8 H. BERENTS AND R. FOSU analysis of the plurality of narratives in the WPS agenda (2008, 2021), narratives have a ‘constitutive effect’ on how agendas are known and enacted (2021). Conditionality of youth inclusion Youth inclusion is a fraught political space. On one hand, the inclusion of young people is seen as an easy social and political project to support. The swift and unanimous adoption of the first YPS resolution by the UNSC attests to this broad-based support for the idea of inclusion. On the other hand, the years since have demonstrated that the practical, mean- ingful implementation of calls for youth inclusion pose more deeply rooted challenges for political actors. This conditionality of youth participation is borne by and fosters insti- tutional compliance, which can discipline the operationalisation of the YPS agenda (see Huelss 2017). Institutional compliance refers to regulating actors’ behaviour according to institutional(ised) norms and processes – ‘how things are done around here’ – to produce desired outcomes (Lowndes 2014, 688). Critical assessments of liberal peace- building, for instance, show that compliance-seeking and conditionality of inclusion in normative agendas undermine effort towards meaningful inclusion (e.g. Richmond 2006). Thus, institutional compliance as a condition of youth participation suggests that progress towards the YPS agenda may be predicated on youth adherence to institu- tional(ised) normative rules, including those that assume their incapacity/delinquency. Such tensions are evident in the documentation of the agenda. Here, we explore the four dominant framings of youth inclusion that emerge from the dataset. These are: youth as future promise, youth as resources, youth as deficient, and youth as exceptional. Together, these four framings illustrate the complexities of normative claims for the inclusion of traditionally excluded populations, the persistence of unequal power relations, and the politicisation of marginal groups for strategic gain. Youth as future promise There is a seemingly natural invocation of the future when youth are discussed. Youth, along with the related and overlapping identity categories of child, adolescent, and ‘young person’ are predominantly framed as having an intrinsic teleological impulse, evident in the language often used: that they will ‘grow’ or ‘develop’ or ‘mature’ or ‘reach’ adulthood. The pervasive idea that youthhood is a time of preparation extends logics identified by childhood studies scholars of children as ‘becomings’ towards full adult ‘beings’ (Katz 2008; Qvortrup 1994). The framing of youth participation in futuristic terms has a particular policy implication on institutional reform required for their meaningful inclusion. It contains a technocratic logic to inclusion which carries the belief that existing institutional structures can deliver a better future for today’s young people without necessarily including them or reforming existing institutional mechanisms. These narratives can be framed in paternalistic language which echoes the popular adage ‘youth are the future’. Such discursive fram- ings position youth as in need of preparation for future leadership and defer inclusion now; they also simultaneously enable institutions their own deferral of responsibility from that preparatory work. Young people’s participation is deferred to some unspecified future, while the institutions rhetorically excuse themselves from the obligation of JOURNAL OF INTERVENTION AND STATEBUILDING 9 preparing them for this participation. Such examples are evident consistently in the docu- mentation, from Lebanon in the 2015 Open Debate: ‘youth are our ray of hope in the midst of all these challenges’ [#2], to the USA’s statement in 2018 on the adoption of the second UNSC resolution describing youth ‘who will be the next generation’s peace- makers’ [#13], to Argentina in 2020:

text: enable institutions their own deferral of responsibility from that preparatory work. Young people’s participation is deferred to some unspecified future, while the institutions rhetorically excuse themselves from the obligation of JOURNAL OF INTERVENTION AND STATEBUILDING 9 preparing them for this participation. Such examples are evident consistently in the docu- mentation, from Lebanon in the 2015 Open Debate: ‘youth are our ray of hope in the midst of all these challenges’ [#2], to the USA’s statement in 2018 on the adoption of the second UNSC resolution describing youth ‘who will be the next generation’s peace- makers’ [#13], to Argentina in 2020: ‘young people, as leaders of the future’ [#23], China at the Arria Formula meeting on the 7th anniversary: ‘youth are the future of each country and of the world’ [#34], or France invoking not only a national writer but an evocation of ‘warm’ language in what young people offer: ‘Victor Hugo wrote that young people are the smiling face of the future. If the future is to smile on us, we cannot ignore it’ [#11]. Beyond these often-superficial statements, the connection of youth to the future is often also framed around the necessity of those already in power preparing them for these roles. This teleological logic also reproduces normative views of both young people and ‘appropriate’ roles within national and international systems of institutiona- lised inequality and power. The impetus to ‘prepare’ youth exists across the dataset. Such language is evident in Egypt’s statement as Chair of the Peacebuilding Commission Ambassadorial level meeting on YPS in 2021, where they argue that ‘more efforts need to be exerted to enable youth to realize their full potential’ [#29], as well as Croatia in the 2018 Open Debate: ‘[y]oung people must be provided with the necessary precondi- tions to fully realize their potential’ [#11], or Nigeria ahead of the first resolution: ‘with a determined will to transform young people all over the world to enable them to take on their role as trustees of the world’s future’ [#2]. The paternalistic assumptions that insti- tutions know best, even as they rhetorically recognise the unique contributions youth can contribute, limit young people’s inclusion, and defer their participation to an unspecified future. There are some limited exceptions, where this future orientation is linked as the ration- ale for contemporary inclusion of youth. Indonesia’s statement at the UNSC Open Debate on the 5th anniversary is representative: And, as youth will inherit the world, it is their right and solemn duty to be part and parcel of creating a new world post-COVID … They are not only influencers but actors in shaping the future [#23]. Here, youth’s stake in the future is not a deferral of responsibility but a reason for their meaningful inclusion and recognition of their actions. Youth as resources If the first framing identified is of youth agency and inclusion deferred to an unspecified future, the second emerges closely related to this: that of youth as resources to be ‘har- nessed’. The documents show the ways in which youth are seen not as possessing agen- tial capacity on their own terms, but rather as resources for government and institutional agendas and intentions. Here, youth inclusion is not seen as youth being partners or even as stakeholders but rather are reduced to their potential usefulness to institutions and government agendas. This language is often economic in linguistic origin and functional intention: ‘young people present a valuable and potential resource for the world’ (South Africa, 2022 [#34]) or ‘youth are an investment in our collective future’ (Advice of the Peacebuilding 10 H. BERENTS AND R. FOSU Commission

text: ways in which youth are seen not as possessing agen- tial capacity on their own terms, but rather as resources for government and institutional agendas and intentions. Here, youth inclusion is not seen as youth being partners or even as stakeholders but rather are reduced to their potential usefulness to institutions and government agendas. This language is often economic in linguistic origin and functional intention: ‘young people present a valuable and potential resource for the world’ (South Africa, 2022 [#34]) or ‘youth are an investment in our collective future’ (Advice of the Peacebuilding 10 H. BERENTS AND R. FOSU Commission [#22]). There is a long history connecting young people’s value to capitalist and colonial labour market processes, particularly identified by youth sociologists (i.e. Far- rugia 2020; Furlong and Cartmel 2007), one which is amply echoed in international devel- opment discourse and in the YPS agenda framing also (Sukarieh and Tannock 2008). Youth serve states’ interests, they ‘constitute a unique resource for their countries’ (Sweden 2015 [#4]) or are ‘the primary resource in building our nation’ (UAE, 2015 [#2]). As future resources, youth are positioned as ‘possibility and panacea’, an ‘ideal peace- builder’ whose participation is seen as critical to countering extremism, radicalisation and building peace (Sukarieh and Tannock 2018, 860). For example, linking youth to both futurity and their value can be framed as a beneficial action: Youth are an investment in our collective future, meaning that it is highly important for States to promote the rights of all young people and to create opportunities for their political, econ- omic, and social inclusion, with the aim of harnessing and investing in the capacities and resi- lience of young people and creative, intuitive, and inspired peacebuilders. (Advice of the Peacebuilding Commission, 2020 [#22]) Youth, here, must be reframed from a ‘demographic deficit’ to a ‘demographic dividend’. This language appears before the formal establishment of the YPS agenda, in the UNSC Open Debate earlier in 2015, where the African Union Commission representative describes the 60 per cent of Africa’s population being youth as a ‘huge potential demo- graphic dividend’ [#2]. However, this language persists despite the clear encouragement from the earlier Missing Peace independent report on YPS which argues that ‘it is impor- tant not to see young people exclusively from the perspective of their economic or devel- opmental benefit to society’ and cannot be used solely to see youth as ‘an investment in the future’ (2018, 22 [#8]). The notion of a demographic dividend links the normative claim for youth inclusion to their potential contribution to Member States’ agendas. In 2021, the Chair’s summary of the Peacebuilding Commission Ambassadorial Level meeting connects this potential divi- dend to the willingness of states to ‘invest’ in youth: more efforts need to be exerted to enable youth to realize their full potential … it was critical to harness the demographic dividend through investing more in youth, which is also an investment in peace [#29]. It is true that actual monetary investment in peacebuilding that is for and with youth is significantly lacking, and the document dataset also evidences repeated calls for ade- quate, sustained, financing of youth peacebuilding (see Claeson, Ghanem, and Howell 2023; Mollica 2022). However, the framing of youth as resources to be ‘harnessed’ is reductive and limits the emancipatory promise of the idea of inclusion laid out in early YPS documentation. Youth as deficient Framings of youth as lacking capacity and needing guidance animates tension between the broad recognition of positive youth agency in peace and security building and young people as (intractable) victims of the causes of conflicts and insecurities

text: is for and with youth is significantly lacking, and the document dataset also evidences repeated calls for ade- quate, sustained, financing of youth peacebuilding (see Claeson, Ghanem, and Howell 2023; Mollica 2022). However, the framing of youth as resources to be ‘harnessed’ is reductive and limits the emancipatory promise of the idea of inclusion laid out in early YPS documentation. Youth as deficient Framings of youth as lacking capacity and needing guidance animates tension between the broad recognition of positive youth agency in peace and security building and young people as (intractable) victims of the causes of conflicts and insecurities (including lack of education/illiteracy, poverty, unemployment, among others). These framings can have disciplining effects and circumscribe youth participation. Failure to take youth’s positive JOURNAL OF INTERVENTION AND STATEBUILDING 11 contributions to peace and security seriously can, either deliberately or unwittingly, sustain youth within narrow conceptualisations as always either perpetrators or victims. These narratives of youth are not specific to international political spheres. Tuck and Yang demonstrate the importance of resisting what they term ‘deficit models’ of youth and community development in African American communities in the United States (2014); and the question of youth delinquency has been explored by youth sociologists, anthropologists and development scholars for decades (see illustratively but not exclu- sively: Honwana and de Boeck 2005; Platt 1977; Tilton 2010). Whether in community engagement in urban neighbourhoods in the US or at the UNSC, when youth are framed as either potentially delinquent or potentially vulnerable, they are not able to be included as positive participants. They must instead be managed or protected, and their agential and normative power is deferred. This can be seen in statements by some Member States who are not hostile to youth inclusion inherently, but whose narrative construction of youth sustains their exclusion, such as those discussed in the above two framings. It can also be seen in more pernicious ways, where discursive framings of youth deficit or incapacity are used to invalidate their inclusion and reinstate assumed moral and political orders. It is here we can see how the question of youth inclusion becomes a venue for broader political tensions to play out. Normative agendas require actors with power to advance them, and given the nature of the global political landscape, it is not surprising that certain actors resist these efforts. An excellent example of the ways in which narratives about youth become politi- cised for broader political contestation is the Russian Federation’s engagement with the YPS agenda. The Russian Federation, as one of the P5 at the UNSC, has been opposed to the YPS agenda since its inception, and despite supporting the first resolution have persistently worked to rhetorically undermine its implementation and value.6 This is most evident in the ways in which Russia relies not only on the same discursive framings, but the same language across years (see Table 1). As well as the pernicious effects of this framing as discussed; it is also worth noting that the statement is disingenuous, as the first YPS resolution, Resolution 2250, unanimously adopted by Council members including Russia, defines youth as from 18–29 years of age making the young people relevant to the agenda of ‘legal age’ already. Russia’s intent with this misdirection is evident from their following sentence: ‘The task of promoting a positive image of youth should not overshadow the real challenges we have to address as members of the Security Council’ [#23]. Youth inclusion, for Russia, is not rel- evant to the ‘real challenges’ of concern to the UNSC – what are often referred to as ‘hard security’ issues such as war, sovereignty, national security and so on. In fact, in 2018 at the Open Debate on YPS, Russia argued that there were other UN bodies better suited to ‘youth’ issues such as ‘education, employment, sustainable development and human rights’ (UNSC 2018 [#11]). The exclusion of youth in peace and security processes is justified, as Table 1 shows, by Russia’s argument that youth lack the ‘knowledge and skills’ to engage in ‘activities of such sensitive nature’. In this regard, peace negotiations, for instance, become one of the means of creating ‘conducive environments’ and ‘necessary mechanisms’ for young people to thrive, without necessarily including them. Given one of the key aims of advocates for the establishment of a YPS agenda was to counter the pervasive idea of youth as vulnerable and/or delinquent, especially in the context of the P/CVE agenda and focus of Member States in 2014–15 (Altiok 2024; Berents and Prelis 2020; Ensor 2021), it is revealing that for Russia, meetings on the YPS agenda are taken as opportunities to reiterate their narrative of youth vulnerability and the appropriate role of the UN: ‘United Nations anti-terrorism mechanisms could

id: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security_13
source: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security.pdf
source_name: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security
text: of creating ‘conducive environments’ and ‘necessary mechanisms’ for young people to thrive, without necessarily including them. Given one of the key aims of advocates for the establishment of a YPS agenda was to counter the pervasive idea of youth as vulnerable and/or delinquent, especially in the context of the P/CVE agenda and focus of Member States in 2014–15 (Altiok 2024; Berents and Prelis 2020; Ensor 2021), it is revealing that for Russia, meetings on the YPS agenda are taken as opportunities to reiterate their narrative of youth vulnerability and the appropriate role of the UN: ‘United Nations anti-terrorism mechanisms could and should be useful in helping young people by promoting stronger cooperation on the part of the international community in shielding young people from the influence of ter- rorist and radical ideologies’ [#23]. Youth, in Russia’s discursive framing, do not have agency or capacity in the present; their position as having future potential is predicated on the State supporting their development, and ‘external stakeholders’ not ‘meddling in the internal affairs of sovereign States’ by ‘indoctrinating and brainwashing’ youth [#23]. Youth as exceptional While most characterisations of youth within the dataset of formal documents fall into the above three framings, there are many examples of places where youth’s positive peace- building activities are profiled or referenced, which we briefly discuss to conclude this section. Youth who are recognised as agential and as positive peacebuilders in the present within the documents are presented as exceptional, and their actions are framed as worthy of highlighting because of their novelty. Sukarieh and Tannock (2018) identify the ways that the idea of ‘youth as ideal peacebuilder’ is deployed to elicit support and turn young people away from radicalisation, violence, and extremism. The ways in which youth’s positive agency is rendered exceptional shows the normative tensions of the agenda – if they are meant to be violent or deficient, then their self-directed peacebuilding activity in the present is the way in which their activities JOURNAL OF INTERVENTION AND STATEBUILDING 13 can be recognised. In this framing, youth who do participate in peacebuilding are made legible by exceeding what is expected of youth. Discursive constructions of youth as not only lacking, but this lack as tied to their future promise, and as valuable only in terms of being non-agential resources for non- youth and institutional stakeholders, collectively work to limit efforts for meaningful inclusion of youth. This frame is evident in other contexts where young people are active or activist about issues, and scholarship notes that this is often gendered in powerful ways; for example Malala Yousafzai (Berents 2016), Indigenous girls’ climate activism (Robinson 2023), or Greta Thunberg’s global status (Ryalls and Mazzarella 2021). These same patterns, with their own specificity, can be seen in other peace and security agendas such as the WPS agenda’s construction of gender and the ideal woman (see Henry 2021), as well as in the pervasive impacts of neocolonial and racist assumptions about who has legitimacy and authority in global politics (see Sen 2023). In limiting claims for inclusion and discursively constructing a certain type of youth who can be included, the dataset shows that despite early enthusiasm for the agenda, its evolution and implementation has often been constrained and interpreted narrowly by some actors. (Re)producing compliance The narrow interpretation, and the constraints of framings of which youth should be included and what their inclusion looks like, serve to (re)produce compliance with existing institutional structures and processes. The YPS agenda is situated by Member States who are supportive of the agenda as an extension of existing agendas and mechanisms. This positioning legitimises

text: Sen 2023). In limiting claims for inclusion and discursively constructing a certain type of youth who can be included, the dataset shows that despite early enthusiasm for the agenda, its evolution and implementation has often been constrained and interpreted narrowly by some actors. (Re)producing compliance The narrow interpretation, and the constraints of framings of which youth should be included and what their inclusion looks like, serve to (re)produce compliance with existing institutional structures and processes. The YPS agenda is situated by Member States who are supportive of the agenda as an extension of existing agendas and mechanisms. This positioning legitimises the agenda as belonging within the remit of concerns of the UNSC (and beyond); what Berents and Mollica refer to as ‘reciprocal institutional visibility’ of mutually reinforcing agendas (2022). However, this positioning is tempered and discur- sively limited. In Resolution 2250, the first YPS resolution, youth participation is called for but only ‘as appropriate’. For example, the resolution: ‘Calls on all relevant actors, including when negotiating and implementing peace agreements, to take into account, as appropriate, the participation and views of youth’ ([#5], italics added). Such caveating reflects the ways in which youth are seen as discussed in the previous section; and they also demon- strate a reluctance to make any significant changes to the way things are done. Youth are praised for their work on-the-ground and in emergency response throughout the dataset [i.e. #4, #34]. However, the documents also evidence youth themselves speaking back to the ways in which these limited sites of ‘appropriate’ engagement are seen. For example, at the Arria-formula meeting in December 2022, Nyanar Koul, a young South Sudanese woman peacebuilder, briefed the meeting. The implications of technocratic approaches were made clear by Koul, who observed that: Once the dust of revolution settles, youth voices get silenced; their abilities are questioned and their critical contributions to security and political discussions are side-line. [#34] As Soo Ah Kwon has noted, high level events can ‘include’ youth, but their partici- pation is ‘a depoliticised practice of consensus’ (2019, 6); even global events that are centrally about youth inclusion can evidence the ‘operationalization of 14 H. BERENTS AND R. FOSU participatory governance, one that allowed those in power to espouse the importance of youth and their participation with minimal engagement and accountability’ (2019, 7). From the ways in which youth are present (or not) in the dataset, this minimal engagement and accountability is even more starkly evident in fora like the Security Council where youth inclusion is seen as a benevolent act rather than a necessary right of the youth themselves. This self-affirming position is reiterated in both the 2020 and 2022 Secretary-General reports where simply the numbers of youth briefers are tallied and represented in graphs [#20 and #31]. This lack of meaningful changes reflects a taken-for granted logic of technocratic efficiency – standardised rules and procedures offer neutral and efficient solutions (Mac Ginty 2012). Technocracy prioritises ‘capacity’ defined here as the ability to operate within standardised rules and procedures. In the context of the YPS agenda, this demands that youth possess specific capacity to ‘fit’ into existing institutional mechan- isms. However, this definition of capacity is unidimensional: the UN is the default capacity defined by established rules and processes that must be learned and internalised. This could mean that youth inclusion is considered appropriate if they demonstrate the capacity to preserve institutional processes. What is often ignored is how the UN as an institution can develop its own capacity to efficiently include diverse capacities that youth possess. Capoccia (2016, 1097) argues that when institutional incumbents control the

id: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security_15
source: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security.pdf
source_name: Constituting  Youth   Conditionality and Compliance in UN Discourses on Youth  Peace and Security
text: and procedures. In the context of the YPS agenda, this demands that youth possess specific capacity to ‘fit’ into existing institutional mechan- isms. However, this definition of capacity is unidimensional: the UN is the default capacity defined by established rules and processes that must be learned and internalised. This could mean that youth inclusion is considered appropriate if they demonstrate the capacity to preserve institutional processes. What is often ignored is how the UN as an institution can develop its own capacity to efficiently include diverse capacities that youth possess. Capoccia (2016, 1097) argues that when institutional incumbents control the timing of the institutional reform agenda, they can resist bottom-up pressure for change by delaying action until the salience of reform among the target population has waned, thus making it harder for political entrepreneurs to assem- ble and sustain a coalition for change. Changes during such moments are hardly automatic and highlight moments of contesta- tion between ruler-makers and rule-takers (Streeck and Thelen 2005). Furthermore, tech- nocracy does not only offer a ‘facilitative framework’ for peace and security, but a determinate effect on the nature of peace, actors involved, and the peace produced (Mac Ginty 2012). Thus, how institutions interact or do not interact with people offer insights into both the structural and agentic dimensions of peace and security (Mac Ginty 2018). Conclusion By their nature, normative agendas emerge in and mark periods where significant changes can occur, because they represent long term consensus that existing institutional mechanisms and structures do not work or respond effectively to issues. Institutionalists theorise this as critical juncture (Capoccia and Kelemen 2007) or moments where agency can influence structure significantly through deliberate and intentional reform to shift material and nonmaterial resources to cause significant changes. In technocratic insti- tutional environments like the UN, institutional resistance occurs discursively with the aim of attenuating rather than rejecting normative change agendas like the YPS. This can occur through initial broad reinterpretation of formal rules to accommodate norm entrepreneurs and subsequent interpretation of a normative agenda for institutional compliance. While the invitation of youth to participate in global peace and security building is an important normative turn towards a positive view on youth agency, JOURNAL OF INTERVENTION AND STATEBUILDING 15 there is need to be attentive to official discourses about youth as peace and security builders. How is youth participation talked about and on what terms are youth invited to participate in peace and security? In examining the discursive environment of the YPS agenda in the UN bodies concerned with peace and security, this article reveals the ways in which the performance of politics in formal statements, meeting minutes, agendas, and plans, can be seen to constitute and be constitutive of the ‘stickiness’ of institutions despite ostensible progress on inclusive peace agendas, and the tensions that animate such encounters. The YPS agenda has been pulled since inception between inherent tensions, where competing narratives of positive youth capacity and always-potential delin- quency shape institutional approaches to ‘inclusive peace’. We have argued that the construction of youth agency in futuristic terms, framing of youth as resources to be ‘harnessed’ towards other stakeholders’ goals, and the sometimes subtle and sometimes overt re-inscription of the common stereotype of youth as deficient and lacking all work to defer or deny youth capacity, while positioning their official invitation to participate as a process of ‘learning to become’. The prevalence of these framings of youth participation produces institutional compliance that falls short of being able to address underlying sources of insecurities such as inequal- ities, global injustices, and structural violence. Lessons from the YPS agenda

They do not represent the broader political discursive contestations evident in formal UN arenas. Disclosure statement No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s). Funding This research was enabled through an Australian Research Council DECRA Fellowship [DE200100937, 2020–24] held by Helen Berents. Notes on contributors Helen Berents is a feminist scholar centrally interested in the interconnected areas of the presence and roles of young people in global politics; everyday experiences of conflict and peacebuilding; and local-global relations in peace and security governance. She is a Senior Lecturer in the School of Government and International Relations at Griffith University. She has recently completed an Australian Research Council DECRA Fellowship that examined youth leadership and inclusion in the context of the emergent global ‘Youth, Peace and Security’ agenda. She is the author of Young People and Everyday Peace (2018), and co-editor of Children, Childhoods and Global Politics (2023, with J Marshall Beier) and Youth and Sustainable Peace (2024, with Siobhan McEvoy-Levy and Catherine Bolten). She lives and works on the unceded lands of the Turrbal and Yuggera Peoples. Richard Fosu is a Lecturer in Politics and International Relations at the Department of Politics and IR at Monash University. He obtained his PhD in International Relations from the same university in 2023 and his thesis examined the politics of inclusion in local peacebuilding in Uganda. He is inter- ested in inclusive approaches to peacebuilding, transitional justice, politics of knowledge pro- duction, critical decoloniality and the African Union. He is currently working with a team on two research projects at the Monash Global Peace and Security (GPS) Centre. The projects funded by Global Affairs Canada and the Australian Civil Military Centre (ACMC) investigate women’s inclusion in security sector institutions and UN peace operations. He has conducted fieldwork in South Sudan, the Central African Republic, the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda, and Ghana as well as with uniformed personnel in the United Kingdom. He has published in Disasters, Africa Spectrum, Inter- national Journal of Qualitative Methods, African Security, and Peacebuilding among others. parties (Nilsson 2012, 246–50). However, one group has been left understudied in this growing field, youths. Meanwhile, in 2015 the United Nations Security Council adopted resolution 2250 on youth, peace, and security which states “that youth should actively be engaged in shaping lasting peace and contributing to justice and reconciliation, and that a large youth population presents a unique demographic dividend that can contribute to lasting peace and economic prosperity if inclusive policies are in place” (2015, 2). The resolution goes on to identify five pillars for action: participation, protection, prevention, partnerships, and disengagement and reintegration. It also urges member states to give young people a greater voice in decision- making at the local, national, and international arena and to set up mechanisms for young people to participate meaningfully in peace processes (United Nations Security Council 2015; UN n.d.). Resolution 2419 further strengthens the commitment of the UN to enhancing youth’s role in conflict resolution arguing that “their [youth’s] marginalization is detrimental to building sustainable peace and countering violent extremism” (United Nations Security Council 2018, 2–3) After the adoption of resolution 2250, many have stated the need to include youths in peace negotiations to create a better and more lasting peace (United Nations Security Council 2018; 2019; 2020; United Nations and Folke Bernadotte Academy 2021, 1–14; Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 35–61; Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 7). However, these claims have not been substantiated with evidence of a relationship between youth inclusion in peace agreements and strong and lasting peace. Advocacy for youths’ participation in peace negotiations is widespread yet there is little evidence in support of the idea that youth inclusion contributes to peace. This is the research gap that I will study. The goal of the thesis is to research the relationship between meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements, the inclusion of youths in peace negotiations in a way where they can meaningfully express their views, and vertical legitimacy, the belief from a population that state bodies have a right to legislate and implement laws and regulations that David Edberg Landeström 2 affect them. Vertical legitimacy is part of the midterm conceptualization of peace called legitimate peace (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 74). My research question is as follows: how does meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements affect youth’s view of the vertical legitimacy? The discussion of youth inclusion is important because, in the long term, the endurance of a peace agreement depends on whether the younger generations accept or reject it and their perceptions of what the peace process has achieved. The future leaders, facilitators, and stakeholders will emerge from the current youths which make their engagement in peace processes today all the more important (Siobhán McEvoy-Levy 2001, 1–40; Siobhan McEvoy-Levy 2001, 89–96). I argue that, in line with previous research on inclusive peace agreements, meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements will lead to more youth-centered peace agreements that are more representative of youth’s views and opinions, which, in turn, will result in more vertical legitimacy. I will conduct a comparative case study using the Colombian peace agreement in 2016 where youths used their voice in different ways to affect the peace process, to the peace agreement in Guatemala in 1996, where there was inclusion of civil society and women’s organizations but not youths. The dependent variable will be measured as the relative change in vertical legitimacy among youths in the year after the agreement was signed, to the five following years. The study does not find any support for the hypothesis. There was not enough data available for Guatemala to make any estimates of

legitimacy. I will conduct a comparative case study using the Colombian peace agreement in 2016 where youths used their voice in different ways to affect the peace process, to the peace agreement in Guatemala in 1996, where there was inclusion of civil society and women’s organizations but not youths. The dependent variable will be measured as the relative change in vertical legitimacy among youths in the year after the agreement was signed, to the five following years. The study does not find any support for the hypothesis. There was not enough data available for Guatemala to make any estimates of vertical legitimacy to compare with Colombia. The implications of this will be further discussed in the analysis and limitations. However, given the data collected it does not indicate any support for the hypothesis either. While Colombia had meaningful youth inclusion in its peace negotiations, it did not achieve a positive change in vertical legitimacy among youths. Trust in the government was generally low in 2017, youths viewed it as absent, and some youths had even begun to return to organized violence. From 2018 to 2022, the situation worsened in Colombia. The lack of implementation of the peace agreement, the handling of the COVID-19 pandemic, and economic developments all led to massive protests led by youths. Further, recruitment of youths into armed groups increased in the years after the peace agreement was signed. Regarding Guatemala, given the empirics that I have collected, my data points to a low vertical legitimacy in the years after the agreement was signed. In 1997, there was a high prevalence of violent youth gangs and excessive violence from police forces against youths David Edberg Landeström 3 was also prevalent. This seemed to stay the same in 1998 to 2002, there was little trust in the government or the police to be able to do anything to improve youth’s situation. Survey data from that time showed low trust in the government and its institutions. However, due to the lack of information, it could just as likely be that I have been unable to capture the data that shows high vertical legitimacy in the country. Future research could try to answer this research question using field research and not to rely on reports and newspaper articles. This paper will proceed as follows. I will first present a literature review where I discuss previous literature within the study on inclusive peace agreements. This will lead me into discussing the research gap. Thereafter I will present important concepts for the thesis. The theory and hypothesis for the study will be presented afterward. Afterwards, I will present and discuss the research design for the study, the chosen method, case selection, how the variables have been operationalized, and other design considerations that are important for the study. Later, I will present the results for both cases variable by variable. The results will then be analyzed. Finally, I will draw conclusions from the study and point ahead where future research could explore. David Edberg Landeström 4 2. Theoretical Considerations In this section, I will conduct a literature review on the study of inclusive negotiations and its relationship to peace and what has been written about youth and inclusive negotiations. Thereafter, I will discuss important concepts for the study. Afterward, I will discuss the theory to be studied and the hypothesis drawn from it. 2.1.
