Edberg Landeström 4 2. Theoretical Considerations In this section, I will conduct a literature review on the study of inclusive negotiations and its relationship to peace and what has been written about youth and inclusive negotiations. Thereafter, I will discuss important concepts for the study. Afterward, I will discuss the theory to be studied and the hypothesis drawn from it. 2.1. Literature Review To discuss inclusion in peace agreements I first need to examine the research around exclusion in peace agreements. There are many arguments for why peace processes should be an exclusionary process. Excluding internal hardliners is argued to lead the parties to be better able to negotiate an acceptable agreement for both sides. Further, more parties in the negotiations mean more positions on the different issues at stake and the possibility that some of these preferences will be so incompatible that it is impossible to reach an agreement. A related issue is that the communicative ability of the negotiations can be reduced with more parties. More parties can also mean less time for each to make their point. Lastly, it can lead to an increased risk of some parties acting in a disruptive manner and drowning out other voices in the negotiations (Kew and Wanis-St. John 2008, 22–23). All in all, from this standpoint, including more parties unnecessarily complicates the negotiations which will reduce the chance of a peace agreement being signed. As a response to these arguments, researchers have argued for different benefits of inclusion in peace agreements. Inclusion can circumvent spoiler problems to a peace process if the excluded actor has limited goals such as recognition, or a share of power. In this way, peace becomes more sustainable (Stedman 1997, 15). Moreover, by including civil society actors, the legitimacy of the peace process among the population is argued to improve. This will lead to greater implementation of the agreement and a more durable peace. Civil society can also assure that there is a people-focused agenda in the negotiations where not only elite interests are represented at the table. This effect is argued to be especially profound in nondemocracies as civil society can speak for the people that are not represented by the warring actors. Lastly, it is argued that civil society inclusion will create dynamics of accountability towards warring parties which improves the implementation of the agreement (Kew and Wanis-St. John 2008, 23–24; Belloni 2008, 199; Pfaffenholz, Kew, and Wanis-St. John 2006, 67–74). David Edberg Landeström 5 Several studies have measured the impact of civil society inclusion on peace agreements. Kew and Wanis-St. John (2008, 11) observed a wide variety of peace agreements and found a correlation between civil society inclusion and the sustainability of the following peace agreement. Likewise, a statistical analysis by Nilsson (2012, 243–66) shows that the inclusion of civil society actors in a peace settlement increases the durability of peace. This effect was particularly profound in nondemocracies. Additionally, Paffenholz (2014b, 75) in-depth study of twelve peace agreements showed that civil society organizations were effective in getting issues addressed during the negotiations or in post-agreement agendas. Though, they usually were not able to maintain involvement after the peace agreement was reached. When civil society was able to engage in monitoring the implementation phase, they were very effective. The study also revealed that the impact of civil society was strongest from mass action outside of the negotiations. Moreover, Aljets, Chacko and Jessop (2008, 106–7) showed that civil society can help to ripen the conditions and facilitate the success of a peace process. Similarly, Blaydes and De Maio (2010, 22–24) compare three peace processes: Mali,

were effective in getting issues addressed during the negotiations or in post-agreement agendas. Though, they usually were not able to maintain involvement after the peace agreement was reached. When civil society was able to engage in monitoring the implementation phase, they were very effective. The study also revealed that the impact of civil society was strongest from mass action outside of the negotiations. Moreover, Aljets, Chacko and Jessop (2008, 106–7) showed that civil society can help to ripen the conditions and facilitate the success of a peace process. Similarly, Blaydes and De Maio (2010, 22–24) compare three peace processes: Mali, the West Sahara, and Sudan. They find that exclusivity in peace negotiations leads to political violence as outside spoilers seek representation at the negotiation table. Together these findings support the argument that civil society inclusion is important for peace agreements to lead to durable peace. However, just including civil society is insufficient to achieve a more durable peace. Paffenholz & Ross (2016, 199–210) argue that peace negotiations need to be correctly designed to allow the parties to be able to reach a consensus on core points of contention. Similarly, Jewett (2019, 117–34) argues that while civil society inclusion is necessary for effective conflict mediation, it is also insufficient. Civil society organizations are uniquely able to contribute with local knowledge, they have broad support bases and are not seen as a threat to the disputants. However, they also lack the full toolkit needed to carry out a mediation process from start to finish. Thus, making a peace process more inclusive by adding civil society to the table is not enough to expect that durable peace will be achieved. While some studies have looked at civil society inclusion in general, others have focused on specific types of actors. Krause et al. (2018, 985–1016) look at the relationship between women’s participation in peace agreements and the durability of peace. They argue that linkages between women signatories and women civil society groups result in better peace agreements and a higher implementation rate which leads to a more durable peace. Through a quantitative study, they find a strong correlation between women signatories and the David Edberg Landeström 6 durability of peace. They further substantiate the argument with qualitative case study evidence that demonstrates how women signatories and women’s civil society groups collaborate to positively impact the peace process. Other studies have, through qualitative case studies found similar results that the strength of women’s influence is strongly correlated with agreements being reached and implemented. What was important here was the influence that women had on the process of peacemaking, not the number of women present at the table (Paffenholz et al. 2016, 7). An example of women’s contributions to peace processes comes from Northern Ireland and the Women’s Coalition which were able to bring social justice into the agreement. Thanks to them, more provisions were written targeting children and young people, education, community development, and also robust statements that promote gender equality and women’s participation (McWilliams 2016, 12–13). While there have been some studies on youth inclusion in peace processes, these have mainly been single case studies or descriptive in nature. Grizelj (2017, 1–26; 2019, 164–188) focuses on the space for youth in dialogue and mediation processes in Myanmar. The author finds that youth are often excluded from many of these mediation processes as they are viewed as inexperienced by older generations while, at the same time, they are not given sufficient capacity development to apply their knowledge and skills in the field. Because of this, young people create their own space for dialogue and mediation.

2016, 12–13). While there have been some studies on youth inclusion in peace processes, these have mainly been single case studies or descriptive in nature. Grizelj (2017, 1–26; 2019, 164–188) focuses on the space for youth in dialogue and mediation processes in Myanmar. The author finds that youth are often excluded from many of these mediation processes as they are viewed as inexperienced by older generations while, at the same time, they are not given sufficient capacity development to apply their knowledge and skills in the field. Because of this, young people create their own space for dialogue and mediation. This takes place predominantly within their youth communities and towards other collectives of youth as well as building state- and national-level youth networks. Because of these networks, youths perceive themselves as having a unique capability of building bridges and networks across conflict lines. Similarly, Altiok & Grizelj (2019, 1–42) assesses youth participation and inclusion in peace processes from young people’s viewpoint. They find that young people’s proximity to peace agreements does not necessarily correlate with influence over the agreement. Youth-led movements outside the negotiations such as mass action can be more powerful than their presence as observers, or documenters. Cabanes Ragandang & Podder (2022, 609–28) look at the causes of youth inclusion in the post-accord Bangsamoro Transition Authority in the Philippines. They study how youth’s vertical integration in political institutions is influenced by the structural association and youth’s agential capacity. Structural association, the extent to which the young Moros are involved in and relate to the formal structures and institutions through which the post-accord peacebuilding mechanism operates, enables elite youth’s entry into the formal space. David Edberg Landeström 7 Meanwhile, agential capacity, the personal attributes of the young Moros as evident in their educational attainment, professional experience, technical savviness, and social skills, shapes the nature of that inclusion over the long term. Thus, the peace processes in Bangsamoro tended to be very exclusionary where mostly elite youths were able to gain entrance. Research Gap The research on inclusive peace negotiations has thoroughly examined the effects that civil society can have on peace negotiations and the following peace. There may be some downsides as to the effectiveness of the negotiations. This is offset by everything that civil society brings to the negotiations such as grassroots perspectives and legitimacy for the process. The research on women’s inclusion has further illustrated the benefits of inclusive negotiations as it can further improve the agreement and the implementation process. Relative to the research on women’s inclusion, the research on youth inclusion has not come as far. It has been able to show how and why youths are excluded from some processes but also the perspectives that youths may be able to contribute to peace negotiations. However, this research has not yet studied whether youth inclusion is effective or not. There have been no comparative studies to gauge the contribution of youth in peace processes. This is the research gap that I aim to contribute to in this thesis. 2.2. Conceptual Definitions I will here discuss and define concepts that are important for the study. Independent Variable I will use the independent variable (IV) meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations. Based on the Paffenholz typology (2014b, 76–78), there are nine models of inclusion for civil society organizations (including youth organizations). These are direct representation at the negotiation table, observer status to the negotiations, official consultative forums, less formal consultations, inclusive post-agreement mechanisms, high-level civil society initiatives before or parallel to official negotiations, public participation, public decision-making, and mass action. Of note, regarding the typology, these models

in this thesis. 2.2. Conceptual Definitions I will here discuss and define concepts that are important for the study. Independent Variable I will use the independent variable (IV) meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations. Based on the Paffenholz typology (2014b, 76–78), there are nine models of inclusion for civil society organizations (including youth organizations). These are direct representation at the negotiation table, observer status to the negotiations, official consultative forums, less formal consultations, inclusive post-agreement mechanisms, high-level civil society initiatives before or parallel to official negotiations, public participation, public decision-making, and mass action. Of note, regarding the typology, these models of inclusion are not mutually exclusive, therefore more than one model can occur in a peace agreement simultaneously. However, the proximity of youth to the negotiations does not automatically correlate with influence over the agreement (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 7; Oczelik et al. 2021, 25). For David Edberg Landeström 8 example, even though the Joint Galkayo Youth Committee were close to the peace process in Galkayo, Somalia they often struggled with being viewed as credible partners and having their youth voices heard by the community elders (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 24). Moreover, the peace negotiations in Myanmar relied on youths in the technical teams for logistical and documentation support. Consequently, inclusion of youth was limited to supportive roles where they were rarely able to contribute to the peace agreement (Grizelj 2019, 175). Therefore, meaningful youth inclusion must entail something more than just their presence. Otherwise, there is a risk that what appears to be inclusion is tokenism where youths have little influence over decision-making and are only present to add a sense of legitimacy to a top-down created peace agreement. Youth inclusion should not be able to contribute to vertical legitimacy unless the inclusion is meaningful. Hence, instead of youth becoming a box to tick or merely represented in youth issues, meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations entails listening to what youth voices express, how they envisage the transition to peace, what relationships they aim to transform, and what solutions and resources they offer (Oczelik et al. 2021, 25). Which models of inclusion are conducive to meaningful youth inclusion will be discussed in the operationalization of the concept. Dependent Variable The dependent variable (DV) I will study is vertical legitimacy. Vertical legitimacy refers to the authority, consent, and loyalty to the state and its institutions given by the people. It thus concerns the population’s relationship to the state and whether the population believes that the government has the right to rule over them. If there is no vertical legitimacy the people might challenge the state which can lead to armed conflict. Without vertical legitimacy, states cannot rule over their constituency (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 73–74). Vertical legitimacy is part of the midterm conceptualization of peace called legitimate peace. Legitimate peace is centered on vertical and horizontal legitimacy. Vertical legitimacy concerns the legitimacy between a state and the population, while horizontal legitimacy has to do with the relationship between different groups within a society. Legitimate peace is a midterm concept in the sense that it falls between the minimalist (absence of war) and maximalist (absence of different types of structural violence and sometimes the presence of social justice) conceptions of peace. Both come with their separate issues. Minimalist peace is too narrow, by equating the absence of war with peace, it hides other forms of violence that can be just as detrimental to a society. Whereas the second concept, maximalist peace, is so David Edberg Landeström 9 expansive that it becomes utopian and often impossible for post-conflict societies to achieve (Themnér

within a society. Legitimate peace is a midterm concept in the sense that it falls between the minimalist (absence of war) and maximalist (absence of different types of structural violence and sometimes the presence of social justice) conceptions of peace. Both come with their separate issues. Minimalist peace is too narrow, by equating the absence of war with peace, it hides other forms of violence that can be just as detrimental to a society. Whereas the second concept, maximalist peace, is so David Edberg Landeström 9 expansive that it becomes utopian and often impossible for post-conflict societies to achieve (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 64–73; Söderström, Åkebo, and Jarstad 2021, 486–88; Wallensteen 2015, 1–21; Davenport, Melander, and Regan 2018, 1:36–71). Comparatively legitimate peace is a reasonable conceptualization of peace as it sets a realistic benchmark for post-conflict states to achieve. Further, it is difficult to perceive how peace can last without the relationship between the state and the population, and the relationship between citizens of different groups. While legitimate peace is important, I only will focus on vertical legitimacy in this paper. When the state has strong vertical legitimacy, peace is more likely to be durable as the state will have the voluntary consent, authority, and loyalty of the population. This reduces the risk that any group in society withdraws their consent to wage armed conflict against the government (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 74). Youth There are many different definitions of the term youth throughout the world. Unlike other forms of identity, youth is a transitional phase understood as the period between childhood and adulthood. While many countries define youth based on a specific age group for statistical reasons, youth is traditionally a period that is defined by rites of passage from childhood to youth, and youth to adulthood. These vary depending on cultures and contexts (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 13; Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, x). The United Nations Security Council resolution 2250 states that “the term youth is defined in the context of this resolution as persons of the age of 18-29 years old, and further noting the variations of definition of the term that may exist on the national and international levels, including the definition of youth in the General Assembly resolutions A/RES/50/81 and A/RES/56/117” (2015, 1). Since there are many different definitions of the term youth circulating globally, it is beyond my scope to specify who is, and who is not, a youth in a specific cultural context. Further, groups in countries culturally viewed as youths should be more like each other than just looking at age brackets as youths face similar transitory milestones in life to progress from youth to adulthood. As such, it makes sense to compare groups who are culturally viewed as youths and not age brackets (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 9–12). Youth Organizations David Edberg Landeström 10 Inspired by the definition used by Paffenholz et al. (2016, 14) for women’s organizations, I will here define youth organizations as more or less organized groups, such as delegations of youth, youth civil society organizations, networks, or coalitions. Youth delegations are defined as groups of actors set up for the specific purpose of inclusion into a negotiation or implementation process. Youth civil society organizations are defined as having the following criteria: they are voluntary organizations interacting in the public sphere whose objectives, interests, and ideologies focus mainly on youth and youth issues. Coalitions are made up of different youths coming together out of concern for a specific cause or issue, and networks are considered interconnected coalitions of different youth

organizations as more or less organized groups, such as delegations of youth, youth civil society organizations, networks, or coalitions. Youth delegations are defined as groups of actors set up for the specific purpose of inclusion into a negotiation or implementation process. Youth civil society organizations are defined as having the following criteria: they are voluntary organizations interacting in the public sphere whose objectives, interests, and ideologies focus mainly on youth and youth issues. Coalitions are made up of different youths coming together out of concern for a specific cause or issue, and networks are considered interconnected coalitions of different youth organizations. 2.3. Theory I will study the research question: how does meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements affect youth’s view of vertical legitimacy? Previous studies on the topic of inclusive peace negotiations have argued that by broadening participation in peace negotiations the process will be more legitimate as it becomes representative of a broader set of identities and interests. This in turn leads to more legitimacy which results in durable peace (Hirblinger and Landau 2020, 307). The reasoning is that broadening participation to civil society actors makes the process more representative of the rest of society. The negotiations are then not only a top-down process where elites from the warring factions divide the country among themselves. Instead, the negotiations contain notions of peacebuilding from below where civil society represents the people that are not present at the table. Civil society will then bring up issues on the agenda which are relevant to the population at large. These are issues that otherwise would be left unattended by the warring parties (Nilsson 2012, 248; Belloni 2008, 198). Peace agreements alone are insufficient to transform conflicts into peaceful societies. All of society needs to be incorporated to build peace. Including civil society in the peace negotiations creates a pro- agreement atmosphere which leads to self-reinforcing and sustainable peace. In comparison, if the population does not perceive the final agreement to be representative of their ideas, they are more likely to revolt against the elite and further violence might ensue. Whereas, if civil society can represent the issues of the population at the negotiation table, they can create a ‘buy-in’ for the agreement resulting in the following peace to be perceived as more legitimate by the population (Kew and Wanis-St. John 2008, 23–24; Paffenholz 2014a, 9–10). David Edberg Landeström 11 These arguments provide a good understanding of why civil society inclusion would lead to improved legitimacy. However, I have not yet made clear why these theories should be extended to youth and youth organizations. Often constituting most of the population in countries with armed conflicts, youths are some of the most affected by war today. Either as victims of violence, combatants, or both (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 9, 13; Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 12–13). Yet, their position in peace processes is still disputed. If youths’ needs and interests are not represented in the political processes for peace they may become alienated and try to achieve their goals through other means such as returning to violence (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 32). Youths are also critical for the future peace in post-war countries as they are the purveyors of historical memory and residual trauma to the next generation (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 103). In sum, it is imperative to include youths in peace negotiations as they often constitute a large part of the population, they can use violent means if not properly represented in the negotiations and will also be part of shaping the

alienated and try to achieve their goals through other means such as returning to violence (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 32). Youths are also critical for the future peace in post-war countries as they are the purveyors of historical memory and residual trauma to the next generation (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 103). In sum, it is imperative to include youths in peace negotiations as they often constitute a large part of the population, they can use violent means if not properly represented in the negotiations and will also be part of shaping the future of the country. According to Themnér and Ohlson (2014, 79), when it comes vertical legitimacy, three factors are presented which together can affect the vertical legitimacy within a state: the idea, physical basis, and institutional expression of the state. To build statehood, a common idea that structures the foundation which the government is built upon is needed. This idea helps to distinguish one political community from another and provide a rationale for why members of a community should accept the rule of the governing elite. These commonly held ideas are formed by aspects such as history, tradition, culture, ethnicity, religion, nationalism, ideology, as well as democratic and economic efficiency. Further, they are usually a product of historical developments: top-down efforts to form a collective identity, bottom-up processes where groups build states around local traditions and symbols, inter-group dynamics, or some combination of all these aspects. The physical basis of the state refers to materialistic features such as well-defined borders, population, resources, and wealth. It is difficult (if not impossible) to uphold a functioning government without the proper income generated from different resources and populations that can be taxed and work within the bureaucracy of the state. Without these resources, citizens of a country may become dependent on other actors within the state, if the government cannot uphold control over its claimed territory (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 79). David Edberg Landeström 12 Lastly, the institutional expression of the state relates to whether there are institutions and a cadre of bureaucrats in place for the government to functionally govern its territory and community. These bureaucratic systems are required for the government to be able to develop and implement policies that can make citizens look favorably upon them (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 79). I suggest that meaningful youth inclusion will affect all three of these factors. The idea of the state will be affected as meaningful youth inclusion changes the inter-group dynamics. By including youth, it becomes clear that they are an important part of the government structures where they have the agency to help rebuild the state after a conflict. It creates a new collective identity where youthhood is de-securitized and not excluded due to traditional patrimonial structures. At the same time, youths are given a reason to accept the ruling elite as youths have helped build the structures upon which the society is ruled upon. By including youths in the peace negotiations and allowing them to voice their opinions on matters important to them, the risk of a return to violence is lessened among youths. This further strengthens the physical basis of the state as the government will better be able to uphold the rule within their territory. Lastly, meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations will also have an impact on the institutional expression of the state as youths will affect the peace agreement which decides what policies the government is supposed implement. Through these three functions, meaningful youth inclusion will contribute to greater vertical legitimacy. Consequently, as seen in

peace negotiations and allowing them to voice their opinions on matters important to them, the risk of a return to violence is lessened among youths. This further strengthens the physical basis of the state as the government will better be able to uphold the rule within their territory. Lastly, meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations will also have an impact on the institutional expression of the state as youths will affect the peace agreement which decides what policies the government is supposed implement. Through these three functions, meaningful youth inclusion will contribute to greater vertical legitimacy. Consequently, as seen in Figure 1, I suggest that the meaningful inclusion of youths in peace negotiations creates more vertical legitimacy. This is because, through meaningful inclusion, youths will have a possibility of providing input into the peace process and create a more youth-focused agenda where issues that are important to them will be included in the peace agreement. When issues important to youths are accounted for, they will perceive the process to be representative of youths as a group. If youth feel represented in the peace process the identity of youth will be de-securitized as they are viewed as part of society and not a group that is excluded from decision-making. Youth will then perceive the state to be a legitimate representative of themselves. The causal mechanism is necessary for the independent variable to lead to the dependent variable. Without it, there is no reason why youths should feel higher vertical legitimacy. David Edberg Landeström 13 Meaningful Youth Youth Input Into Youth Vertical Legitimacy Inclusion Peace Agreement Representativeness Figure 1: Causal Mechanism 2.4. Hypothesis Based on the presented theory, I will test the hypothesis that: meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements will lead to higher vertical legitimacy among youths in the post-conflict period. 2.5. Scope Conditions The scope condition for my study is that there needs to have been an intra-state conflict where a peace agreement has been signed. I thus aim to speak to all cases of intra-state conflict with signed peace agreements and whether youth inclusion could contribute to vertical legitimacy. David Edberg Landeström 14 3. Research Design In this section I will go through the choice of method, the case selection strategy used, how the theoretical concepts have been operationalized and other design considerations relevant to the study. Important to note about the subject of this study is that youth inclusion in peace negotiations is a rare event. What this means is that youth are rarely included in peace negotiations today (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 9; Nilsson and Svensson 2023, 8). This has begun to change after resolution 2250 was adopted. However, it is still a rare event which has different implications for my study and will be discussed further concerning the research design of the study. 3.1. Choice of Method To study the presented hypothesis, that meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements will lead to vertical legitimacy, I will conduct a comparative case study. This is done for multiple reasons. To begin with, as youth inclusion is a rare event there is not enough data available to conduct a large-n statistical study. However, the choice of method is not only a choice of necessity. By conducting a comparative case study I can capture the concepts more deeply and look at non-comparable observations from my chosen cases (Gerring 2017, 19). This allows me to achieve a greater understanding of my cases as opposed to a statistical study. This is useful as there is not much known about youth’s contributions to peace negotiations yet (Gerring 2017, 20). Lastly, by conducting a

reasons. To begin with, as youth inclusion is a rare event there is not enough data available to conduct a large-n statistical study. However, the choice of method is not only a choice of necessity. By conducting a comparative case study I can capture the concepts more deeply and look at non-comparable observations from my chosen cases (Gerring 2017, 19). This allows me to achieve a greater understanding of my cases as opposed to a statistical study. This is useful as there is not much known about youth’s contributions to peace negotiations yet (Gerring 2017, 20). Lastly, by conducting a comparative case study I can attempt to open the black box of my cases to gain greater causal inference than a statistical study could (Powner 2015, 98). To conduct my comparative case study, I will use the method of structured focused comparison. The method is structured in that the researcher writes general questions that relate to the research objective and asks them to all the chosen cases, thereby making a systematic comparison between them. The method is focused as it only looks at the aspects of the cases that the researcher is interested in (George and Bennet 2005, 67). I am only interested in the effect of youth inclusion on vertical legitimacy and no other factors. I will therefore only ask questions relating to those topics. This approach helps to steer the empirical analysis in a theoretically relevant direction. Structured focused comparison is also advantageous as it does not fall into the trap of single case studies, where data is not collected David Edberg Landeström 15 comparably, and therefore cannot contribute to the cumulative knowledge of the subject (George and Bennet 2005, 70). While a comparative case study is mainly interested in the correlation between the IV and DV, I will also operationalize the causal mechanism to study whether it is present or not. A common technique for studying causal mechanisms is process tracing (Beach and Pedersen 2013, 1). However, I will not go in-depth enough into the cases for process tracing. Therefore, that method is not used in the study. 3.2. Case Selection In this thesis, I aim to study the relationship between meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations and vertical legitimacy. To do this, the case selection strategy should exemplify quasi-experimental properties. Such properties attempt to replicate the virtues of a true experiment even though it is lacking in the manipulated treatment. Qualitatively, this procedure is best done by picking cases that vary across space, or time (Gerring and Cojocaru 2016, 397). I will pick two cases that vary across space as those cases are more likely to provide generalizable results. As the purpose of the study is to assess the IVs effect on the DV, I will pick cases based on the variation in the IV (Gerring and Cojocaru 2016, 401). Another reason to pick cases on the IV is because youth inclusion is a rare event. Picking cases on the DV is not likely to reveal any information about youth inclusion. Based on the proposed theory, I will use a most-similar research design, where both cases are as similar as possible on all control variables while exhibiting differences in the IV, and the DV is unknown. This means that one case will have meaningful youth inclusion, and the other will not exhibit any meaningful youth inclusion in its peace negotiations. This allows me to gauge the effect of the IV on the DV while controlling for other explanations (Gerring and Cojocaru 2016, 402). A most-different research design does not seem appropriate for this study as it

any information about youth inclusion. Based on the proposed theory, I will use a most-similar research design, where both cases are as similar as possible on all control variables while exhibiting differences in the IV, and the DV is unknown. This means that one case will have meaningful youth inclusion, and the other will not exhibit any meaningful youth inclusion in its peace negotiations. This allows me to gauge the effect of the IV on the DV while controlling for other explanations (Gerring and Cojocaru 2016, 402). A most-different research design does not seem appropriate for this study as it involves selecting cases on extreme values on the DV which introduces selection bias. A most-different research design can be useful in some cases to identify causal variables or build theories, bring light to anomalies that current theories cannot explain, or as theory- infirming cases about theories with necessary conditions (Gisselquist 2014, 479–80). However, that is not the case here and therefore a most similar design is more appropriate. David Edberg Landeström 16 The unit of analysis is peace processes. By peace process, I refer to the negotiation period and the signing of the peace agreement. Therefore, the results could be relevant to both countries and regions in a country going through peace processes. While youth inclusion in peace agreements is a rare event, Altiok & Grizelj’s (2019) paper covers different occurrences of youth participation in peace processes. However, not all of them are relevant to my study as they cover post-agreement processes, or for other reasons are not suitable for my study. For example, South Sudan had a peace agreement signed where youth had a significant presence in the negotiations with direct representation at the table, observer status, consultative forms, and mass action. Though, the agreement was signed in 2018 and therefore not enough time has passed for me to be able to study the peace following the signing of the peace agreement (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 17–18; UCDP 2022c). Still, there are cases where youth have participated in some capacity that are suitable for the study. The chosen cases, based on the presented selection criteria are the Colombian peace negotiations (2012-2016) between the Government of Colombia and the Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, FARC), and the Guatemalan peace negotiations (1994-1996) between the Government of Guatemala and Unidad Revolucionaria Nacional Guatemalteca (Guatemalan National Revolutionary Unity, URNG). These cases provide a good cross-case comparison as they differ in the amount of youth inclusion in the peace processes while also being similar in many ways as they are part of the same region. As can be seen in Table 1, they are similar in factors that are possible alternative explanations for the DV. Both peace agreements contain provisions relating to disarmament, demobilization, and reintegration (DDR). DDR is important as it ensures that the state regains a monopoly on violence throughout its territory. Without DDR it is difficult for the state to claim that it is the sole source of government and may therefore not achieve legitimacy among its constituency (“FINAL AGREEMENT TO END THE ARMED CONFLICT AND BUILD A STABLE AND LASTING PEACE” 2016, 221; “Agreement on a Firm and Lasting Peace” 1996, 2; Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 80). Both agreements had civil society and women’s inclusion which contributed to civilian-focused peace agreements. In this sense, the agreements are not based on exclusive identities that only represent certain groups in society and national interests are not imposed from above that dictate different groups in society. This is important for vertical legitimacy as it allows the different groups

source of government and may therefore not achieve legitimacy among its constituency (“FINAL AGREEMENT TO END THE ARMED CONFLICT AND BUILD A STABLE AND LASTING PEACE” 2016, 221; “Agreement on a Firm and Lasting Peace” 1996, 2; Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 80). Both agreements had civil society and women’s inclusion which contributed to civilian-focused peace agreements. In this sense, the agreements are not based on exclusive identities that only represent certain groups in society and national interests are not imposed from above that dictate different groups in society. This is important for vertical legitimacy as it allows the different groups in society to build confidence and trust in the state (“FINAL AGREEMENT TO END THE ARMED David Edberg Landeström 17 CONFLICT AND BUILD A STABLE AND LASTING PEACE” 2016, 62–79; Inclusive Peace & Transition Initiative 2017, 2; Bramsen 2022, 1; Herbolzheimer 2019, 48–51; Carl 2019a, 52–55; 2019b, 59–60; Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 80). One important difference between the cases is that the Guatemalan peace agreement did contain security sector reform (SSR) while the Colombian agreement did not. This is important as SSR regulates the behavior and norms of the armed forces, police force, and intelligence units so that they do not constitute a threat to citizens and the government. However, the implementation process of the security sector reform in Guatemala has been lacking (Isacson 2022; “Peace Accord Matrix: Accord for a Firm and Lasting Peace” n.d.; Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 80–81). Therefore, I do not think that it is a problem for my study that they are different in this regard. Cases Colombia Guatemala Meaningful Youth Yes No Inclusion Civil Society and Yes Yes Women’s Inclusion DDR Yes Yes Yes, but not SSR No implemented properly Table 1: Case Selection While the theory speaks to all intra-state peace processes, the cases picked are not able to represent all these cases. The conflicts in Colombia and Guatemala are conflicts over government mainly fought over political incompatibilities (UCDP 2022a; 2022b). This can have important implications for what other cases the findings of the study will apply to. Vertical legitimacy is likely to look very different in countries where the conflict is over other issues than political incompatibilities. Youths from different groups might have different amounts of trust for the government whether their ethnic group is represented in the David Edberg Landeström 18 government or not. Similarly, conflicts over territory, or where the incompatibility is religious might also look different than the cases picked in this study. Thus, while the study can further the knowledge of meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations, the generalizability of these findings to conflicts fought over other issues might be limited. 3.3. Operationalization Independent Variable The operationalization of meaningful youth inclusion is guided by Paffenholz (2014b, 76– 77) typology on nine models for civil society inclusion in peace negotiations. However, not all the models of inclusion are conducive to meaningful inclusion. As previously mentioned, inclusion is meaningful if the actor can voice their opinion and influence the peace agreement through their actions. Looking at the typology of civil society inclusion, I argue that youth will be meaningfully included if they are present through direct representation at the negotiation table, official consultative forums, less formal consultative forums, public participation, or mass action (Paffenholz 2014b, 76–77). Firstly, through direct representation at the negotiation table as a delegation or as members of another delegation youth will have the most theoretical possibility to meaningfully influence peace negotiations. It gives youth the same status as the main parties to the conflict. Like other stakeholders in the negotiations, youth will be able to bring

agreement through their actions. Looking at the typology of civil society inclusion, I argue that youth will be meaningfully included if they are present through direct representation at the negotiation table, official consultative forums, less formal consultative forums, public participation, or mass action (Paffenholz 2014b, 76–77). Firstly, through direct representation at the negotiation table as a delegation or as members of another delegation youth will have the most theoretical possibility to meaningfully influence peace negotiations. It gives youth the same status as the main parties to the conflict. Like other stakeholders in the negotiations, youth will be able to bring up issues that matter to them and build provisions that can make it into the agreement (Paffenholz 2014b, 77–78). Secondly, through official consultative forums, youths can be part of forums that run parallel to the negotiations. Through the forums, youth would be able to advise the negotiations on specific issues while not being present at the negotiation table. A problem here can be the distance to the negotiation table where the possibility to affect the negotiations is limited. The forums can also be ignored by the official negotiations (Paffenholz 2014b, 80– 81). Thirdly, less formal consultative forums suffer even more than official consultative forums from a distance to the negotiation table. These are forums that lack the approval of all the stakeholders in the negotiations. However, they can be used to mobilize civil society and put pressure on the stakeholders. Thus, while youths are further from the negotiations they can still contribute by pressuring the conflict parties and advocating for the youth’s needs on different issues (Paffenholz 2014b, 82). David Edberg Landeström 19 Fourthly, public participation such as hearings, citizen panels, and opinion polls can be an important part in connecting the peace negotiations to large segments in the population. Youths can organize themselves and together bring forth issues that matter to them in these public hearings. They can be powerful tools to put pressure on the stakeholders and to sustain the peace process. It also allows people to have a say in the process and the negotiators and mediators to better understand the public’s opinion and need’s (Paffenholz 2014b, 85–86). The last form of meaningful inclusion is mass action such as protests or online campaigns to mobilize significant portions for their cause. While this is the model furthest away from the negotiation table it can still have a big effect if enough youth are mobilized for a certain goal. When not included in any other way, this allows youths to still be able to affect and be a part of the peace process (Paffenholz 2014b, 87–88). As mentioned earlier, mass action outside of the negotiations can be one of the most effective ways of affecting the peace agreement (Paffenholz 2014b, 75; Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 1–42; Paffenholz and Zartman 2019, 5). An issue facing all these models of inclusion is that youths can be ignored or sidelined in different ways in all of them. However, I argue that the stipulated types of inclusion are the ones that are most likely to be conducive to meaningful youth inclusion. I will operationalize meaningful youth inclusion as follows: • Are youth included in the peace process either as a delegation to negotiations, as members of official delegations, through official consultative forums, public participation, or mass action? Meaningful youth inclusion will be measured as the presence or absence of any of these types of inclusion. While only one type of inclusion is needed for there to be meaningful youth inclusion, more types of inclusion should lead youth to be better able to voice

stipulated types of inclusion are the ones that are most likely to be conducive to meaningful youth inclusion. I will operationalize meaningful youth inclusion as follows: • Are youth included in the peace process either as a delegation to negotiations, as members of official delegations, through official consultative forums, public participation, or mass action? Meaningful youth inclusion will be measured as the presence or absence of any of these types of inclusion. While only one type of inclusion is needed for there to be meaningful youth inclusion, more types of inclusion should lead youth to be better able to voice their opinion on the issues important to them which should lead to a more positive effect on vertical legitimacy. The variable will be measured during the negotiation phase before the final peace agreement has been signed. Data for the variable will be obtained from news articles, reports, and research articles. This operationalization of meaningful youth inclusion is reliable because the risk of measuring a case differently in different instances is low (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 99– 101). There is no question about interpreting whether youth were included in the peace negotiations. The operationalization is valid in that it has high face validity, the measurement captures what it says it will capture. The concept has high content validity in that all the David Edberg Landeström 20 central elements of the concept are there (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 102). Meaningful youth inclusion does not only refer to whether youth were present but also how they could use their voice. Regarding construct validity, I see no issue in the measurement as the operationalization does not aim to capture the views that youth have of either the government or other groups in society (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 102). Dependent Variable The DV, vertical legitimacy, refers to the authority, consent, and loyalty to the state and its institutions given by the people (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 74). It will be operationalized as the relative change in the vertical legitimacy of a given state (Themnér and Ohlson 2014, 76). The variable will be measured in the years following the agreement, to measure the change in legitimacy over time. For Colombia, I will study 2017–2022, and for Guatemala, I will study 1997–2002. Testing the hypothesis will be done by comparing the relative change in vertical legitimacy between the states. Therefore, I will estimate a baseline vertical legitimacy in the countries the first year I collect data and then evaluate how vertical legitimacy has changed in the following years. As mentioned earlier, it is hypothesized that there will be higher vertical legitimacy in the country where youth had meaningful inclusion in the peace agreement. Thus, for the hypothesis to be supported in the study, the change in vertical legitimacy needs to be higher in Colombia than in Guatemala. To measure the DV, I will focus on youths as a group. This is because my theory only speaks to how youths are affected by youth inclusion. Moreover, as Themnér and Ohlsson (2014, 77) states, it can be desirable to focus on groups that have been identified as having much more violent agency. Youth represents one of these groups. Youth then serve as a least likely case. If they see state institutions and other groups positively, then there is reason to believe that vertical legitimacy is present in society at large. Vertical legitimacy is understood as the relationship between the people of a country and the state. It will be operationalized as: • How do youths in the country view the government? Do they believe that it represents their interests?

and Ohlsson (2014, 77) states, it can be desirable to focus on groups that have been identified as having much more violent agency. Youth represents one of these groups. Youth then serve as a least likely case. If they see state institutions and other groups positively, then there is reason to believe that vertical legitimacy is present in society at large. Vertical legitimacy is understood as the relationship between the people of a country and the state. It will be operationalized as: • How do youths in the country view the government? Do they believe that it represents their interests? Are youths protesting the government? • How do youths in the country view state institutions such as the police, local administration, and courts? • Are youths cooperating or working together with the government in any way? • Do we see youths taking part in organized violence? David Edberg Landeström 21 Data on vertical legitimacy will be collected from news articles, reports, and research articles. Regarding how to measure vertical legitimacy, there might be some problems with the reliability of the concept as it is difficult to capture the attitudes of a populace without going into the field. For example, youth is a heterogenous group (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 12), one set of youth can support the government while other youths might not. This issue should be reduced by systematically capturing data from similar sources for both cases. There are also issues with measurement bias as the youth voices that I can gain access to might not be representable to the greater youth population in the country. And some youths can be systematically underrepresented in English written media. This is an issue that is difficult to overcome. However, it is important to be aware that it exists. And a biased but reliable operationalization is better than the other way around as there is then knowledge of how the data may be skewed (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 99– 101). Concerning validity, I argue that the operationalization of vertical legitimacy accurately captures the concept. There is high face validity as the measurements capture the attitudes of youths in the country (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 102). For example, if there are youth riots or protests against the government then it would be a sign of low vertical legitimacy as they are showing a lack of loyalty to the state and the government's right to rule over them. Content validity is high as all the central elements of the concept are captured by the measurement (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 102). By capturing both attitudes and actions I can gain a more complete picture of legitimacy than if I only measured one of them by itself. Lastly, there is high construct validity as it is not close in its measurement to the independent variable (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 102). Causal Mechanism In the theoretical reasoning presented earlier, I suggest that meaningful youth inclusion in a peace agreement will lead to a higher vertical legitimacy. This is because the peace agreement will include youth-related provisions and therefore be more representative of youth’s needs and interests. Based on this I will measure the causal mechanism as: • Are there provisions in the peace agreement that mention/target youth? • Have youth in the country felt that the peace negotiations have been able to represent their thoughts and opinions? David Edberg Landeström 22 Data on whether the agreement includes provisions targeting youths will be collected from the peace agreements signed by the parties, and data on the representativeness of the agreement will be collected from news

vertical legitimacy. This is because the peace agreement will include youth-related provisions and therefore be more representative of youth’s needs and interests. Based on this I will measure the causal mechanism as: • Are there provisions in the peace agreement that mention/target youth? • Have youth in the country felt that the peace negotiations have been able to represent their thoughts and opinions? David Edberg Landeström 22 Data on whether the agreement includes provisions targeting youths will be collected from the peace agreements signed by the parties, and data on the representativeness of the agreement will be collected from news articles, reports, and research articles. Data collection for the causal mechanism faces similar challenges as collecting data for the DV. It can be difficult to collect data on youths’ ideas, whether they felt that the peace agreement was representative of them or not through desk research if no one else has asked these questions before. Further, not all youths might be able to voice their opinion on the agreement in English written media on the issue. These are issues that affect the possibility to collect data on the causal mechanism in a systematic and unbiased way. The reliability of my measurement may thus be hampered (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 99–101). I argue that the operationalization has high validity. The face validity of the measurement is high because the measurement is very close to the causal mechanism itself. Further, content validity is high as all the central parts of the concept of representativeness are included in the operationalization. Lastly, the operationalization has high construct validity as it is not related to other concepts used in the study (Kellstedt and Whitten 2013, 101–2). 3.4. Design Considerations Before proceeding to the results of the study, some design considerations need to be discussed. Regarding case selection, there are many good reasons to compare the peace process in Colombia with the peace process in Guatemala. They are similar in significant ways while being different on the IV, meaningful youth inclusion. However, there are also downsides regarding the generalizability of the study. Both conflicts were conflicts over government between leftist guerillas and the government. Some armed actors in the Guatemalan conflict stated that they fought for the rights of indigenous people and recruited members from these groups as well however the incompatibility itself was political (UCDP 2022a; 2022b). These structural features make the conflicts different in many ways as compared to for example conflicts that are mainly fought between ethnic groups. These circumstances can have substantial implications for vertical legitimacy since it may be very different whether the conflict was fought over ethnic issues. Therefore, the findings might not be generalizable to the wider population to which the theory speaks. Future research could investigate the relationship between meaningful youth inclusion and vertical legitimacy in other contexts to build a broader understanding of the relationship. David Edberg Landeström 23 Further, while the cases are similar in many ways, there are also differences between them that can act as confounding variables for the study. One big difference that could affect the possibility of vertical legitimacy is that the agreements were signed almost 20 years apart with the Guatemalan agreement being signed in 1996 and the Colombian agreement being signed in 2016. In 20 years, a lot can change which I cannot account for in the study. One significant difference because of this time difference is the Covid-19 pandemic which was in full force and Colombia was hit especially hard being ranked 22nd out of 187 countries in deaths per 100,000 people. The country also faced the worst economic

as confounding variables for the study. One big difference that could affect the possibility of vertical legitimacy is that the agreements were signed almost 20 years apart with the Guatemalan agreement being signed in 1996 and the Colombian agreement being signed in 2016. In 20 years, a lot can change which I cannot account for in the study. One significant difference because of this time difference is the Covid-19 pandemic which was in full force and Colombia was hit especially hard being ranked 22nd out of 187 countries in deaths per 100,000 people. The country also faced the worst economic recession in its history with real GDP contracting 7% in 2020 (Prada, Garcia-Garcia, and Guzman 2022, 1). In 2020 the implementation of the provisions was slowed down because of the pandemic. Especially processes that require the mobilization of civilians and resources on the local level since the pandemic had debilitated citizen agencies and resources were diverted to crisis management (Joshi et al. 2020, 1). These are consequences of the pandemic that can have substantial effects on vertical legitimacy that were not present in the Guatemalan peace process. However, these factors can also make the Colombian case a least-likely case for the theory. If the hypothesis is supported in the study then it would be a strong argument for the theory (Gerring and Cojocaru 2016, 404–5). Another factor that is different between the cases is that after the peace agreement in Guatemala, armed conflict in the country subsided, while Colombia, after the peace agreement with FARC, still had an active conflict with Ejército de Liberación Nacional (National Liberation Army, ELN). After the agreement was signed in 2016, ELN showed willingness to begin negotiations with the government. However, president Iván Duque was more hardline than his predecessor and the negotiations broke down early. Moreover, some FARC dissidents did not agree with the negotiations and the signed agreement and decided to continue their armed conflict in 2018 (UCDP 2022a). Thus, while the conflicts in Colombia after 2016 were on a much lower scale than previously there still was conflict in the country. This might bias my results against my theory as the other conflicts in the country might affect how youths view the legitimacy of the state. Another consideration to make for the study is that youth inclusion is a rare event. While this has been discussed concerning the choice of method and case selection, it can also have other implications for the study. For example, the cases where there is youth inclusion may be fundamentally different in some way from cases without youth inclusion. The results might then not be generalizable to the wider population of cases. Looking at other studies about David Edberg Landeström 24 inclusion in peace agreements, I do not find this likely. The inclusion of civil society and women’s groups has grown in recent years into more and more peace processes. Peace agreements with civil society and women’s inclusion have not turned out to be systematically different from other agreements and youth inclusion is likely to become more prevalent after resolution 2250 was adopted (United Nations Security Council 2015; Krause, Krause, and Bränfors 2018; Nilsson 2012). If peace agreements with civil society or women’s inclusion are not systematically different from cases where they are not included, I cannot see any reason why peace agreements with youth inclusion should be any different either. It is possible that youth inclusion has been ignored, as their contribution to peace processes has not been recognized until now (Simpson, UNFPA, and UN. Peacebuilding Support Office 2018, 35–61).
