David Edberg Landeström 25 4. Colombia 4.1. Background Information The conflict between the Government of Colombia and left-wing guerillas started in the 1960s after ‘La Violencia’ (1948-1958), a period where landowners organized peasant clients to launch attacks on neighboring villages. During ‘La Violencia’ the government also fought against liberal guerillas wishing to overthrow the government. The groups fought the government until an agreement was reached between the parties. After this, the Government's conflict with left-wing guerillas intensified. It was during this time in the early 1960s that the Southern Bloc Guerilla was formed with an outspoken agenda to overthrow the government. In 1966 the group renamed itself FARC and adopted a more pro-soviet agenda. During the 1970s the guerilla groups started financing their armed conflict using coca cultivation. The conflicts between the government and various left-wing guerilla groups continued during the 1980s and 1990s and while there were attempts at peace negotiations, they were unsuccessful (UCDP 2022a). During the years 2000 to 2010 there were further conflicts between FARC and the Government. Attempts to begin negotiations were also made, though these did not lead anywhere. In 2012, peace talks began again, this time supported by Norway and Cuba. The talks centered on larger issues than only to cease the armed conflict, such as socioeconomic reforms and discussions on land issues. The negotiations continued until August 2016 when both parties signed the agreement to end the conflict, cease hostilities and surrender weapons. However, during the referendum that was held regarding the agreement, a small majority of Colombians voted against the agreement. The parties met again and revised the agreement which was sent to the Colombian Congress and accepted on the 24th of November by President Santos (UCDP 2022a). 4.2 Inclusion of Youths in the Colombian Peace Negotiations The peace negotiations in Colombia were structured as a bilateral negotiation between the Colombian government and FARC. Thus, there were no seats at the table or any other kind of direct participation for youths in the negotiations. However, the process included several other participation mechanisms available for outside parties (Mendes 2019, 287). When the negotiations began in 2012, the Colombian government and FARC requested that the UN mission to Colombia together with the National University of Colombia organize David Edberg Landeström 26 public consultations on different themes that would shape the peace negotiations and the agreement. During the negotiations, a total of five national and four regional forums were organized on the topics of Comprehensive Rural Development Policy, Political Participation, Illicit Drugs, Victims of the Conflict, End of the Conflict, and Implementation and Verification. In total, almost 8000 people participated in these forums including women, young people, political parties, human rights organizations, academics, indigenous people, the private sector, trade unions, farmers, and victims. Key discussion points and proposals were captured by facilitators and streamlined into a consolidated report and submitted to the negotiating parties for consideration. There were different criteria for participation in each thematic forum, and quota systems were put up for women and youth. Young people constituted a

on the topics of Comprehensive Rural Development Policy, Political Participation, Illicit Drugs, Victims of the Conflict, End of the Conflict, and Implementation and Verification. In total, almost 8000 people participated in these forums including women, young people, political parties, human rights organizations, academics, indigenous people, the private sector, trade unions, farmers, and victims. Key discussion points and proposals were captured by facilitators and streamlined into a consolidated report and submitted to the negotiating parties for consideration. There were different criteria for participation in each thematic forum, and quota systems were put up for women and youth. Young people constituted a particularly large role in the victim’s forum as they were especially affected by forced recruitment and gender-based violence. At these forums, young participants proposed that the sons and daughters of combatants should be treated as victims and that the use of child soldiers by both military and FARC forces be addressed. They further asked for two young former combatants to be invited to the Havana negotiations to share their stories, reflecting on their human rights violations and challenges of transitional justice for FARC members who were recruited as children. Lastly, young people pushed for investment in public education as a tool for building peace, alternative economic avenues for rural areas to minimize dependence on illegal crop growth, and a truth commission for victims of the conflict (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 26). During the negotiations in Havana, five sets of victims’ delegations were sent to present their testimonies (The Coalition for the International Criminal Court, n.d., 27; Brett 2022, 475). These delegations were not meant to represent Colombia’s eight million victims, but aimed at illustrating what the negotiating parties defined as a universe of victims and victimizing events within the confines of the conflict. In total, 60 individuals were selected. The victims were between 19 and 78 years old (Brett 2022, 481–82). After the negotiations in Havana had finished, the peace agreement was put to a public referendum on the 2nd of October 2016. To many, the agreement was contentious and divided the population. People opposing the agreement argued that FARC had been given too much. As a result, the public rejected the agreement with a slight majority (50.2%). But within a few hours after the rejection a large youth pro-peace movement had been created. Through social media hashtags (#PazAlaCalle, #RespiraPaz, #LaPazesImparable, and #MarchaporlaPaz), networks of university students mobilized over 30,000 young people onto the streets of David Edberg Landeström 27 Bogota. The demonstrations called for the government and FARC to restart their negotiations. The student-led movement was within a week joined by people from all socioeconomic backgrounds that were calling for the parties to renegotiate the agreement. A month later a revised peace agreement was signed by the parties and passed in Congress (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 30; Henao-Izquierdo, n.d., 6). In sum, youth were included in the Colombian peace negotiations through public participation in the consultations organized by the National University of Colombia and the UN mission to Colombia, official consultative forums as youths took part in the delegations sent to Cuba where the negotiations were held, and mass action as they initiated the pro-peace protests after the agreement had failed to achieve popular support in the referendum. 4.3 Vertical Legitimacy Among Colombian Youths Regarding youth’s view of the government, in interviews done by Berents (2018, 170–71) in Los Altos de Cazucá, some young people began to express a real hope for peace in Colombia after the peace agreement was signed while others still bore a deep skepticism towards the agreement. There was a deep distrust that a government

official consultative forums as youths took part in the delegations sent to Cuba where the negotiations were held, and mass action as they initiated the pro-peace protests after the agreement had failed to achieve popular support in the referendum. 4.3 Vertical Legitimacy Among Colombian Youths Regarding youth’s view of the government, in interviews done by Berents (2018, 170–71) in Los Altos de Cazucá, some young people began to express a real hope for peace in Colombia after the peace agreement was signed while others still bore a deep skepticism towards the agreement. There was a deep distrust that a government that is often absent, corrupt, or unwilling to help is now invested in helping marginalized communities. Similarly, there was distrust about whether FARC, who were directly responsible for the deaths of family members and damage to their communities, was committed to the process and the role of political life in Colombia. One young person told Berents in early 2017 that their community is still facing the same problems as it did during the conflict. Nothing had changed. Whereas another young person she knew had posted a picture on social media celebrating the peace agreement. Youths have also described their understanding that the government does not provide them with enough tools to take part in the peace process. Youth representatives in Valledupar, Santa Fé de Antioquia, and Villavicencio all described the governing authority as weak or absent in their rural communities. This affected their ability to implement the accord as they lacked budgetary authority and local autonomy, which made them reluctant to become engaged in the process (Leiva 2017). Other youths also talked about the challenges they faced as the government failed to provide them with adequate political and financial support for peacebuilding efforts (John and Secretariat for the Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security 2017, 12). David Edberg Landeström 28 Further, surveyed youths reported a breakdown in trust in the government and politics overall. This came from a history of conflict and recent political developments. Young Colombians in the survey felt disengaged with traditional forms of political participation and did not believe that politics and politicians could create a change in society (British Council 2019, 37–47). More than 40% of them also reported an unwillingness to vote as they believed that it could not change the country: ...really, we’re tired of the politicians, of political parties, of anything that has anything to do with politics because they tell you, ‘If you support us, we’ll help fix up the school or we’ll pave the streets’, and in the end, when they're elected, they dust off their hands and it's goodbye. If I saw you, I don't know you, and if I saw you, I don’t remember. So it's tough because they’re always lying to you. (British Council, University of Los Andes, and University of Rosario 2018, 42) While many of them believed that citizen participation was important, few of them believed that voting in local elections or joining a political organization is the best way for citizens to get involved. As a female student said, “Seeing as everything is getting worse and... we [young people] don’t have the chance to weigh in, to get involved in the political narrative ourselves... I’d rather make a difference for myself and go to school, study and get ahead” (British Council, University of Los Andes, and University of Rosario 2018, 38–39). Though this was not the only picture that was drawn by youths questioned. One of the youths who said they were willing to vote stated that “I would be interested in voting. Voting

organization is the best way for citizens to get involved. As a female student said, “Seeing as everything is getting worse and... we [young people] don’t have the chance to weigh in, to get involved in the political narrative ourselves... I’d rather make a difference for myself and go to school, study and get ahead” (British Council, University of Los Andes, and University of Rosario 2018, 38–39). Though this was not the only picture that was drawn by youths questioned. One of the youths who said they were willing to vote stated that “I would be interested in voting. Voting is the voice of the people. It’s how we show what we want, we let in a good person, someone who wants to improve the country or someone who just wants to benefit themselves” (British Council, University of Los Andes, and University of Rosario 2018, 44). In 2018, Colombia elected Iván Duque as their new president. The conservative politician won a decisive victory. However, his presidency was a turbulent time for Colombia. Both, 2019, 2020, and 2021 saw major protests led by youths over the developments in the country. The implementation of the peace agreement had stalled, there was anger over the country’s handling of the COVID-19 pandemic, and many middle-class and poor people were disappointed in his policies. In the 2019 protests, university students went to the streets over issues of corruption and cuts in public education (Grattan 2019). The protests in 2021, which were mainly led by the nation’s youths, started due to a tax reform that proposed increasing David Edberg Landeström 29 taxes for the middle class while 13 billion dollars were lost every year due to corruption, and the Ministry of National Defense was spending 4.5 million dollars on military airplanes. Youths had already felt the consequences of the COVID-19 pandemic and an economic crisis. Many felt that within the current system, they had no future, and they viewed the government as a distant entity that would not listen to them (CIVICUS 2021a; 2021b; Dangond 2021; United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021a, 1–2; Cárdenas 2021; Garcia Cano and Suárez 2021). In 2022, the country was gearing up for its presidential election. The election came at a difficult but important time for Colombia after COVID-19, several big youth-led protests, and lackluster implementation of the peace agreement. Dissatisfaction with the ruling party and the President was high. Gustavo Petro, senator, and former Mayor of Bogota, running for the presidency targeted the younger demographic who represented a quarter of the electorate. Petro promised to overhaul the country’s capitalistic economy, expand social programs, introduce guaranteed work with a basic income, shift the country to a publicly controlled health system, increase access to higher education, and raise taxes on the rich population. Promises that closely correlate with the demands of young protesters from previous years. Some analysts were also saying that youths were energized by his running mate, Francia Márquez, an environmental activist with a gender, race, and class-conscious focus who would be the first black vice president in the country’s history. According to a poll in May, 53% of voters aged 18 to 24 and 45% of the voters aged 25 to 34 said they would vote for Petro. Meanwhile, less than half those numbers said they would vote for the other two presidential candidates, Gutierrez, or Hernández. However, demands on Petro were still high, as Nina Cruz, 27, a café worker said “if Petro can’t follow through, I know we will see the same thing that happened with the Duque government: a social explosion” (Turkewitz 2022; Glatsky 2022).

who would be the first black vice president in the country’s history. According to a poll in May, 53% of voters aged 18 to 24 and 45% of the voters aged 25 to 34 said they would vote for Petro. Meanwhile, less than half those numbers said they would vote for the other two presidential candidates, Gutierrez, or Hernández. However, demands on Petro were still high, as Nina Cruz, 27, a café worker said “if Petro can’t follow through, I know we will see the same thing that happened with the Duque government: a social explosion” (Turkewitz 2022; Glatsky 2022). Even though Petro was directly trying to target young voters, many still felt disillusioned with Colombian politics. Sebastian Rodriguez, 22, who was planning to hand in a blank vote said, "I feel the same people as always are coming back to govern." Similarly, in a survey with 2,200 people aged 18 to 32, two-fifths said they had no faith in the executive branch, congress, or political parties. Additionally, more than half said none of the first-round candidates represented them (France24 2022). After all the votes had been counted, Petro won the election against Rodolfo Hernández with 50.42% of the votes against 47.35% of the votes for Hernández. Turnout for the election David Edberg Landeström 30 was 58%, the highest since the 1988 presidential election (The Carter Center 2022, 3). To some young people the results indicated not only a change in politics but also a change for the entire country, “[i]n 24 years, I have never seen peace, and I feel that now, this new government is a starting point for building this peace we have yearned for. […] Peace would be being able to walk around, going from corner to corner, from neighbourhood to neighbourhood, spending time with our brothers and sisters in the community, as we call it in Buenaventura” (United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2022a). Based on this information, youth’s trust in the government was very low in 2017. Few youths seemed to believe that the government could help them in any way. They believed that there were other ways of engaging and working towards helping their communities. Though some were hopeful about the future and believed that politics was still an important way to make a change in the country. Meanwhile, looking at 2018 to 2022, this was a turbulent time. With big protests and a change in president. Gustavo Petro was elected in large part thanks to the youths and their support of him, the COVID-19 pandemic, lack of implementation of the peace agreement, and economic policies brought forth during the previous government still had a huge impact on youth’s trust in the government. Moving on to the second indicator of youth’s view of state institutions, in 2017, youths had generally low trust in government institutions. In a study by the British Council (2018, 34) Colombian youths reported the highest amount of trust in teachers while other institutions such as the police, municipality, and national government had a much lower reported trust level. However, there was also considerable heterogeneity depending on the size of the city of residence and their distance from a large city which shows a great variety within the country. In the protests in 2021, youths, especially those from the lower classes, demanded dignity and not to be humiliated by the police, which is the main representative of the outrages and humiliation they face daily. Youth described how they felt rejected by society and were persecuted like criminals by the police just because they were young and poor (CIVICUS 2021b; ICG

national government had a much lower reported trust level. However, there was also considerable heterogeneity depending on the size of the city of residence and their distance from a large city which shows a great variety within the country. In the protests in 2021, youths, especially those from the lower classes, demanded dignity and not to be humiliated by the police, which is the main representative of the outrages and humiliation they face daily. Youth described how they felt rejected by society and were persecuted like criminals by the police just because they were young and poor (CIVICUS 2021b; ICG 2021b, 8). Meanwhile, young people from the middle class tended to emphasize the political repression, human rights violations, assassinations of social leaders, and the criminalization of protestors (CIVICUS 2021b). The protestors, who were mostly 16 to 28 years old accused the police of excessive violence against the people marching. The United Nations and human rights organizations expressed concern over the bloodshed, especially in Cali where members of the police fired David Edberg Landeström 31 against protesters. The government failed to give truthful information on the number of victims of police violence during the protests. It was reported between the 28th of April and the 12th of May that 2,110 cases of police violence were reported in Colombia (Dangond 2021; United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021a, 1–2). To protect against police violence, rioters created an organic riot army, the Primera Línea (the First Line), and declared war on the police anti-riot unit, the Mobile Anti-disturbance Squadron (Botero 2021, 11). The generally low trust in state institutions in 2017 looks to be similar for 2018 to 2022. Police violence, political repression, and criminalization of protestors are all things expressed in 2021. Of note is that the protests in 2021 were partly targeted against the police and the justice system. I could not find other data on teachers and municipal authorities for example. This data might therefore be skewed toward the most negative opinions of state institutions. Regarding youths taking part in organized violence or other kinds of violent behavior, while reintegration programs were ongoing, young FARC members continued with the illegal activities that FARC used to do. Around 2017, several mid-ranking FARC officers exited the reintegration process to reclaim the land and illicit trade that the rebels had left behind. These officers popped up in several areas where FARC had previously been present. According to a former combatant, they were often “nobodies” in the guerilla but were well-versed in criminal activity and saw a chance to carve out influence to make their fortune. They were overwhelmingly in their twenties and had little ideological training from their older leadership. They had also started to extensively recruit young people who had no experience or connection to the old FARC. In some areas, residents reported that nearly every family had a member with some connection to the group. Many come from places that are in economic distress in part due to the unmet promises of coca crop substitution and rural development programs (ICG 2021a, 25). In August 2021, the Agency for Reintegration and Normalization reported that there were 2,230 former combatants between the age of 18 and 28 years old (1,457 men and 773 women). 45% of them were involved in productive projects while 80 percent reside outside of former territorial areas for training and reintegration (United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021b, 14). It can often be both difficult and dangerous for young ex-combatants to demobilize since there are security challenges for them relating to their previous status as combatants. This is especially

of coca crop substitution and rural development programs (ICG 2021a, 25). In August 2021, the Agency for Reintegration and Normalization reported that there were 2,230 former combatants between the age of 18 and 28 years old (1,457 men and 773 women). 45% of them were involved in productive projects while 80 percent reside outside of former territorial areas for training and reintegration (United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021b, 14). It can often be both difficult and dangerous for young ex-combatants to demobilize since there are security challenges for them relating to their previous status as combatants. This is especially prevalent in areas challenged by a lack of access where David Edberg Landeström 32 government presence and support structures remain minimal. As the young leader of one project focused on helping youth escape violence in eastern Cali said: With politicians in Colombia, every single one is the same. They all talk tango. They take entire years to execute programmes or to even process the money they promise us for our initiatives. This causes frustration and therefore we find alternative resources from people who like our work. There is not enough support from the government; they do not care about us. Ever since 2008, since we started this project, I have had to fight for resources, which I do not understand since we are giving something meaningful to the community, we are socially aware, we have talent. There is relapse of course—some of the members are now in jail as they had no economic basis or contracts or professional degrees. Beyond a point, we do not have the means to sustain them. It hurts to realize that we cannot do enough, despite the tools that we already provide them with. (Mitra 2022, 93, 96–97) Since the low point in 2016 after the peace agreement was signed, it is estimated that recruitment to armed groups has grown sharply. Community testimonies, and military, government, and civil society estimates of armed group numbers all indicate that they have grown significantly in the past few years. The pandemic and general economic hardships made young people unable to attend school or find gainful employment. Armed groups, therefore, seemed like an enticing option. Many of these recruits were under the age of 25. As one single mother in the outskirts of Bogotá explained, “As a young person, finding work or education opportunities is difficult and costly. But what is easy is becoming a trafficker or a lookout for these groups” (ICG 2023, 12–13) All in all, while reintegration of armed combatants was progressing in 2017, young FARC members were going back to their old ways. This problem seems to have grown bigger in the years afterward with more young people recruited into armed groups. The government also did not seem to be doing enough to aid the reintegration programs. Concerning the indicator of cooperation between youths and the government, some youths in 2017 described how their engagement with different government actors, such as mayors and governors, was important to reestablish communication between young people and the government to influence public policy. Youth-led movements have at times been acknowledged by local governments and have advanced the inclusion of youth-led proposals and institutional agendas on the local level. Further, pressure from young people on Congress David Edberg Landeström 33 led to the launch of an oversight committee on the implementation of peace throughout the country (John and Secretariat for the Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security 2017, 10). As a response to the nationwide protests in 2021, the government announced several policies centered on youth engagement. President Duque

important to reestablish communication between young people and the government to influence public policy. Youth-led movements have at times been acknowledged by local governments and have advanced the inclusion of youth-led proposals and institutional agendas on the local level. Further, pressure from young people on Congress David Edberg Landeström 33 led to the launch of an oversight committee on the implementation of peace throughout the country (John and Secretariat for the Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security 2017, 10). As a response to the nationwide protests in 2021, the government announced several policies centered on youth engagement. President Duque pledged to carry out the first elections of municipal youth councils in November 2021. The councils were for youths aged 14 to 28 years old. Over 1.2 million Colombian youths voted to elect 10,824 of their peers to serve in the council. Using this new platform, youth council members would be able to design, drive, and oversee local-level social initiatives for the benefit of their peers and communities. However, the Constitutional Court indicated that there was a lack of sufficient polling stations in rural areas which affected the participation in the municipal youth council elections. In addition, the Office of the Inspector-General warned of delays and limited support from authorities for the operation of several of these councils. After the election in 2022, young council members expressed their willingness to work closely with the new administration while highlighting the need for broader dialogue and participation of young people. (United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021a, 14; ACDI/VOCA 2022; United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2022b, 15). Moreover, the Government announced the Pact for Youth initiative, which was launched to gather views from youths through workshops held across the country on education, culture, sports, economic inclusion, health, democracy and participation, rural development, peacebuilding, and sustainability. They further announced a public policy on youth contemplating investments of over 8 billion dollars within the next decade on youth-related issues. However, some participants and other youth representatives claimed that the initiative had limited representation as it did not include youths engaged in protests during the national strike or the issues they put forward. The policy also required the Government to inform on the progress in the allocation of monthly allowances to younger former combatants and to support policies designed by youth councils in municipalities with development projects with a territorial focus (United Nations Verification Mission in Colombia 2021b, 14). Cooperation between youths and the government seems to have been about the same from 2017 to the following years. While not a lot, there has been some cooperation between the government and youths in the country that has existed throughout these years. David Edberg Landeström 34 4.4 Causal Mechanism The peace agreement between the Government of Colombia and FARC contains several provisions targeting youths. When discussing political participation in Colombia, the agreement stipulates the promotion of social movements and organizations for young people to gain access to politics and the democratic debate (2016, 35, 42). It also promoted the non- stigmatization of young people to create a culture of reconciliation, coexistence, and tolerance (2016, 46–47). When discussing the cessation of hostilities, youth are targeted in different ways. It is stipulated in the agreement that there would be a gender-based approach where special emphasis is put on the protection of women, children, and adolescents who had been affected by crimes of criminal organizations. A special investigation unit for dismantling criminal organizations would be created which would take a multidimensional approach to deal with the entire criminal chain of the organization including criminal conduct towards women, children,

It also promoted the non- stigmatization of young people to create a culture of reconciliation, coexistence, and tolerance (2016, 46–47). When discussing the cessation of hostilities, youth are targeted in different ways. It is stipulated in the agreement that there would be a gender-based approach where special emphasis is put on the protection of women, children, and adolescents who had been affected by crimes of criminal organizations. A special investigation unit for dismantling criminal organizations would be created which would take a multidimensional approach to deal with the entire criminal chain of the organization including criminal conduct towards women, children, and adolescents (2016, 81, 84, 87). Regarding a solution to the illicit drug problem, the agreement emphasizes that women and young people have been particularly affected by criminal economies. The agreement, therefore, stipulates a new vision seeking evidence-based alternatives and providing a differentiated treatment to the issue of drug use, the problem of crops used for illicit purposes, and organized crime associated with drug trafficking which unduly exploits young people. When tackling the issue of drug use, they specified that their approach should be aware of the relationship between illicit drug use and violence against women, adolescents, and young girls. Special attention will also be given to preventing illicit drug use among youth and children and to supporting strong youth leadership in their communities for them to make a positive contribution to their communities (2016, 104–5, 124, 126). Concerning victims of the conflict, the agreement stipulates the creation of a commission on truth, coexistence, and non-recurrence. It is stated that the commission will take a fitting approach to learn about how the conflict affected youths. To help raise awareness in Colombian society of the specific ways in which the conflict reproduced historical mechanisms of discrimination, as a step towards a more just, and inclusive society (2016, 140, 143). In the same chapter, the agreement stipulates the promotion of the human rights of youths and to adopt measures of affirmative action to fully guarantee the rights of those affected by the conflict (2016, 200–201). David Edberg Landeström 35 Lastly, as part of the chapter on implementation, verification, and public endorsement a special reintegration program would be created in collaboration with the representative organizations of the ethnic peoples, for the reincorporation of demobilized individuals to ensure territorial harmony. For this program, an educational and communication strategy would be agreed upon for the dissemination of the principles of non-racial and ethnic discrimination against women, youth, and girls demobilized from the conflict (2016, 221). Moving on to the representativeness of the Colombian peace agreement among youths. Support for the agreement among youths in Colombia was strong. They have been one of the largest sectors of Colombian society to embrace the agreement and to mobilize and defend the negotiations, adoption, and implementation of it as well (Petcu 2020, 5). To some, the signing of the agreement showed how peace is meant for everyone, not just the government and the rebels. When the revised peace agreement was signed between the Government and Colombia after the protests calling for restarted negotiations a young woman was interviewed about her experience of the movement: “We had never had opportunity to participate in the decisions of our country. The peace process was not for us. But, when the referendum happened, we realized the process is for us. Before the referendum we were spectators. When the referendum happened, we put on the shoes and decided “we are responsible to save our country. Social media was the way we could raise our voices. We are using social media to ask for accountability

was signed between the Government and Colombia after the protests calling for restarted negotiations a young woman was interviewed about her experience of the movement: “We had never had opportunity to participate in the decisions of our country. The peace process was not for us. But, when the referendum happened, we realized the process is for us. Before the referendum we were spectators. When the referendum happened, we put on the shoes and decided “we are responsible to save our country. Social media was the way we could raise our voices. We are using social media to ask for accountability and say to leaders, “this peace process has to be implemented”” (Altiok and Grizelj 2019, 30). However, other youths felt that the process of reaching an agreement had excluded young people and failed to recognize the distinctive peace and security challenges experienced by young Colombians. For the youth that were a part of FARC, it was difficult to contribute to the negotiations in any meaningful way. Subordination and trust in the decisions of experienced senior people in FARC led the youth perspective from the guerilla to be sidelined. As one former combatant said, youths were viewed as recruiters with a military purpose. They were never able to participate in the leadership of internal processes (Henao- Izquierdo, n.d., 7). Young peacebuilders in the country have stated that they did not feel that the government had included them enough in the peace talks (John and Secretariat for the Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security 2017, 12). This sentiment can be seen by other youths who view the agreement as a pact between the government and the guerilla for the laying down of arms whereas it is not them who in the end will build the peace “Peace will be built by civil society and by the victims...the institucionalidad [institutional infrastructure] David Edberg Landeström 36 does not take into account the knowledge, and much less the recommendations, of people who live in the territory, who have suffered the conflict, who in their day to day [lives] are working [for peace]. Until they begin to work with communities, the [accords] will remain in the air and will not be grounded. . .The work of peacebuilding is distinct from laying down of arms and peace accords. Civil society builds the social fabric that was lost during the conflict so that there can be peace in this country. What is required is that we sit together and unite. There are many processes, associations, and youth peace committees in the territory, if we could unite these processes, that would permit us to build, as the accords say, “a stable and lasting peace”” (Lederach 2020, 215–16). Youth representatives in Santa Fé de Antioquia stated that they were not familiar with the details of the peace agreement and that speaking about it openly could get them in trouble with a variety of armed groups still present in the area. Further, along with youth representatives from Valledupar and Villavicencio, they said that the peace process looked too much like a top-down initiative where local involvement was lacking (Leiva 2017). There are several youth provisions or provisions targeting youths in the peace agreement, though that does not seem to have led all youths to perceive the agreement as representative. Youths are the strongest supporters of the agreement and many feel represented by the agreement. However, that sentiment is not shared by everyone. Many still felt underrepresented by the negotiations and the agreement that was signed by the parties. David Edberg Landeström 37 5. Guatemala 5.1. Background Information Conflicts in Guatemala

Background Information Conflicts in Guatemala began in the 1960s after the CIA supported an insurgency in 1954, forcing the sitting president to resign and turn power over to military officers. The following years saw right-wing presidents being elected through fraudulent elections. As a response, left-wing armed groups were formed to overthrow the sitting government. To combat the armed groups the government used different repression tactics which left thousands of civilians dead. In 1982, the left-wing guerillas formed Unidad Revolucionaria Nacional Guatemalteca (Guatemalan National Revolutionary Unity, URNG). The union occurred at the peak of the guerilla group's power with around 7500 members. Following the union, the conflict escalated with the government using a ‘scorched earth’ tactic, indiscriminately targeting villages thought to be supporting the guerilla. Consequently, around 440 villages were destroyed. By the late 1980s, the negotiations between the government and URNG had started, following a stalemate in 1983. In 1996 the parties finally came to an agreement signaling the end of the 36 yearlong conflict. The Commission for Historical Clarification published a report after the conflict ended which found that 200 000 had been killed or ‘disappeared’ between 1962 and 1996 (UCDP 2022b). 5.2 Inclusion in the Guatemalan Peace Negotiations In 1994, the parties of the conflict signed the Framework Accord for the Renewal of the Negotiating Process between the Government of Guatemala and the URNG. In the framework, it is stated that the negotiation parties agreed to set up a civil society assembly, Asamblea de la Sociedad Civil (Civil Society Assembly, ASC), comprising all the non- governmental sectors of Guatemalan society based on the condition that they could demonstrate their legitimacy, representativity, and legality. The role of the assembly was to write consensus documents on substantive themes of the negotiations. Thereafter they would present the documents to the bilateral talks between the government and the URNG. The documents would be treated as recommendations and would not be binding in any way. Lastly, ASC was supposed to endorse the final accords to give them the force of national obligations. The ASC consisted of multiple sectors of organizations. There were religious organizations, trade unions, CACIF (one of Guatemala’s most important business associations however it decided to withdraw from the assembly), the Atlixco conglomeration, political David Edberg Landeström 38 parties, Mayans, women’s organizations, non-governmental organizations, research centers, human rights organizations, and the press. Of note is that youth organizations were not included among these. Together, these sectors represented more than 100 organizations and produced five consensus documents. After writing the consensus documents, ASC continued to play an active role in the Guatemalan peace process by assuming positions on national political matters based on consensus positions already reached, or positions that can be reached. The organization, therefore, situated itself as a reference point for consensus among civil society organizations in Guatemala. For example, they demanded transparency in the 1995 election and denounced human rights violations (Krznaric 1999, 1–6; Brett 2017, 50). In sum, civil society was included in the Guatemalan peace negotiations through the ASC as an official

represented more than 100 organizations and produced five consensus documents. After writing the consensus documents, ASC continued to play an active role in the Guatemalan peace process by assuming positions on national political matters based on consensus positions already reached, or positions that can be reached. The organization, therefore, situated itself as a reference point for consensus among civil society organizations in Guatemala. For example, they demanded transparency in the 1995 election and denounced human rights violations (Krznaric 1999, 1–6; Brett 2017, 50). In sum, civil society was included in the Guatemalan peace negotiations through the ASC as an official consultative forum. However, youths were not included as an organization in this assembly. Therefore, there was no youth inclusion in the Guatemalan peace agreement. 5.3 Vertical Legitimacy Among Guatemalan Youths Regarding youth’s view of the government or their trust in state institutions, little information is available to gain a clear picture. Figure 2 (Latinobarómetro n.d.) shows confidence in the judiciary, national congress, police, political parties, and the president among people aged 15– 25. We see that in 1997, between 60–80% of the respondents in the survey had low or no confidence in these institutions. Whereas in 2002 that same number has risen for all institutions where only the judiciary had less than 80% of young respondents who had low or no confidence in it. These facts indicate that there was very low confidence in the government and its institutions and that was decreasing. David Edberg Landeström 39 Figure 2: Confidence in Government Institutions, 15–25-year-olds The low trust in government institutions, such as the police, can be explained through their excessive violence. Police abuses against youths were a common occurrence. Human Rights Watch (Tucker, Human Rights Watch Children’s Rights Project, and Human Rights Watch/Americas 1997, 9–15) also reported regular police abuse against street youths in 1997. One doctor reported seeing a few beatings a day. Street youths themselves said that the police would accuse them of different crimes and then harass or beat them. They would even steal money and other valuables that the youths were carrying at the time. Young girls would also report sexual harassment from the police forces. When youths were interviewed in the year 2000 about what to do about the problems with gangs in Guatemala and how to find a solution to the country’s problems with them, many felt that the police were too inefficient and irresponsible to properly deal with the issue at hand. This was also a broader reflection of dissatisfaction with the state in general. As one 20-year- old woman said, “...the government isn’t going to worry about you. The government isn’t interested in what happens to families here or young people” (Winton 2004, 91–92). Other youths interviewed said their low trust in the government stemmed from the state not doing what they were promising to do; that the government does not worry about what is important, such as violence against civilians. David Edberg Landeström 40 In sum, while there is very little information, this data points to the low trust among youths in the government and its institutions. This trust furthermore seemed to decrease during the years studied. Moving on, no data has been found that indicates that youths were or were not cooperating with or working together with the government in different ways. This does neither indicate that it has or has not occurred. Simply that no information could be found about it. information, this data points to the low trust among youths in the government and its institutions. This trust furthermore seemed to decrease during the years studied. Moving on, no data has been found that indicates that youths were or were not cooperating with or working together with the government in different ways. This does neither indicate that it has or has not occurred. Simply that no information could be found about it. Concerning youths taking part in organized violence, in 1997, after the agreement had been signed between the Government and URNG, the country was left with easy access to weapons and a large pool of young men with little education and few marketable skills other than handling a gun. While there is no reliable data on the number of youth gangs operating in Guatemala at the time, the extent of the problem with their presence in the communities cannot be underestimated. These gangs were reported to engage in pickpocketing, mugging, theft, bus robberies, kidnappings, bank robberies, and arms and drug trafficking. They were also responsible for a lot of territorial conflicts, rape, and vandalism. Territorial conflicts between gangs are the type of violence that has the biggest ramifications for gang members and non-gang members in their communities. The conflicts may be about controlling a few blocks. The proliferation of firearms has resulted in death from firearms as the most common cause of death among 14–24-year-olds in Guatemala in 1997. The use of violence from these groups is essential in gaining and maintaining social and economic power. Not only against others but also to enforce solidarity within the gangs. The enforced solidarity or loyalty can be through forcing members to commit different acts of violence or threatening people trying to leave gangs (Brands 2010, 12; Winton 2004, 86). Given the danger faced by gang members, it would be logical to think that people would not want to join. But overall gang membership does not seem to be motivated by violence, but rather by wanting to belong to a cohesive social group. As one young woman said, “...you’re protected – you feel like you’re more than other people ... and they support you” (Winton 2004, 87). Gangs are often proliferating in marginal urban areas with precarious living conditions, lack of economic opportunities, and generally poor levels of education which means that youths often face high levels of economic exclusion. In these circumstances, joining a gang becomes a rational choice for youths due to economic incentives (Winton 2004, 87–90). David Edberg Landeström 41 It is difficult to estimate if organized violence and violence against the state have risen among youths since 1997. What can be said is that it has stayed at a high level throughout this period. 5.4 Causal Mechanism The peace agreement between the Government of Guatemala and URNG consisted of multiple agreements written from 1994 to 1996. These agreements rarely mention youths as a group that will be targeted with provisions. In the Agreement on Social and Economic Aspects and Agrarian Situation (1996, 10–12), the parties agree that they aim to improve education and training as they have a fundamental role in the country’s economic, cultural, and political development. This would be done through a greater allocation of resources to education and a change in the curricula to include more bilingual students. The agreement further states paying urgent attention to abuses subjected in the context of hiring through middlemen, sharecropping, payment in kind, and the use of weights and measures against for example young tenant farmers (1996, 25). In the Agreement on the Implementation, Complicance and Verification Timetable for

and Agrarian Situation (1996, 10–12), the parties agree that they aim to improve education and training as they have a fundamental role in the country’s economic, cultural, and political development. This would be done through a greater allocation of resources to education and a change in the curricula to include more bilingual students. The agreement further states paying urgent attention to abuses subjected in the context of hiring through middlemen, sharecropping, payment in kind, and the use of weights and measures against for example young tenant farmers (1996, 25). In the Agreement on the Implementation, Complicance and Verification Timetable for the Peace Agreements (1997, 8), the parties agree to establish a joint commission on education reform, comprising representatives of the Government and of indigenous organizations, to design educational reforms consistent with Guatemala’s cultural and ethnic diversity. The same agreement also stipulates improving the higher education system in the country in multiple ways. They agreed to develop and implement programs such as scholarships, study grants, economic assistance, and other means to help students from lower-income backgrounds. They would develop programs to train teachers and school administrators and provide the University of San Carlos with more funding (1997, 19). In the Agreement on the Basis for the Legal Integration of the Unidad Revolucionaria Nacional Guatemalteca (1997, 18) the parties agreed to set up programs for the integration of all former URNG combatants. The agreement also states that it would treat former combatants, women, young people, and disabled persons as sectors requiring specific priority attention. No information was available whether youths thought the peace agreements were representative of them or not. Therefore, I cannot discuss it here. David Edberg Landeström 42 5. Discussion The analysis will proceed as follows, I will first discuss how I have coded both cases and thereafter whether the theory finds support in the study. Afterward, I will discuss alternative explanations and limitations of the study. Before commencing with the analysis of the cases, I must discuss an important limitation of the study, the availability of sources. Finding sources on Colombian youths’ opinions on the state, government institutions, and if they thought the peace agreement was representative was not an easy task. Though in the end, there was enough information available to gain a comprehensive picture. Concerning Guatemala, this was not the case. Information on Guatemalan youths’ thoughts about the government and state institutions was very rare, if not nonexistent. Most information on youths available from the years after the peace agreement in Guatemala was only focused on the issue of violent youth gangs while rarely discussing any other issue. There is to my knowledge one source, Los Jóvenes Guatemaltecos a Finales del Siglo XX: Informe de Investigación [Young Guatemalans at the end of the 20th Century: Research Report] that surveyed and interviewed young Guatemalans in 1999 about various topics that include young people’s opinions on democracy, the peace agreements, military service, how they would like Guatemala to be and many more topics (United Nations Population Fund 2005, 29). However, I have not been able to gain access to this paper as it only seems to exist in a physical library in Guatemala and another library in Tennessee. My study is therefore limited by the sparse information available. The possibility to draw conclusions from the cases will be restricted. This is for two reasons. Firstly, I am unable to estimate a change in vertical legitimacy in Guatemala if I do not have enough data for the time period that I am examining. After all, I cannot gain a clear picture of the vertical legitimacy was in 1997, and how

However, I have not been able to gain access to this paper as it only seems to exist in a physical library in Guatemala and another library in Tennessee. My study is therefore limited by the sparse information available. The possibility to draw conclusions from the cases will be restricted. This is for two reasons. Firstly, I am unable to estimate a change in vertical legitimacy in Guatemala if I do not have enough data for the time period that I am examining. After all, I cannot gain a clear picture of the vertical legitimacy was in 1997, and how it changed from 1998 to 2002. Enough information is available about organized violence by youths to make an estimation on that side of vertical legitimacy. But that is not enough to say that vertical legitimacy increased or decreased during those years. Secondly, with a lack of information, there is a risk that the information that I have been able to collect is biased. The data I have may create a false image of vertical legitimacy in Guatemala as much lower or higher than was the case. Youths might have cooperated with the government a lot on different issues and have high confidence in its institutions, but I will not know that from my data. With the limited data, I cannot say that is not the case. Therefore, I David Edberg Landeström 43 can only indicate what my data for Guatemala points to, but not draw any conclusions from it. Consequently, if I cannot estimate the change in vertical legitimacy in Guatemala, I cannot compare it to Colombia and conclude if the hypothesis is supported or not. 5.1. Analysis Before commencing with the analysis of the data I will repeat the research design of the study. The purpose of the thesis is to test if meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements leads to vertical legitimacy. To test this, I looked at whether youth were included in the peace negotiations through direct representation at the negotiation table, official consultative forums, less formal consultative forums, public participation, or mass action. Thereafter, I collected data on vertical legitimacy among youths for both my cases, how youths view the government and state institutions, if they are protesting the government or cooperating with them, or if youth are taking part in organized violence. Data was collected starting the year after the agreement was signed for both cases (2017 for Colombia and 1997 for Guatemala) and the five following years. To collect the data for the study I used the peace agreements signed between the parties and I also looked at news articles, reports, and research articles that have been written about youth in the periods of interest for the study. In this section, I will analyze the data to determine if youth were meaningfully included in the negotiations and how vertical legitimacy changed from the year after the agreement was signed to the five following years. This change in vertical legitimacy is what I will compare between the cases to establish if there has been a greater change in the country where youth were meaningfully included. Looking at the Colombian peace process, youth were meaningfully included in different ways in the negotiations. While youths were not present as a party in the negotiations, they were able to participate in the negotiations through the public consultations. There they were able to bring forth different topics and issues that they felt were important in the peace negotiations. This can be understood as public participation. Second, youths were part of the delegation sent to the official negotiations between the

between the cases to establish if there has been a greater change in the country where youth were meaningfully included. Looking at the Colombian peace process, youth were meaningfully included in different ways in the negotiations. While youths were not present as a party in the negotiations, they were able to participate in the negotiations through the public consultations. There they were able to bring forth different topics and issues that they felt were important in the peace negotiations. This can be understood as public participation. Second, youths were part of the delegation sent to the official negotiations between the Government of Colombia and FARC. This can be understood as an official consultative forum to the negotiations. Lastly, after the referendum had failed to gain public support for the peace agreement, youths used mass action to organize themselves and people who voted both for and against the agreement in favor of continued peace negotiations. Based on this information, I argue that youths were David Edberg Landeström 44 meaningfully included in the Colombian peace process through official consultative forums, public participation, and mass action. To gauge if there has been a change in vertical legitimacy, I first need to establish vertical legitimacy in 2017. The picture from the data collected is that youths viewed the government as absent. Some youths described them as corrupt, others said they did not provide enough resources for youths to take part in the peace process. Other governmental institutions seemed to have similarly low confidence from youths. It was often related to the government and politicians not being interested or present in trying to improve their lives. Although, this was not a picture shared by everyone as some youths believed that voting was still very important to get a president that wants to represent youth. Similarly, other youths have described how they have been able to establish connections to different government actors to establish a way for youths to influence youth policy. Accordingly, this shows that youths were cooperating with the government. There is likely a lot of variation in the country depending on the youth organizations and local governmental institutions. But collectively I argue that in 2017 there was a low confidence in the government and its institutions from youths. While youths did not protest the government in 2017 there were instances where young FARC members took part in organized violence against the state as they left the reintegration process to reclaim land and to continue with illicit trade. They also started to recruit other young people. All in all, I estimate that vertical legitimacy was low in 2017, as youths described a lot of distrust in the government and youths had begun to return to organized violence. The years 2018 to 2022 were a turbulent time for Colombia. Both 2019, 2020, and 2021 saw major protests led by youths over the developments in the country under President Iván Duque, the implementation of the peace agreement, and the country’s handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. Young people felt angry that they were not listened to by the government, and trust in government institutions was low. One important point that came up again and again from the protesters was violence from the police and other kinds of repression committed by the government. During these years the protests also seemed to become more violent and fatalistic as the protesters thought they had nothing to lose. Confrontations between young protesters and security forces were reported during the protests. Ex-combatants and young people working with reintegration processes also showed disappointment in the government. Further, the recruitment of young people into

people felt angry that they were not listened to by the government, and trust in government institutions was low. One important point that came up again and again from the protesters was violence from the police and other kinds of repression committed by the government. During these years the protests also seemed to become more violent and fatalistic as the protesters thought they had nothing to lose. Confrontations between young protesters and security forces were reported during the protests. Ex-combatants and young people working with reintegration processes also showed disappointment in the government. Further, the recruitment of young people into armed groups increased during this time. While many were able to reintegrate into society others still David Edberg Landeström 45 felt excluded from political life. Meanwhile, young leaders felt that the government did not contribute enough resources for them to help their communities. 2022 could be viewed as a small upswing in vertical legitimacy among youths. Gustavo Petro ran his presidential campaign and won. He was propagating many issues important for youths, and many of them were supportive of his campaign. Youths were also hopeful that if he won the election, it would be a turning point for the country. However, others were more skeptical and believed that the politicians were still all the same. There was, at the same time still cooperation between the government and youths as the government implemented the municipal youths’ councils and elected members who expressed willingness to work together with local administration. In sum, I argue that vertical legitimacy was low in the years 2018 to 2022. There still seems to be a lot of hesitation expressed towards the government and whether it can work in favor youths or not. There have been major protests against the government and the recruitment of youths to armed groups increased during this time. The upswing in 2022 does not counterweight the downturn with the protests from 2019 to 2021. Thus, looking at vertical legitimacy from 2018 to 2022, I argue that there has not been a positive change in vertical legitimacy over these years as compared to 2017. Vertical legitimacy was low in 2017 and it did not improve in the following years. Moving on to the causal mechanism, while there were provisions targeting youths in the agreement it is less certain whether youths perceived the agreement as representative of them. The agreement stipulates greater access for youths in the democratic debate, learning about how youth were affected by the conflict, and other ways to be aware of the youth perspective in different provisions. However, while many youths are in support of the agreement, many still seem to feel that this was only a process concerning the government and FARC. Therefore, based on the data collected, I cannot say that the agreement was fully representative of youths. Instead, it was somewhat representative. Looking at Guatemala, while the ASC was created for civil society to influence the agreement, youth or youth organizations were not present in this assembly. Therefore, no youths were included in the Guatemalan peace agreement. There were no other ways for youths to engage in the peace process and no mass action related to the negotiations has been captured in the data. Regarding vertical legitimacy, in 1997 the situation for youths in Guatemala seemed to be very grave as many youths joined gangs and police violence was very excessive. Therefore, no youths were included in the Guatemalan peace agreement. Regarding vertical legitimacy, in 1997 the situation for youths in Guatemala seemed to be very grave as many youths joined gangs and police violence was very excessive. While I have not found a lot of sources on youths’ opinions from this time, the data from Latinobarométro David Edberg Landeström 46 (n.d.) shows that many of the surveyed youths had very little trust in the government and its institutions in 1997. These sentiments were expressed by one young woman interviewed who said that the government was not going to care about them. That Guatemala had a lot of organized violence among youths during this time is a strong indicator of vertical legitimacy being low among youths, because in that case they do not accept the government’s right to rule over them. Based on the data collected it looks like vertical legitimacy in Guatemala was the same or decreased from 1998 to 2002. I could not find any information whether youths believed the agreement was representative or not, and I therefore cannot discuss that part of the causal chain for Guatemala. Table 2: Summary of findings Looking at both cases together (see Table 2) it becomes clear that the hypothesis is not supported in the study. It was expected that vertical legitimacy in Colombia would increase from 2018 to 2022, though it did not. And due to the lack of data for Guatemala, I cannot confidently determine if the change in vertical legitimacy in Colombia was higher than in Guatemala. Given the data that I have collected, Guatemala did follow the expected trajectory that no meaningful youth inclusion in the peace agreement would lead to low vertical David Edberg Landeström 47 legitimacy among youths. However, there is still a risk that my data is biased and that my other indicators, where I lack data, could show Guatemalan youths to have a high degree of trust in their government. Therefore, I do not have enough information about the case to confidently determine the levels of vertical legitimacy. Given enough information about Guatemala, it is possible that the hypothesis could have been supported if vertical legitimacy in Guatemala was significantly lower in the measured period than in Colombia. The hypothesis would then have been supported as the change in vertical legitimacy would have been higher in Colombia than in Guatemala. Additionally, both cases had youth provisions in the peace agreements, and based on what youths have said about the agreement in Colombia the agreement was somewhat representative of youths. The causal mechanism was thus not present in any of the cases. However, as both agreements had provisions targeting youth, part of the mechanism was present for both. To me, this points to limitations in the theory and design tools which will be discussed further in the limitations.

Alternative Explanations Since the hypothesis did not find support in the cases studied, it is relevant to discuss what alternative explanations there might be. One possible alternative explanation is that everything that happened during those measured years had a bigger effect on the change in vertical legitimacy than meaningful youth inclusion had. For Colombia as mentioned in the design consideration, the COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated already existing problems within the country which also seemed to have a big effect on the trust in the government. Further, the change of presidency in 2018 did seem to have a big effect on the vertical legitimacy of youths as Iván Duque implemented unpopular policies and halted the implementation of the peace agreement. These were two sticking points in the protests that occurred. Likewise, the implementation of the agreement halted in Guatemala as well, although for different reasons. According to Salvesen (2002, 20–25), while formally committed to implementing the peace accord, the government seemed to lack both the vision, will, and capacity to follow through with it. There also appeared to be a lack of ownership of the process as the ASC had much more influence over the negotiations than during the implementation phase. I find it plausible that external factors such as these had a bigger effect than what occurred in the peace negotiations. David Edberg Landeström 48 Another possible explanation is that youths were not included enough in the Colombian agreement. While they were included in many ways that I deemed meaningful it might still not have been enough for youths to have a major presence in the negotiations themselves. If there would have been more youth inclusion such as presence at the table, it might have had a larger effect on the change in vertical legitimacy. While such a case is rare, it did occur during Yemen’s National Dialogue Conference (NDC). There, 40 seats were allocated to youth, women, and civil society. In addition, 20 percent of the delegations from the various political factions had to be youth (Paffenholz and Ross 2016, 203). Even though the conference was extensive in its inclusion, women and youth groups still felt unfairly marginalized by quotas less than what they hoped for and accused the process of being hijacked by traditional political elites. They still had a lot of power in the negotiations and had they operated as a cohesive bloc of youth, women, and civil society, they could have had veto power in the negotiations which is an unusually high degree of influence to be given to such constituencies. However, the NDC failed to come to an agreement that everyone was happy with. The design of the process was not sufficient to allow the parties to reach a consensus on important topics and a focus on inclusion in the NDC was not accompanied by attention to the dysfunctional and elite nature of the current government, which lead to a loss of public support (Paffenholz and Ross 2016, 203–9). While this case is very different

civil society, they could have had veto power in the negotiations which is an unusually high degree of influence to be given to such constituencies. However, the NDC failed to come to an agreement that everyone was happy with. The design of the process was not sufficient to allow the parties to reach a consensus on important topics and a focus on inclusion in the NDC was not accompanied by attention to the dysfunctional and elite nature of the current government, which lead to a loss of public support (Paffenholz and Ross 2016, 203–9). While this case is very different from Colombia and looked at a different outcome than the change in vertical legitimacy, the case study does indicate that more youth inclusion does not necessarily lead to better results or more public support if the rest of the process is not properly designed. Based on the study by Paffenholz and Ross (2016), it is possible that more meaningful youth inclusion in the Colombian agreement could have led to a bigger effect on the change in vertical legitimacy, but this effect is also reliant on how the rest of the process is designed to allow the parties to reach an agreement that everyone could be content with. Thus, no assumptions can be made that more meaningful inclusion necessarily leads to a greater change in vertical legitimacy. Moving on, while youths found their ways of contributing to the implementation process in Colombia, there were no provisions in the agreement stipulating youths’ involvement in the implementation process, which could have been an important way for youths to feel represented, and for the state to gain vertical legitimacy. Such a procedure may be an important part that was not studied in this paper. Future research could examine the issue further. David Edberg Landeström 49 5.3 Limitations There are many limitations to this study. As mentioned earlier, there was not enough data available for Guatemala to estimate the change in vertical legitimacy and compare it to Colombia. There are very few sources that have gathered information from the years after the peace agreement was signed about youths’ opinions of the government and the peace agreement itself. The scarce access of data in the Guatemalan case hindered my possibility of drawing conclusions about the case. Methodologically, I see three potential remedies for this problem. Firstly, to find another way to measure vertical legitimacy. Secondly, look at some other DV that would have been easier to measure. Third, to pick another case where that data is available. All solutions could have worked though they all include their specific problems. The first solution is problematic as there is a risk that the validity of the measurement is reduced by changing the operationalization. The operationalization might then have moved further away from the concept, vertical legitimacy, that I was studying. The second solution can be problematic as it might be perceived as fitting the data to the theory. Lastly, picking another case was not realistic. After having examined different cases, I found that there are few cases that fit study well. Many had their issues. Further, due to time restrictions, it would not have been possible to pick another case within the time limit of the study. A consequence of the lack of data for Guatemala is that I had to use survey data to try and get an understanding of the vertical legitimacy among youth. This is not ideal for a qualitative study where I have the opportunity to describe my variables in more detail than in a quantitative study. Questions of how or why youths have

different cases, I found that there are few cases that fit study well. Many had their issues. Further, due to time restrictions, it would not have been possible to pick another case within the time limit of the study. A consequence of the lack of data for Guatemala is that I had to use survey data to try and get an understanding of the vertical legitimacy among youth. This is not ideal for a qualitative study where I have the opportunity to describe my variables in more detail than in a quantitative study. Questions of how or why youths have low or high trust in their government are not captured as well in surveys. I therefore cannot explain the relationship between the variables as extensively. Another limitation of the survey data is that the number of respondents was around 230-330 for each question asked. This is a very small sample which is likely not to be representative of other young people in Guatemala. Moreover, Latinobarómetro (n.d.) did not contain the same kind of data for my measured timespan for Colombia. This further reduces my ability to draw conclusions from the cases as I have different types of data for both cases. As mentioned in the analysis, both agreements had provisions targeting youths’ even though I did not expect that to be the case for Guatemala. This leads me to believe that the theory was not well-developed enough. Just because there are provisions targeting youths present in the agreement does not mean that young people took part in the process. A way to David Edberg Landeström 50 circumvent this problem in the future is to look at how closely provisions in the peace agreement align with what youths are demanding. It would also be important to look at whether those provisions were implemented or not. Because it does not matter if a provision is in an agreement if they are not implemented afterward. As discussed in the alternative explanations, it is possible that youths being part of the implementation process can have a big effect on whether they perceive themselves to be represented or not and thus their vertical legitimacy. These two adjustments could improve the theory for future studies. Another limitation of the study is the operationalization of the second part of the causal mechanism, the representativeness of the agreement. It is a blunt operationalization that did not consider the multiplicity of views among youths that has been mentioned previously in this article. The result of this is that it becomes unclear how the variable should be operationalized in different circumstances such as now when there are differing views of the representativeness of the agreement. A clearer indication from the beginning of what should be seen in the data for the variable to be coded as a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ would have solved this issue. Further, regarding youths’ vertical legitimacy, there is a risk that I have not been able to capture a representative picture of youths in Colombia. Accordingly, it is then possible that the data is skewed towards showing more or less vertical legitimacy than there was at the time. However, I do not think this is the case. I captured the big events that occurred in the country, the protests, the 2022 election, and the rising recruitment into armed groups. These are large events where many youths are included, and they form the basis of my analysis. If something has been missed it is on the smaller side and my results should not be biased from missing it. David Edberg Landeström 51 6. Conclusion The research question
