Conclusion The research question for this paper was how meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements affect youth’s view of the vertical legitimacy. The purpose was to contribute to the field of inclusive negotiations and policies on youth, peace, and security, with a comparative case study on youth inclusion in peace agreements. Previous studies have looked at civil society inclusion or women’s inclusion, but few have studied youth. The papers that have studied youth inclusion in peace negotiations have been descriptive. The theory was that meaningful youth inclusion should lead to more provisions in the peace agreement targeting youths which would make the agreement representative of youths; this would then lead to vertical legitimacy. I studied the peace agreement in Colombia in 2016 and compared it to the Guatemalan peace agreement in 1996. After establishing if and how youth were included in the peace negotiations, I studied the vertical legitimacy between youth and the state in both countries. I established a baseline vertical legitimacy the year after the peace agreement was signed and compared it to the vertical legitimacy in the five following years. Thereafter, I looked at the causal mechanism, if there were youth provisions in the peace agreement, and if the peace agreement was representative of youths. The results of the study were inconclusive. Based on the data collected, vertical legitimacy among youths in Colombia was lower in the five years after 2017. It seemed to be the same for Guatemala which did not have any meaningful youth inclusion in its peace agreement. However, there was not enough information available to estimate the DV in Guatemala. Therefore, I could not compare the cases to each other. The hypothesis did thus not find support in the study. Alternative explanations for the results could be that many events occurred after the peace agreement was signed that had a bigger effect on vertical legitimacy among youths. It could also be possible there simply was not enough inclusion in this case and that youth need to be more meaningfully included for it to have an effect of vertical legitimacy. Lastly, provisions stipulating the inclusion of youths in the implementation process could also be important to increase vertical legitimacy. The study has many limitations. Firstly, there was not enough information available for Guatemala to draw any conclusions from the case. The theory was not developed enough in the causal chain of how meaningful youth inclusion in peace agreements should lead to vertical legitimacy. Provisions targeting youth may be present even though youths are not David Edberg Landeström 52 included in the agreement. Also, the operationalization of representativeness of the agreement was not clear enough between whether it should be coded as a ‘yes’ or ‘no.’ However, these objection does not mean that the theory is incorrect. Future research could look at other cases with youth inclusion to establish if there is a relationship with vertical legitimacy or not. They could also use field research to collect the data instead of relying on secondary information. Moreover, future research could

should lead to vertical legitimacy. Provisions targeting youth may be present even though youths are not David Edberg Landeström 52 included in the agreement. Also, the operationalization of representativeness of the agreement was not clear enough between whether it should be coded as a ‘yes’ or ‘no.’ However, these objection does not mean that the theory is incorrect. Future research could look at other cases with youth inclusion to establish if there is a relationship with vertical legitimacy or not. They could also use field research to collect the data instead of relying on secondary information. Moreover, future research could explore the theory and causal mechanism of how meaningful youth inclusion in peace negotiations can lead to a positive change in vertical legitimacy and the operationalization to distinguish between cases where youth feel represented in the peace agreement and where they do not. One reflection concerning the study on Colombia is that after the peace agreement is signed, are equally important as the peace agreement itself. Youth inclusion in the peace negotiations does not automatically lead to increased vertical legitimacy among young people. Therefore, work with youth, peace, and security must go beyond just including youths at the adult’s table. David Edberg Landeström 53 7. Master’s thesis Context-Specific Peace Education Initiatives within local communities in Western Cape, South Africa: Lessons from Ubuntu Author: Martina Lastikova Supervisor: Lennart Wohlgemuth Examiner: Manuela Nilsson (Ph.D.) Department and Course Code: SAFU1 Faculty of Social Sciences, Peace and Development Work Level: Graduate Thesis Seminar: 2nd of June 2023 Word count: 21,886 Abstract This thesis explores the role of context-specific peace education initiatives in promoting social cohesion in the Western Cape, South Africa, with an emphasis on drawing lessons from the philosophy of Ubuntu. Against the backdrop of South Africa's colonial, apartheid and post- apartheid historical and social significance, including issues of social cohesion, the study addresses the country's complex socio-political dynamics and ongoing challenges in fostering unity and inclusivity. By applying the Social Capital Theory, which highlights the importance of social networks, trust, reciprocity, shared norms and values, as well as collective action in achieving shared goals and cooperation, this study explores how context-specific peace education initiatives can be customized to local contexts and their potential to promote positive social change. With a focus on the Western Cape province, characterised by high levels of violence and socio- economic disparities, this research delves into peace education initiatives to comprehend their adaptation to local circumstances and their capacity to drive constructive social transformation. The findings underscore the significance of relationships, social networks, and individuals with strong social capital in building positive peace and fostering social cohesion. Creating inclusive spaces for dialogue through storytelling, art, music, and other means emerges as a key finding. These spaces provide opportunities for people to come together, share their experiences, and engage in difficult conversations. The research also emphasizes the role of young people and their active engagement in civic participation as crucial for the success of peace education initiatives. By valuing and incorporating local wisdom, traditions, and practices such as Ubuntu, peace education initiatives can better address the specific needs and challenges of the Western Cape. The study recommends further research on the integration of gender-sensitive and intersectional approaches. Understanding and addressing gender-related factors are essential for promoting inclusivity and equality within peace education programs. Furthermore, I am indebted to Prof. van Wyk, Emeritia Wyk and Amanda Jones for their kindness and hospitality during my fieldwork. Their willingness to share their home and stories provided me with a deeper understanding of the communities and cultures of the Western Cape. I would also like to express my gratitude to Barbara Nussbaum, prof. John Volminik and Dr Heinrich Volminik for their inspiring commitment to Ubuntu philosophy and their unwavering dedication to peacebuilding, education and social justice. Their insights and wisdom helped me to better understand the potential of Ubuntu's philosophy for promoting peace and social cohesion. Finally, I would like to thank all the people of the Western Cape who generously shared their experiences and perspectives with me. Your contributions were instrumental in shaping this research, and I am deeply grateful for the time and energy you invested in this project. 5 1 Introduction South Africa bears the weight of a complex socio-political landscape, with enduring challenges in fostering social cohesion and post-apartheid reconciliation. By delving into this concept and examining peace education initiatives within this unique context, this research aligns with the profound relevance of South Africa's historical journey and ongoing efforts to cultivate unity, inclusivity, and a shared sense of belonging. With a deep sense of responsibility and a desire to contribute to the field, I embark on this research journey. By exploring the specific context of South Africa and its socio-political dynamics, I hope to shed light on the critical factors that contribute to the success of context-specific peace education initiatives. By weaving together personal experiences, academic exploration, and a commitment to social transformation, I aspire to make a meaningful contribution to the field of peace education and inspire positive change not only in South Africa but also in other contexts facing similar challenges. Is social cohesion a fallacy? In isiXhosa, one of the South African and several other Bantu languages, there is a saying, Umntu ngumntu ngabantu which can be translated as a person is a person through other people. It points to the shared humanity and interconnectedness. In Peace and Development Discourse, the emphasis on the Triple Nexus offers a more comprehensive and integrated approach to address the complex and interconnected challenges faced by communities affected by crises and conflicts. The Triple Nexus approach has gained importance in recent years because it recognizes the interconnectedness and complexity of humanitarian, development, and peacebuilding efforts. Traditionally, these three sectors have operated separately and often in silos, with limited coordination and cooperation between them. Similarly, the sector has started emphasising local ownership to highlight the already existing context-specific solutions and practices. When local ownership is emphasized in the Triple Nexus approach, it creates an environment where local actors are empowered to lead their development, humanitarian and peacebuilding efforts, working in partnership with external actors as necessary. 6 1.0 Unleashing the Power of Local Communities: A Development from the Inside Out Perspective Development from the Inside Out is an approach that emphasizes the importance of local communities and their assets in driving sustainable development. It differs from traditional top- down and bottom-up approaches, which often rely on external actors and interventions to drive change. Wilson points to the duality of these approaches by suggesting that ‘the community-based economic development movement will be located at the heart of this change’ (1996:628) and should be important to involve local actors in each step of the development projects. Similarly, Hoffman's book

1.0 Unleashing the Power of Local Communities: A Development from the Inside Out Perspective Development from the Inside Out is an approach that emphasizes the importance of local communities and their assets in driving sustainable development. It differs from traditional top- down and bottom-up approaches, which often rely on external actors and interventions to drive change. Wilson points to the duality of these approaches by suggesting that ‘the community-based economic development movement will be located at the heart of this change’ (1996:628) and should be important to involve local actors in each step of the development projects. Similarly, Hoffman's book titled, The Answers Are There: Building Peace From the Inside Out, highlights the value of the ‘local culture, of the talents, desires, experience, ingenuity, courage, commitment, knowledge and caring relationships within the community itself- all of that had been unacknowledged and uninvited’ (2022:475) and says that ‘community-led development isn’t about changing the local but it is about changing the way international peacebuilding and development works’ (2022:146). Building on Hoffman’s arguments, here are some reasons why the Development from the inside-out approach is important and could be seen as a helpful lens for context-specific peace education initiatives. Firstly, development from the inside-out approach emphasizes the importance of local ownership of development initiatives. This means that local communities are actively involved in the planning and implementation of development projects, rather than being passive recipients of aid. Secondly, it could be seen that by focusing on local assets and strengths, the development from the inside-out approach empowers local communities to take control of their development. This means that communities can identify their own needs and priorities, and work towards achieving their own development goals. Thirdly, this approach recognizes that development challenges are context specific. Therefore, development initiatives such as peace-education initiatives need to be tailored to the specific needs and challenges of each community- echoing Hoffman’s argument for the locals to be invited to the table. Development from the inside out can be seen as an asset-based approach. This approach in peacebuilding is a strategy that focuses on identifying and utilizing the strengths and resources that already exist within a community or society to build peace and resolve conflicts such as operationalising the philosophy of Ubuntu. Instead of focusing on problems and deficits, an asset- 7 based approach looks for positive qualities and assets such as skills, knowledge, relationships, and networks that can be harnessed to promote peace and resilience. An asset-based approach in peacebuilding recognizes the importance of identifying and building upon existing strengths and resources within a community, rather than solely focusing on addressing weaknesses and deficiencies. This approach is closely tied to the Social Capital Theory, which emphasizes the importance of social relationships, networks, and norms in promoting cooperation and achieving shared goals. By utilizing an asset-based approach and drawing upon the principles of social capital, peace education initiatives can be more effective in promoting social cohesion and preventing conflict in local communities. 1.1 Western Cape, South Africa South Africa has a long history of conflict and violence, particularly related to political and socio- economic issues. The high rate of violence reflects its ongoing vulnerability and continuing fragility reflected in the country’s weakened social fabric and persistent inequalities (World Bank Group et al, 2019). As stated by Vision of Humanity (2021), ‘South Africa’s GPI has been steadily leaning toward ‘less peaceful’ scoring ‘very high’ on ‘perceived criminality in society’, ‘homicides’, ’violent demonstrations’ and ‘safety and security’. Despite South Africa's economic growth and participation in BRICS, the country has been scoring low on the Global Peace Index (GPI) in recent years,

has a long history of conflict and violence, particularly related to political and socio- economic issues. The high rate of violence reflects its ongoing vulnerability and continuing fragility reflected in the country’s weakened social fabric and persistent inequalities (World Bank Group et al, 2019). As stated by Vision of Humanity (2021), ‘South Africa’s GPI has been steadily leaning toward ‘less peaceful’ scoring ‘very high’ on ‘perceived criminality in society’, ‘homicides’, ’violent demonstrations’ and ‘safety and security’. Despite South Africa's economic growth and participation in BRICS, the country has been scoring low on the Global Peace Index (GPI) in recent years, indicating a lack of peace. This has led to an increased focus on peace education on local, regional, and international agendas. The African Development Bank Group has developed the Skills for Employability and Productivity in Africa (SEPA) Action Plan, 2022-2025, to address fragility in post-conflict states and promote social inclusion and peacebuilding. UNESCO has emphasized the importance of incorporating pedagogies of respect, inclusion, belonging, peacebuilding, and conflict transformation into the educational curriculum to address social exclusion and inequality (2021). The Agenda 2030 and African Union (AU) also support peace education efforts. However, creating a context-specific peace education is crucial, as relying solely on international frameworks may not be effective. International organizations recognize the need for a multi-level approach to 8 peacebuilding, such as the UNDP's efforts to localize the Agenda 2030 with a high focus on context-specific solutions. Western Cape province is still among the most violent provinces in South Africa with a high crime rate (figure 1). The persistently high levels of violence in the Western Cape can be attributed to a combination of complex factors. These include historical legacies of apartheid, socio-economic disparities, inequality, unemployment, gang-related activities, drug trafficking, inadequate policing, and limited access to quality education and social services. Additionally, the spatial segregation and fragmented communities within the province contribute to the perpetuation of violence and crime. Addressing these multifaceted challenges requires comprehensive strategies that address socio-economic inequalities, strengthen community engagement, improve law enforcement, and promote educational opportunities and job creation. While the complex issues contributing to the high levels of violence in the Western Cape will be acknowledged in this thesis, it is important to note that they will not be extensively analysed or explored due to the limitations of the study. The focus of this thesis is on examining context-specific peace education initiatives and their potential role in fostering positive social change, rather than providing an in-depth analysis of the underlying causes of violence in the Western Cape. In Peace and Development scholarship, there has been an increasing focus on context-specific peace education initiatives that underpin social cohesion. In post-conflict contexts and emerging democracies like South Africa, these initiatives can be viewed as important means to promote social cohesion. This is reaffirmed in the report by the Southern African Development and Reconstruction Agency (SADRA) stating that there is a need for bottom-up interventions aiming to ‘transform a culture of violence into one of peace and reconstruction’ (2019:3). Moreover, this need is also highlighted in the report by the Western Cape government and social capital is emphasised. They suggest that building connected communities through social capital is crucial for bridging divides across various socio-demographic factors, including race, gender, language, and socioeconomic status (n.d.). Thus, examining the role of social capital in fostering community connectedness can provide insights into how government and civil society can effectively support this process. There has been a significant change in the peacebuilding discourse in both academia and practice. The second half of the 20th century has been dominated by top-down and

reconstruction’ (2019:3). Moreover, this need is also highlighted in the report by the Western Cape government and social capital is emphasised. They suggest that building connected communities through social capital is crucial for bridging divides across various socio-demographic factors, including race, gender, language, and socioeconomic status (n.d.). Thus, examining the role of social capital in fostering community connectedness can provide insights into how government and civil society can effectively support this process. There has been a significant change in the peacebuilding discourse in both academia and practice. The second half of the 20th century has been dominated by top-down and determined- designed approaches to conflict resolution. As criticised by de Coning et al, these interventions 9 were ‘too linear in its planning assumptions’ (2023:5) and failed ‘sufficiently involve national and local stakeholder’ (ibid). The authors go on to suggest that context-specific approaches to peacebuilding that underline local ownership have the potential to be more sustainable (de Coning et al, 2023). However, it is important to underline that the debate about top-down and bottom-up approaches can become too deterministic as designing sustainable peace interventions requires a multi-faceted approach. Furthermore, de Coning et al highlight the importance of shifting the discourse to more adaptive peace-building interventions that are more ‘pragmatic and complexity informed (2023:9) and ‘highlight context-specific local solutions’ (ibid). This research project aims to build on that gap and explore context-specific peace education initiatives within local communities in the Western Cape, with a focus on the lessons that can be learned from the philosophy of Ubuntu. The research will aim to explore how these initiatives have been tailored to local contexts. This project fits within the broader area of peace and development, as it seeks to explore how context-specific peace education initiatives can contribute to the development of peaceful and sustainable communities. It also fits within the sub-disciplines of peace education, which is concerned with how education can be used to promote peace, and social justice and foster social cohesion. In terms of policy and practice, the project has the potential to contribute to the development of more effective and context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape and beyond. By identifying the key factors that contribute to the success of these initiatives and the challenges that need to be addressed, the project can inform the development of policies and practices that are better tailored to local realities and promote social cohesion. 10 Figure 1: murder per ratio: provincial distribution, per capita (*ratio per 100,000 of the population), adapted from businesstech.co.za, 2022 1.2 Fostering Social Cohesion through context-specific peace education initiatives Peace education is a relatively new field that emerged in the 20th century. The debate on contextualising peace education is focused on different themes, each with its objectives. There is a distinction between “peace education” and “education for peace”, where the latter emphasises a more learner-centred and active approach. Local realities are crucial in the development of education initiatives. Ubuntu, an African ontology that emphasises interconnectedness has been used in different contexts and sectors, including social cohesion policies and measures in South Africa. However, the challenges such as political polarisation, social fragmentation and the persistence of historical injustices and post-Apartheid policies prevail. Therefore, there is a need for both a theoretical and tangible embodied and embedded Ubuntu that is relevant in the present context to cultivate a sense of shared identity for South Africa. Even though this African ontology can offer resources for social cohesion and people-centred transformation and development- there is a need to explore how Ubuntu can be operationalised as a framework for education for peace in specific

different contexts and sectors, including social cohesion policies and measures in South Africa. However, the challenges such as political polarisation, social fragmentation and the persistence of historical injustices and post-Apartheid policies prevail. Therefore, there is a need for both a theoretical and tangible embodied and embedded Ubuntu that is relevant in the present context to cultivate a sense of shared identity for South Africa. Even though this African ontology can offer resources for social cohesion and people-centred transformation and development- there is a need to explore how Ubuntu can be operationalised as a framework for education for peace in specific local contexts in South Africa. 11 In the context of the Western Cape in South Africa, context-specific peace education initiatives based on Ubuntu philosophy can have several key benefits. Firstly, they can increase the relevance of peace education by tailoring it to the specific needs and context of the community, increasing its effectiveness in promoting peaceful attitudes and behaviours. Secondly, by empowering local communities to take ownership of their own peacebuilding and conflict resolution processes, these initiatives can increase their sustainability and promote long-term social cohesion. Thirdly, effective peace education can prevent future conflict and violence by addressing the root causes of conflict, such as social inequality and prejudice, and promoting empathy and understanding. Finally, by promoting shared values and building trust between different groups within a community, context-specific peace education initiatives can help to address underlying social divisions and promote greater social harmony. By tailoring peace education initiatives to the specific needs and context of the community, stakeholders are more likely to take ownership of the peacebuilding process and achieve lasting change. As highlighted by Nkondo, ‘ubuntu offers resources for social cohesion and people-centred transformation and development’ (2007:99). Thus, promoting positive values and behaviours through peace education can contribute to positive peace by addressing root causes, building trust and understanding, encouraging peaceful behaviour, and strengthening social cohesion. 1.2.1 Research Problem and Research Gap The topic of this master’s thesis is the role of Ubuntu philosophy in contributing to peace education and social cohesion in local realities in South Africa. This topic is important and interesting because South Africa has a long history of conflict and violence, particularly related to political and socio-economic issues. The ongoing vulnerability and continuing fragility of the country's social fabric and persistent inequalities are reflected in the high rates of violence, especially in the Western Cape province (figure 1). The research problem for this study is the research gap between peace education, multi-level context, and what frameworks could be utilized to foster social cohesion. The literature review chapter will provide an in-depth discussion of the research gap and its relevance to the field. In summary, the research gap is the lack of context-specific peace education initiatives that underpin social cohesion in post-conflict contexts and emerging democracies like South Africa. The purpose 12 of this study is to contribute to the ever-evolving field of context-specific education for peace and to how theoretical concepts such as "Ubuntu" can inform practitioners, policymakers, and educators to operationalize it in a local context. The research objective is to explore context-specific peace education initiatives within local communities in the Western Cape and to examine how these initiatives have been tailored to local contexts. To achieve this objective, the study will answer the following research questions: What are the key factors that contribute to the success of context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape? What are the challenges that need to be addressed to develop more effective and context- specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape? How can Ubuntu philosophy

can inform practitioners, policymakers, and educators to operationalize it in a local context. The research objective is to explore context-specific peace education initiatives within local communities in the Western Cape and to examine how these initiatives have been tailored to local contexts. To achieve this objective, the study will answer the following research questions: What are the key factors that contribute to the success of context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape? What are the challenges that need to be addressed to develop more effective and context- specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape? How can Ubuntu philosophy inform the development of context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape? The thesis will be structured as follows: Section 1 will introduce the topic, research problem, purpose, research objective, and research questions. Section 2 will present a comprehensive review of the literature on peace education, social cohesion, and Ubuntu philosophy, with a focus on the research gap and its relevance to the field. Section 3 will describe the theoretical framework for the study. Section 4 will touch upon the analytical framework. Section 5 will describe methodology including the qualitative research approach and data collection methods such as semi-structured interviews and observation. Section 6 will present the findings of the research and Section 7 will discuss the key factors that contribute to the success of context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape, the challenges that need to be addressed, and how Ubuntu philosophy and Social Capital theory can inform the development of context-specific peace education initiatives. Finally, Section 8 will provide a summary of the research findings, conclusions, and recommendations for policymakers, practitioners, and educators on the operationalization of peace education and Social Capital theory. In conclusion, this study aims to contribute to the understanding of the role of Ubuntu philosophy in local peace-education initiatives and its role in social cohesion in the Western Cape, South Africa. Although the scope of the research is limited to the Western Cape province of South Africa, the findings of this study could contribute to the development of context-specific peace education 13 initiatives and inform policymakers, practitioners, and educators on the operationalization of peace education drawing from the Social Capital Theory and Ubuntu. As we delve deeper into the complexities of social cohesion in this research, we must ask ourselves: can a sense of Ubuntu truly be fostered in societies that have been torn apart by conflict and division? 2 Literature Review 2.0 Introduction to the Section This literature overview provides a summary of the existing literature on the topic of peace education, social cohesion and the Ubuntu philosophy. The section begins with an exploration of the concept of peace and its different dimensions, and how it has been understood and operationalised in various contexts. It then moves on to examine the role of education in promoting peace and social cohesion, including an overview of different approaches and models of peace education. Ubuntu philosophy is then introduced as a theoretical framework for understanding the relationship between social relationships, education and peace followed by an exploration of how it has been applied in different contexts. This section also aims to identify gaps in the existing literature and highlight the need for further research in this area. By synthesising the existing literature conceptual frameworks mentioned above, it aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the current state of knowledge in the field and contribute to the development of context-specific frameworks for promoting peace and social cohesion. 2.1 Peace Education Since Galtung's original definition of ‘Negative Peace’ as an ‘absence of violence’ (1969:190) and ‘Positive Peace’

the relationship between social relationships, education and peace followed by an exploration of how it has been applied in different contexts. This section also aims to identify gaps in the existing literature and highlight the need for further research in this area. By synthesising the existing literature conceptual frameworks mentioned above, it aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the current state of knowledge in the field and contribute to the development of context-specific frameworks for promoting peace and social cohesion. 2.1 Peace Education Since Galtung's original definition of ‘Negative Peace’ as an ‘absence of violence’ (1969:190) and ‘Positive Peace’ as an ‘integration and cooperation’ (ibid) and later added the term ‘social justice’ (ibid), research on peacebuilding has shifted toward promoting cooperation and integration at the structural level. In addition, it is important to acknowledge the widely accepted categorizations of direct, structural and cultural violence proposed by Galtung (1969). Understanding the distinction between direct (causing direct harm by an actor) and structural violence (invisible forces leading 14 to social injustices) is crucial as Galtung argues that the absence of direct violence contributes to negative peace while the absence of structural violence fosters positive peace (ibis). The Institute of Economics and Peace (IEP) annual Positive Peace report provides a framework to contextualise the interconnected global challenges the countries are facing. Moreover, IEP points out that ‘countries with high Positive Peace are more likely to maintain their stability and recover more easily from internal and external shocks’ (The Institute of Economics and Peace (IEP), 2021). In the context of the Western Cape, promoting positive peace and social justice can be critical for maintaining stability and resilience, particularly in the face of various internal and external challenges. According to the IEP, countries that prioritize positive peace tend to be better equipped to recover from shocks, including those caused by conflict, economic instability, and natural disasters. Therefore, efforts to promote positive peace through education and other initiatives may be particularly important in the Western Cape, where social and economic inequalities, historical legacies of conflict and violence, and other factors may pose ongoing challenges to peace and stability. Peace education has been an emerging field in scholarship and scholars only started engaging with it in the twentieth century (Thomas, 2012). There has been a debate about contextualising peace education around different themes, each with its objectives. Harris (2004) emphasizes five postulates for peace education: 1.’it explains the roots of violence; 2. it teaches alternatives to violence; 3. it adjusts to cover different forms of violence; 4. peace itself is a process that varies according to context; 5. conflict is omnipresent. (p. 6) 15 Harris’s (2004) focus on violence points to what Galtung also tries to address; the fact that peace education tries to offer a new modality toward more peaceful societies (Galtung, 2008) and as a field deal with the potential emergence of conflict and violence. He also suggests the importance of ‘peace research, peace action, and peace education’ (Galtung, 1973: 317) in tackling the potential unfolding of violence and promoting peace (Galtung, 1973: 317). Apart from emphasizing aspects of stopping violence through “peace education”, the scholarship makes a distinction between “peace education” and “education for peace”. Both Galtung (2008) and Reardon (2000) point to a more learner-centred approach and active collaborative methods in Education for Peace rather than a teacher-centred approach typical in peace education. As Bar-Tal & Rosen suggest the primary objective of peace education is to ‘construct society members’ worldview in a way that facilitates conflict resolution and peace process and prepares them to live in an era of peace and reconciliation’

Both Galtung (2008) and Reardon (2000) point to a more learner-centred approach and active collaborative methods in Education for Peace rather than a teacher-centred approach typical in peace education. As Bar-Tal & Rosen suggest the primary objective of peace education is to ‘construct society members’ worldview in a way that facilitates conflict resolution and peace process and prepares them to live in an era of peace and reconciliation’ (2009:559). They also argue that this encompasses all members of society, including students, leading to two distinct approaches to peace education: focusing on students and schools as well as encompassing the socialization and persuasion of society members to support these processes (Bar-Tal & Rosen, 2009). The latter approach necessitates the involvement of various societal institutions in driving the necessary changes for a more peaceful society. Lin also emphasises the importance of fostering a culture of peace and recognises the interconnectedness of our shared humanity (2007). However, it is not just about the pedagogy used to teach peace or teach “for” peace, but rather finding context-specific education or education initiatives that are embedded in local realities. Gittins discusses this when talking about the importance of ‘context-specific approaches’ (2021:1004). Similarly, Bajaj (2016) also criticises the scholarship by suggesting that ‘peace education efforts are often decontextualized’ (in Gittins 2021:1004). In addition, Gittins offers that most peace education initiatives and frameworks have been designed with top-down perspectives (2021) often excluding local realities. 2.2 Social Cohesion, Ubuntu, and local realities in South Africa Local realities are important. Building off Gittins’s argument about ‘context-specific approaches’ (2021:1004), Ubuntu- the African Ontology has been used in different contexts and sectors. Although definitions across the scholarship differ, its essence speaks to the interconnectedness. As 16 defined by Oviawe, ‘Ubuntu is a philosophy of being that locates identity and meaning making within a collective approach as opposed to an individualistic one’ (2016:3). The author also points out that it can become a framework used as a bridge to reflect on the ubuntu ideal values of community-driven development and education (Oviawe, 2016). This connection between Ubuntu philosophy and local development is pointed out by the UNDP report highlighting clear linkages between Ubuntu and social cohesion (2016:15) and showcasing the importance of such philosophies in the African context. It also observes that ‘many social cohesion policies and measures...are founded on theory from the industrialised countries...’ (ibid). Rather, by operationalising social cohesion based on context-specific frameworks such as ubuntu in policymaking, societies can become more resilient, and peaceful. However, there are also challenges to promoting social cohesion and ubuntu in South Africa, including political polarisation, social fragmentation, and the persistence of historical injustices. Addressing these challenges requires a multifaceted approach that includes both policy interventions and grassroots initiatives that draw on the strengths and resources of local communities. To address these- it is important to conceptualise Ubuntu in a particular context rather than what Thakkathi and Netshitangani call ‘over-idealisation’ (2020:408) of Ubuntu. The authors suggest that to achieve this, it is necessary to rethink the essence of ubuntu in a way that aligns with the contemporary demands of the societies it exists in (Thakkathi and Netshitangani, 2020). This means that there is a need for both a theoretical and tangible ubuntu that is relevant in the present context. Scholars have criticized the use of Ubuntu as a model in various settings, including education. Enslin and Horsthemke criticise this idealised concept of ubuntu questioning

important to conceptualise Ubuntu in a particular context rather than what Thakkathi and Netshitangani call ‘over-idealisation’ (2020:408) of Ubuntu. The authors suggest that to achieve this, it is necessary to rethink the essence of ubuntu in a way that aligns with the contemporary demands of the societies it exists in (Thakkathi and Netshitangani, 2020). This means that there is a need for both a theoretical and tangible ubuntu that is relevant in the present context. Scholars have criticized the use of Ubuntu as a model in various settings, including education. Enslin and Horsthemke criticise this idealised concept of ubuntu questioning ‘the value and efficiency of ubuntu as a practical guide for action and policy’ (2010:549) and conclude that it can be at best a part of an ideal democratic model for South Africa’ (ibid). However, Moetsi Letseka in the article titled, ‘In Defence of Ubuntu’ responds to the arguments and assaults by Enslin and Horsthemke by suggesting that Ubuntu has ‘the potential to be used as both a moral theory and public policy’ (2011:47). This emphasises the argument by Nkondo who says that education should establish a method for cultivating a social disposition of ubuntu (2007). Especially if utilising an Ubuntu- based framework as a catalyst for transformation together with a distinct methodology that incorporates its principles. Nkondo also concludes his paper titled, ‘Ubuntu as public policy in South Africa: A conceptual framework’ by saying that ‘Ubuntu offers resources for social cohesion and people-centred transformation and development’ (2007:99). Both Letseka (2011) and Nkondo 17 (2007) highlight that the educational policy framework in South Africa requires the promotion of ubuntu and the development of learners who can participate in society as active and critical citizens, which is critical for social cohesion in a country previously divided by apartheid policies. They also emphasise the significant role that ubuntu plays in promoting a shared moral discourse and the development of a tolerant society in South Africa. The conversation about operationalising concepts such as Ubuntu has not received much attention in peacebuilding discourse globally. As pointed out by Udo, ‘its global relevance is discernible’ (2020:314). He also highlights that the ‘ubuntu worldview is capable of playing a key role in conflict mediation, reconciliation and peacekeeping’ (ibid). Ubuntu's significance was brought to the forefront by the actions of Nelson Mandela and Archbishop Desmond Tutu, who utilized its principles in the Truth and Reconciliation Committee (TRC), effectively elevating it into a symbol of hope for reconciliation and healing. Furthermore, as Strozenberga et al (2015) argue, ubuntu symbolism highlights the importance of intercultural dialogue and cultural disarmament. This reinforces the conclusion drawn by Udo (2020) that the education of Ubuntu needs to be integrated into all levels and fields of the educational system. Furthermore, educating for peace with the principles of Ubuntu holds value in creating context-specific peace education initiatives in fragile democracies such as South Africa. This theory emphasises the importance of social networks, trust, and shared norms and values in promoting collective action, social cohesion, and sustainable development. At the same time, the Ubuntu philosophy, which is deeply rooted in African ontology and traditions, also stresses the significance of interconnectedness, reciprocity, and human dignity in building harmonious relationships and communities. In this section, I explore the interconnectedness of the Social Capital Theory and the Ubuntu philosophy in the context of my research on context-specific peace education initiatives and peacebuilding in the Western Cape. Drawing on the existing literature and my fieldwork, I examine how the Social Capital Theory can be informed by and complemented with the principles of Ubuntu, and how this can contribute to more context-specific and culturally appropriate approaches to community development, peacebuilding and peace education. Specifically, I analyse how social networks, trust, reciprocity, shared norms and values, and collective action, as identified in the Social Capital Theory, can be enhanced and strengthened through the application of Ubuntu principles, such as the importance of community participation, the recognition of diversity, the promotion of mutual respect, and the cultivation of empathy and compassion. I argue that such an approach can lead to more effective and sustainable peacebuilding initiatives and a deeper understanding and appreciation of the cultural and social contexts in which they operate. Overall, it aims to contribute to a more comprehensive and nuanced understanding of the Social Capital Theory and its potential for advancing community development and peacebuilding in the Western Cape, by highlighting the relevance and value of the Ubuntu philosophy in this context. 19 3.1 Definition of Social Capital Theory The concept of social capital has been around for some time but only gained attention in academic and policy discussions during the 1990s. Its supposed relevance has been evident in explaining the social and economic phenomena in recent years. Most scholars have identified that social capital is a multifaceted and intricate concept that encompasses a wide range of dimensions, types, and measurement levels (Bhandari and Yasonubu, 2009). The social capital theory has its roots in the works of early sociologists such as Durkheim, Weber, and Marx. However, the modern concept of social capital as it is commonly understood today can be traced back to the works of Pierre Bourdieu, James Coleman, and Robert Putnam in the 1980s and 1990s. Bourdieu was among the first to use the term "social capital" in a sociological context, defining it as the resources that individuals and groups have access to through their social networks. Coleman and Putnam also contributed significantly to the development of social capital theory. Coleman argued that social capital can be a powerful source of social order, while Putnam demonstrated the importance of social capital for civic engagement

the modern concept of social capital as it is commonly understood today can be traced back to the works of Pierre Bourdieu, James Coleman, and Robert Putnam in the 1980s and 1990s. Bourdieu was among the first to use the term "social capital" in a sociological context, defining it as the resources that individuals and groups have access to through their social networks. Coleman and Putnam also contributed significantly to the development of social capital theory. Coleman argued that social capital can be a powerful source of social order, while Putnam demonstrated the importance of social capital for civic engagement and community development. Since then, the social capital theory has gained widespread attention in academic and policy circles, with many scholars exploring its applications in various fields such as economics, political science, and public health. Today, the social capital theory continues to be a valuable framework for understanding the role of social relationships in promoting cooperation, trust, and collective action for mutual benefits. Social Capital can still be seen as a new concept in various fields. Woolcock (1998) called it to have great potential when properly ‘defined, operationalised and proven to have explanatory power’ (in Bhandari and Yasunubu, 2009:7). Castle (1998) echoed this by suggesting that the social capital concept should be used comparatively to have value as an analytical framework. 20 One of the challenges in social capital theory is the lack of a widely accepted definition. There are varying definitions and understandings of the concept, which can lead to different interpretations and applications. This lack of consensus on the definition can also result in difficulties in measuring and operationalizing the concept. Thus, the development of the theory and its application in practice can be hindered. The definitions describe it as a multidimensional concept that involves social relationships, networks, norms, values, trust, and institutions that facilitate cooperation and collective action for mutual benefits. 3.2 Definitions Name Definition Pierre Bourdieu ‘the sum of the actual or potential resources that are linked to the possession of a durable network of more or less institutionalised relationships of mutual acquaintance and recognition—in other words, to membership in a group’ (1986:248). Economic Cooperation and Development) 21 ‘The institutions, relationships, and norms that shape the quality and The World Bank quantity of a society’s social interactions. Social capital is not just the sum of the institutions which underpin a society—it is the glue that holds them together’ (2007). Figure 2: Definitions Bourdieu's definition of social capital can be operationalised in context-specific peace education by focusing on building durable networks of relationships and mutual recognition among individuals and groups involved in peacebuilding efforts. This can involve fostering trust and reciprocity between community members, promoting community participation and engagement, and creating opportunities for individuals to build their skills, knowledge, and social connections. In the context of peace education, social capital can be developed through initiatives such as community dialogue sessions, peer mentoring and support programs, and collaborative problem- solving activities. By leveraging social capital, peace educators can create a more supportive and collaborative environment for peacebuilding efforts, ultimately contributing to sustainable and inclusive peace. The concept of social capital in Putnam's definition is closely related to the ubuntu philosophy as both emphasize the importance of

can involve fostering trust and reciprocity between community members, promoting community participation and engagement, and creating opportunities for individuals to build their skills, knowledge, and social connections. In the context of peace education, social capital can be developed through initiatives such as community dialogue sessions, peer mentoring and support programs, and collaborative problem- solving activities. By leveraging social capital, peace educators can create a more supportive and collaborative environment for peacebuilding efforts, ultimately contributing to sustainable and inclusive peace. The concept of social capital in Putnam's definition is closely related to the ubuntu philosophy as both emphasize the importance of social networks, civic engagement, and reciprocity in promoting social cooperation and coordination. In both cases, the richness of social capital in a society is measured by the number of civic associations and the degree of participation in those associations, which are seen as indicators of the level of trust and norms of reciprocity in that society. In terms of operationalisation, peace education programs could focus on promoting civic engagement, participation in voluntary organisations, and the formation of social connections to strengthen social capital and foster sturdy norms of reciprocity and trust, which can help to resolve dilemmas of collective actions and improve overall productivity and well-being. Fukuyama’s contribution to Social Capital Theory by emphasising trust, reciprocity and shared norms has been seen as an important indicator of social capital theory. However, this measurement 22 alone cannot capture the full multidimensional nature of social capital. This concept of trust and social relationships is central to Ubuntu’s philosophy, which emphasizes the importance of community, relationships, and shared values in promoting social cohesion and development. As Migheli highlighted, ‘the sense of community is generated and maintained by the ties between its members’ (2017:1222). The author emphasises the sense of community that is generated and maintained by the ties between its members is central to both the concept of social capital and the philosophy of Ubuntu. The definitions by OECD and The World Bank are similar to previous definitions in that they also describe social capital as a collection of social networks, relationships, and norms that promote cooperation and mutual benefit. Both definitions emphasise the importance of shared values and understandings in facilitating social interactions and suggest that social capital is essential for maintaining the social fabric of society. The World Bank's definition specifically highlights the institutional aspect of social capital, emphasising that it is not just the individual institutions that matter, but also the relationships and norms that bind them together. Overall, these definitions provide a more comprehensive understanding of social capital by recognising its multidimensional nature and the importance of both formal and informal institutions in its development. Despite its multi-faceted nature, different definitions of the Social Capital theory have faced some criticisms. The pushback has mainly come from economists who questioned the conceptualisation and disapproved of its possible measurement and chosen indicators (Smith and Kulynych 2002, Harriss, 2002, Roberts, 2004, Halpern 2005). Additionally, Bowles and Gintis (2002) argue that the term "community" is more suitable for social capital than the term "capital" because it shifts the emphasis from individual ownership to collective actions and relationships. Despite the criticisms, multifaceted theories like social capital can offer a wide range of concepts and tools that can be applied to different contexts. In the context of peace education, the different dimensions of social capital theory can be used to inform the design of interventions that promote trust, social cohesion, and dialogue among different groups. The various concepts within the social capital theory, such as trust, norms, and networks, can be operationalized in peace education

"community" is more suitable for social capital than the term "capital" because it shifts the emphasis from individual ownership to collective actions and relationships. Despite the criticisms, multifaceted theories like social capital can offer a wide range of concepts and tools that can be applied to different contexts. In the context of peace education, the different dimensions of social capital theory can be used to inform the design of interventions that promote trust, social cohesion, and dialogue among different groups. The various concepts within the social capital theory, such as trust, norms, and networks, can be operationalized in peace education to foster cooperation, communication, and understanding between different groups. For example, 23 building trust and strengthening social networks between individuals and groups can help reduce tensions and conflicts. Additionally, promoting civic engagement and participation in voluntary organizations can help build social capital, which in turn can contribute to more peaceful and cohesive societies. Therefore, while the social capital theory may have its limitations, it can still offer valuable insights and tools for promoting peace and development. 3.3 Social Capital and Social Cohesion Social capital has also been discussed in social cohesion frameworks- highlighting the importance of horizontal dimensions in cohesive societies. According to UNDP, these dimensions usually involve ‘cohesion among citizens, reflected in the extent to which civil society, social organizations and institutions (such as religious institutions) exhibit trust and a sense of interdependence and common destiny’ (2020:12). Portes described social capital as ‘the goodwill resulting from social relations’ (2000 in Menés and Donato, 2015:324). This sense of interdependence and common destiny is reflected in Ubuntu's philosophy of promoting social cohesion and development through community, relationships, and shared values. 3.4 Ubuntu and Social Capital Defining and explaining Ubuntu as an African ontology presents a complex task due to its multifaceted nature and the challenge of capturing its essence within Western conceptual frameworks. Nussbaum points to some of the concepts captured within the philosophy as, ‘the capacity in African culture to express compassion, reciprocity, dignity, harmony and humanity in the interest of building and maintaining community with justice and mutual caring’ (2003:2). Additionally, Gade (2012) recognizes two aspects of Ubuntu through his research, ‘people defining Ubuntu as a moral quality of a person’ and ‘people defining Ubuntu as a phenomenon according to which persons are interconnected’ (in van Stam, 2015: 43). The interconnectedness inherent in Ubuntu is manifested through various means, as Van Stam emphasizes its role in cultivating a collective consciousness through the exposure to the lived experiences of others 24 (2015) which ‘facilitates a process of building, restoring and growing of an open-ended becoming, an aligned future’ (p.43). Building on van Stam’s point on Ubuntu’s role in facilitating national building, Nelson Mandela played a significant role in popularising the by embedding it in the TRC processes and the South African constitution highlighting the importance of promoting reconciliation and justice in the country. In the epilogue to the South African interim Constitution, it states, ‘there is a need for understanding but not for vengeance, a need for reparation but not for retaliation, a need for ubuntu but not for victimisation’ (Government of South Africa, 1993). Despite ongoing efforts to operationalize Ubuntu, its inherent complexity poses challenges in effectively implementing it across various sectors. Furthermore, Ubuntu has also been deeply ingrained in the cultural fabric of Southern Africa through the art of storytelling. Nelson Mandela himself narrated an illustrative tale of Ubuntu, stating, ‘In the old days when we were young, a traveller through our country would stop at a village, and he didn't have to ask for food or water.

but not for vengeance, a need for reparation but not for retaliation, a need for ubuntu but not for victimisation’ (Government of South Africa, 1993). Despite ongoing efforts to operationalize Ubuntu, its inherent complexity poses challenges in effectively implementing it across various sectors. Furthermore, Ubuntu has also been deeply ingrained in the cultural fabric of Southern Africa through the art of storytelling. Nelson Mandela himself narrated an illustrative tale of Ubuntu, stating, ‘In the old days when we were young, a traveller through our country would stop at a village, and he didn't have to ask for food or water. Once he stops, the people will give him food and entertain him. That is one aspect of Ubuntu’ (youtube.com, 2006). Mandela's anecdote about the inherent hospitality and generosity embedded in Ubuntu reinforces the connection between Ubuntu and the notion of social capital, demonstrating how the values of interconnectedness and communal support form the foundation for fostering strong relationships and collective well-being within communities. Social capital and Ubuntu are two concepts that have gained attention in various fields of study, including sociology, political science, and international development. Both concepts focus on the importance of social relationships and networks in promoting collective action and achieving common goals. While social capital theory emerged from the Western academic tradition, Ubuntu is a concept rooted in African ontology and philosophy. Despite their different origins, both social capital and Ubuntu share some similarities in their emphasis on the importance of social networks, trust, and reciprocity. In the paper titled, ‘Ubuntu and Social Capital: a strong relationship and a possible instrument of socio-economic development’ (2017), Migheli makes a case that there is a close relationship between Ubuntu and social capital and highlights the potential benefits of promoting Ubuntu in 25 the development of sub-Saharan societies- recognising that the governments should consider Ubuntu as a valuable tool in addressing poverty and conflicts and implement ‘Ubuntu-based policies’ (p. 1224). These Ubuntu-based policies could be reflected in different sectors. The association between social capital and education has become an important part of the discourse. This is not just through in- school learning but ‘through informal and extra-curricular encounters such as those provided through sports and clubs’ (Glaeser, 2003:391 in Field, 2017:82). In the South African context, the legacy of apartheid has had a significant impact on social capital. The segregation policies of the apartheid regime created deep divides between racial groups, eroding trust and cooperation among them. The transition to democracy in 1994 marked a significant step towards rebuilding social capital, but the effects of apartheid are still felt today. Promoting Ubuntu as a central element of African identity and a means of socio-economic development could be a valuable tool in rebuilding social capital in South Africa and help to rebuild trust and cooperation among different groups in South African society. 3.5 Types of Social Capital Social capital is a multifaceted concept that encompasses various forms such as structural and cognitive social capital, strong and weak social capital, horizontal and vertical social capital, and bonding, bridging, and linking social capital. In this discussion, the focus will be on the latter three forms, which are commonly identified in the literature. 26 Type of Social Capital Definition Example Bonding Ethnic groups, religious Social capital exists within a specific group, often Social communities, sports based on shared characteristics or experiences. Figure 3: Type of Social Capital, adapted from UNDP (2020) 3.5.1 Bonding Social Capital Bonding social capital is usually characterized by a sense of commonality and trust that fosters loyalty and attachment within the group. 27 In the context of South Africa, bonding social capital is especially relevant due to its diverse ethnic and cultural groups through shared cultural practices and traditions of various ethnic groups, such as Zulu or Xhosa, which promote a sense of loyalty and attachment within these groups. This can be harnessed in peace education initiatives by highlighting the shared values and cultural practices that exist among different groups in South Africa, emphasizing the importance of respect and understanding between them. By promoting the idea of "ubuntu," which emphasizes the interconnectedness and interdependence of individuals within a community, peace education can help to foster a sense of common identity and purpose that transcends differences and builds stronger bonds of social capital. As Bhandari suggests, ‘bonding promotes communication and relationships necessary to pursue common goals’ (2009:500) and ‘it influences creation and nurturing of community organisations’ (ibid). 3.5.2 Bridging Social Capital It refers to the relationships and connections between individuals or groups who belong to different social, economic, or cultural backgrounds. It involves networks that cut across group lines and create social cohesion among diverse groups. These networks can take the form of civil society organizations, community groups, or social clubs, and they serve as bridges between groups that might otherwise have little interaction or communication with one another. As stated by Bhandari, ‘bridging is crucial for solving community problems through helping people to know each other, building relationships, sharing information, and mobilising community resources’ (2009:500). Bridging social capital is important because it helps to promote understanding, tolerance, and collaboration between different groups and networks, which can contribute to social and economic development, and can also prevent conflict and promote peace. According to the UNDP report, ‘when such bridging networks and relationships are present, cross-communal mobilization and crisis management are more likely to prevent conflict escalation’ (2020:20). In post-conflict contexts such as South Africa, promoting collaboration and cooperation among diverse groups, peace education initiatives can strengthen bridging social capital and facilitate conflict resolution- helping to build more resilient and peaceful communities. 28 3.5.3 Linking Social Capital Linking social capital refers to networks and relationships that connect different groups or communities and facilitate communication, cooperation, and collaboration. These relationships can help prevent conflicts from escalating by providing a channel for dialogue and understanding between different groups. UNDP report called these ‘insider mediators’ (2020:20) who can serve as a ‘visual symbols of cooperation and common destiny’ (ibid). In the context of South Africa, linking social capital can be seen in various initiatives that bring together people from different backgrounds, such as interfaith councils, community development projects, and advocacy groups. For example, the South African Council of Churches is an interfaith council that brings together leaders

relationships that connect different groups or communities and facilitate communication, cooperation, and collaboration. These relationships can help prevent conflicts from escalating by providing a channel for dialogue and understanding between different groups. UNDP report called these ‘insider mediators’ (2020:20) who can serve as a ‘visual symbols of cooperation and common destiny’ (ibid). In the context of South Africa, linking social capital can be seen in various initiatives that bring together people from different backgrounds, such as interfaith councils, community development projects, and advocacy groups. For example, the South African Council of Churches is an interfaith council that brings together leaders from different religious groups to promote peace, justice, and reconciliation. This council has played a critical role in facilitating dialogue between different groups during times of conflict and has helped to bridge divides between communities and ‘uniting denominations to stand against the injustices of apartheid’ (Womack and Pillay, 2019:1). 3.6 Conclusion One of the critiques of social capital theory is its deficiency in conceptualisation and application to development processes. Therefore, my research will focus on operationalising the concepts and drawing a comparison to the principles of Ubuntu philosophy to provide a better understanding of social capital and its potential contribution to development practitioners and policymakers. Furthermore, this research also aims to contribute to the understanding of the Triple Nexus approach by exploring the potential linkages between social capital, peace education, and sustainable development, which can inform the design and implementation of effective development interventions. The Social Capital Theory provides a valuable framework for understanding the complex social networks and relationships that underpin successful peacebuilding and conflict resolution initiatives. By emphasizing the importance of trust, reciprocity, and mutual obligations, social capital theory highlights the need to build and sustain strong community relationships to achieve 29 lasting peace and social cohesion. This framework will guide the analysis of the empirical data gathered in this study and help to identify the key factors that contribute to the success of context- specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape. By using the Social Capital Theory as a lens through which to view the data, this study aims to contribute to the ongoing debate on the role of social capital in peacebuilding and to inform the development of effective and sustainable peace education initiatives. Specifically, I will use the concepts of social networks, trust, reciprocity, shared norms and values and collection action identified in the Social Capital Theory. Additionally, it can provide a framework for evaluating the impact of such interventions and identifying areas for improvement. Overall, the multifaceted nature of social capital theory allows for a more nuanced understanding of social dynamics, which can inform effective strategies for promoting positive social change. 4 Methodological Framework To address the research question, a cross-sectional research design with a qualitative, abductive approach will be employed. 4.0 Research Design By studying multiple cases, I aim to capture and understand the variation that exists across different individuals and organizations within the Western Cape region. This design allows me to explore the nuances and finer distinctions between cases, enabling a comprehensive analysis of the variables I am interested in. Additionally, the selection of multiple cases provides a larger sample size, which is beneficial for encountering more variation and ensuring the validity of my sampling procedure. As Bryman suggests, ‘A fairly typical form of such research is when the researcher employs unstructured interviewing or semi-structured interviewing with a number of people’ (Bryman, 2012:62). By incorporating a cross-sectional design and examining more than one case, this research aims to provide a comprehensive and nuanced understanding of the subject matter. According to Bryman,

It also emphasizes the importance of grounding my analysis and interpretation in the lived experiences and perspectives of the participants, which aligns with the qualitative nature of this research. 4.1 Qualitative study A qualitative study can be seen as an essential research approach that focuses on understanding the meaning, experiences, and perspectives of individuals within a specific context. In the realm of peacebuilding and social cohesion, qualitative research provides valuable insights into the intricate dynamics and complex social phenomena that quantitative data alone cannot capture. As described by Bryman, ‘Qualitative researchers are much more inclined than quantitative researchers to provide a great deal of descriptive detail when reporting the fruits of their research’ (2012:401). By employing methods such as semi-structured interviews and observations, qualitative research allows for an in-depth exploration of the lived realities of individuals, their interactions, and the contextual factors that shape their experiences. As echoed by Bryman, ‘One of the main reasons why qualitative researchers are keen to provide considerable descriptive detail is that they typically emphasize the importance of the contextual understanding of social behaviour. This means that behaviour, values, or whatever must be understood in context’ (ibid). This research approach is particularly relevant to this study as it enables a comprehensive examination of the context-specific education initiatives in the Western Cape, uncovering the nuances, challenges, and potential solutions that are embedded within the social fabric of the region. Through qualitative inquiry, I was able to capture the rich narratives, diverse perspectives, and contextual nuances that inform the understanding of social capital, peacebuilding, and the promotion of social cohesion in the Western Cape, contributing to a more holistic and nuanced understanding of this research topic. 31 4.2 Snowball sampling with purposive sampling Purposive sampling with snowball sampling was chosen for participant selection due to its relevance to the research objectives. As highlighted by Bryman, ‘it aims to sample cases/ participants in a strategic way, so that those sampled are relevant to the research questions that are being posed (2012:714). Purposive sampling allowed for the deliberate selection of participants with specific knowledge and experiences related to context-specific education initiatives and social cohesion in the Western Cape. Snowball sampling complemented this approach by leveraging existing social networks to identify additional participants who may provide valuable insights. To complement this sampling strategy and broaden the sample, the snowball method was also used. This strategy is echoed by Bryman that, ‘purposive sampling often involves more than one of the approaches outlined above. One noteworthy source of insights, the Transformation Education forum at Stellenbosch University, will be particularly emphasized and justified. This summit laid a solid groundwork for comprehending social cohesion and peace education initiatives, with several topics discussed aligning with findings from other research methods utilized in this study. By integrating insights from the summit alongside other research sources, it aims to deliver a comprehensive analysis of the research questions and objectives, ultimately contributing to the advancement of effective strategies for fostering peaceful and inclusive societies. Furthermore, following the presentation of insights from the summit, the research findings from the interviews will be presented, offering further depth and understanding of the study. 5.0 Creating Safe Spaces for Dialogue: Insights from the Transformation Education Forum South Africa still continuously ranks as the most unequal country in the world (The World Bank et al, 2022) and Stellenbosch located in the Western Cape has been historically linked to the development and implementation of apartheid in South Africa. The town served as a centre for the development of apartheid policies by the National Party, which came to power in 1948. 37 Stellenbosch University, located in the town, was also a major site of academic support for apartheid ideology, with prominent scholars and leaders advocating for the separation of races and the subjugation of black South Africans. The town's role in creating and enforcing apartheid has made it a site of ongoing debate and reflection on South Africa's past and future. Given Stellenbosch's historical link to apartheid and the role of Stellenbosch University in supporting apartheid ideology, it begs the question: what is the university's responsibility moving forward in promoting social justice and addressing the ongoing inequalities in South Africa? How can the university use its influence and resources to create a more equitable and just society, and what steps can it take to confront and redress the legacy of apartheid? These are important guiding questions to consider and keep in mind. There is a history of the conversation around the ‘transformation’ at the Stellenbosch University. The University’s Transformation Unit acknowledges the urgency of transforming the University’s programmes to fit within its proposed model of a new African University to create a more ‘just, sustainable and democratic future’ (Stellenbosch University, 2019). The Transformation Education Forum held at Stellenbosch University was

How can the university use its influence and resources to create a more equitable and just society, and what steps can it take to confront and redress the legacy of apartheid? These are important guiding questions to consider and keep in mind. There is a history of the conversation around the ‘transformation’ at the Stellenbosch University. The University’s Transformation Unit acknowledges the urgency of transforming the University’s programmes to fit within its proposed model of a new African University to create a more ‘just, sustainable and democratic future’ (Stellenbosch University, 2019). The Transformation Education Forum held at Stellenbosch University was an important event that brought together scholars, educators, activists, and policymakers to discuss strategies for promoting transformative education in South Africa. The forum was designed to explore ways to address the challenges of inequality, discrimination, and social exclusion that continue to affect the country. The focus was on creating an inclusive, equitable, and just society, where everyone can thrive regardless of their background or identity. Through the presentations, discussions, and workshops, the forum provided a platform for participants to share their insights, experiences, and ideas on how to promote social cohesion and build more sustainable communities. Three main themes kept coming up in the conversations. Theme 1: The importance of operationalising shared values such as Ubuntu in conversation about Social Cohesion During the Transformation forum, there was a consensus among participants that to achieve social cohesion, it is important to operationalise shared values such as Ubuntu. By operationalising 38 Ubuntu, participants meant that these values should not just be used as empty rhetoric, but rather should be reflected in everyday practices, policies, and programmes such as Stellenbosch University’s policies. The forum highlighted the need for communities to be grounded in values such as empathy, respect, and collective responsibility. Participants agreed that such values should form the foundation of efforts to build social cohesion in society. Theme 2: Creating safe spaces where open dialogue happens across different identities. Another key theme that emerged from the Transformation forum was the need for safe spaces where people from different identities can have open dialogues. Participants agreed that such spaces are critical in building trust and promoting understanding between different groups. They highlighted the importance of creating safe spaces that encourage people to share their experiences and perspectives in a non-judgmental and respectful environment. The forum emphasised the need for these spaces to be inclusive, where all voices are heard, and all identities are valued. Theme 3: The role of universities as actors of change by implementing transformative policies and cross-pollinating knowledge into communities and society. The Transformation forum also discussed the role of universities as actors of change in building social cohesion. Participants acknowledged that universities have a responsibility to promote transformative policies that promote social cohesion and inclusion. They highlighted the need for universities to engage with communities and share knowledge and resources. Participants emphasised the importance of cross-pollination of knowledge from universities to communities, as well as the need for universities to promote active citizenship and civic engagement among their students. Attending the Transformation Education Summit at Stellenbosch University was a valuable experience that allowed me to gain insights into the complexities of transformation and the challenges faced in promoting social cohesion. However, as a researcher with limited prior knowledge of the South African context, I also faced some limitations in fully understanding the nuances of the discussions and debates that took place. Moving forward, I was able to reflect on the importance of contextual knowledge and the need to continually educate myself on the histories and cultures of the communities
