Additionally, the forum highlighted the importance of creating safe spaces for dialogue and the operationalisation of shared values, such as Ubuntu, in promoting social cohesion. This reaffirmed my research on the role of social capital in promoting community resilience, and I sought to incorporate these concepts into my analysis and findings. 5.1 Insights on Social Cohesion and Ubuntu: Findings from Interviews This part presents the findings from a series of 13 interviews conducted with individuals who have direct or indirect experience with topics related to Social Cohesion, Ubuntu, and Social Capital in the Western Cape region. The interviews were conducted as part of a research project aimed at exploring how these concepts can be operationalized and applied in practice to promote positive peace and social cohesion. These were also aimed to answer the research questions aimed to explore the contributing factors, challenges, and the potential role of Ubuntu philosophy in developing effective and context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape. The participants included are from a range of backgrounds and sectors, including community leaders, academics, civil society activists, and policymakers. Through these interviews, I gained valuable insights into how these concepts are understood and applied in different contexts, as well as the challenges and opportunities that exist in promoting them. This chapter is divided into several sections, each focusing on a specific theme that emerged from the interviews. 5.1.1 Going Beyond Ubuntu This category pertains to the importance of moving beyond Ubuntu as a mere abstract philosophy and making it a practical, contextualized concept that can be applied in real-life situations. Interviewees have shared ideas on how Ubuntu can be operationalized and made relevant to different contexts, such as workplaces, schools, and communities. The findings from this category emphasize the need to move beyond Ubuntu as a mere philosophy and make it practical and relevant to various contexts. Interviewees suggested that Ubuntu can be operationalized by acknowledging and nurturing the self-sufficiency of communities. They also highlighted the 40 importance of consulting with people and exchanging the spirit of Ubuntu to solve problems. One interviewee suggested that “Ubuntu should be acknowledged in our poorer communities. They are self-sufficient. They will look after their own with the scarce resources that they have”. Some of the interviewees’ quotes illustrate the importance of fighting for justice to achieve true peace, as well as the need for deliberate bridge-building to bring different worlds together. As one participant pointed out, “I grew up in this ideology (Apartheid) and we were raised on the steady myth about each other, but somebody came and said that we are branches of the same tree and that person was Nelson Mandela. I will always be grateful to Nelson Mandela as we built bridges between people”. Despite the progress made in bridging divides and promoting unity,

scarce resources that they have”. Some of the interviewees’ quotes illustrate the importance of fighting for justice to achieve true peace, as well as the need for deliberate bridge-building to bring different worlds together. As one participant pointed out, “I grew up in this ideology (Apartheid) and we were raised on the steady myth about each other, but somebody came and said that we are branches of the same tree and that person was Nelson Mandela. I will always be grateful to Nelson Mandela as we built bridges between people”. Despite the progress made in bridging divides and promoting unity, there remains a sense of lingering hopelessness among some interviewees which they attributed to the lasting effects of apartheid, such as socioeconomic inequalities, systemic discrimination, and deep-rooted trauma. One participant highlighted that “post-1994 we have a new Enemy and the need for Ubuntu is even greater now”. This is echoed by another participant who stated that “it was difficult to break down the old attitudes of Apartheid and perhaps not enough was done at the beginning”. The concepts of interdependence and interconnectedness were emphasized, along with the ethics of care and servant leadership to characterise it. As emphasised by one interviewee, “Ubuntu is missing in our communities” and characterised Ubuntu as “sharing of resources and respect for other people”. Overall, these findings suggest that Ubuntu should not be treated as an abstract concept but should be practically applied in various contexts. By nurturing self-sufficiency within communities and promoting dialogue and consultation, it can be operationalized and made relevant. The concepts of interdependence, interconnectedness, and servant leadership should be emphasized in promoting Ubuntu, and deliberate bridge-building is necessary to bring people from different worlds together. 41 5.1.2 Building Positive Peace and Fostering Social Cohesion through Social Capital This category focuses on the importance of relationships and social networks in fostering social cohesion and building positive peace. Interviewees have shared their experiences of how strong social capital and relationships can help individuals and communities navigate difficult situations, resolve conflicts, and build trust. The findings in this category highlight the critical role that relationships and social networks play in fostering social cohesion and building positive peace. The interviewees emphasized that strong social capital and relationships can help individuals and communities navigate difficult situations, resolve conflicts, and build trust. Tone interviewee pointed out that “schools, youth and community centres can bring people together to have conversations about social issues” which can “help build bridges and break down barriers between different communities”. However, the findings also suggest that there are significant challenges in building social cohesion and positive peace. The legacy of apartheid in South Africa continues to impact communities today, and there is a significant disparity in resources between different schools, with public schools struggling to provide adequate resources for their students. Additionally, the interviewees noted that there is a growing trend towards exclusion and building walls rather than building bridges. One interviewee pointed out the problem saying that “It is not the people, but the situation some of us are in. We are angry and hopeless”. In addition to the challenges mentioned above, interviewees also highlighted the difficulty in addressing the fact that individuals from public schools with fewer opportunities to interact and integrate are missing out on the opportunities to create stronger social capital. This disparity can exacerbate existing social and economic inequalities, which can further hinder efforts to build social cohesion and positive peace. Furthermore, some interviewees also suggested that it may be necessary to redefine what social capital means as one interviewee pointed out that “everybody 42 has some kind

but the situation some of us are in. We are angry and hopeless”. In addition to the challenges mentioned above, interviewees also highlighted the difficulty in addressing the fact that individuals from public schools with fewer opportunities to interact and integrate are missing out on the opportunities to create stronger social capital. This disparity can exacerbate existing social and economic inequalities, which can further hinder efforts to build social cohesion and positive peace. Furthermore, some interviewees also suggested that it may be necessary to redefine what social capital means as one interviewee pointed out that “everybody 42 has some kind of social capital” and “it is about recognising that and utilising it in our communities”. This category emphasizes the critical role of relationships and social networks in fostering social cohesion and positive peace while acknowledging the challenges posed by the legacy of apartheid, resource disparities, exclusionary trends, and limited opportunities for creating social capital. 5.1.3 Creating Safe Spaces as a Means for Integration This category looks at the value of creating safe and inclusive spaces where difficult conversations about sensitive topics such as race, gender, and sexuality can happen. Interviewees have shared their experiences of participating in dialogues facilitated through means such as storytelling, art, music, and other creative outlets. One interviewee highlighted that “forums such as the Transformation Education summit can be critical in bringing different perspectives in one room” because “we don’t always interact”. Another interviewee highlighted an experience of participating in a storytelling event that aimed to heal wounds and bring peace into their community in the Western Cape. They said that “through telling my story and listening to other people’s stories, I was more empathetic that we all have burdens and wounds to heal from the past”. The interviewees emphasized that integration is crucial for building peace and resolving conflicts in the community. There was a sentiment that by integrating individuals from different backgrounds, fostering inclusivity, and addressing the issues of violence, such as gender-based violence, gangsterism, and xenophobia, the region can create an environment where peace can flourish. They also stressed the need to respect each other's cultures and social capital, as well as to promote peace education and intergenerational peace. Literature, art, sporting events such as rugby and telling stories in a safe space have been highlighted as important tools for educating society and promoting understanding among people from different backgrounds. One interviewee highlighted that “‘literature plays a very important role in educating society’ and “showing society how things should and could develop.” Despite progress, interviewees noted that South Africa still faces 43 challenges related to isolation and insularity, scarcity of resources, broken homes, and hopelessness in different spaces, such as schools and workspaces. One interviewee highlighted that “we are still very isolated and insular with very few contacts with people from different background”. Overall, the category underscores the importance of creating safe spaces for dialogue and understanding as a means of integration and promoting peace. It also highlights the need to address historical legacies of discrimination and promote intergenerational peace through context-specific solutions. 5.1.4 Young People as Bridge-Builders This theme highlights the significance of young people in bridging gaps and creating social cohesion through their active participation in civic engagement. The interviewees emphasized the critical role of universities in positioning themselves to achieve social cohesion by providing opportunities for community engagement, nurturing leadership skills, and promoting a culture of dialogue and inclusivity. They also stressed the importance of equipping young people with the necessary skills and knowledge to become effective agents of change in their communities. The interviewees further discussed the challenges in

legacies of discrimination and promote intergenerational peace through context-specific solutions. 5.1.4 Young People as Bridge-Builders This theme highlights the significance of young people in bridging gaps and creating social cohesion through their active participation in civic engagement. The interviewees emphasized the critical role of universities in positioning themselves to achieve social cohesion by providing opportunities for community engagement, nurturing leadership skills, and promoting a culture of dialogue and inclusivity. They also stressed the importance of equipping young people with the necessary skills and knowledge to become effective agents of change in their communities. The interviewees further discussed the challenges in bringing people together and promoting change, including the need to address deep-rooted issues such as communication barriers and racial tensions. A few interviewees highlighted what one interviewee said about their experience at the University: “I didn’t always have a positive experience in university spaces- I’ve encountered discrimination and felt that me not speaking Afrikaans excluded me from entering some important spaces where decisions are being made”. The interviewees also recognized the importance of working with parents and teachers to create safe and supportive environments that foster emotional well-being and capacity-building. One interviewee said that “To change the child, you need to change the parent”. However, they acknowledged that the lack of funding and resources can hinder efforts to promote social cohesion and active civic engagement. 44 5.1.5 Local Reconciliation Practices and Repairing the Social Fabric This category focuses on the importance of embracing local reconciliation practices to heal societal wounds and repair the social fabric. Interviewees have shared examples of local reconciliation practices that have been successful in their communities and discussed how these practices can be replicated and scaled up to promote social cohesion on a larger scale. One of the reoccurring themes in these initiatives was creating a safe space and embracing already existing practises such as Ubuntu to create a sense of shared understanding and values. As one interviewee puts it, “It is not enough to just bring people together, we need to create a meaning around it and it needs to be meaningful for them so that’s where Ubuntu comes in”. The interviewees mentioned that these practices can take many forms, such as dialogue, mediation, and community-building initiatives. One interviewee stressed the need for action-oriented young people to lead these efforts, while another emphasized the value of the private sector in promoting entrepreneurship and self-sufficiency among young people. A few interviewees highlighted there is a need for more resources and capacity building being given to schools and educational institutions to take on the position to teach about peace education, and reconciliation practises. One interview leading their own CBO noted that “there are so many projects happening in South Africa all the time. We are on the ground leading the efforts. We are helping families heal. We are helping communities heal, but there is still so much that needs to be done.” However, interviewees also noted the challenges faced in promoting reconciliation and peace in South Africa. They spoke of the prevalence of violence in communities and the need for more conversational spaces where people can come together to understand one another. They also acknowledged the existence of hatred and misinformation among some people and stressed the importance of addressing these issues through dialogue and education. Despite these challenges, interviewees expressed a strong desire to help and highlighted the important role that churches can play in promoting peace and reconciliation in their communities. One participant noted that “NGOs and Churches and other religious institutions are trying to fill the void of government’s failures to provide basic services”.

They spoke of the prevalence of violence in communities and the need for more conversational spaces where people can come together to understand one another. They also acknowledged the existence of hatred and misinformation among some people and stressed the importance of addressing these issues through dialogue and education. Despite these challenges, interviewees expressed a strong desire to help and highlighted the important role that churches can play in promoting peace and reconciliation in their communities. One participant noted that “NGOs and Churches and other religious institutions are trying to fill the void of government’s failures to provide basic services”. 45 The value of the private sector and NGOs in promoting peace and reconciliation has also been highlighted. Participants have emphasized that the government has failed to provide adequate spaces for conversation and reconciliation and that it is up to individuals and community organizations to fill this void. The role of the Church has also been recognized as important in promoting social cohesion, and there was a consensus that more needs to be done to bring peace to South African communities that are still dealing with the aftermath of apartheid. 5.1.5.1 Addressing Violence in Western Cape As evident from the interviews- the issue of violence in the Western Cape is a pressing concern that demands immediate attention. Various forms of violence, including gender-based violence, gangsterism, and xenophobia, have been particularly alarming in recent years. These issues have compounded the challenges faced by communities, exacerbating the overall sense of insecurity and fear. Gender-based violence continues to affect women and girls, with high rates of domestic abuse and sexual assault. Gangsterism, fuelled by socio-economic disparities and limited opportunities, has led to the proliferation of gangs and criminal activities, posing a significant threat to both individuals and communities. Additionally, incidents of xenophobia have created tensions among different population groups, perpetuating a cycle of discrimination and violence. Addressing these specific issues requires targeted interventions, including education, awareness campaigns, improved law enforcement, and support services for victims. By acknowledging and actively confronting these issues, the Western Cape can work towards building a safer and more inclusive society for all its residents. As highlighted by Leggett (2016), Western Cape keeps coming up as the ‘country’s most crime-ridden province’ (p. 15), which causes significant confusion as it is one of the ‘best developed provinces in the country, with the highest employment levels’ (ibid). This discrepancy underscores the complex root causes of violence in the region, which demand multi-faceted approaches for effective resolution. Understanding and addressing these issues requires comprehensive research on the socio-economic dynamics taking into consideration the history of the Western Cape. By recognising the need for a holistic approach, one that combines efforts to promote social cohesion, and integration and address the underlying factors of violence, we can begin to unravel the intricate web of causes and work towards sustainable solutions. 46 The next section of this report will analyse the findings through the lens of social capital theory. By examining the findings of this study through the lens of social capital theory, we can gain a better understanding of how social connections and networks can impact civic engagement and social cohesion. Drawing upon the experiences and lessons learned from these initiatives, I explore the intricate relationship between education, social cohesion, and violence prevention. By examining the factors that contribute to building social capital and promoting Ubuntu values within communities, valuable insights into the challenges and opportunities for creating sustainable change in the Western Cape can be gained. Through a comprehensive analysis, it aims to shed light on the complexities of violence and social cohesion in the region, while offering valuable recommendations for future educational strategies that contribute to a more inclusive and harmonious society. These reflections not only contribute to the richness of the narrative but also highlight the researcher's subjective engagement with the research process and the impact it has had on the understanding and interpretation of the findings. 47 6.0 Introduction As I embarked on my research journey in the Western Cape, I couldn't help but notice the many manifestations of poetic justice that were evident throughout the region. The legacy of apartheid and the struggle for freedom has left an indelible mark on the people and communities of this region. As I delved deeper into my research, I began to see how the principles of poetic justice were intertwined with the stories and experiences of those I interviewed. From the powerful role of storytelling to the importance of bridging divides and building relationships, my research has illuminated the many ways in which it is being realized in the Western Cape. Somebody’s words resonated with me deeply and added another layer to the context. They spoke of the simple pleasure of being able to sit in a cafe, sipping coffee without fear of being chased away. The joy of living by the sea was also a treasured experience for them, one that they never could have imagined during their upbringing. It was a reminder of how far society has come, but also of the work that still needs to be done to ensure that everyone can enjoy such basic freedoms without fear of discrimination or persecution. 6.0.2 Context-specific Solutions for Peace Context-specific solutions are gaining increasing attention in contemporary peacebuilding discourse due to their relevance in addressing the unique challenges faced by different communities. These initiatives are grounded in local, cultural, historical and political understanding of what constitutes peace. As de Coning points out, in context-specific peacebuilding, the process is geared towards promoting participatory and sustainable peace that is locally and nationally owned and led (2023). The importance of context-specific peacebuilding initiatives is also highlighted by the Social Capital theory and the findings of this research. As

such basic freedoms without fear of discrimination or persecution. 6.0.2 Context-specific Solutions for Peace Context-specific solutions are gaining increasing attention in contemporary peacebuilding discourse due to their relevance in addressing the unique challenges faced by different communities. These initiatives are grounded in local, cultural, historical and political understanding of what constitutes peace. As de Coning points out, in context-specific peacebuilding, the process is geared towards promoting participatory and sustainable peace that is locally and nationally owned and led (2023). The importance of context-specific peacebuilding initiatives is also highlighted by the Social Capital theory and the findings of this research. As discussed earlier, the theory emphasizes the role of relationships and social networks in fostering social cohesion and building positive peace. The research findings further suggest that young people can play a crucial role in this process by creating spaces where constructive dialogue can happen. By engaging in these conversations, young people can bridge gaps and break down barriers between different communities, promoting 48 understanding and trust. This aligns with the principles of context-specific peacebuilding, as it involves locally led efforts to address the unique challenges faced by communities, with an emphasis on participation and ownership. In addition to the role of young people, the findings in this study also underscore the importance of the involvement of other key actors such as teachers, community leaders, religious leaders, and universities. These actors play a critical role in creating safe spaces for constructive dialogue and facilitating the development of strong social capital within their communities. For instance, teachers and university educators can provide opportunities for students to engage in meaningful discussions and critical thinking around issues related to social justice and peacebuilding. Community and religious leaders can foster relationships of trust and mutual understanding between different groups within their communities by encouraging intergroup dialogue and collaboration. Moreover, as de Coning (2023) notes, context-specific peacebuilding approaches place a strong emphasis on local ownership and leadership. This means that community leaders and other actors from the local context are best positioned to understand the unique challenges facing their communities and to develop context-specific solutions that are appropriate and effective. The involvement of these actors can also help to build social capital by fostering relationships of trust and mutual support within and between different groups in the community. With this understanding of the context-specific peacebuilding initiatives and their role in promoting participatory and sustainable peace, the following section analyses the findings of the research through the lens of the social capital theory, highlighting the critical role of social networks, trust, reciprocity, shared norms and values and collective action in fostering social cohesion and positive peace in the Western Cape region. 6.1 The Strength of Ties: Unpacking the Role of Social Networks in Fostering Social Cohesion Despite the progress made in dismantling the apartheid regime in South Africa, its legacy still looms large in the spatial organization of many towns and cities in the Western Cape. In almost every place I visited during this research, a clear divide was evident, with communities separated by the road or other physical barriers. This is a testament to the enduring impact of apartheid spatial 49 planning, which aimed to segregate communities by race and privilege whites over non-whites. The legacy of apartheid can also be seen in the disparities in access to resources and opportunities in different communities. For example, some communities may have limited access to educational institutions, community centres, or other public spaces where social networks can form, and social capital can be generated. These limitations can have a significant impact on the ability of individuals and communities

divide was evident, with communities separated by the road or other physical barriers. This is a testament to the enduring impact of apartheid spatial 49 planning, which aimed to segregate communities by race and privilege whites over non-whites. The legacy of apartheid can also be seen in the disparities in access to resources and opportunities in different communities. For example, some communities may have limited access to educational institutions, community centres, or other public spaces where social networks can form, and social capital can be generated. These limitations can have a significant impact on the ability of individuals and communities to build social capital and access the resources they need to thrive. The findings from my research in the Western Cape indicate that social networks play a significant role in fostering social cohesion and building positive peace. Through interviews with community members, it became evident that strong social ties and relationships between individuals and groups can help mitigate conflict and promote understanding. These social networks can be developed through various avenues such as schools, youth centres, community centres, and religious institutions. Furthermore, the study highlights the importance of the role of young people in the creation of these networks, as well as the importance of supportive and inclusive leadership from community leaders, religious leaders, and educators. Overall, the findings suggest that building strong social networks and promoting positive relationships between individuals and groups can contribute significantly to creating a more peaceful and cohesive society. While social networks and social capital can play a significant role in fostering social cohesion and positive peace, it is important to acknowledge that not everyone has equal access to these resources. For example, individuals who are excluded from certain spaces such as universities and community centres may not have the same opportunities to build strong social networks and relationships. This exclusion from certain spaces is not only evident in physical barriers but can also be seen in social networks, where certain groups of people are excluded and marginalized. As one participant in the research put it, "Poor people feel that they have been pushed aside- we need to be peacebuilders and to bring people in”. This can perpetuate existing inequalities and create barriers for those who are already marginalized. Furthermore, certain groups may not have access to the resources necessary to participate in building social capital, such as technology or transportation. These limitations must be recognized and addressed in any effort to promote social cohesion and positive peace. It is crucial to ensure that everyone has access to opportunities to build relationships and social networks, regardless of their background or circumstances. 50 Throughout my research, several participants have highlighted the issue of exclusion and lack of integration in the Western Cape, which has significant impacts on people's access to social capital. As one participant noted, "There is a need for integration in the community as a means to build peace." Another participant highlighted the reality of the problem, stating, "It happens in other towns in South Africa as well, but the problem with integration in Western Cape is real." Participants emphasized the importance of integration, with one noting that "for people to tolerate one another, they have to integrate." As another participant put it, "You need to know about my poverty, and you need to tell me what it is to be privileged." Integration, they argued, is essential for understanding and building empathy between people. Nussbaum's remarks in the interview resonate with the findings. The findings also support Nussbaum's observation that "At its best, social cohesion happens in small groups, one conversation at a time." This is emphasised by remarks by Kolm that ‘social capital is created one room at a time’ and ‘the small group is the unit of transformation and the container for the experience of belonging’ (2008:190). In addition to the challenges of exclusion and lack of integration, participants in the study also highlighted the limitations posed by capacity building, inadequate funding, and a lack of interaction between schools in accessing social capital. I had heard about this group before and was eager to experience their sense of community first-hand. As I approached, I was greeted with warm smiles and the words "Molo sisi, Unjani?" which means "Hello sister, how are you?" in isiXhosa. It was a small gesture, but it immediately made me feel welcome and reinforced the sense of Ubuntu that I’ve been feeling since coming to South Africa. I could sense that this was a place where people came as they were, without fear of judgement or exclusion. It was clear to me that this group had created a safe space where trust and mutual respect were the foundation of their community. It was a refreshing atmosphere filled with joy and openness, but it was also contradictory to the poem that they started reciting about the mistrust and exclusion the young people of colour still feel today walking the streets of Cape Town. The poem highlighted the daily experiences of people of colour who are forced to prove their existence and constantly face scrutiny and exclusion in society that they cannot completely claim as their own. It was a stark reminder of the work that still needs to be done to create a truly inclusive and trusting society in South Africa. I know who you are; beneath those fine clothes ticks the heart of a thief.” (1971) This analysis part explores the role of trust in building social capital within communities, based on the findings of the research. Trust is a critical component of social capital and is essential for creating a sense of community and fostering relationships based on shared values and norms. As Algan points out, trust contributes to and improves community life and governance (OECD, 2018). Trust can also contribute to the development of networks and social ties, which are essential for accessing resources and opportunities. The research findings suggest that trust is an important factor in the development of social capital, particularly in underprivileged communities where social networks and resources are limited. It will examine the various dimensions of trust that emerged from the research, including trust in institutions, trust in individuals, and the role of cultural values such as Ubuntu in fostering trust. Ultimately, it argues that trust is an essential component of social capital, and building trust can contribute to the development of stronger, more resilient communities. 54 This section specifically focuses on the importance of embracing local reconciliation practices to heal societal wounds and rebuild trust in communities. It explores successful reconciliation practices that have been implemented in various communities, with interviewees sharing their experiences and ideas on how these practices can be scaled up to promote social cohesion on a larger scale. The interviewees emphasized the need for action-oriented young people to lead these efforts and the value of the private sector in promoting entrepreneurship and self-sufficiency among young people. They also highlighted the role of NGOs and community organizations in filling the void created by the government's lack of capacity and political will. However, the interviewees also spoke about the challenges faced in promoting reconciliation and peace in South Africa, including the prevalence of violence in communities and the existence of hatred and misinformation among some people. They stressed the importance of addressing these issues through dialogue and education and the need for more conversational spaces where people can come together to understand one another. Despite these challenges, interviewees expressed a strong desire to help, and the important role that churches can play in promoting peace and reconciliation in their communities. Overall, it underscores the urgent need for promoting peace and reconciliation in South African communities that are still dealing with the aftermath of apartheid. 6.2.1 Social Trust The discussion about the importance of trust has gained attention due to its association with the Social Capital discourse

They stressed the importance of addressing these issues through dialogue and education and the need for more conversational spaces where people can come together to understand one another. Despite these challenges, interviewees expressed a strong desire to help, and the important role that churches can play in promoting peace and reconciliation in their communities. Overall, it underscores the urgent need for promoting peace and reconciliation in South African communities that are still dealing with the aftermath of apartheid. 6.2.1 Social Trust The discussion about the importance of trust has gained attention due to its association with the Social Capital discourse as well as socio-economic development. As highlighted by Cook and Cook, 2011; Zmerli and Newton, 2008, there are two primary dimensions of trust mainly social trust and institutional trust. Social trust also referred to as interpersonal trust or relational social capital has been named an ‘essential component of social capital’ (Putnam et al, 1993:170) pointing to trust between individuals (Kwon, 2019). It is important to acknowledge that the concepts of social trust and institutional trust are complex and interconnected and go beyond simple definitions. It is essential to delve deeper into the multifaceted causes and nuances of trust and distrust in society, including historical legacies, economic disparities, and cultural factors, among others. Only by doing so can we begin to build a more comprehensive understanding of trust and work towards repairing and rebuilding broken trust in our communities. 55 Despite the lack of an agreed definition, the conversation around the lack of interpersonal trust was a recurring theme in the interviews. This can be seen through the conversation and discourse of exclusion, division and a lack of understanding. As one participant put it, "To be able to sit in a warm room with a warm meal and with your family and happiness and togetherness, it is difficult to understand the person living on the street who has nothing." Another participant highlighted the need for greater understanding, stating, "When somebody does break in or there is a petty crime, then we are quick to say, Oh, they're good for nothing, they should be taken away or put in jail, but there needs to be more of an understanding of why this is happening. What is the root cause of the unhappiness that is happening, and what can we do?" It emphasizes the need for building trust in institutions that can facilitate understanding, provide support, and address systemic issues. Without this trust, it can be difficult to address the complex and multifaceted challenges facing communities. 6.2.2 Institutional Trust The lack of trust in institutions and the state's ability to provide for its citizens is a significant source of frustration for many people in South Africa. The pervasive corruption in the country further erodes institutional trust and undermines people's confidence in the state's ability to address their needs. These complex issues require multifaceted solutions that address both the systemic and interpersonal dimensions of trust. Institutional trust has been more broadly defined as ‘trust in the government and political systems’ (Scrivens and Smith, 2013:36). In the Western Cape, NGOs and other institutions such as churches are playing a critical role in bridging divides and fostering a sense of trust that has been broken for so long. As one community member notes, "I think one of the things that is lacking is peace in South Africa in our communities." Unfortunately, this lack of peace often manifests itself in violence, as another community member explains, "We see lots of violence in our communities because people don’t 56 have peace at all." However, there was a strong

in the government and political systems’ (Scrivens and Smith, 2013:36). In the Western Cape, NGOs and other institutions such as churches are playing a critical role in bridging divides and fostering a sense of trust that has been broken for so long. As one community member notes, "I think one of the things that is lacking is peace in South Africa in our communities." Unfortunately, this lack of peace often manifests itself in violence, as another community member explains, "We see lots of violence in our communities because people don’t 56 have peace at all." However, there was a strong agreement among the interviewees about the value of exposing to the ‘other’, as another community member notes, "Having to sit down and having a conversation is always bringing a peaceful solution." Sadly, the sense of hopelessness with the Government was felt in the conversations, as one community member explains, "government failed to provide that conversational space for everybody." NGOs and other institutions can also help to address deep-seated issues like hatred, as one community member notes, "somewhere somehow- there is an element of hatred that still exists in some people in South Africa." By providing accurate information and creating opportunities for dialogue, NGOs and other institutions can help to correct misinformation, as another community member notes, "People have wrong information, but that information will never be corrected until we sit down and have a conversation." It's important to note that these conversations are not always easy as another community member explains, "We always exchange hateful words towards one another." Nevertheless, the need for peace and understanding is so great that everyone must do their part to help, as one community member concludes, "The need is so high out there, but we must all try to help." Throughout the interviews, the significance of NGOs in restoring shattered trust and promoting a sense of unity in the Western Cape was palpable. As one respondent noted, "In my experience, it is only up to NGOs to carry out the work of what the state should be doing." The state has failed to provide the necessary support, and NGOs are often left to fill the gap. These organizations collaborate, respecting the unique cultures and needs of each community. However, NGOs often struggle with funding, and donor fatigue is a real issue in South Africa. Yet, as another respondent pointed out, "Rather put your support into NGOs - that's where you eliminate people asking for money and eliminate people being on the street." Many of the interviewees who work in the NGO sector highlighted the importance of collaboration and working together, with one respondent from an NGO stating that "from the NGO perspective- Ubuntu is big. We all should support each other and collaborate." This was especially evident during the COVID-19 pandemic when many NGOs were struggling with funding, yet "everybody pulled together and helped where they could." The spirit of Ubuntu was also emphasized, with one interviewee stating that "everybody can practise Ubuntu in their own homes," and defining Ubuntu as "kindness and support." This collaborative approach and the promotion of Ubuntu values by NGOs helped to bring communities together and foster a sense of trust and support. 57 Apart from the NGOs, the interviewees kept pointing to the role of religion in South Africa. As one community member notes, "The role of the Church is very important in the community." These can provide spaces for worship, but they can also serve as important community centres where people can come together and address difficult issues. Additionally, the interviewees were highlighting the role of religion

their own homes," and defining Ubuntu as "kindness and support." This collaborative approach and the promotion of Ubuntu values by NGOs helped to bring communities together and foster a sense of trust and support. 57 Apart from the NGOs, the interviewees kept pointing to the role of religion in South Africa. As one community member notes, "The role of the Church is very important in the community." These can provide spaces for worship, but they can also serve as important community centres where people can come together and address difficult issues. Additionally, the interviewees were highlighting the role of religion as an “individual transformation” and “healing process”. This can be echoed by van Wyk’s research and findings highlighting the value of religious rituals by suggesting that, ‘Healing rituals as a force for reorienting their social reality – a material world that is permeated with the legacy of Apartheid – and symbolically transforming it into a life-enhancing reality.’ (2017:3). Religion played a vital role in the struggle against apartheid and has continued to play a crucial role in the process of reconciliation and healing in the post-apartheid era. During apartheid, religion was a key site of resistance, providing sanctuary for activists and offering moral guidance and support to those fighting for freedom and equality. In the aftermath of apartheid, religion has worked to promote healing and reconciliation between different racial and ethnic groups, particularly through the Truth and Reconciliation Commission. Through its teachings, practices, and community-based initiatives, the church has been an important force for positive change in South Africa. While the role of the church as a transformative healing practice and community space emerged as a prominent theme in the research, it is important to note that this study did not delve deeper into the complexities of individual healing transformations. Therefore, further research could explore the nuances of how religion and the church can contribute to personal transformation and healing, and how this intersects with broader societal and structural factors in post-Apartheid South Africa. 58 6.3 Building Peace through Reciprocity and Ubuntu: “I wash your hands- you wash mine”. In my work on peace education and community development in the Western Cape, I have had the privilege of interacting with a diverse range of individuals who are working towards creating more peaceful and just communities. These individuals come from different backgrounds and work in a variety of ways, yet there is a common thread that runs through their work: the concept of Servant Leadership. This idea of leadership, which prioritizes serving others and empowering them to become agents of change, resonates deeply with the principles of reciprocity and Ubuntu. As I have engaged with these individuals, I have been struck by their embodiment of the principles of Ubuntu, which emphasizes the interconnectedness of individuals and the importance of community. Their welcoming and inclusive attitudes have made me feel a sense of belonging, and their actions have demonstrated the power of reciprocity and the importance of working towards the collective good. In this section I will explore the role of reciprocity, shared values and ubuntu in peace education initiatives in the Western Cape, drawing on my experiences and observations of the individuals I have worked with. Through this exploration, I aim to highlight the importance of these principles in creating more peaceful and just communities and to offer insights into how they can be integrated into peace education initiatives. It was evident from the interviewees that Ubuntu is a philosophy that emphasizes togetherness, support, and kindness. As one interviewee put it, "I define Ubuntu as kindness and support." Another interviewee explained

In this section I will explore the role of reciprocity, shared values and ubuntu in peace education initiatives in the Western Cape, drawing on my experiences and observations of the individuals I have worked with. Through this exploration, I aim to highlight the importance of these principles in creating more peaceful and just communities and to offer insights into how they can be integrated into peace education initiatives. It was evident from the interviewees that Ubuntu is a philosophy that emphasizes togetherness, support, and kindness. As one interviewee put it, "I define Ubuntu as kindness and support." Another interviewee explained that "togetherness and support and kindness are my definition of peace but also my definition of ubuntu." Practising Ubuntu means living your life in a way that reflects these values. "It has to be something that involves your whole life, and it has to be what you believe in," said one interviewee. The legacy of Apartheid was a recurring theme among the interviewees, with a strong sense of anger, disappointment and hopelessness. Ubuntu is particularly important in South Africa because it offers an alternative to the divisions and inequalities that characterized the Apartheid era. As one 59 interviewee explained, "Apartheid fostered the idea that people are different" and "was based on the idea that white people are the masters of the society." Ubuntu and peace, on the other hand, are "very much opposed" to this ideology. By promoting togetherness, support, kindness, and interdependence, Ubuntu offers a way to bridge the social and economic gaps that continue to divide South African communities. The interviewees emphasized that Ubuntu must be “practised on an individual level to be effective on a broader scale and that it requires a deliberate process of bridge-building to connect different worlds.” Though practising Ubuntu is not just about opposing apartheid, but also about building bridges between different communities and promoting interdependence. "The whole idea of Ubuntu is to bring people outside of the society inside," explained one interviewee expanding that "We have to have people in the community who are deliberate bridge-builders." These bridge-builders help connect different worlds and promote interdependence. Schools can also play a role in promoting Ubuntu and peace by focusing on peace education. "When learners would learn about peace, they would also learn about Ubuntu," said one interviewee. But promoting Ubuntu and peace is not just the responsibility of schools. "It needs to start with parents," said another interviewee. At its core, Ubuntu is about interdependence and the ethics of care. "If I humiliate you or make you less than who you are, I do it to myself," said one interviewee. "I cannot hurt you without hurting myself." This leads to a "gentler community" that values interconnectedness and interdependence. Another interviewee added that Ubuntu is also characterized by "servant leadership." Ultimately, the interviewees emphasised the day-to-day experiences of Ubuntu such as “Everybody can practice Ubuntu in their own homes," said one interviewee. "If you're not practising Ubuntu as an individual, what is the purpose? You're not going to practice it on any other level in your life." Despite the challenges facing South Africa, many interviewees remained hopeful that Ubuntu can help build a more peaceful and interconnected society. 60 This is emphasised by Murithi’s insights: ‘In order to initiate the social reconstruction of war-affected communities, a key step will be to find a way for members of these communities to “re-inform” or educate themselves in a way that emphasises sharing and equitable resource distribution’ and ‘emphasising the importance of reviving cultural attitudes and values that can promote education in order for peace to flourish’

You're not going to practice it on any other level in your life." Despite the challenges facing South Africa, many interviewees remained hopeful that Ubuntu can help build a more peaceful and interconnected society. 60 This is emphasised by Murithi’s insights: ‘In order to initiate the social reconstruction of war-affected communities, a key step will be to find a way for members of these communities to “re-inform” or educate themselves in a way that emphasises sharing and equitable resource distribution’ and ‘emphasising the importance of reviving cultural attitudes and values that can promote education in order for peace to flourish’ (2009:225). The author points to the findings of this research, especially the revival of shared norms such as Ubuntu in promoting social cohesion and sustainable peace within communities. 6.3.1 Complexities at Hand A common thread that emerged from the interviews was the complexity of the issues at hand. The challenges of promoting peace, building bridges, and practising Ubuntu were not straightforward. This complexity was evident in many ways. For example, one interviewee noted that the legacy of apartheid was still strongly felt, leading to anger and disappointment. Another spoke of the challenges of addressing broken homes and the need to start with parents. Others talked about the difficulties of changing entrenched attitudes and behaviours and the need for sustained effort and commitment. To navigate this complexity, the interviewees emphasized the importance of education and cultural revival. They stressed the need to infuse education with peace and shared values such as ubuntu and support cross-sectorial collaborative processes. In short, the interviewees point out that addressing complex issues requires a multifaceted approach. 6.4 Building Bridges: The Power of Collective Action in Civic Engagement ‘Ours was a generation that had to find its way on a road not well travelled before’ (Mamphele Ramphele, 2012:15) As I sat in a bustling café/bar in Stellenbosch called Casa Carveza, I couldn't help but be inspired by a conversation I’ve had with young South African emerging leaders passionately discussing 61 politics and the importance of civic engagement. Their energy and enthusiasm for making a positive difference in their communities was contagious. I was struck by the fact that this was not an isolated incident, but rather a common thread throughout my time in the Western Cape. Everywhere I went, from community meetings to university seminars, young people were actively engaging with issues of identity, capacity building, transformative education, and peacebuilding efforts. This experience reinforced my belief in the power of collective action and the potential for young people to drive positive change in their communities. As they said, they understood the urgency of being politically engaged in the new Democracy like South Africa. This part is focused on the importance of collective action in civic engagement. As one of the participants in a discussion pointed out, "Change needs to happen when every citizen sees themselves as a role-player to bring about change." In the South African context, peace and harmony are crucial, and bridging gaps between people who disagree is essential. Among the NGOs and grassroots organisations, I have interviewed, there was a strong agreement that a cross- sectorial collaboration between different NGOs, schools and the private sector is necessary to achieve better peace education efforts in the region. As highlighted by Kapucu, ‘by increasing social capital, we as a society should be able to reduce the collective action problem through increased trust, stronger community, bridging bonds, and reciprocal kind works towards each other’ (2011:29). As supported by Kapucu- the research findings have highlighted the importance of building social capital. One of the main themes that emerged

who disagree is essential. Among the NGOs and grassroots organisations, I have interviewed, there was a strong agreement that a cross- sectorial collaboration between different NGOs, schools and the private sector is necessary to achieve better peace education efforts in the region. As highlighted by Kapucu, ‘by increasing social capital, we as a society should be able to reduce the collective action problem through increased trust, stronger community, bridging bonds, and reciprocal kind works towards each other’ (2011:29). As supported by Kapucu- the research findings have highlighted the importance of building social capital. One of the main themes that emerged from these quotes is the idea of building bridges between different groups and sectors to bring about positive change through collective action. This type of bridge-building is a form of social capital known as bridging social capital, which involves creating connections between diverse groups and facilitating collaboration and cooperation. The importance of bridging social capital is highlighted by quotes such as "There is a need to bring people from different worlds into the room" and “Just because you don’t agree with somebody- it doesn’t mean that you need to be in the conflict and there is the gap that you need to bridge”. These quotes emphasize the need for diverse groups to come together and work towards a common goal. 62 Other quotes, such as "You need to build relationships before doing work together" and "Just build understanding," speak to the importance of trust and shared understanding in building bridges between groups. This is an essential component of bridging social capital, as it involves developing relationships and mutual respect across boundaries. Overall, these quotes underscore the importance of bridging social capital in fostering collective action and building stronger, more cohesive communities. The concept of social capital is a key element in bridging different groups and fostering cooperation towards a common goal. 6.4.1 Linking Social Capital Besides focusing on building connections and trust between individuals from different diverse groups, the research findings also indicate that linking social capital can play a crucial role in peace education initiatives. This involves not only fostering connections between different schools, as discussed earlier but also between different sectors. Such initiatives can provide vital support, capacity, and funding when necessary. Linking social capital refers to the connections and bridging bonds between diverse groups, such as different social classes, ethnicities, or organizations. Healy and Cote (2011) refer to it as, relations that exist between groups and individuals coming from different social layers of society which allows them to leverage resources and ideas (in Woolcock, 2001). Cross-sectorial collaboration can be seen as an important aspect that came from the interviews. As suggested by one of the interviewees as "the missing element in social capital is standing together and collaborating." This form of linking social capital is where actors from different sectors, such as the private, public, and non-profit sectors, work together towards a common goal. By collaborating across sectors, organizations and individuals can share resources, expertise, and networks to achieve more effective and sustainable outcomes. Cross-sectorial collaboration can also facilitate the exchange of knowledge and ideas, promote innovation, and create opportunities for collective action. Furthermore, the interviewees highlighted the need for greater cross-sectorial collaboration in the Western Cape in their organisations as well as between the major universities. They suggested that this can potentially help to address complex societal issues that require a multi-faceted approach. 63 This can be echoed by Kapucu suggesting that cross-sectoral collaboration could be linked to people becoming more civically engaged within their communities (2011). For example, in addressing poverty, it may be necessary

to achieve more effective and sustainable outcomes. Cross-sectorial collaboration can also facilitate the exchange of knowledge and ideas, promote innovation, and create opportunities for collective action. Furthermore, the interviewees highlighted the need for greater cross-sectorial collaboration in the Western Cape in their organisations as well as between the major universities. They suggested that this can potentially help to address complex societal issues that require a multi-faceted approach. 63 This can be echoed by Kapucu suggesting that cross-sectoral collaboration could be linked to people becoming more civically engaged within their communities (2011). For example, in addressing poverty, it may be necessary to collaborate across sectors to address issues related to education, health, housing, and employment. This is emphasised by one of the interviewees working in the NGO sector: “Here are NGOs collaborating so why all do the same thing. Collaboration and working together are important”. They also linked the collaboration to the principles of Ubuntu by saying that from their NGO experience, working in the region “Ubuntu is big. We all support each other and collaborate”. The findings further point out that Ubuntu could be operationalised in the context-specific peace education initiatives in South Africa through cross-sectorial collaboration. This is also highlighted by Iline and Philip who point out that, ‘interconnectedness and working together constitute the centre and basis of ubuntu’ (2019:12). This means bringing people from different sectors and backgrounds into the same room, listening to each other's perspectives and experiences, and working towards a shared goal. By embracing the principles of Ubuntu, such as empathy, compassion, and interconnectedness, individuals and organizations can build stronger bonds of trust and understanding, which can ultimately lead to more effective collaboration and positive outcomes for all involved. In this way, Ubuntu can be seen as a powerful tool for promoting cross- sectorial collaboration and fostering collective action towards shared goals. 64 7 Conclusion This thesis set out to investigate the implementation and adaptation of context-specific peace education initiatives in local communities of the Western Cape, with a focus on understanding how these initiatives have been tailored to suit the unique local contexts. Through a series of 13 interviews with key stakeholders in the region, several key themes emerged that shed light on the complex nature of peace education in a diverse and challenging context. Through this study, I have sought to address several important research questions regarding context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape. Firstly, it has explored the key factors that contribute to the success of these initiatives. The findings have revealed the significance of moving beyond the rhetoric of Ubuntu and focusing on practical ways to promote social cohesion and positive peace. It has also emphasized the importance of understanding the local context and engaging in collaborative partnerships with various stakeholders. Additionally, the value of relationships, social networks, and strong social capital has been highlighted as crucial in building positive peace and fostering social cohesion. Secondly, this research has investigated the challenges that need to be addressed to develop more effective and context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape. This research has shed light on the need for creating safe and inclusive spaces for difficult conversations to take place. Specifically, the importance of storytelling, art, and music as means to facilitate dialogue and address local needs. Furthermore, there was a strong emphasis on the role of young people as connectors and the need to enhance their capacity to become active agents of change in promoting civic engagement. Lastly, it has explored how Ubuntu philosophy can inform the development of these initiatives in the Western Cape. The study has demonstrated

effective and context-specific peace education initiatives in the Western Cape. This research has shed light on the need for creating safe and inclusive spaces for difficult conversations to take place. Specifically, the importance of storytelling, art, and music as means to facilitate dialogue and address local needs. Furthermore, there was a strong emphasis on the role of young people as connectors and the need to enhance their capacity to become active agents of change in promoting civic engagement. Lastly, it has explored how Ubuntu philosophy can inform the development of these initiatives in the Western Cape. The study has demonstrated that embracing local reconciliation practices and involving local communities in the development and implementation of peace education programs are essential for their success. It has highlighted the inside-out approach to peacebuilding and the potential benefits of integrating Ubuntu into policy-level interventions. 65 By focusing on adaptive peacebuilding with the inside-out approach and focusing on local ownership, the potential for success is greatly increased. This requires building the capacity of local NGOs and CBOs as well as allocating adequate funding to support their work. Without these resources, it is difficult for peace education initiatives to achieve lasting positive impact. Therefore, policymakers and stakeholders must prioritize funding and support for local organizations working towards peacebuilding and social cohesion in the Western Cape and beyond. There is a need for context-specific approaches that consider the unique social, cultural, and political landscape of the region. This study has also highlighted the importance of considering gender in peace education initiatives, and there is a need for further research in this area. Specifically, future studies should investigate how gender dynamics influence peacebuilding efforts and how gender-sensitive and intersectional approaches can be integrated into peace education programs. Additionally, the study suggests exploring how Ubuntu philosophy can inform policy-level interventions aimed at promoting social cohesion and integration not only in South Africa but also in other contexts with similar challenges. Understanding the role of gender and Ubuntu philosophy can contribute to the development of more effective and context-specific peace education initiatives that promote positive peace and lasting social change. These issues are particularly relevant given the upcoming general election in South Africa, as they speak directly to the need for policy frameworks that promote inclusive and sustainable peace. Future research should aim to explore these issues more deeply and provide policy recommendations for promoting gender equity and social cohesion in South Africa and beyond. This study has attempted to contribute to the field of peace education in the Western Cape and has highlighted several key factors that contribute to its success. A context-specific approach is essential to achieving lasting peace, and collaborative partnerships between a range of stakeholders are critical to its success. This research journey has been personally enriching and transformative. It has deepened my understanding of the complex dynamics surrounding peace education in the Western Cape and the challenges that hinder the realization of lasting social cohesion. Through engaging with 66 stakeholders and delving into the intricacies of context-specific peace education initiatives, I have witnessed the power of collaborative efforts and the potential for positive change. As I reflect on the findings of this study, I am reminded of the immense importance of embracing our shared humanity and interconnectedness, as encapsulated in the concept of Ubuntu. This philosophy resonates deeply with me on a personal level, as it aligns with my belief in the fundamental value of empathy, respect, and inclusivity in fostering harmonious and thriving communities. Furthermore, the recognition of the critical role of young people as connectors and catalysts for change has struck a

intricacies of context-specific peace education initiatives, I have witnessed the power of collaborative efforts and the potential for positive change. As I reflect on the findings of this study, I am reminded of the immense importance of embracing our shared humanity and interconnectedness, as encapsulated in the concept of Ubuntu. This philosophy resonates deeply with me on a personal level, as it aligns with my belief in the fundamental value of empathy, respect, and inclusivity in fostering harmonious and thriving communities. Furthermore, the recognition of the critical role of young people as connectors and catalysts for change has struck a chord within me. As a young person myself, I am inspired by the potential for youth to shape the future and drive meaningful societal transformations. It reinforces my commitment to actively participate in civic engagement and contribute to the advancement of peacebuilding and social cohesion initiatives in my community and beyond. Ultimately, this research has reinforced my conviction that peace education is not merely a theoretical concept but a tangible catalyst for positive change. I hope that the findings and recommendations of this study will inspire further exploration and action, both within the Western Cape and in other regions grappling with similar challenges. Perhaps the notion of social cohesion as a static and elusive concept is a fallacy, but the active pursuit of social justice, equity, and inclusion may pave the way for a more cohesive and connected society. Volume 01 1 INTRODUCTORY NOTE Lettie Tembo Longwe1 The Institute for Peace and Security Studies (IPSS) prioritises a lessons-learned and forward-looking approach to Africa’s peace and security discourse. As you would see in this compilation, some practical points to enhance youth (young men and women from age 15 to 35, according to the African Youth Charter) position and their agency are presented. This compilation is made up of perspectives from experts at the Tana Forum Secretariat; two countries that already have a Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) National Action Plan (NAP) authored by individuals who actively contributed to the process in their respective countries; the African Youth Commission (AYC); and African Union Youth for Peace (AUY4P) Programme Team. Some of the key points of this compilation include: • Implementing the outcome of the 1183rd decision of the AU PSC. • Safe communication spaces for youth. • Prioritising a youth-led approach to the development of National Action Plans, inter- ministerial coordination, resource mobilisation, and youth and institutional capacity building. • Creating resource hubs/centers on YPS for the sustainability of the agenda. • Evidence-based approaches and data availability. • Maximising digital tools and addressing the digital divide. • Ensuring effective planning and adherence to implementation plans. • Alliance building and avoiding undue competition. • The need to invest in creating an enabling environment to harness the African youth advantage. • Valuing inter-generational and multi-sectoral partnerships. • The importance of policy dialogues and its instrumentality for YPS NAPs processes. The Tana Forum Secretariat is confident that this piece will prove resourceful for youths and youth-led civil society organisations in relation to NAPs, African governments, the African Union and regional institutions, and other policy- and decision-making audiences, towards the implementation of the youth, peace and security agenda of the African Union. Notably, this piece is an opportunity for the contributors to tell a story we can learn from for the advancement of Agenda 2063. 1 Interim Director, Africa Peace and Security Programme and Head of Secretariat Tana Forum Secretariat 2 IPSS Occasional Papers WHAT NEXT FOR NATIONAL ACTION PLANS ON YPS IN AFRICA? AU Youth for Peace (Y4P) Africa Program Team Introduction The position paper takes a deep dive into the implementation of the Continental Framework on Youth Peace and Security (CFYPS) and its 10-year implementation plan with a focus on the lessons learned in the development of National Action Plans on YPS by AU Member States. The paper will also peruse through the challenges encountered and provide concise recommendations for effective development and implementation of NAPs. Continental Framework on YPS and NAPs Adopted in 2020 in the 933rd Meeting of the AU Peace and Security Council, the CFYPS is the first continental normative framework that focuses exclusively on Youth, Peace, and Security in Africa. It derives its mandate from the AU Constitutive Act, the AU Peace and Security Council Protocol, Agenda 2063 emphasizing on the Africa We Want, Silencing the Guns in Africa, the African Youth Charter, the African Governance Architecture, as well as the United Nations Security Council Resolution 2250 on Youth Peace and Security. The overall objective of the CFYPS is to: Facilitate the meaningful engagement and participation of African Youth in Peace and Security, Re-enforce the call for action in recognizing that the majority of young Africans are advocates for non-violence, tolerance and peaceful co-existence, Facilitate the recognition and appreciation of efforts of young Africans in Peace and Security, Enhance partnerships and collaboration among different stakeholders on YPS, Facilitate the development of youth-led and youth-centered strategic plans, initiatives and programs by the AUC, RECs/RMs and Member States. The objectives of the continental framework are in line with the five priority areas on youth peace and security namely, Participation, Protection, Prevention, Partnership and Coordination, Disengagement and Reintegration. In order to ensure effective participation of youth in the peace and security affairs in the continent, the AU, Members States and RECs/RMs agreed to develop National Action Plans as guiding document to implement the continental framework on youth peace and security. The 933rd Meeting of the PSC also emphasize the need by member states to domesticate and implement the continental framework as well as develop NAPs in line with national policies and priorities. It’s worth noting that, some members states have taken significant stride to develop and adopt the National Action Plan, including the DRC and Nigeria, which already have NAPs, as well as Cameroon, the Gambia and Zimbabwe who are expected to launch their NAPs in 2024. Additionally, other member states have shown interest through political will to commence the development of NAPs, including, Burundi, Ethiopia, Kenya, Tunisia, Liberia to mention but a few, reflecting the increased importance placed by Member States in implementing YPS agenda on the continent.
