Volume 01 3 Challenges and Opportunities In recent years, the world has witnessed an increase in violent conflict and instability, fueled by competing geopolitical interests, the impact of covid-19, poor governance including mismanagement of diversity and growth of corruption, the impact of climate change, a lack of opportunity for the youth. This trend is prevalent globally, which including in Africa. Specifically, the Sahel belt has been affected by armed conflicts, resurgence of unconstitutional governance and the proliferation of non-state armed groups. The rise of the new conflicts, particularly in Sudan, have had a devastating impact on civilian populations. Youth are often at the risks of recruitment into terrorists’ groups, armed groups, and forced to migrate under enormous circumstances exacerbated by the effects of climate change. Youth involvement in armed violence and forced movement can be attributed to high levels of poverty and unemployment, limited access to education, marginalization and phycological pressure, ideological manipulation and sometimes coercion. It is worth noting that the impact of climate change as a contributor to peace and security challenges in the continent especially on the young population cannot be over overemphasized enough. However, this young population presents an opportunity for meaning contribution of youth to stability and development of the continent when continuously tapped effectively through intentional empowerment. Although much progress has been made so far by some member states towards the development of NAPs, the goals of the 10-year implementation plan of the CFYPS which targets 25% of AU Member States to have adopted their NAPs by 2024, is still lagging due to significant factors and reasons as seen below: i. Lack of political will: The development of NAP is a responsibility of member states through the allocation of sufficient resources both human resource and financial, the lack of political will not only derail the process but deny youth the opportunity to be equal contributors to the peace and stability at the national level. ii. Minimal coordination among stakeholders and partners: Visible lack of effective coordination especially between the implementation of UNSC 2250 NAPs and CFYPS NAPs pose a great challenge to the implementation of NAPS at the continental level. There is need for a seamless coordination and incorporation of both agendas while supporting member states in developing NAPs. iii. Weak organizational and technical capacities of youth groups: There is weak and overlapping competition among youth led initiatives at the national levels that require clear coordination to steer the agenda in the right direction. iv. Limited awareness on YPS: The YPS agenda is not fully grasp and owned by youth across different divides of the society. For effective ownership and contribution of youth in the society, there is need to continuously raise awareness on the agenda. v. Limited financial and technical resources on youth programming and youth-led initiatives derail the development and implementation of the policy document. 4 IPSS Occasional Papers Recommendations i. Clear coordination among stakeholders; there is need for a clear coordination among stakeholders at the national, regional, and continental level towards the development and implementation of NAPs. ii. The allocation of sufficient resources by member states and partners; Development of NAPs and its implementation is a resource intensive exercise hence the need for sufficient allocation by member states and partners. iii. Devise innovative approaches to increase visibility of the YPS agenda; this should be led by youth organizations and initiatives. iv. Resource hubs/centers on YPS; to ensure the sustainability of the agenda there is need for resource hubs to document the progress made towards NAPs to

need for a clear coordination among stakeholders at the national, regional, and continental level towards the development and implementation of NAPs. ii. The allocation of sufficient resources by member states and partners; Development of NAPs and its implementation is a resource intensive exercise hence the need for sufficient allocation by member states and partners. iii. Devise innovative approaches to increase visibility of the YPS agenda; this should be led by youth organizations and initiatives. iv. Resource hubs/centers on YPS; to ensure the sustainability of the agenda there is need for resource hubs to document the progress made towards NAPs to continuously inform the effective implementation of the NAPs. v. Implement the outcome of 1183rd decision of the AU PSC; Specifically noting the need to invest in the youth in terms of research, capacity building, mentorship, and development initiatives that empower young people to actively carry out their roles in advancing peace, security, and stability at local, national, regional, and continental levels. Volume 01 5 YPS NAPs DEVELOPMENT THROUGH DRC’s LENS Joseph Akilimali2 and Jacqueline Kitoga3 Introduction The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) has made significant strides in promoting UN Security Council Resolution (UNSC) 2250 and other related resolutions on youth, peace, and security. This progress is evident in the increasing involvement of associations, structures, and youth movements that have started working on the Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) Agenda in the country. DRC is the second (of two African Union member states) that now have a YPS National Action Plans (NAP). Procedure Followed for the Development of the NAP From 2015 to 2020, some Congolese youth participated in international and regional summits and conferences organized by the United Nations (UN) and the African Union (AU). These summits were instrumental in informing young people about their role as agents of change in peacebuilding. Campaigns to popularize UNSCR 2250 were conducted in various provinces of the DRC, as many young Congolese activists were working on this resolution and using it as a tool to reclaim their place in decision-making processes through various initiatives. This eventually led the Congolese government to institutionalize it in December 2020 by establishing the National Technical Secretariat for the Implementation of Resolution 2250 in the DRC (STN-2250/RDC) within the Ministry of Youth, New Citizenship, and National Cohesion.4 Organizations, structures, movements, and youth associations from civil society working on peace, security, and development came together to launch the Congolese Youth, Peace, and Security Coalition (CJPS) in December 2021. The CJPS was utilized by the National Technical Secretariat 2250 to lead the campaign to popularize Resolution 2250 and collect data for the development of the NAP. This campaign covered all 26 provinces of the country and the capital, Kinshasa. More than 50,000 young people were reached by the campaign, and nearly 5,000 responded to the survey questionnaire made available to gather their aspirations and proposed activities for the five pillars of the Resolution. Four methods were used for data collection: the survey questionnaire administered to respondents; data collection via SMS through U-Report; a chatbot (WhatsApp); and online via a KoboCollect link.5 It should be noted that the target of this campaign was young people aged 15 to 35,6 including students, young people with disabilities, young people with albinism, young people from indigenous communities, young people living in IDP camps and military camps, young people from religious denominations, young people from political parties, as well as those grouped in various associations, structures, and movements across the country. 2 Joseph Akilimali, Executive Secretary of the Youth for Peace DRC network, Co-Chair of the National Youth, Peace, and Security Coalition of the DRC.

Report on the R 2250 outreach campaign and data collection for the National Action Plan -PAN 2250 6 According to the African Union Youth Charter, the youth age is 15 to 35 years. 6 IPSS Occasional Papers Challenges and Opportunities Challenges Some of the challenges encountered include: • Difficulty in reaching remote areas for data collection for the NAP, • Low mobilization of financial resources before and even during the NAP development process, • Insufficient funding for the implementation of the NAP, and • Inefficient coordination of support activities by partners. Opportunities Despite the challenges, these were the opportunities we leveraged: • Existence and demonstration of the political will to ensure that youth participate meaningfully in DRC’s affairs, • The existence of the NAPs, and • Commitment of youth organizations from civil society and youth-serving organizations to support the implementation of the YPS agenda at the national level. Lessons Learned • Before launching the NAP development process, ensure the availability of technical and financial resources in advance, and • It is essential to consider all segments of the youth population and leave no one behind. Recommendations For Regional Economic Communities (RECs): • Institutionalize a quota for youth participation and representation in governance, peace, and security processes, and • Encourage member states to sign, ratify, and implement youth policies and to regularly report on progress in integrating youth participation in peace and security.7 For Governments: • Create and facilitate safe communication spaces for youth, and between youth and decision-makers, to promote intergenerational learning and mentoring, and to ensure that youth can express and engage with their concerns and grievances, and 7 African Union. (2020). A study on the role and contributions of youth to peace and security in Africa: An independent expert report commissioned by the Peace and Security Council of the African Union. Volume 01 7 • Establish and institutionalize a quota for leadership positions for youth to facilitate representation at the decision-making level, • Promote partnerships with youth and special programs related to youth. For Youth and Youth-Led CSOs: • Mobilize peers for the NAP process through awareness-raising and sharing experiences and best practices, • Conduct strategic advocacy actions to engage stakeholders in the process, and • Establish strategic partnerships, build alliances, and avoid undue competition. For Development Partners: • Support initiatives to strengthen the capacity of youth networks and organizations in peacebuilding, • Support projects aimed at implementing the youth, peace, and security agenda, and • Coordinate support for youth initiatives, particularly those focused on peace research and consolidation. 8 IPSS Occasional Papers LOCALISING YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY IN AFRICA: NIGERIA’S

For Development Partners: • Support initiatives to strengthen the capacity of youth networks and organizations in peacebuilding, • Support projects aimed at implementing the youth, peace, and security agenda, and • Coordinate support for youth initiatives, particularly those focused on peace research and consolidation. 8 IPSS Occasional Papers LOCALISING YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY IN AFRICA: NIGERIA’S EXPERIENCE Rafiu Adeniran Lawal8 and Queen Ameh9 Introduction Undoubtedly, the development of a National Action Plan (NAP) is one of the best strategies for the advancement of the Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) agenda. If rightly supported, it has the potential to enhance the Africa’s push for Agenda 2063, on paper and in action. The Youth, Peace and Security agenda has continued to gain traction across all levels with several African countries at various levels of development of NAP on YPS. YPS NAP captures the commitment of peace and security actors and strategies for engaging young people. Nigeria’s: State of Affairs and Experience In 2021, Nigeria became the first country in Africa to adopt a National Action Plan on YPS as mandated by the United Nations Security Council Resolution (UNSCR) 2250 (2015) and the African Union Continental Framework on Youth, Peace and Security (2020). Since then, there have been several efforts aimed at strengthening the agenda and localising the national framework both locally and nationally. At the moment two State Governments (Nasarawa and Kogi) already developed State Action Plans on Youth, Peace and Security and more states are at an advanced stage in the development of their SAP. There are also several ongoing awareness-raising and capacity-strengthening initiatives targeted at youth and youth actors as part of the implementation strategies. Nigeria’s YPS NAP was achieved through advocacy, lobbying, hosting of dialogues and consultations among youth groups and youth stakeholders, hosting of Youth4Peace conferences and summits; organising in-person consultations in the form of zonal workshops, online consultations and meetings; and national validation meetings across Nigeria that lasted for more than three years. Some of the lessons from the entire process that ensured the achievement of this feat were the establishment of a national planning platform, alliance building and intergenerational and multisectoral partnerships. All these were important in driving the conversation, tracking progress and galvanizing resources and all little efforts that are being made by youth stakeholders across countries towards the vision of pushing and achieving a NAP. Evidence based research, campaigns and awareness both online and offline played a major important role in keeping the hope alive and sustaining the momentum while the development process lasted. Social media hashtags like #NigeriaYouth4Peace #ActOn2250, #Yes4YPS, #Youth4Peace, are currently being used by youths to call for the operationalization of YPS agenda in the country. 8 Rafiu Lawal has more than eight years of experience in youth, peace and security. He is the Executive Director of the Building Blocks for Peace Foundation- a youth-led network working on conflict prevention and peacebuilding in Nigeria. He co-edited the book ‘Connecting and Amplifying Voices of Youth Building Peace in Nigeria (2020)’. 9 Queen Ameh is the Executive Director of Advocate for Women and Girls Initiative, where she passionately advocates for the rights and well- being of youth, women, and children. She holds a Bachelor's degree in English and Literary Studies and a Masters in Gender Studies. Volume 01 9 Challenges and Opportunities • Short life cycle for NAP implementation:

Volume 01 9 Challenges and Opportunities • Short life cycle for NAP implementation: Both Nigeria and DRC existing NAPs are less than 5 years. For these two NAPs, implementation started fully one or two years after it had been developed. It is crucial to have elongated cycles for emerging NAPs to allow sufficient time for concrete implementation and monitoring. We can emulate from the Philippines NAP on YPS which has an implementation plan of 10 years. • Constitutionalising YPS: The future of YPS is in the constitutionalising of the agenda’. This is important so that the YPS agenda is not subject to the frequent changes of government and the leanings of political leaders, and that the agenda enjoy the full backing of the law. Recommendations • Coordination and Monitoring: The biggest problem for the Youth, Peace and Security agenda is not the implementation of the national action plans. If we want the NAP to be implemented, it must not only be realistic but must be monitored especially by a broader group of stakeholders other than those who developed and drafted it. • Multi-layer Partnerships are crucial: For the Youth, Peace and Security to advance, there is a need to mobilise and redirect all funding instruments towards the implementation of the agenda. The private sector, foreign missions/embassies, and development partners must invest concretely in supporting youth-led initiatives. • Intergenerational Collaboration is key: Government coordination of the implementation of the NAP centrally is good, however, the implementation must be decentralized and open to all. We must avoid the temptation to monopolize the development and implementation process and unhealthy competition. Competition will do more harm to the Youth, Peace and Security agenda. 10 IPSS Occasional Papers ADVANCING YOUTH, PEACE, AND SECURITY IN EAST AFRICA David Abuga Momanyi10 and Dennis Sankale Keis11 Introduction The development of National Action Plans (NAPs) is crucial to advance the Youth Peace and Security (YPS) agenda. The African Union (AU) Peace and Security Council (PSC) protocol at its 807th meeting in November 2018 convened the first open session on YPS, wherein it called on member states to develop NAPs and remove all structural barriers to youth participation in peace and security. Building on this momentum, several countries and regional organizations began developing National and Regional Action Plans on Youth, Peace and Security. While the urgency to address the unique needs and contributions of young people in conflict and post-conflict settings is evident, the progress in adopting comprehensive NAP on YPS in the East Africa remains uneven. Some YPS NAPs Efforts in East Africa In East Africa, a variety of initiatives are shaping the discourse and action on YPS. In Burundi, President Evariste Ndayishimiye championing the YPS agenda signals high-level commitment. On June 1st, 2023, Burundi made the official launch of national consultations for the elaboration of the national strategy on YPS (2023 -2027) and its NAP in Gitega in a move towards the implementation of the YPS agenda. Similarly, Kenya's Ministry of Youth affairs has spearheaded consultations and formed technical committees to advance the NAP framework. The government of Kenya through the

East Africa remains uneven. Some YPS NAPs Efforts in East Africa In East Africa, a variety of initiatives are shaping the discourse and action on YPS. In Burundi, President Evariste Ndayishimiye championing the YPS agenda signals high-level commitment. On June 1st, 2023, Burundi made the official launch of national consultations for the elaboration of the national strategy on YPS (2023 -2027) and its NAP in Gitega in a move towards the implementation of the YPS agenda. Similarly, Kenya's Ministry of Youth affairs has spearheaded consultations and formed technical committees to advance the NAP framework. The government of Kenya through the Ministry of Youth Affairs Creative Arts and Sports has since held a series of consultations involving Youth from marginalized communities, Rural Youth, youth from learning institutions, civil society, and experts on YPS among others. Furthermore, the Ministry established a technical committee on the YPS agenda to deliberate on strategies to better support, promote and invest in young peacebuilders in the country and build a stronger & inclusive National Action Plan on YPS agenda. In addition, the African Centre for the Constructive Resolution of Disputes (ACCORD) partnered with the Multifunctional Youth Forum to host a High-Level Peer Sharing Dialogue on YPS. In attendance were members of the DRC YPS National Action Plan drafting team who shared their experiences with the Kenyan team. With that came a series of consultations and forums meant to train stakeholders, actors, and decision makers including Youth on the process of developing a National Action Plan on YPS. Uganda's grassroots efforts, exemplified by initiatives in Isingiro district, underscore the importance of community engagement. In Somalia, the Forum on Youth, Peace, and Security facilitated meaningful dialogue and advocacy. With the assistance from UNDP Somalia, a Forum on Youth, Peace, and Security was organized to create a peaceful society and effectively connect the voices of young people. The forum was vital in enabling young people to advocate for peace in Somalia amidst the ongoing crisis. Also, various organisations in South Sudan, including the National Ministry of Peace Building, the National Ministry of Youth 10 David Abuga Momanyi is the Executive Director at the Horn of Africa Youth Network. 11 Dennis Sankale Keis is currently the Acting Executive Secretary of the African Youth Commission. Volume 01 11 and Sports, civil society, young parliamentarians, and youth groups, signed a commitment to localize UNSCR 2250 with an aim of developing a National action plan on youth peace and security. Challenges and Opportunities Despite progress, challenges persist. Limited resources, institutional capacity gaps, and political instability pose significant hurdles. However, these challenges also present opportunities for innovation and collaboration. Engaging diverse stakeholders, leveraging technology, and mobilizing international support are key strategies to overcome obstacles. • Resource limitations pose a significant challenge to the development of NAPs on YPS in the region. For instance, in Burundi, limited financial resources hinder the government's ability to conduct comprehensive consultations and provide adequate support for youth-led peacebuilding initiatives. Similarly, in Kenya, budgetary constraints may restrict the Ministry of Youth's capacity to implement youth-focused programs effectively. • Political instability and conflicts in countries like South Sudan and Somalia present formidable challenges to the development and implementation of NAPs on YPS. In South Sudan, ongoing conflict and power struggles among political factions divert attention and resources away from peacebuilding efforts, making it difficult to prioritize youth participation in peace processes. Similarly, in Somalia, the lack of a stable government and the presence of armed groups create an uncertain environment for youth engagement in peacebuilding activities. • Some countries in the region struggle with a lack of institutional capacity to coordinate

Youth's capacity to implement youth-focused programs effectively. • Political instability and conflicts in countries like South Sudan and Somalia present formidable challenges to the development and implementation of NAPs on YPS. In South Sudan, ongoing conflict and power struggles among political factions divert attention and resources away from peacebuilding efforts, making it difficult to prioritize youth participation in peace processes. Similarly, in Somalia, the lack of a stable government and the presence of armed groups create an uncertain environment for youth engagement in peacebuilding activities. • Some countries in the region struggle with a lack of institutional capacity to coordinate and implement NAPs on YPS. For example, Uganda may face challenges in data collection and analysis due to limited technical expertise within government agencies responsible for youth affairs. Institutional weaknesses in Burundi hinders the effective monitoring and evaluation of youth peacebuilding programs, leading to gaps in accountability and performance assessment. • Despite efforts to promote youth participation, ensuring inclusivity and representation in the development of NAPs on YPS remains a challenge. Marginalized groups such as young women and rural youth may face barriers to participation in consultations due to factors like gender discrimination and limited access to information and resources. Similarly ethnic divisions and political tensions may impede the meaningful engagement of youth from minority communities in peacebuilding processes. • Sustaining momentum and commitment to NAPs on YPS beyond the initial stages of development is a significant challenge. For instance, the turnover of government officials and changes in political leadership often disrupts continuity in youth peacebuilding initiatives, leading to inconsistency in policy implementation and resource allocation. Similarly, in Somalia, the lack of long-term funding mechanisms for youth-focused programs hinders the sustainability of peacebuilding efforts, making it difficult to achieve lasting impact in conflict-affected communities. • The digital divide poses a challenge, with disparities in access to information and communication technologies. Limited connectivity and digital literacy among youth in certain areas impede their ability to leverage technological advancements for education, employment, and civic engagement. Disparities in digital access are evident 12 IPSS Occasional Papers across the region. Countries like Kenya and Uganda have relatively higher internet penetration, while others, like South Sudan, face challenges in digital connectivity. Recommendations • Inclusivity: Platforms for dialogue, including virtual consultations, community meetings, and youth forums, to gather input from a wide range of voices should be made accessible. • Financing YPS NAPs: Governments, in partnership with development partners, should explore bilateral and multilateral grants, private sector partnerships, and innovative financing mechanisms tailored to youth-led peacebuilding initiatives. Development partners may also work with national governments and civil society and provide technical assistance in grant writing, proposal development, and budget planning. • Monitoring and Evaluation: Robust monitoring and evaluation mechanisms should be established to track progress, measure impact, and ensure accountability in the implementation of NAPs on YPS. This involves developing clear indicators, benchmarks, and reporting frameworks to assess the effectiveness of youth peacebuilding programs and initiatives. • Inter-ministerial coordination: As one of the lessons learned from Nigeria and DRC, an Inter-ministerial Committee or Task Force with an inclusive constitution and clear mandate is needed. This also concerns inter-agency collaboration to ensure that the NAPs addressed various aspects of youth empowerment, peacebuilding, and security. • Integration of NAPs in existing national frameworks: Like Nigeria and DRC, countries currently developing their YPS NAPs should explore means through which the NAPs process is integrated in existing national frameworks. This will aid coherence with existing policies and strategies, avoiding duplication of efforts and maximizing resources. Volume 01 13 THE ESSENCE OF POLICY DIALOGUES: LESSONS FOR YPS NAPs DEVELOPMENT AND IMPLEMENTATION Jesutimilehin O. Akamo12 and Rubiyat Mohammed Seid13 Introduction Intelligible interaction between and amongst stakeholders of Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) National Action Plans (NAPS) is vital to ensure that the agency of African youths stays efficient. One of the forms of such intelligible interaction is policy dialogues. This paper highlights what it is not, what it is, and the value it brings to the table for YPS NAPs. It also presents recommendations on how to improve the quality and impact of policy dialogues. The content of this piece is based on lessons from a series of careful observations of African governments, institutions and organisations, think tanks, and civil society within the last two decades. Policy Dialogues: What it is (not) The primary aim of a policy dialogue is policy progress, and the objective is to arrive at a set of workable policy recommendations to ensure that progress. Thus, policy dialogues are not meant to be a gathering of only like-minded stakeholders reinforcing their position, and in that vein developing a set of recommendations or programme of action. Diversity is key. Policy dialogues are also not a debate of solely conflicting stakeholders where the conversation is dominated by criticism and pulling down of existing policies, actions of authorities, and/or relevant individuals associated with the subject matter. Rather, policy dialogues are a tool to promote informed policy making. It is a platform to share perspectives, find common ground, and reach a consensus on policy solutions. Policy dialogues are a process of reflective and constructive interactions between and amongst two or more stakeholders to understand the past, examine what exist and understand the present, and explore opportunities to progress. It may also be understood as a convening of stakeholders and experts on an identified subject matter by the convener to advocate for progress vis-à-vis the subject matter. While policy dialogues may involve one or more events where stakeholders meet, physically or virtually, the process of a policy dialogue does not start and end with an event. The methodology and progress should be trackable. Amongst other things, this can be done by accounting for the planning strategies, identifying knowledge and awareness level on the subject matter, increased interest or curiosity around the topic, policy or programmatic action, and outlets for more action (research or more dialogues and outreaches). It should also be noted that policy dialogues do not guarantee policy impact, although it is a key element in achieving policy impact. Policy dialogues may be formal, semi-formal or informal. Diversity and inclusion is key to a strong policy dialogue process. Notably, conflicting and converging positions, as well as what they mean, should be accounted for. 12 Jesutimilehin O. Akamo is the Coordinator of the Research and Policy Analysis Unit at the Africa Peace and Security Programme (APSP) of the Institute for Peace and Security Studies (IPSS) and Tana Forum Secretariat, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. 13 Rubiyat Mohammed Seid is the Policy Dialogues Lead at the Africa Peace and Security Programme (APSP) of the Institute for

a key element in achieving policy impact. Policy dialogues may be formal, semi-formal or informal. Diversity and inclusion is key to a strong policy dialogue process. Notably, conflicting and converging positions, as well as what they mean, should be accounted for. 12 Jesutimilehin O. Akamo is the Coordinator of the Research and Policy Analysis Unit at the Africa Peace and Security Programme (APSP) of the Institute for Peace and Security Studies (IPSS) and Tana Forum Secretariat, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. 13 Rubiyat Mohammed Seid is the Policy Dialogues Lead at the Africa Peace and Security Programme (APSP) of the Institute for Peace and Security Studies (IPSS) and Tana Forum Secretariat, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. 14 IPSS Occasional Papers Overall, policy dialogues are a platform for African leaders, stakeholders, and thought leaders to collaboratively engage in exploring and exchanging ideas on African-led solutions to security challenges. Moreso in the African context, it provides strategic support to the “intellectual challenge” to find African-led solutions to peace and security hurdles.14 The Value of Policy Dialogues to YPS NAPS One of the major dearth impeding policy success is the gap between theory and practice. The enormous and vast knowledge produced on youth often times struggle to make its way into policy debate to influence action. Sometimes, they do not. Policy dialogues provide an entry point to bridge this gap. It allows youth experts (academic, programme or practitioner) to present their findings to policymakers. In turn, policymakers are able to express their position, gaps and challenges, as well as existing opportunities where these experts and other stakeholders can intervene to ensure policy progress. Within this context, the peer-to-peer learning, solution-oriented, lessons-learned, and forward-looking approach finds expression. In this vein, Policy dialogues are critical to institutional, expert, policymakers, civil society, development partners, and other stakeholders’ learning of youth issues, as well as policy and programmatic options. Politically, policy dialogues offer a platform of mutual understanding and respect for trust building with respect to policy implementation. It also offers a platform to enhance the responsiveness of policies and helps prevent the breakdown of cooperation and partnerships. Similarly, and perhaps more importantly, it is an entry point to building constituencies and a network of stakeholders to follow up on the movements between policy and practice, and everything in-between. In this regard, it creates an opportunity that serves to influence policy and promote healthier communication and information exchange between and among the different stakeholders in the YPS NAPS of a country. Challenges and Opportunities: Lessons for YPS NAPS Stakeholders Having hosted and participated in about a hundred policy dialogues since its existence, one of the major issues observed by IPSS is defining the scope and agenda. It can neither be too broad nor narrow. Also, identifying the suitable speakers and moderators to ensure that the briefing notes are adhered to could also sometimes be a challenge. The political sensitivities (and to whom) must also not be left out. Furthermore, there is the problem of (un)necessary politicking, and this is linked to the last point on political sensitivities. Sometimes, institutions and governments participate in a policy dialogue with a fixed mind on what they want to hear or achieve, or misperceptions and misconceptions within the context of youth discourse. This often constitutes a challenge for the moderator and the convener to ensure that their participation does not undermine the aims and objective of the event. For the sake of quality of a policy dialogue, however, the convener should, to a reasonable degree, be aware and current with youth debates, related political sensitivities, challenges faced by policymakers and other stakeholders, and available expertise.

the last point on political sensitivities. Sometimes, institutions and governments participate in a policy dialogue with a fixed mind on what they want to hear or achieve, or misperceptions and misconceptions within the context of youth discourse. This often constitutes a challenge for the moderator and the convener to ensure that their participation does not undermine the aims and objective of the event. For the sake of quality of a policy dialogue, however, the convener should, to a reasonable degree, be aware and current with youth debates, related political sensitivities, challenges faced by policymakers and other stakeholders, and available expertise. Time and resources are also a key issue. First, Africa’s politics, peace and security landscape is constantly evolving. New issues emerge, as well as new dimensions to existing ones. Thus, being able to (1) cover all key issues within the limited time frame of relevance or urgency and (2) Being able to cover key points within a policy dialogue session; might all together 14 See IPSS Policy Dialogues Concept Note Volume 01 15 be a herculean task. For (1), prioritisation and foresight is of uttermost important; and for (2), having an efficient set up of speakers and moderators is key. In terms of resources, one must consider the financial and non-financial cost and efficiency of a virtual and physical meeting. It should be noted that some conversations are best held in-person. Furthermore, one of the most effective ways to address technical and financial insufficiencies is strong partnerships and collaborations. Openness to partnerships and collaborations and a partner- friendly modus operandi is vital. Sometimes there are also challenges in terms of the reception of some stakeholders to the idea of having a dialogue on an issue that concerns them or has been deliberately left out of the scope of their work. The response of a convener to this is often political, to get their buy- ins and the political will to participate. Such challenge is most common with governments and multilateral institutions. Lastly, it is not always easy to identify what conversations changed what (policy process, implementation or monitoring and evaluation). Thus, the convener should develop a tracking method (even if imperfect) before the policy dialogue. This should be done in partnership with relevant stakeholders. Conclusion Policy dialogues are one of the most important tools in the YPS NAPs development. Effective policy dialogues can ease political buy-ins and encourage the political will of national governments and supporting partners to ensure the successful development, adoption, and implementation of YPS NAPs. Finally, policy dialogue conveners should remain aware of context-specificity challenges and opportunities. Recommendations • Alignment: Ensure that partners have a meeting point on the meaning and purpose of a policy dialogue and what it is meant to achieve. • Inclusivity: Develop a standard approach for ensuring diversity, representation, and meaningful participation. • Pool of Expertise: Build and maintain a network of subject-matter experts to assist where and when necessary, in policy dialogues processes. • Impact Tracking: Develop a strong methodology for stakeholder mapping and impact tracking. We recommend a participatory approach. • Partnerships and coordination: This should not be solely about funding. There are technical, political and logistical gaps that various partners can fill. Furthermore, policy dialogue conveners should seek means through which political entry points and cordiality with governments and institutions are efficient for policy impact, without compromising the integrity of the process and its stakeholders. 16 IPSS Occasional Papers CONCLUSION Jesutimilehin O. Editor-in-Chief: Dr Jeremy A Rinker Publisher: Abhijit Mazumder ADVISORY BOARD Dr Amii Omara-Otunnu l Dr Ashwani Kumar l Dr Bandana Purkayastha Dr Jacint Jordana l Dr John Doyle l Dr Lenin Raghuvanshi l Ms Michelle Ndiaye Dr Rahul Mukherji l Dr Shankari Sundararaman l Dr Vandana Shiva JTPP (https://jtpp.uk), bringing together peace practitioners and academics, fills an important transdisciplinary gap that exists in current academic literature. JTPP endeavours to support scholarship that highlights m the human potential for transformation, m a holistic and collaborative approach to complex systems, m the transdisciplinary nature of solutions to wicked social problems, and m a shared sense of purpose in human transnational activism for positive change. Editor-in-Chief’s Welcome and Introduction In the last issue of this journal, I wrote about the visceral feel of wasted potential, remembering the loss of a 15-year-old Palestinian cousin of my former student. I was, and am, appalled by how the world just moves on past such injustice. My own recent family losses reminded me of the collective pain and anguish of feeling like a story is closed to me now, and nobody seems to take notice. While I know that transdisciplinary scholarship cannot heal the grief of death and loss, I do believe that it can help build the knowledge necessary for life and peace. In grief there is hope, one cannot be without the other. This issue of the Journal of Transdisciplinary Peace Praxis ( JTPP ) aims at this hope in realising that transdisciplinary peace is always a collective and collaborative endeavour. Our aspirations are, by the nature of reality, always already collective. We humans are all interdependent beings, we rely on each other even to remember and memorialise, just as we rely on each other to make peace. To do effective peace praxis requires an understanding of our dependence on others is required. The start of 2025 has been a difficult one for me personally. I lost a sibling, and a favourite uncle just weeks apart as the New Year began. They cannot be replaced, but they will be remembered. As I wrote out my ideas on what to say as a eulogy to my only biological brother, I kept thinking this is not about me, it is about the amazing big brother that I lost. But, as I reflected further, I came to realise that it is about me; a piece of me has passed from this earth with his passing. A piece of my story is now only in the past tense; a remembrance, the present and future parts of my story with my brother have been abruptly closed off. But just because my story . . . the collective story . . . becomes closed off by death, that does not mean stories and storytelling have also ended. Stories are what make us human. Stories of, and relationships with, those passed, can,

lost. But, as I reflected further, I came to realise that it is about me; a piece of me has passed from this earth with his passing. A piece of my story is now only in the past tense; a remembrance, the present and future parts of my story with my brother have been abruptly closed off. But just because my story . . . the collective story . . . becomes closed off by death, that does not mean stories and storytelling have also ended. Stories are what make us human. Stories of, and relationships with, those passed, can, and do, continue to grow and breathe in our hearts and minds. This gives me hope that life has some enduring meaning. Despite the grief of loss, the hope of fond memories leads us on towards peace. It is with such a heavy heart that I welcome you to our thirteenth (XIII; Vol. 7, No. 1) issue of the Journal of Transdisciplinary Peace Praxis ( JTPP ). I continue to believe that the theory and practice of transdisciplinary peace is, not only, desperately needed but also a means of honouring the knowledge of those long passed from this earth. The first article in this thirteenth issue tells the story of one American University’s approach to renaming university property after a past namesake’s beliefs have come to light. In ‘Renaming with Purpose: One University’s Approach to Reconciling with Its History’ authors Ruchti, Thomas, Donkor, Evans, Mercy & Isaac work to articulate a critically reasoned renaming process after a former professor is found to be an avowed eugenicist. The collaborative interdisciplinary assessment of the university’s path forward is a perfect fit for the JTPP as it aims at policy and praxis-based social change. Following this initial praxis-based committee report-cum-paper, the second piece in the thirteenth issue takes on a more macro view of the world. David Hornung writes in ‘Weaponised Automation: Social Services in a Militarised World’ about how neoliberalism and Artificial Intelligence (AI) are revolutionising the social work profession. Hornung aims to ‘challenge the notion that the greatest concern autonomous weapons systems present to the world are the presence of trained human beings overseeing the deployment of these weapons systems’ (p.39). Spelling out how ‘social workers in places like Allegheny County have first-hand experience’ of ‘automated systems with human-in-the-loop capabilities’ (p.49) having detrimental effect on clients and society. This second piece brings diffuse disciplines together to raise important questions about the future of automation and human interactions. The third paper takes us to the African continent. Kebede Bekere’s ‘Religion- Induced Cultural Violence: The Case of Cultural Violence Committed by the Ethiopian Orthodox Church Against the Oromo People (1878-1974)’ takes on an historical case study of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church (EOC) to enlighten us about cultural violence within religious institutions. Exploring the drivers of cultural violence and mapping them onto the EOC’s role in nation building in Ethiopia, the piece provides an insightful reading of the complexity of conflict in this war-torn northeast corner of Africa. The next two pieces in this issue delve into cultural issues on the Indian subcontinent. Nambiar, Raghnathan, Sood & Wei’s ‘Approaching Peace Education through History Textbooks in India’ provides an important critique of national education policy through analysis of textbooks. The piece ‘traces the pathways that Peace Education has undergone in the Indian Peninsula’ (p.74) and deepens our understanding of the diverse universe of peace education curriculums around the world. Nambiar et al provide a clarion call for further research on peace education and the need for people to be educated about peace and not just

northeast corner of Africa. The next two pieces in this issue delve into cultural issues on the Indian subcontinent. Nambiar, Raghnathan, Sood & Wei’s ‘Approaching Peace Education through History Textbooks in India’ provides an important critique of national education policy through analysis of textbooks. The piece ‘traces the pathways that Peace Education has undergone in the Indian Peninsula’ (p.74) and deepens our understanding of the diverse universe of peace education curriculums around the world. Nambiar et al provide a clarion call for further research on peace education and the need for people to be educated about peace and not just war. Staying in India, Reverend Paul Pudussery and Rema Devi A tell a story, in the fifth article, of families with expatriate husbands in the Persian Gulf, as many do in South India. ‘Stress Among Wives in a High-Stress Society: A Comparative Study’ provides a detailed study of the stressful realities in transnational families. All the articles in this issue take an in-depth look into a unique corner of our human experiences, flip it around and inspect it from many angles. Editor-in-Chief’s Welcome and Introduction 11 We round out the thirteenth issue with an extended book review by Jane Duran. In reviewing ‘Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West’ written by late Benazir Bhutto, Duran’s analysis is a meditation on the political philosophy of human rights and peace in society. Given the tragic history of the Bhutto’s in Pakistan, Duran brings Benazir’s sharp voice for women’s rights and modernist nationalism into focus. Although unorthodox, Duran’s review unearths important questions about what it means to reconcile and build solidarity across difference. Last, but not least, I urge readers to explore the Kaleidoscope section of this issue to engage with current topics in peace journalism. While academic language as a medium often falls short of creative expression in conveying the lived emotional realities of peace, I hope this issue gives you some new ways of thinking about things anew. Research will always be inadequate to express the pain and suffering that loss and grief met against us all, still transdisciplinary scholarship is one artistic opportunity for creative healing. I believe what we need most now is healing from the stress and grief of our human footprint on this earth. I hope readers will enjoy the pieces in this lucky thirteenth issue. / jr@jtpp.uk February 2025 Renaming with Purpose: One University’s Approach to Reconciling with Its History Simon E Ruchti, David Thomas, Martha Donkor, Carolyn D Evans, Karen Mercy & Demetrius Isaac ABSTRACT In the winter of 2023, the president of our university asked us to convene a committee to review the namesake of our university’s science centre. We were tasked with conducting a thorough investigation and making a recommendation regarding the removal of the building’s namesake in light of recently discovered information linking him to the eugenics movement. Because of the tension on our campus around the science centre name and the backlash in the United States against de-naming or renaming monuments, we determined it was necessary to have an unassailably thorough and transparent process. It was critical that we produce a model document clearly articulating and defending our decision, making clear the process by which we made it. To this end, we consider the purpose of monuments as historical markers and statements of values. Integral to our analysis is the exploration of the history of eugenics and its impact on our decision. We believe the document we produced can serve as a template for others to use in their own investigations of namesakes. What follows is our final report, recommending that the name of our science centre be changed, and we offer it as a template for others investigating the names and legacies memorialised on dedicated spaces. KEYWORDS Memorials, Renaming, Eugenics, Campus History, Higher Education PREFACE Being committed to creating safe and empowering spaces for all, we must begin to examine how these spaces are experienced by those who encounter them. Thus, we must investigate not only the spaces but also the names that grace them. As we do so, we will likely find some of our namesakes do not engender the values we espouse. Once these discoveries are made, it is crucial we act to remove those names. To this end, we want to share our own experience with a namesake change that was well-received and accepted by our campus community despite anticipated resistance. In the Fall of 2021, a student researcher, Aaron Stoyack, at West Chester University of PA (WCU), discovered that our campus science centre was named after a eugenicist, Samuel Christian Schmucker (SCS). After further research, he published this information in the student paper, The Quad (Stoyack). In the fall of 2022, students organised to have the name removed from the building; however, they met resistance, and the debate spilt onto social media. There, those against keeping the name accused SCS of being a eugenicist and the university of giving tacit support to eugenics by keeping the name. While a supporter of keeping the SCS name made accusations of censorship and erasing history. They argued that SCS wasn’t really a eugenicist and, even if he was, eugenics in-and- of-itself isn’t all that bad. namesake when one learns about the complexity of what has been memorialised (John, 2019; Marcus & tells the rest of the Woodward, 2020). In the sense that people rarely world: This is the seem prompted by the symbol to learn more about best of us. This is the memorialised (thus why many in our campus who we aim to be. community were so shocked and offended to learn about SCS), it seems more accurate to suggest that memorialisation serves more to symbolise our collective values than to act as a testament to accurate historical representation (John, 2019). We memorialise to assert what we think is important, publicly and without shame. We commemorate a person through a statue or a building name because that person has performed deeds that society thinks are good. That person, and by extension their symbol of memorialisation, in turn, stands as a symbol of our values to others. This namesake tells the rest of the world: This is the best of us. This is who we aim to be. Of course, this leads us to an important question: who constitutes the ‘we’, the ‘collective’, whose values are being memorialised? In the case of the Samuel Christian Schmucker Science Center, we are referring, in one sense, to the students who petitioned for him to be the namesake. He was a beloved professor and remained so to his former students, it seems, long after his death. He informed and represented the values they associate with the school. So, like, all forms of monuments, the namesake of our science centre represents the values of SCS and the students he taught. They had been, from all accounts, profoundly influenced by his teaching. He had, as we will explain later, seen his teaching as his religious and moral calling. He worked to impart his values to his students. In the 1960s, when West Chester University memorialised him by putting his name on a science complex, the institution embraced SCS’s values and, as memorialisation is intended to do, they proclaimed his values as theirs, publicly and without shame. By maintaining SCS as the namesake of our science centre, the university signals, loudly and without shame, that its values align with SCS, that he is the best of us and who we aim to be. Some might argue that keeping the name does not say who we are, but who we were, and to change the name of the building is to erase history. We counter that the opposite is true. By changing the name, we are looking more carefully at history. Thanks to the rigorous work done by alumnus Aaron Stoyack and his professor, Brent Ruswick (History), we have come to know more about SCS and the history of WCU and 19th century American politics and science that we would have known otherwise. As Ruswick noted in his statement to the committee, Far from canceling history, our students are recovering and creating history, and doing so in a manner that epitomizes our university’s highest values. This ought to be a moment of pride and celebration for our students and the humanities education offered by our History Department (Ruswick, 2023: 6). We wish to note, as well, that other history scholars, including those of the American History Association, have made similar points in response to other memorialisation controversies (AHA Statement, 2017; Stroud & Henson, 2019; Marcus & Woodward, 2020). Our knowledge of history grows constantly, social values evolve, and our understanding of—and relationship to—our memorials must as well. This is what it means to do history. We wish to note, as well, that other history scholars, including those of the American History Association, have made similar points in response to other memorialisation controversies (AHA Statement, 2017; Stroud & Henson, 2019; Marcus & Woodward, 2020). Our knowledge of history grows constantly, social values evolve, and our understanding of—and relationship to—our memorials must as well. This is what it means to do history. Removing a statue or other form of memorialisation does run the risk of inhibiting critical conversations about our histories and their often-hidden impacts on our lives today. As Alan Marcus and Walter Woodward (2020) write: Expired monuments are a lesson: They teach that people can be tragically wrong about something even when that belief once had widespread Weaponised Automation Social Services in a Militarised World David Hornung ABSTRACT The increasing use, development, and investment by governments around the world in developing battlefield ready weapons that rely completely on automated technology has been the concern of peace activists and scholars. However, it is the often-accepted semi-autonomous weapons that are already in use that pose the greatest risk to individual safety and human rights. These technologies should be the focus of the United States peace movements advocacy as we enter a time of increasing automation in all sectors of society and the economy. Examples from the field of social work regarding how semi-autonomous systems, even with well-established human oversight, can go wrong and violate the rights of innocent civilians is explored. Policy recommendations to help focus the United States peace movements efforts in this are also offered. KEYWORDS Militarisation, Welfare, Automation, Artificial Intelligence, Drone Warfare, Social Services, Welfare State INTRODUCTION On 21 October 2022 a group composed of 70 states made an official statement to the United Nations General Assembly regarding the use of Automated Weapon Systems (AWS). The statement, delivered by a representative of the 70 concerned countries, addressed the need for international cooperation regarding the regulation and use of autonomous weapon systems that are either partially controlled by a human being (Semi-autonomous) and those weapon systems that require no human oversight to surveil, engage, and kill enemies on the battlefield (Fully Autonomous). The statement recognised the ‘urgent need’ for the international community to adopt rules and measures on autonomous weapons and emphasised ‘the necessity for human beings to exert appropriate control, judgement and involvement in relation to the use of weapons systems.’ (ADR, 2024) The role of the ‘human’ in the use of these weapons has been the concern of many international advocacy groups and human rights activists. Human Rights Watch (2023) in a study found: Australia, China, India, Iran, Israel, South Korea, Russia, Turkey, United Kingdom, and the United States are heavily investing in the military applications of artificial intelligence and related technologies to develop air, land, and sea-based automated weapons. This ‘arms race’ will make understanding the uses of automated weapons systems and the potential dangers they pose to the human rights of civilian populations very important. There are no internationally agreed upon definitions as to what constitutes a semi-autonomous weapon system from a fully autonomous weapon system. The United States Government refers to all weapons systems that use elements of Weaponised Automation: Social Services in a Militarised World 41 automation as Lethal Autonomous Weapon Systems (LAWS). LAWS are defined by the United States Government as ‘a special class of weapon systems that use sensor suites and computer algorithms to independently identify and target and employ an on board weapon system to engage and destroy the target without manual human control of the system’ (Congressional Research Service, 2024). These ‘fully autonomous’ weapons require no human control to deploy weapons, surveil enemy targets, and engage those targets. In popular culture these are what Human Rights Watch refers to as ‘killer robots’ (2024). Thankfully, the United States Government has confirmed that as of 2019 no ‘fully autonomous Weapon Systems’ have been successfully tested or used on the battlefield (Congressional Research Service, 2023). What has been developed and used successfully on the battlefield are ‘Semi-Autonomous Weapon Systems’. Department of Defense distinguishes these weapon systems from fully autonomous systems by their design ‘which does not automatically select and engage individual targets or specific target groups that have not been previously selected by an authorized human’ (DOD DIRECTIVE, 2023). Human oversight, and the ability to override and control what an autonomous weapon system does when used in battel are what differentiate semi-autonomous weapon systems from fully autonomous systems. Historically, the United States has employed weapons with some form of automation since World War II (Work, 2021). The United States used the Mark 24 Mine, referred to as ‘fido’ in the pacific. This early technology used sound to detect enemy submarines in the pacific. If a vessel big enough and capable to reach the depths of the ocean, these mines would explode. These autonomous weapons would fire on enemy combatant indiscriminately; however, they were placed strategically by humans. Drones are an example of semi-autonomous weapons that are currently used on battlefields throughout the globe. Military Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV’s) also referred to popularly as ‘drones’ have become the premier tool used to pursue, surveil, and now kill enemy combatants in the United States war on terror (Lubin, 2021). These weapons are preferred by military leaders for their ability to ‘destroy specific targets, spread terror, and weaken the resolve of soldiers and civilians. Due to their success as precision weapons, drones are potent, difficult to stop, and cost-effective’. In an economic environment where austerity defines budgetary formation, and state governments continue to look for technology to replace traditionally staffed sectors of the bureaucracy, drones are an enticing weapon to use in modern warfare. It becomes an ugly manifestation of evil when it is used as an instrument to suppress, exploit, and degrade certain groups of people. This article argues that the Ethiopian Orthodox Church (EOC) is a showcase of how the State used religion as a weapon to commit cultural violence against a group of people in Ethiopia. EOC supported the legitimacy of the Amhara political, economic, and cultural supremacy for more than a century. The Church tried to justify the inequality, injustice, and suppression caused by the Amhara empire by preaching that God ordained them to rule and the people to submit. The Church existed to serve the interests of the Amhara authorities and religious elites. The Church was intentionally blinded to see the sufferings of the people due to the direct and structural acts of violence of the Amhara imperial regime. The paper explained that the denial of the Church of committing cultural violence sustained the legacy of historical harm. Healing the past wrongs done by the Church would help it to remain relevant to all regardless of their ethnic backgrounds. However, the Ethiopian Orthodox Church has a dark side that was not explored and discussed in academia in detail. This paper focuses on one of the atrocities committed by the EOC, the cultural violence the Church perpetrated on the Oromo in Ethiopia in the late 19th and 20th centuries. The aim is to investigate the cultural violence committed and invite the Church to address the past wrongdoings to remain relevant to all people regardless of their ethnic backgrounds, be a model of repentance by making a public apology, and amend essential policies and structures enhance the efforts to resolve the ethnic violence between Amhara and Oromo in some parts of the country. Religion-Induced Cultural Violence 57 Cultural Violence Committed by EOC The Ethiopian Orthodox Church was not a missionary church. The Church’s growth has happened primarily on natural growth. The children born from Orthodox followers are baptised to be Christians. Another strategy the Church used to expand was the coercive conversion of people to Orthodox Christianity. EOC was introduced to Oromia, the land of the Oromo people in the south of Abyssinia, following the territorial expansion of the Abyssinian kings. Orthodox Christianity was the religion of the Tigrean and Amhara. The Amhara and the Tigrean did not take any initiative to preach to other people and nations in the neighbouring areas. At the turn of the 20th century, Menelik, the King of Shewa, declared war on the other kings of Abyssinia and defeated them. Then, he wanted more territories in the south of Abyssinia. Menelik II conducted a major expansion into the hearts of Oromia at the end of the 20th century. In some parts of Oromia, the military forces of the emperor faced fierce resistance. However, he crushed the resistance using modern firearms he purchased from European powers (Darkwah, 1975). There were hundreds of thousands of Oromo people died fighting against the invaders/expansionists. The defeated people suffered humiliation and were subjected to serving the emperor’s soldiers and officials. The mutilation of the right arms of men and the breasts of women in Arsi by the soldiers of Menelik was to humiliate the survivors and send a warning message to others who thought of resisting the invading

end of the 20th century. In some parts of Oromia, the military forces of the emperor faced fierce resistance. However, he crushed the resistance using modern firearms he purchased from European powers (Darkwah, 1975). There were hundreds of thousands of Oromo people died fighting against the invaders/expansionists. The defeated people suffered humiliation and were subjected to serving the emperor’s soldiers and officials. The mutilation of the right arms of men and the breasts of women in Arsi by the soldiers of Menelik was to humiliate the survivors and send a warning message to others who thought of resisting the invading army (Hassen, 2002). In some parts of Oromia such as Wollega and Jimma, Menelik II won over the local rulers through diplomatic means. Abba Jifar of Jimma negotiated with Menelik and prevented the direct attack on his people. He agreed to pay tribute and assist Menelik in occupying other places south of Jimma. In return, Menelik gave him local autonomy (Gemeda, 2002). Wollega also followed a similar pattern (Jegnie, 2024). The governors agreed to accept the emperor’s terms mainly embracing the culture of Amhara and being loyal to the emperor. Following the victories, the Amhara from the north came and settled in the lands previously owned by the Oromo (Marcus, 1975). The difference between those defeated by force and those who peacefully surrendered was suffering from physical violence by the former. Both groups went through a painful Amharaisation process. To subdue the Oromo to the Amhara political system, the EOC tried to manipulate the Oromo to obey the Amhara political elites without questioning Approaching Peace Education through History Textbooks in India Diya Nambiar, Aaryan Raghnathan, Vivaan Sood & Allen Wei ABSTRACT This paper discusses the foundational and overlapping concepts, issues, and debates that form the basis of conceptualising peace education. Further, it maps the historical trajectory of peace education, specifically in the Indian context and globally. Beginning from religious and moral education aiming to promote peace to more organised and direct approaches led by different United Nations (UN) bodies have been traced. The paper then focuses on reviewing different policy documents in education released in India over time as a case in point. The paper traces the pathways that peace education has undergone in the Indian Peninsula, the journey from moral education to education for peace and towards peace education. Further, in light of National Education Policy 2020 (NEP, 2020), the paper analyses four history textbooks for major boards taught in Indian schools and maps them with the goals and aims of teaching social science and history as mentioned in the policy. A comparative analysis of these textbooks on aspects such as the promotion of peaceful values and behaviours, understanding and respecting diversity, human rights and social justice, global citizenship, interconnectedness, the role of dialogue and socio-emotional skills have also been presented. Finally, the paper identifies and suggests plausible future directions, such as curriculum revision, a more strengthened teacher education, and others, that peace education advocates, keeping in mind the latest policies vis-à-vis the needs of contemporary times, both nationally and globally. KEYWORDS Peace, Education for Peace, Culture of Peace, History Textbooks, Textbook Analysis BACKGROUND The conceptualisation of the idea gained impetus for the lead author, Ms Diya Nambiar, during one of her history classes, in which the discussions focused on the wars and armed conflicts in current global politics. This initiated a discussion Approaching Peace Education through History Textbooks in India 75 on genocides and armed conflicts. The discussion moved towards the preparedness and sensitivity events such as genocides and mass killings in the name of amassing power and political supremacy and how we can nudge nations to resolve conflicts without violence. The most obvious and plausible answer seemed to be peace education and peace initiatives. However, it was interesting to note that during the discussion of possible strategies to resolve conflicts, it was

in which the discussions focused on the wars and armed conflicts in current global politics. This initiated a discussion Approaching Peace Education through History Textbooks in India 75 on genocides and armed conflicts. The discussion moved towards the preparedness and sensitivity events such as genocides and mass killings in the name of amassing power and political supremacy and how we can nudge nations to resolve conflicts without violence. The most obvious and plausible answer seemed to be peace education and peace initiatives. However, it was interesting to note that during the discussion of possible strategies to resolve conflicts, it was revealed that each student had a different narrative of the same event that was chosen to argue a point. It was safe to deduce that each student/participant had been taught a different narrative of the same event, even though the goals were more or less the same. While all aimed at promoting peace, some focused on inclusion, others on conflict resolution, and some others on sensitivity, citizenship, and nationalism. All values were aimed at upholding peace education. However, peace and peace education was rarely talked about directly which seemed a bit peculiar given the relevance of peace education observable in contemporary times. This variation of narratives inspired the lead author to organise a symposium titled ‘Peace Education: Concepts, Debates and Future Directions’, which aimed at understanding peace, peace education, and various concepts that revolve around and hold major implications for the discourse of peace and peace education. Soon, she put out a call for the same within her student network with the following themes: w Understanding Peace Education, w Objectives of Peace Education, w History of Peace Education, w Approaches and Strategies to Peace Education, w Peace Education in Classrooms and the Role of Textbooks in Peace Education. A range of students showed interest and sent in their entries from across the globe. Inspired by her passions, Ms Nambiar shortlisted four entries after working out the timeline, mediating time zones and finalising a schedule that worked best for participants from different countries. The goal was to develop a better understanding of peace education and gauge its current standing in schools and school education. The symposium also had space for a brief meet and greet session for networking at the end. During the meet and greet, participants from different countries shared classroom experiences related to peace education, genocides, and armed conflicts. They realised that their narratives and experiences were highly varied. They wondered if the reason was their national contexts, pedagogy, or something else. The discussions revealed that some textbooks referred to were common in more than one country. This sparked the idea of developing a paper containing textual analysis of history textbooks of different curriculum boards in a country and mapping their scope with the goals and objectives of peace education mandated globally and nationally. It was also exciting to explore whether the global and national mandates for peace education through various policies aligned with each other or not. Further, the nature and intensity of this alignment merits inquiry. This paper presents a consolidated report of this exploratory symposium initiated and conducted independently by the authors. THE HISTORY OF PEACE EDUCATION From indigenous tribes to modern societies, there have always been traditions and procedures to settle and resolve disputes. During the indigenous ages, these methods and procedures were transmitted from generation to generation in the name of traditions (Gregor, 1996). Anthropological inquiries have claimed to observe at least 47 societies in the world that are comparatively peaceful (Bonta, 1993). However, these attempts at maintaining peace in society only qualify for informal peace education. There is no said pedagogy, no explicated goals other than to keep people and groups safe and happy, and no written maintenance of records around the same. Peace education took a more formal form by maintaining and transmitting ideas

and procedures to settle and resolve disputes. During the indigenous ages, these methods and procedures were transmitted from generation to generation in the name of traditions (Gregor, 1996). Anthropological inquiries have claimed to observe at least 47 societies in the world that are comparatively peaceful (Bonta, 1993). However, these attempts at maintaining peace in society only qualify for informal peace education. There is no said pedagogy, no explicated goals other than to keep people and groups safe and happy, and no written maintenance of records around the same. Peace education took a more formal form by maintaining and transmitting ideas in a written format through religions. The preaching of the lord ‘Buddha, Baha’u’llah, Christ, Muhammad, Moses, and Lao-Tzu’ was provided to spread peace, and sometimes with specific discussions about maintaining and practising peace (Gur-Ze’ev, 2010). However, with time, the nature of conflicts became complex, and so did the resolution methods. Stress Among Wives in a High-Stress Society A Comparative Study Rev Paul Pudussery & Rema Devi A ABSTRACT Marriage is an enduring relationship between an adult male and an adult female based on religious-socio-legal sanction which is considered as social duty towards the family and the society. Stress is a stimulus or external force acting on an organism and the response may change physiological and psychological functions. The purpose of this study is to explore the impact of prolonged absence of husbands and the stress experienced by their wives i.e., wives of expatriate husbands and to compare with the stress experienced by the wives of returnees and wives who are staying with their husbands. In this descriptive research, survey method and random sampling are used. Personal Data Sheet and Stress Rating Scale with three sub-scales are used for the data collection. Quantitative analysis of the collected data is done by statistically to find out the stress levels among wives according to their marital living status. Thereafter compared the mean scores of stress of wives with different marital living status based on different socio-demographic variables selected. The mean scores of all wives with different marital living status possess comparatively same stress. There is no significant difference in the mean scores of stress of wives with different marital living status based on all socio-demographic variables considered. There exists no significant difference in components of stress such as physical stress, psychological stress, and behavioural stress of wives with their marital living status. It has deeply and visibly affected every aspect of life including economic, social, political, cultural, and even religious aspects of the Malayalee in Kerala (Zacharia et al, 2003: 2). The available statistics shows that one out of every eight married women has her husband working abroad. In this circumstance, marriage may be reduced to two-and-a-half months of joy in every two years for the wives and the children hardly get to know their fathers during this short period. A prolonged separation or absence in the early period of marriage seriously curtails the marital life of many young couples. Despite the financial advantages for the left behind families, absence of the father for the children and husband for the wives lead to major psychological problems. Sujatha Ganguly (2006: 1-13) stresses that the household structures matter in the lives of the left-behind women due to migration. The lives of left-behind women differ widely depending on the type of the household structure they live in—she adds. Women in the non-nuclear households do not have much decision-making power than those who are in nuclear household, who take their own decision or take it collectively with their husbands. According to a study on Kerala migration Zacharia, Mathew and Rajan (2001), these left-behind women are hardly equipped to cope up with the situation of separation. With the passing of years, the impact of separation seems to have become worse. In addition to the increased household chores and child care responsibilities, the wives of expatriate husbands are subjected to prolonged hard work, loneliness, and frequent economic crisis. Male migration not only brings positive effects on the left-behind families but also the negative impacts especially on their wives. It is observed that patriarchal control over household resources either by husbands and father-in-law would depend upon women’s living arrangement in the left-behind communities. Munira Beebi (2012) in her comparative study conducted in patrilocal systems and matrilocal systems in Muslim community in Kannur and Kozhikode regions of Malabar of Kerala, where exists two residential patterns highlight the impact of Gulf Migration on left-behind women. Male migration forces the women to take a lot of added responsibilities and challenges which leads to mental stress and emotional difficulties. This study observed that women in matrilocal families enjoys more freedom and flexibility than women in patrilocal families and migration improved the personality and self-confidence in the respondents. It is also found that the women in nuclear family enjoy more privilege than women in the joint family. A multi-dimensional interview schedule which contains 141 questions under nine headings along with an opinionnaire i.e., a structured questionnaire is used to gather the data. Several studies showed that left-behind women suffer from a series of physical, psychological, behavioural, and social problems

responsibilities and challenges which leads to mental stress and emotional difficulties. This study observed that women in matrilocal families enjoys more freedom and flexibility than women in patrilocal families and migration improved the personality and self-confidence in the respondents. It is also found that the women in nuclear family enjoy more privilege than women in the joint family. A multi-dimensional interview schedule which contains 141 questions under nine headings along with an opinionnaire i.e., a structured questionnaire is used to gather the data. Several studies showed that left-behind women suffer from a series of physical, psychological, behavioural, and social problems induced by separation. Research findings have also shown that women in the migrant households are facing various tensions, pressures, conflicts, and anxieties. There are several cases of accusations of infidelity and extra marital relationships also. Misunderstandings on the part of husbands abroad is yet another pain for these wives. The burden and burn out of these women will be doubled when they begin to work (employed) or start a career of their own. The dual role as working women and a caretaker at home, that too without even a moral support, makes these women depressed and more helpless. Irudaya Rajan and Balasubrahmanyam Pattath (2021), in Observance of International Migrants Day, 18 December, state that Return Emigration is an optional yet natural consequence of emigration, especially when the prospectus of the destination country is limited. While labour policies for international migrants in their host countries are well documented—and show the raw deal for many of them, especially low-skilled workers—the repatriation exercises carried out during COVID-19 by origin countries revealed more opportunities for improvement. The contagious nature of the crisis necessitated quick policy responses like border closures and Book Review Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West By BENAZIR BHUTTO pp.352, HarperCollins, 2008, PB $18.00 Reviewed by JANE DURAN ABSTRACT Benazir Bhutto’s Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West (2008) is examined for its relevance to political philosophy taken as a whole, and especially the more recent endeavours that focus on peace and exercising rights within societies. Bhutto tries to make the case that Islam and contemporary democracy are not incompatible with each other, despite what Huntington and some others have seemed to imply. Shedding light on differing types of Islam, and addressing the standards for anything that might be deemed to be a democracy, Bhutto provides several examples of the intersection of democracy and Islam. In addition to her political analysis, Bhutto does not shy away from analysing material taken from current Muslim societies, and showing how a number of forces are at work. She is also adept at characterising what the West perceives as salient differences among various forms of Islam, and illustrating that the alleged or purported differences may not be as important as some have believed—Bhutto names five types of Islam, and acquaints the reader with the fact that some exist only on the Saudi Arabian Peninsula. It is concluded that Bhutto gives us a nascent peace-oriented political view, and that her work is worth reading because of the insights she provides on Islamic cultures. The work of Huntington (1993), Rawls (1972) and others is cited.
