KEYWORDS Politics, Islam, Women’s Rights, Fundamentalism INTRODUCTION Benazir Bhutto, former Prime Minister of Pakistan, is the author of an intriguing Book Review 117 work that sheds new light on contemporary issues of statecraft and policy.1 Her work Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West is noteworthy for a number of political theses, but perhaps makes its strongest statement in her overview of Islam and its relationship with the West. Bhutto makes key distinctions, and sets out important notions. The work is well-placed to assist us in our current endeavours to relieve tensions between the West and Muslim societies. Much of the commentary on the place of religion, religious artefacts and other manifestations of belief systems in Western cultures has to do with the presence of Muslims in a society, and Bhutto is not only sympathetic and understanding of Eurocentric views with regard to Islam in given societies, but she is well-versed in a number of theoretical matters. Questions surrounding the notion of the state, the roles of women in a state and how those roles might be constrained by religion, and so forth, are issues that Bhutto is prepared to discuss. Our notions of a post-enlightenment civil and peaceful society often seem to take precedence over other sorts of views, but not everyone agrees that this should be the case. The question then arises of whether it is possible to obtain a state that has a more focused view with regard to religion—and if that is the case, how that can be accomplished. Bhutto examines a number of stances that are both associated with Islam itself by scholars in the field, and that—for better or for worse—are linked to what is taken to be Islamic thought by Westerners and others who may, on the whole, not have the requisite familiarity. She displays a wide range of scholarship, and is more than cognisant of most of the relevant political theory. As she says, we must learn more. I Writing with an overview that was to be cut tragically short, Bhutto begins one of the early chapters of the work as follows: It is the tradition of Islam that has allowed me to battle for political and human rights, and this same tradition strengthens me today. Islam denounces inequality as the greatest form of injustice.2 Early portions of the book are devoted to a setting out of interpretations of Islam that are not only consistent with the Qu’ran, but that do damage to the pronouncements of some of the contemporary radical clerics who seem to feel that a ‘jihad’ is a war against unbelievers. What separates Bhutto’s commentary from that of many is the precision of her argument, and her extensive use of quotations from various suras within the Qu’ran. She is also extremely able at making that controversial line of argument that has been repeated again and again, but that has sometimes been misunderstood—the line of analysis that indicates that much of what takes place in contemporary Muslim societies has to do with local custom, and not actual Islamic views. As a former leader of state, Bhutto understands that there is a feeling in many nations that Islam is somehow incompatible with modernity, or with the notion of a pacific contemporary life. If those values may be thought of (at least in the European and English-speaking countries) as somehow vaguely Rawlsian, Bhutto is enough of a political thinker and philosophically-oriented intellectual to know that a strong

and again, but that has sometimes been misunderstood—the line of analysis that indicates that much of what takes place in contemporary Muslim societies has to do with local custom, and not actual Islamic views. As a former leader of state, Bhutto understands that there is a feeling in many nations that Islam is somehow incompatible with modernity, or with the notion of a pacific contemporary life. If those values may be thought of (at least in the European and English-speaking countries) as somehow vaguely Rawlsian, Bhutto is enough of a political thinker and philosophically-oriented intellectual to know that a strong argument needs to be made by Muslims that an Islamic nation can indeed participate in an egalitarian polity.3 She is quick to point out that there is nothing inherently anti-knowledge or, indeed, anti-modernity in Islam; again, traditions have arisen in a number of states that have more to do with local custom. Bhutto’s political thought is, in a sense, mainstream Western in that she is for democracy and the various aspects of it that those in the so-called ‘developed’ world have come to expect.4 But one aspect of her commitment that might be thought to be somewhat unusual is that it is remarkably barebones in its nature—that is, Bhutto does not appear to be concerned about the complexities of democratic procedure, or, indeed, any of the redistributive foci of the various European cultures which, recently, have come under attack. These more philosophically- sophisticated questions are, in general, elided by Bhutto, and for good reason. The core conundrum with which she is presented, as she says, has to do with whether democracy and modernity are incompatible with Islam. Thus the point of what she does is to try to make the argument that they are not, and the construction of that argument is her biggest challenge. Bhutto proceeds both by showing how democracy plays out in a number of Muslim-majority countries, and also by showing how it has fared in her native land of Pakistan. But along with her general commentaries on the importance of democracy, and of the merging of Islamic cultural values and democratic procedures, she is also concerned to provide views taken directly from contemporary Islamic scholars that seem to leave the door open for at least some modernising. Crucial to the strength of a democracy, as she repeatedly says and emphasises in the text, is respect for knowledge and knowledge-acquisition. Towards the end of her work she notes: Muslim scholars and leaders have bemoaned the community’s loss of power—political, intellectual, scientific and economic—since the colonial era . . . [Some] have proposed strategies for reconciling the Islamic world Book Review 119 with modern scientific ideas and with the modern economic, political, and social environment.5 These strategies are important to Bhutto because she believes that a more forward- looking polity is not really possible without greater education and advancement in other areas. As is the case with many commentators on matters in the developing world, part of Bhutto’s thesis revolves around her assertion that colonialism had a great deal to do with fostering a lack of growth in Muslim nations—she sees this factor as crucial. When we think of the long history of commentary on the burgeoning democratic practices in European societies, we can begin to see that at least some of Bhutto’s motivation is historical. In other words, as someone educated in the relevant tradition, Benazir Bhutto is more than familiar with, for example, Mill’s (1988) critique of the treatment of women, or the notions of distribution and equality that underlie much of Rawls’s (1972) work. The

thesis revolves around her assertion that colonialism had a great deal to do with fostering a lack of growth in Muslim nations—she sees this factor as crucial. When we think of the long history of commentary on the burgeoning democratic practices in European societies, we can begin to see that at least some of Bhutto’s motivation is historical. In other words, as someone educated in the relevant tradition, Benazir Bhutto is more than familiar with, for example, Mill’s (1988) critique of the treatment of women, or the notions of distribution and equality that underlie much of Rawls’s (1972) work. The question then becomes how she can use some of what she knows about political philosophy to drive the establishment of a greater degree of equality in Muslim-majority nations. Her argument will revolve around the notion, already partially articulated here, that there is nothing inherently anti-democratic in the Qu’ran or in most of the hadith associated with the Islamic tradition. Although this may be an oversimplification, she pushes it forward. But what is meant by democratic practices is a difficult question that, beyond a certain anticipatory point, Bhutto apparently does not want to address. Consider the remarks made by Richard Miller in a journal article published on the possibility of merging some sort of Marxian view with the Rawlsian original position: I shall be arguing that certain aspects of Marxism and not very hard-line ones would preclude the requisite agreement to uphold the difference principle throughout the circumstances of justice.6 Obviously, aspects of a redistributive overview might have a lot to do with being inconsistent with the difference principle. But there is a real and genuine sense in which at least some of parts of even more moderate Islam have a great deal of difficulty meshing with Western democracy, and this is a point that needs to be made. Bhutto emphasises the parts of the tradition that rely on ijma, or consensus, and this is, indeed, an emphasis that can be made if one wants to build up a type of argument. But the problem, as Bhutto knew, is that this particular strand of Islamic thinking is alive and well only in types of Islam and in some geographical areas—it appears to be barely functioning in others. Western-style democracy requires not only reasoning and consensus, but a value that is in short supply in many parts of Europe and North America—a willingness to accept loss with comparatively little blame, and a desire to coexist with the other side. All of these characteristics, and more, are necessary for democracy to work. Rawlsian argument takes this into account; it is a necessary part of the whole picture. II If we can take a state as exemplary of effort in some ways, Bhutto’s own Pakistan might fulfil the desiderata. She herself spends a great deal of time in her work on this very area. As she writes at the beginning of a chapter that is devoted almost entirely to the history of Pakistan and its political structures: ‘[O]ne billion Muslims around the world . . . look to Pakistan as a model of moderation, civility, progress and democracy.7 As she contends, the history of the Partition of India (into what later became the nation-states of India and Pakistan) is, interestingly enough, itself a history of the use of democratic structures for certain sorts of ends. British rule in India and the subcontinent in general had meant that long before independence was achieved, the very democratic procedures that would later be called into question had already been used to establish some forms of home rule—the

its political structures: ‘[O]ne billion Muslims around the world . . . look to Pakistan as a model of moderation, civility, progress and democracy.7 As she contends, the history of the Partition of India (into what later became the nation-states of India and Pakistan) is, interestingly enough, itself a history of the use of democratic structures for certain sorts of ends. British rule in India and the subcontinent in general had meant that long before independence was achieved, the very democratic procedures that would later be called into question had already been used to establish some forms of home rule—the style of home rule that because it allowed freedom of assembly and the promulgation of ideas, gave rise to the voices that insisted on independence of the Muslim-majority areas at Partition. Bhutto herself notes how central the Anglo concepts were to the decisions made in the 1940’s when she writes: [In] 1940, the Muslim League called for an independent Pakistan composed of the Muslim majority areas of undivided India. The call for the independent state of Pakistan was made in the city of Lahore, the capital of the provincial parliament in Punjab. It is known as the Lahore Resolution.8 So it might be argued that democracy had a beginning in Pakistan that was far back in time—but it might also be argued that something was lost along the way. Part of the difficulty, as Bhutto indicates, was an unfair distribution of goods and services under Partition. This, of course, was not helpful in creating a peace- bound society. Pakistan received comparatively little in the way of educational institutions, or any of the sorts of benefits left over from colonialism that might actually have proven to be of help. With a disproportionate share of the military, one might think that Pakistan was under a heavy burden even to begin with. A greater difficulty is that the very system of government instantiated by the British—modelled, of course, on the parliament of the UK and relying heavily on the notion of political discussion—was simultaneously suited and unsuited Book Review 121 to areas such as the East and West wings of the original post-Partition state (the East wing comprising what is today Bangladesh). In Europe itself, or in the United Kingdom, intense political discussion is usually accompanied by freedom of the press, freedom of assembly and at least minimal adherence to freedom of religion. But this is not the case for much of what was colonial India—even in India itself there are difficulties, and the notion of ‘discussion’ can take some unsettling turns. In Pakistan the problems with discussion have been exacerbated both by local tradition and the very Islamic notions that Bhutto at least to some extent wants to defend—Western-style democracy does not seem to sit well here. Given all of the foregoing, it is at best shaky and at worst untruthful to try to make the case that there is a model state in Islamic regions of the subcontinent for the instantiation of democracy. If one were to try to examine the situation with Pakistan historically, Bhutto indicates that the very real tensions that developed between India and Pakistan almost immediately after Partition had a great deal to do with the decline, caused at least to some extent by lack of economic growth, of some democratic institutions in Pakistan itself. As she notes: Pakistan was . . . handicapped in other key areas, which contributed to the failure to institutionalize democracy. First, the 1948 war with India made Pakistan feel vulnerable to the Indian threat. Consequently, a large portion of the budget

of democracy. If one were to try to examine the situation with Pakistan historically, Bhutto indicates that the very real tensions that developed between India and Pakistan almost immediately after Partition had a great deal to do with the decline, caused at least to some extent by lack of economic growth, of some democratic institutions in Pakistan itself. As she notes: Pakistan was . . . handicapped in other key areas, which contributed to the failure to institutionalize democracy. First, the 1948 war with India made Pakistan feel vulnerable to the Indian threat. Consequently, a large portion of the budget was spent on defense to counter India’s military. India’s military, of course, was backed by a much larger population and economy. Between 1947 and 1950, approximately 70 percent of the Pakistani budget was spent on defense.9 It does not take professional training in the social sciences to discern that the lack of funding for schools, medical facilities and anything having to do with social welfare would, of course, affect the very areas related to the ‘four freedoms’ mentioned at an earlier point. Although Bhutto is reluctant to ascribe any of these alterations, post-independence, to religion, one would have to wonder what role was played by Pakistan’s fears of a Hindu-oriented state when so many of those who came into the region—especially West Pakistan—were refugees from that same state. (The population increased enormously after Partition.) In fact, Bhutto writes that the thinking that religion is closely involved in these difficulties is, as she phrases it, ‘simplistic and flawed’. One would be tempted to disagree with her, especially given the overall importance of religion and religiously-based cultural activities in the region. In recounting the history of difficulties that Pakistan encountered in attempting to instantiate democracy in its early years, Bhutto is also candid about the problems involved in starting up the basic trappings of democratic states, such as a constitution, an array of public offices, voting activities and the economic platform that might sustain these services. As she recounts: In 1962, a new constitution was promulgated for Pakistan . . . Ayub Khan believed that economic development would ultimately lead to democracy, or so he said . . . He claimed economic progress, with a GDP growing at 5.5 percent rate. However, the so-called fruits of this development did not trickle down to the poor masses.10 In other words, it is not enough for those in office to discuss growth and elections, or even to make some steps towards their instantiation. Greater efforts are needed, as she says, so that a coming ‘down’ to the poorer groups of goods and benefits yields a situation where these individuals can actually achieve literacy, vote, and then make changes through the various democratic offices. Interestingly enough, some of what Bhutto says with respect to the development of Pakistan is reminiscent of remarks made a century earlier by Harriet Taylor Mill (1998), who had been influenced by John Stuart Mill’s thoughts on British colonialism in South Asia. Writing in the chapter titled ‘On the Probable Futurity of the Laboring Classes’, a part of Mill’s Principles that is attributed by almost all (including Mill) to Harriet Taylor, she notes: ‘The most striking instances of prosperity are in the case of those who have had nothing to rely on but their own slender means . . .’ .11 Democracy must begin somewhere, and clearly it is more than an array of institutions. Although Bhutto does not indicate what societal changes would need to be made with respect to the status of women, she has given us much to

Asia. Writing in the chapter titled ‘On the Probable Futurity of the Laboring Classes’, a part of Mill’s Principles that is attributed by almost all (including Mill) to Harriet Taylor, she notes: ‘The most striking instances of prosperity are in the case of those who have had nothing to rely on but their own slender means . . .’ .11 Democracy must begin somewhere, and clearly it is more than an array of institutions. Although Bhutto does not indicate what societal changes would need to be made with respect to the status of women, she has given us much to think about in her work. Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West is an important book that should be read by all concerned about contemporary societies and their future. NOTES 1 Bhutto, Benazir, Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West, New York: HarperCollins, 2008 2 Ibid, p.17 3 Rawls, John, A Theory of Justice, Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1972. Even Rawls himself later admitted, addressing the criticisms of Susan Moller Okin and others, that parts of his work neglected such issues as gender. 4 Bhutto makes this clear in Ch 3 of her work, ‘Islam and Democracy’, pp.81-156 5 Ibid, p.276 Book Review 123 6 Miller, Richard, ‘Rawls and Marxism’, Philosophy & Public Affairs, Vol. 3, No. 2, Winter 1974, pp.167-91; this citation: p.170 7 Bhutto, Benazir, Reconciliation: Islam, Democracy, and the West, New York: HarperCollins, 2008, p.158 8 Ibid, p.164 9 Ibid, p.167 10 Ibid, pp.170-71 11 Mill, Harriet Taylor, The Complete Works of Harriet Taylor Mill, ed., Jo Ellen Jacobs, Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 1998, p.306 REFERENCES Huntington, Samuel, ‘The Clash of Civilizations?’, Foreign Affairs, Vol. 72, No. 3, 1993, pp.22-49 Mill, John Stuart, The Subjection of Women, Indianapolis: Hackett Publishing Co, 1988 Kaleidoscope The wars of 2024 brought together rivals-—but created new enemies 2024 has been one of the most eventful years since the World War II in the aftermath of the 9/11 attacks in 2001. The sudden toppling of Syria’s President Assad, North Korean soldiers fighting for Russia. British and US missiles sent to Ukraine and fired at Russia, Iranian missiles shipped to Russia. US-armed Israeli air strikes in Lebanon and Gaza, Yemeni missiles fired at Israel. It’s a complex and confusing web of conflicts and it prompts the inevitable question: Are the world’s battle lines becoming ever more interconnected? Let’s get one thing straight: this is not World War III, although President Putin does like to dangle that menace to scare the West away from sending more powerful weapons to Ukraine. But it’s clear that many of the conflicts on our planet have an international dimension, so how do these lines join up? (Read more on: https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cm2ewemgjw1o) World’s conflict zones increased by two-thirds in past three years, report reveals Wars have spread and intensified, with far-reaching impacts on global economic growth and food security, according to latest Conflict Intensity Index (CII). The proportion of the world engulfed by conflict has grown 65%—equivalent to nearly double the size of India—over the past three years, according to a new report. Ukraine, Myanmar, the Middle East and a ‘conflict corridor’ around Africa’s Sahel region have seen wars and unrest spread and intensify since 2021, according to the latest CII, published by risk analysts Verisk Maplecroft. While there was a lull in the levels of conflict globally during the COVID-19 pandemic, experts say there has been a rising trend of violence for at least a decade, while many longstanding crises continue unabated. (Read more on: https://www.theguardian.com/global-development/2024/nov/21/world-conflict- zones-increased-by-two-thirds-past-three-years-report-ukraine-myanmar-middle-east-africa) Worldwide climate continues to heat up Humanity cannot say it did not know. Scientists have been warning for decades that our addiction to fossil fuels will change the climate, potentially permanently. Kaleidoscope 125 The facts back them up. The level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere continues to grow, and 2024 will go down as the hottest year on record. For the first time, the average global temperature was 1.5°C hotter than during pre-industrial times, a dangerous sign given that the 2015 Paris Agreement seeks to keep the world from breaching that level permanently. Some of the consequences of a changing climate are easy to see. The US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration reported that the first ten months of 2024 produced twenty-four weather-related natural disasters in the United States that inflicted at least $1 billion in damage. Record drought has wracked northern South America, causing parts of the Amazon, the world’s largest river system, to dry up. But many of the consequences of climate change are hard to see because they involve incremental changes, like the intrusion of ocean water into freshwater drinking sources, that will become obvious only with time. COP 29, the annual international conference on climate, made modest progress on helping developing countries to finance emissions reductions and climate adaptation, but not much else. Scientists made headway on a range of technologies designed to mitigate the effects of climate change and hasten the transition to a green economy. However, their successes may be a case of too little, too late. (Read more on: https://www.cfr.org/article/ten-most-significant-world-events-2024) Ghana’s Supreme Court dismisses challenges to anti-LGBT bill Ghana’s Supreme Court has unanimously decided to dismiss two legal challenges to new anti-LGBT legislation that has been criticised by rights groups. In early 2024, lawmakers passed a bill imposing three years in prison for people identifying as LGBT and five years for forming or funding LGBT groups. Fear and uncertainty has gripped Ghana’s LGBT community, already facing limited rights. The bill, considered one of Africa’s most draconian anti-LGBT laws, has been condemned by the UN. (Read more on: https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/crrw2r8epw8o) Marriage Equality around the World There are currently 38 countries where same-sex marriage is legal: Andorra, Argentina, Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Colombia, Costa Rica, Cuba, Denmark, Ecuador, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Iceland, Ireland, Liechtenstein, Luxembourg, Malta, Mexico, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Slovenia, South Africa, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Taiwan, Thailand, the United Kingdom, the United States of America and Uruguay. These countries have legalised marriage equality through both legislation and court decisions. A throng of demonstrators near the US Senate office buildings carried signs reading ‘Republicans, where are your spines?’ and ‘USAID makes America safe, strong and prosperous.’ (Read more on: https://georgiarecorder.com/2025/02/05/dc/protest-erupts-in-d-c-against-trump- administration-plans-to-spike-global-humanitarian-aid/) Thousands of new species have been discovered by scientists in 2024 – here are some of the weirdest From the depths of the Pacific Ocean to the mountains of Vietnam, thousands of weird and wonderful new plants, animals and fungi have been discovered all over the world this year: 2024. Whether they are creatures unearthed in a little explored corner of the globe, or a specimen lying hidden in a drawer of a museum, they give us an exciting glimpse of the precious variety of life on our planet. The discoveries came amid growing frustration at the lack of progress being made on the international stage to halt unprecedented rates of destruction of the natural world. Human activities have increased the extinction of species to between 100-1,000 times faster than scientists say they would expect. Their voices often go against the rose-coloured tones that the multilateral system presents when speaking of YPS issues, and we are determined not to shy away from amplifying their invaluable views and lived experiences. On 3 33 this note, we, as editors, genuinely thank all contributors who shared their experiences, stories, and ideas on our platform. Editing and managing a youth-led network and journal on peace and security is an intricate mission. Ensuring youth leadership has been one of the core challenges for the past year. Maintaining youth leadership of the Journal requires replacing previous editors with younger ones periodically. Within these processes, it is essential that the previous editors transmit lessons learned to new editors. This knowledge transmission process involves intensive communication and mentoring. While this process requires a lot of time commitment and mentorship, it is rewarding and impactful for both the outgoing and incoming editors. Building on the previous editor’s vision, the current editorial team and the members of the YPS Network put intergenerational learning at the core of their work by instilling a truly nurturing, collaborative, youth-led and youth-friendly methodology. To this end, the call for contributions for this second issue of the YPS Journal was widely shared throughout public channels and with youth networks, inviting young people to submit abstracts or ideas for the research topics they felt important. The selection of contributors for the issue was done in a collaborative process with four reviews from the editorial team, paying attention to the diversity of topics and mediums of contributions as well the m essage each contributor included with their submission. After contacting the shortlisted candidates, the kick-off meeting of this second cohort was organised to orient the contributors and present the Network and the Journal. Over the course of seven months, the contributors began a process of creation and knowledge production in a close relationship with the editorial team. KNOWLEDGE INCUBATION SEMINARS To facilitate the process and sustain intergenerational awareness on the Youth, Peace and Security agenda, the editorial team organised a series of knowledge incubation seminars with guest experts ranging from practitioners to researchers and academics. These informed both the participants and th e e d ito ri al te am as th ey f oc us ed on different dimensions of the agenda and brought closer relevance to

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: the Network and the Journal. Over the course of seven months, the contributors began a process of creation and knowledge production in a close relationship with the editorial team. KNOWLEDGE INCUBATION SEMINARS To facilitate the process and sustain intergenerational awareness on the Youth, Peace and Security agenda, the editorial team organised a series of knowledge incubation seminars with guest experts ranging from practitioners to researchers and academics. These informed both the participants and th e e d ito ri al te am as th ey f oc us ed on different dimensions of the agenda and brought closer relevance to the cross-cutting discourse in the academic and institutional spheres. These seminars were often described as safe spaces in which both discussants and experts addressed topics that are ‘elephants in the room’ when discussing the Youth, Peace and Security agenda in policy platforms. The contributors connected their personal experiences with the global ongoing narrative of the agenda as well as relevant emerging issues that the agenda has not yet tackled. Each contributor identified the existing gaps and dedicated their contribution to depicting them as well as demonstrating their personal lived experiences while facing these gaps. The contributors further submitted their outlines and the first draft of their contributions over which there were multiple rounds of one-on-one personalised guidance sessions and written feedback. These sessions were organised with the objective of supporting the contributors and creating an enabling atmosphere to guide their submissions to be in a form of language used by institutions while also remaining loyal to their lived experiences. This was then turned into reviews of the first drafts, with editors offering feedback on ways to strengthen the contributors’ messages. PEER REVIEW PROCESS What added incredible value to the process was the peer-reviewing of submissions. The editorial team organised peer review sessions during which all contributors presented their drafts and pitches and requested feedback from their peers. The contributors received perspectives from their peers, exchanging not only expertise but offering support to one another. They were then paired together based on interest and topic expertise and continued to provide feedback on one another’s work. This peer-to-peer learning process is central to our approach to knowledge production methods. Network members, editors, and contributors learn through dialogue and receiving open feedback. We also secured contributions from the previous cohort in the peer review process. The young researchers of the first issue of the Journal peer-reviewed all the selected submissions. The previous editor of the Journal, Ali Altiok, also contributed to reviewing process and supported current editors during this processes. In our peer-review processes, we, unfortunately, had to postpone the publication of three articles for our next issue, as contributors could not manage to respond to edits requested by 44 their peers or had to drop out due to personal circumstances. Lastly, we also worked with two young copy-editors, Maarten Van Bijnen and Jo Coenen, to improve language and minimise grammatical errors. THE EDITORIAL TEAM As part of the Journal’s young and youth-led editorial team, we gained invaluable experience in supporting the research of these young contributors. It has been a precious learning and growth opportunity that has allowed us to gain experience in guiding and managing research outputs in an organised and relevant manner. It was particularly useful to discuss openly with other young contributors on the limitations of traditional scholarship and the need for vibrant and dynamic modes of sharing research in new and emerging artistic formats. It was a process that also held the challenges that come with translating information and data to different mediums. Additionally, our team had to

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: editorial team, we gained invaluable experience in supporting the research of these young contributors. It has been a precious learning and growth opportunity that has allowed us to gain experience in guiding and managing research outputs in an organised and relevant manner. It was particularly useful to discuss openly with other young contributors on the limitations of traditional scholarship and the need for vibrant and dynamic modes of sharing research in new and emerging artistic formats. It was a process that also held the challenges that come with translating information and data to different mediums. Additionally, our team had to study different contexts in more depth to be able to better understand the challenges that young peacebuilders face. In the end, it was a horizontal two-way training because editors learned from the contributors as much as contributors learned from editors. We were immersed in the context in which they live and their experiences of conflict and violence as well as how they overcome it on a daily basis. Our collective goal became ensuring that their voices are heard globally because their unique experiences deserve it. SECOND ISSUE OF THE JOURNAL ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY The YPS Journal’s second issue presents five stories exploring the emerging issues that are sidelined in the traditional, international policymaking processes. Following the example of gender transformative methodologies in knowledge creation, this issue importantly offers a strong analysis on the unique resilience of young women who are resisting oppression in various contexts. The contributors to this journal use diverse methods to bring the voice and agency of young people to the centre of political discussion on the YPS agenda. They use storytelling to share their own experiences working with people impacted by armed conflict; sharing how they implement peacebuilding programs in challenging contexts, how they interact with their young and ambitious peers, and how they memorise and draw from previous peace processes. Their insights also uncover the emerging connection between the YPS agenda and issues around the international drug prevention regime and environmental protection. In short, the issue is a testimony to the transformative agency of youth countering the exclusionary practices applied in the broader peacebuilding field (Simpson 2018). Shataakshi Verma’s artistic submission, titled Demanding Freedom: Art as a Tool for Peacebuilding, consists of paintings from Afghan women in exile or hiding after the withdrawal of US troops and the Taliban’s takeover of the country in August 2021. Shataakshi, rooted in her experience of living and working with young women across Afghanistan, transmits the voices of young women who do not have access to platforms and institutions to share their views with the world. Through presenting and interpreting paintings of young Afghan women, she shows the resilience of young women in reclaiming their space by forging alternative forms of peacebuilding. These stories show how young women creatively strive for their rights and freedoms despite their daily exposure to violent repression and intimidation. It is a testament to their agency. In her story, Take Five: Redefining Participation and Partnerships, Dorsa Babaei shares the challenges her organisation, PeaceMentors, faces in Iran. Dorsa’s storytelling skilfully brings the invisible obstacles young peacebuilders face to the light, exposing the disconnect between the international rhetoric of supporting young women peacebuilders and the reality of working with international organisations. When young women peacebuilders try to build partnerships with international organisations on equal terms, they often face subtle barriers ingrained in the bureaucracies of international organisations. This frustrates them as their struggle is appreciated in political discourse, but this appreciation rarely extends to tangible support beyond words. PeaceMentors, as a young women-led collective,

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: Participation and Partnerships, Dorsa Babaei shares the challenges her organisation, PeaceMentors, faces in Iran. Dorsa’s storytelling skilfully brings the invisible obstacles young peacebuilders face to the light, exposing the disconnect between the international rhetoric of supporting young women peacebuilders and the reality of working with international organisations. When young women peacebuilders try to build partnerships with international organisations on equal terms, they often face subtle barriers ingrained in the bureaucracies of international organisations. This frustrates them as their struggle is appreciated in political discourse, but this appreciation rarely extends to tangible support beyond words. PeaceMentors, as a young women-led collective, 55 finds creative solutions to overcome the lack of support from external actors in a country where room for civic engagement has been shrinking for decades. Dorsa also invites readers to reflect on the dynamics and relationships between different stakeholders in peacebuilding processes and to question what ‘leaving no one behind’ means in practice. Monserrat Angulo’s submission focuses on an under-researched area of the YPS agenda: Young Peacebuilders Challenging the War on Drugs. Drawing parallels between the youth bulge narrative of youth depicted as problems, reduced to being seen as violent threats or vulnerable to consequences of violence, Monserrat demonstrates how the war on drugs either infantilises youth by grouping them with children as vulnerable and in need of being protected, or as dangerous substance abusers highly susceptible to violence. To counter these premises, she illustrates examples of young peacebuilders from the Americas (Mexico) whose activities serve as stellar examples of how entire institutions rely on (can support and integrate) the work of young people, especially on drug-related issues. By highlighting examples of youth agency, the author tackles the dichotomic narrative of victim-and- perpetrator young people are often placed in and show that young people are already taking the lead in policy and programme solutions. Esihle Makitshi contributed to the current issue with an informative article on the nexus between climate change and the YPS agenda. Her article, titled The Scourge of Electronic Waste and Its Abuses of Human Rights, identifies the e-waste crisis as a humanitarian crisis. This cutting-edge analysis explains not developing sufficient measures for electronic waste management is creating a looming health crisis that particularly affects the well-being of youth. Esihle also argues that poor e-waste dumping practices have caused life-threatening situations and rights abuses for young people in some countries, offering examples from Ghana. Some waste management sites have developed into entire communities within towns and cities, with distinctive names, such as the Agbogbloshie e-waste site. Young people working for their livelihood in such sites are exposed to toxic metals. She also proposes solutions in the form of formalising the e-waste sector in an effort to offer safe working environments and reintegrate youth who are exposed to violence. What do young people think after a peace process fails? Berfin Coşkun and Mehmet İlhanlı respond to this question in the context of the 2013-2015 Turkish - Kurdish (PKK) Peace Processes. Their article, The Changing Peace Perception of Kurdish Youth in Diyarbakir after the Failed Peace Process, employs a youth participatory methodology to analyse youth perceptions. Through focus group discussions with young people affected by the conflict, their work provides a snapshot of what young people think about the previous peace efforts, and what they expect from future political processes aimed at ending violence. The 2013-2015 Turkish - Kurdish Peace Processes brought together high-level politicians, high-profile civil society representatives and scholars, but left young people behind. The failure of the Peace Process, paired specifically with an absent mechanism for youth participation or representation, disappointed young people. Berfin and

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: Kurdish Youth in Diyarbakir after the Failed Peace Process, employs a youth participatory methodology to analyse youth perceptions. Through focus group discussions with young people affected by the conflict, their work provides a snapshot of what young people think about the previous peace efforts, and what they expect from future political processes aimed at ending violence. The 2013-2015 Turkish - Kurdish Peace Processes brought together high-level politicians, high-profile civil society representatives and scholars, but left young people behind. The failure of the Peace Process, paired specifically with an absent mechanism for youth participation or representation, disappointed young people. Berfin and Mehmet highlight that young people memorise the failed attempts from a positive lens and hope for the establishment of a new process that is youth inclusive and accountable to the demands and desires of young people, in line with the YPS resolutions 2250 (2015), 2419 (2018), and 2535 (2020) that were adopted after the Peace Process took place. To conclude, our goal as a collective is to maintain the Journal on Youth, Peace and Security as a platform that adds value to the process of youth-led and inclusive knowledge production, acting as a repository for data, stories, art, and experiences that are not caught in the majority of institutional outlets and platforms. While the tone and information shared in some of the contributions to each issue might not be in line with policy language or scholarly jargon, each contributor spent great effort to make the YPS agenda a more reflective and critical space. With this note, the editorial team is immeasurably grateful to the experts who presented their work in our knowledge incubation seminars and all contributors who worked under tight deadlines - not only participating in lively and rich discussions during the knowledge incubation seminars but also taking into account numerous rounds of feedback from different reviewers. We encourage readers from all backgrounds to contact the editorial team or the contributors to learn more about the activities of the YPS Journal, on the experiences of each contributor, and on upcoming opportunities to contribute to the YPS Network. 6 REFERENCES Altiok, Ali, 2021. “New Frontiers in YPS on its 5th Anniversary” Journal of Youth Peace and Security, 1, no. 1. Security Council Resolution 1325, subsequent Women, Peace and WHEN WORDS Security (WPS) resolutions, as well as research and policy findings, have also strongly underlined the importance and FALL ON effectiveness of women’s broad and diverse participation in peacebuilding, as well as the need for a gender- DEAF EARS. transformative approach to peacebuilding processes. Each painting shared in this piece calls on international organisations, Member States, and other decision-making bodies to acknowledge the challenges faced by Afghan 8 women due to the education ban and the restricted freedom of movement for young women and girls. It is a direct violation of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and stands against gender equality. The artistic expressions of resilient young women as well as the role of culture and art in peacebuilding are empowering and alternative ways of participation in peacebuilding in an alarmingly shrinking civic space. The mutual pain experienced by young women in exile has bled out into images that mobilises solidarity for women and girls in Afghanistan, expressing their hopes for the future and their resilience against shared struggles. Art has become a tool for inclusivity and empowerment by Afghan youth. PERSPECTIVES OF YOUNG AFGHAN WOMEN Many young people work tirelessly to prevent conflict and sustain peace in their communities. Despite the fact that they are often disregarded and excluded from formal participation settings in peacebuilding, youth “are strikingly creative in forging alternative places of belonging and meaning through which to express themselves” (Simpson, 2018). Recognising and supporting the many ways in which young people engage in peacebuilding is an essential step toward developing peaceful and prosperous societies. The stories and art of young Afghan women exemplify the way in which positive youth resilience manifests itself in the face of conflict. In some instances, resilience of youth takes the form of “adaptive” or “survivalist” ways of dealing with violence by seeking protection, avoiding recruitment, evading dangerous territories, providing support to others, stopping the immediate fighting, contributing to humanitarian responses, or undertaking internal or cross-border migration (Simpson et al., 2016). In other instances, the resilience of young people may be more transformative by driving political change, rebuilding damaged relationships, and even addressing the root causes of conflict and violence. Standing at the crossroads between risk and resilience, young people provide a unique perspective on factors that may enable them to address their experiences of marginalisation and exclusion through either positive or negative manifestations of resilience (Simpson, 2018). The positive forms of resilience young people have in relation to peace and conflict are vital to building and sustaining peace. It helps societies in addressing manifestations, causes, and legacies of violent conflict, and can be critical to how communities and societies prevent the re-emergence of conflict (Simpson et al., 2016). text: factors that may enable them to address their experiences of marginalisation and exclusion through either positive or negative manifestations of resilience (Simpson, 2018). The positive forms of resilience young people have in relation to peace and conflict are vital to building and sustaining peace. It helps societies in addressing manifestations, causes, and legacies of violent conflict, and can be critical to how communities and societies prevent the re-emergence of conflict (Simpson et al., 2016). A FALSE HOPE On 21 March 2022, the Taliban promised to reopen all schools in Afghanistan, ending their seven- month de-facto ban on girls attending secondary school. Two days later, as many girls readied themselves for the first day of school, the Taliban reversed their decision. They announced that girls’ secondary schools were to remain closed indefinitely, until the Taliban put in place new policies - policies that are, in the words of the Taliban, compliant with “principles of Islamic law and Afghan culture”, which only includes further restrictions on schoolgirls’ clothing (Al Jazeera, March 2022). In many ways, the decision has crushed the hopes and dreams of the younger Afghan generation. Yet, art and storytelling have the power to inspire hope, even in the most repressive situations. Young Afghan women have chosen to share their message with the world by depicting what has been an accumulation of years of suffering to months of despair by sharing their paintings and artistic expression of the self. No matter how long they have lived in exile, their feeling of collective pain inspires action and transformation. 99 MURSAL HAIDERI’S STORY Mursal Haideri vividly remembers the day the Taliban rose back to power in Afghanistan. Panic and fear engulfed the streets of Kabul. Having grown up in the 1990s, she never experienced the repressive Taliban rule herself. Before the power grab in August 2021, Mursal (then 28 years old) was a secondary school teacher for girls. Within a few months, Mursal saw a mas- sive restriction of women’s rights and on her ability to do her job. It was then that she knew that she had to fight for the freedom of women in Afghanistan - a freedom that they had tenu- ously achieved over the past two decades. Mursal used the tool she knew best: art. Art, she thought, would be a way for women to voice their stories, opinions, and feelings – a tool for empowerment in powerless times. Mursal’s efforts did not go unnoticed. She and her family were forced to flee to Germany after receiving death threats. In spite of that, Mursal hasn’t stopped making art to express herself. She still hopes that more women like her are able to realise their strength through tools, such as art, to express their opinions. That is why Mursal advocates for the use of art as an informal educational tool to bring attention to issues being faced by women in conflict and post- conflict countries. Despite the current context in Afghanistan, Mursal continues to believe that education is a tool that will lead to the emancipation of women in Afghanistan and that their voices will one day be included in decision-making. The Lost Dream by Mursal Haideri Mursal is an Afghan school teacher who uses art as an educational tool for other young women like herself to speak up and raise awareness. She hopes to demand the attention from decision-makers through her work. 10 A Restricted Woman By Mursal Haideri Mursal’s art is all about shedding light on the issues faced by Afghani women. Beyond that, it reflects on her own struggles to reach freedom. She believes that with

Beyond that, it reflects on her own struggles to reach freedom. She believes that with her art she can raise awareness about the situation in her country and push those with power to act. A Girl's Dream By Malala Mehraban When the Taliban reversed their decision to let young girls go back to school, it left many of them heartbroken. Malala Mehraban is an Afghan artist with a strong wish that young Afghan girls get the education and future they deserve. Malala uses art to paint her thoughts and feelings. That way, she brings attention to the voices of young Afghan women for the world to see. 11 ZOLAI SULTAN’S STORY Four months after the Taliban takeover, Zolai was forced to leave her home. At only 17 years of age, she had lost the country she grew up in and still yearns to return. Oftentimes, her art is a reflection of her inner sadness, but beyond that, it portrays the sacrifices Afghan women have made to achieve their freedoms. Zolai’s art speaks of their resilience and, in turn, channels Zolai’s hopes and dreams. Art, she believes, gives younger women like herself the strength to share their difficult journeys and struggles. Art has been an empowering tool for Zolai. With it, she hopes that she can empower other young girls to speak up and come together in solidarity to demand their freedom. Dreams Vs Reality By Zolai Sultan Do Afghan women deserve their own voices? Can art become a vehicle for women from minority communities to use for declaring their choices? Those are the questions that Zolai Sultan explores through her art. As a young Afghan girl, she brings in her own experiences and history to imagine a better future. In this artwork, called Dream vs Reality, she shares her own dreams and the harsh conflict with reality. WAHIDA JABARZAI’S STORY: REBUILDING PEACE Wahida was born in Afghanistan and left the country as a young child. Being aware of the situation in her homeland and her background, she started to organise art events from the age of 11 to raise money for young kids like her who were back home. On the one hand, Wahida remembers her home in Jalalabad with the many fruit trees in the garden and on the other, the reality of living in war. She heard about the restricted human rights, the lack of access to education and the severity of those living below the poverty line.The realisation of knowing that her life as an Afghan woman could have been very different has been a catalyst in aiding others. Therefore, she uses art to show her country from a different angle - namely the human side. She felt that Afghans had been degraded and portrayed as inferior by the media and she wanted to change that narrative. Her art focuses on the hope that is often unimaginable given 12 the dire situation in Afghanistan. Wahida often uses children in her art, symbolising the next generation and the ‘blank canvas’ of hope that they give. Rebuilding Peace Almost From Scratch Despite being pushed

Rebuilding Peace Almost From Scratch Despite being pushed to the margins, young women and girls in Afghanistan have flour- ished, making the best of alternative and informal spaces for solidarity. Their artistic self- expression highlights the importance of art for young Afghan women to portray their pain and share their hopes for the future in a situation without any other formal alterna- tives. Through art, they are expressing their wishes for a peaceful Afghanistan and de- manding equal rights and education for girls and women. These young Afghan women who have been deprived of this right, are instead using art and storytelling to raise aware- ness, increase solidarity and create alternative forms of education for a peaceful future. While we stand to celebrate their resilience, we must be aware of their fundamental needs: formal education, human rights, and freedom. We must still establish coherent policies and frameworks to provide youth in Afghanistan and abroad with the formal and informal education opportunities they strive to achieve. It is a world WORTH in which non-youth stakeholders do not tread away from lived experiences of young people without reflecting, taking HEARING. inspiration, and recognising how they can support and amplify their work for co-creating a more peaceful world. source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: But none of what I am saying here is new, is it? There have been many research projects, national guidelines, online courses, guidance notes, training programmes, reports, and Security Council Resolutions that have developed a body of knowledge that, in theory, is supposed to support youth and non-youth actors in creating the world that I just described. Yet, here we are, at square one in so many contexts, explaining the very basic notions of meaningful youth participation and youth inclusion. From time to time, I ask myself if this is a comprehension issue or a question of political will. And then, I remind myself that it could be the lack of information and/or empathy coming from the ‘others’ who happen to be non-youth actors or institutions 15 with whom young people work daily. Maybe the knowledge outputs aren’t easy to read or tangible or maybe there are other competing priorities for decision-makers. But I ask myself, what if all of our efforts were like a TV show with a few episodes that they could sit and watch? A show with us young people taking the stage and the space to demonstrate how we navigate a day of our life in peacebuilding activities. Maybe then, the content would be intriguing enough to keep our non-youth partners at the edge of their seats and keep watching for a cathartic experience and ideally [and hopefully] a resolution. So, since I have this space today, I am going to treat it as an open mic to share a story that I wish could air on Netflix or a public venue. I hope that it will serve its purpose: an invitation to rethink how we do things in partnership with young people to facilitate their participation in peacebuilding. PROLOGUE I am the Co-Director of PeaceMentors, a young-women-led initiative founded in 2018 in Tehran, Iran. As one of the safe spaces for young people engaged in peacebuilding, our work focuses on two things: first we raise awareness on peacebuilding by working on primarily substantial topics such as positive peace, structural and cultural violence and secondly, we work on teaching youth creative ways of transforming their existing circumstances. For this we use tools and methods such as non-violent communication, future studies, negotiation, and mediation. The combination of these approaches help youth understand how they, as individuals, can create and facilitate change in their community. With almost no resources, we have maintained a team of 5 volunteers, implemented more than 15 projects, and partnered with 3 NGOs and the National Peace Museum. We have re-designed and adjusted our training repeatedly to ensure what we offer is relevant and practical for our context because what is usually offered at international level in terms of capacity development is rarely relevant to our realities. Additionally, we try to always dedicate a part of our time and effort to marginalised communities in our society, such as differently abled individuals or refugee communities. Due to the complex security challenges and bureaucratic barriers impeding youth participation as well as the financial burden that these factors bring in our context, we have not been able to register our initiative and we are not state-recognised. Many international donors, including embassies and pooled funds, have no understanding of how financing works in our context. So far, we have only received funds once through a Youth360 grant making process in 2020 facilitated by the United Network of Young Peacebuilders. We cannot benefit from national funds either. Due to lack of funding, we have always struggled to sustain volunteer membership beyond the core team to expand the work of

text: youth participation as well as the financial burden that these factors bring in our context, we have not been able to register our initiative and we are not state-recognised. Many international donors, including embassies and pooled funds, have no understanding of how financing works in our context. So far, we have only received funds once through a Youth360 grant making process in 2020 facilitated by the United Network of Young Peacebuilders. We cannot benefit from national funds either. Due to lack of funding, we have always struggled to sustain volunteer membership beyond the core team to expand the work of our organisation. This means, PeaceMentors is a space for young people whose dedication to peacebuilding transcends the barriers that they face due to international sanctions, national legislations, economic challenges, poor yet expensive access to the internet, visa barriers and extreme international marginalisation. We used to love this resilience until we realised that it is taking its toll on us. [Mic-drop] EPISODE ONE: LOCAL STRATEGIES FOR LOCAL CHALLENGES PeaceMentors started its work at a small scale by organising workshops and training of trainers programmes. Our partnerships were one-off events, usually held on single occasions such as 16 Nelson Mandela International Day or International Youth Day. Within the first two years of our work, we realised that participant feedback to our workshops and activities indicates significant impact. We felt more secure in trusting our skills and abilities even further and we thought of expanding our work by increasing the number of our partnerships and workshops into a more sustainable and long-term type of programming. But the plot twist here is that we faced two new challenges: First, most NGOs asked us for our state-recognised registration document as a prerequisite for partnership and considered it as a sign of our legitimacy and an indication to evaluate our skills. Secondly, some volunteers and team members asked if they could have a stipend or some form of compensation in return for what they did. Unless we were covered or co-funded by a partner organisation who would cover costs such as refreshments, transportation or reimbursement of the expenses they made for workshop materials, all the expenses were on the team. To overcome these challenges, we tried explaining to NGOs that as a young team which does not have donors nor generates income, meeting the basic requirements of the legal registration such as renting an office, purchasing a landline, paying for registration fees, hiring bookkeepers and auditors and insurance experts was next to impossible. Moreover, being registered would mean that our work was constantly audited for compliance and our organisation would not be considered a safe space for youth. To the volunteers, we offered more learning and development opportunities that we could find for free. We explained the reason behind holding many open and free events which was to ensure participation going beyond those who could afford a registration fee in a +50% inflation rate. Our efforts did bring us a few opportunities and retained some volunteers, but a big reality was that the lack of economic empowerment and the absence of financial sustainability was continuing to create big gaps in our work. EPISODE TWO: INTERNATIONAL PARTNERSHIPS IN THE SHORTAGE OF INTERNATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES Given the local circumstances and the challenges we faced and recognising that our solutions weren’t always 100% effective nor transformative, we shifted our attention towards international opportunities. This meant starting to apply for funds and grants. This was a whole new world for our team! We didn’t know how to find such opportunities and when we did come across them, we struggled filling all

EPISODE TWO: INTERNATIONAL PARTNERSHIPS IN THE SHORTAGE OF INTERNATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES Given the local circumstances and the challenges we faced and recognising that our solutions weren’t always 100% effective nor transformative, we shifted our attention towards international opportunities. This meant starting to apply for funds and grants. This was a whole new world for our team! We didn’t know how to find such opportunities and when we did come across them, we struggled filling all the paperwork, composing a proposal that matched the theme of the grant and yet stayed true to our mission and vision. We applied for many grants, and surprise, we got rejected many times! Sometimes it was because of not having the necessary registration documents or the perfect English skills, other times because of not having international bank accounts to receive the funds, and sometimes because we did not have a lot of media visibility for our own safety. It was honestly difficult to find grants that would align with activities that left a meaningful impact in our context without endangering our members and participants. In the utmost state of frustration and after attending many capacity development training sessions on financial sustainability that did not meet our needs, we finally received our first and so far, only grant through a Youth360 grant-making process. The grant was facilitated by the United Network of Young Peacebuilders . We received it in 2020, at the height of the pandemic, and it came as immense support to our operations. Yes, there are some happy parts in this story too. However, despite being a wonderful and participatory methodology for project-based funding, Youth360 was not a sustainable way to support the scope of the work of a youth-led organisation beyond the project we had in Iran. Therefore, PeaceMentors went back to the former model of partnerships and rethought how to change the culture of its partner organisations while benefiting from the support that they provided, albeit minimal. The goal was to start creating 17 partnerships that were more inclusive and equal while being receptive to youth-friendly ways of managing operations. We knew that our partners would not be fully onboard or aware of what youth-friendly peacebuilding or partnerships were and we were ready to share knowledge and build their capacities on these topics. The process was definitely not devoid of challenges; therefore, we move to the next episode to find out how it went! EPISODE THREE: [NON-]YOUTH-SENSITIVE PARTNERSHIPS On our quest to create and curate meaningful and youth-inclusive local partnerships, our initiative established a partnership with an NGO that works on education and empowerment for Afghan refugee children who have been victims of child labour in Tehran and its suburbs. We attended a few sessions with some of the staff and we agreed to contribute to some of their projects and see what kind of added value we can bring to the NGO. With the predominantly negative securitisation lens in our context which considers the refugee community as a threat with the potential desire to harm public property and social cohesion, our partner NGO has convinced its donors that education will decrease acts of vandalism of refugee and asylum-seeking children. At that time the NGO was exploring the possible projects they could implement using the funds they had received. After having a meeting with them, and hearing about both office staff and the teachers’ experiences of working with these children, we immediately saw a range of different

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: we can bring to the NGO. With the predominantly negative securitisation lens in our context which considers the refugee community as a threat with the potential desire to harm public property and social cohesion, our partner NGO has convinced its donors that education will decrease acts of vandalism of refugee and asylum-seeking children. At that time the NGO was exploring the possible projects they could implement using the funds they had received. After having a meeting with them, and hearing about both office staff and the teachers’ experiences of working with these children, we immediately saw a range of different emotions among the teachers: anger, frustration, and confusion. We realised the unsuccessful relationship between the teacher and these children causes inefficiency in the NGO’s efforts and creates more harm than good. That was the gap that we could fill and our chance to implement the ‘Do No Harm’ approach! Hopefully, that would be the cue for the NGO to understand that it has been partnering with a group of young skilled and motivated experts. So, we got started right away. To prevent the divide between the teachers and the children, PeaceMentors decided to set a phase of needs analysis to research the challenges that young immigrants and asylum seekers as well as their families face. These challenges were to some extent obvious to everyone because it is common sense that asylum seekers and refugees or irregular migrants do not enjoy a lot of rights and luxuries. But the depth and details of the impact of these deprivations were not very well- recognised by the teachers and the staff. The NGO didn’t have a critical analysis towards these challenges and their staff didn’t have the means to access young Afghans outside Iran for further research. Thanks to the United Network of Young Peacebuilders and other connections that the PeaceMentors team has we managed to talk to Afghan youth whose inputs both in written format and over a series of calls became central to gain cultural understanding on important notions such as education, labour and work, immigration, friendships, and gender. We understood the violent behaviours of children come with underlying causes such as anger management, the pressure that comes from children’s families and the pressure that is imposed on them by the segregation and discrimination against the Afghan community in the Iranian society. So, to implement our first project under the partnership, we developed two non-formal education courses: one for the children on the topics of anger management, communication, and emotional intelligence and one to train the teachers and increase their knowledge and capacity to manage classroom situations. We took a pre-test from the teachers to understand their cultural awareness of Afghan culture. We also interviewed them and understood that they didn’t know much about activities in non-formal education spaces. We believed such activities would facilitate their classroom management and interpersonal relationships with children which would build trust. To develop these courses in ways that would meet their needs, we did a great deal of programming, consulting, curriculum development, intercultural learning, and outreach. Before implementation, we consulted with 2 psychologists among our friends, took many hours 18 of non-violent communication training facilitated by our founder, learned about the basics of coaching, and finally reached out multiple times to create transnational partnerships for a better understanding of the culture and needs of the children and teachers by consulting with Afghan youth-led NGOs and an Indian NGO that worked with victims of child labour. Instead of focusing on words such as vandal and vandalism in our proposal to that NGO, we focused on empowerment,

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: great deal of programming, consulting, curriculum development, intercultural learning, and outreach. Before implementation, we consulted with 2 psychologists among our friends, took many hours 18 of non-violent communication training facilitated by our founder, learned about the basics of coaching, and finally reached out multiple times to create transnational partnerships for a better understanding of the culture and needs of the children and teachers by consulting with Afghan youth-led NGOs and an Indian NGO that worked with victims of child labour. Instead of focusing on words such as vandal and vandalism in our proposal to that NGO, we focused on empowerment, facilitation, cultural sensitivity, and non-violent communication. That seems like a lot of work and energy dedicated to a good cause for free, right? We thought at least we will get the recognition and appreciation as well as feedback for our earned expertise that the NGO wouldn’t otherwise be able to access – let alone for free! So, yes, in the next episode, we’ll see how it went. EPISODE FOUR: ‘LESSONS LEARNED’ FOR EVERYONE Did it go well? Spoiler alert, no. Straight from the beginning, our cause and approach were considered non-conventional and consequently, we were not recognised as an equal partners. Although the NGO enjoyed bragging about their new “free” and unique training for the children and staff to their donors, they didn’t consider remunerating us for our service or even reimbursing our basic costs. Discussions around further collaborations were not facilitated, and our work was not evaluated at any stage except for when the training had long ended, and the teachers were applying the learnings – only because we pushed and inquired directly and indirectly. The fact that we were a youth-led unregistered entity was the ace up the NGO’s sleeve. When we referred to our initial meetings and requested any form of evaluation such as feedback forms from management or the teachers, they mentioned that they only spend time conducting such processes for official and registered partners. Our meeting requests for discussing future collaborations kept being postponed, cancelled, or set on hold due to more important priorities. We were tired of following up and not receiving an answer or any transparent guidance on the next steps after the initial project. When we finally did get a meeting with them, we referred to their initial promise of continuing our training and potentially turning our contributions and programmes into paid services within their project budgets. But of course, we faced dismissal. They mentioned that any form of long-term collaboration, especially with remuneration is only considered for official NGOs or contractors that meet certain criteria (such as age, expertise and registration). Devastated, we were hoping to at least see a social media piece featuring us. Although they posted on their social media that their teachers are educated on concepts such as non-violent communication, we were never mentioned or tagged as the trainers of those topics. With frustration and the pain of feeling invisible, we went back to thinking about how to shape our upcoming projects and partnerships. The only things that made us feel better about the situation were the messages from the teachers and their gratitude as they implemented their learning and saw transformed results with the children. What could have gone better for us and our partner NGO in the partnership from the last episode? First and foremost, in the negotiation phase, they could be open to assessing our ideas and skills. Next, depending on their financial situation and planning, they could agree to a pilot project with us with cost reimbursement for our team or even with providing

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: shape our upcoming projects and partnerships. The only things that made us feel better about the situation were the messages from the teachers and their gratitude as they implemented their learning and saw transformed results with the children. What could have gone better for us and our partner NGO in the partnership from the last episode? First and foremost, in the negotiation phase, they could be open to assessing our ideas and skills. Next, depending on their financial situation and planning, they could agree to a pilot project with us with cost reimbursement for our team or even with providing a stipend if not contracts. Then, they could evaluate the pilot project by monitoring our performance and encouraging more teaching staff and volunteers to participate so they could also evaluate the impact of our programme. Upon satisfactory performance, they could, then, form a longer partnership with more equal and sustainable conditions with us because of the value that we 19 delivered to the children under their cover. They could feature us on their social media and website and tell the stories of the impact from our projects which could significantly help with the legitimacy of our initiative in the absence of state-recognized registration. The experiences coming out of this partnership consolidated two important concepts in our minds: First, meaningful participation should not be reduced to a motto or a mandate. It needs a mindset shift that actually enables meaningful interactions, power balance and relations between different generations and stakeholders. This means that non-youth partners, including local and national NGOs and Member States, should learn to think outside the box and become more receptive to trust the impact of non-conventional forms of peacebuilding - especially when witnessing the impact first-hand. Second, a good partnership has several elements: trust, alignment of values, common understanding of the problems, parties adding value to each other’s work and recognizing it, and effective collaboration. This means the power dynamics between two partners should be balanced and both should stand in equal positions, and each should believe the other party is essential and adds unique value to the project. This belief affects attitudes and facilitates collaboration between different parties. Each partner will have a clear understanding of the purpose of the partnership and will be able to witness their own contribution as well as that of the others. Such partnerships prevent tokenization and instrumentalization of youth because of meritocracy – why would someone not want to hire a group of young peacebuilders based on their skills, talents and added value? EPISODE FIVE: IN THE NEXT SEASON… Just like some producers, I have come to a bit of a struggle to decide whether the continuation of the show behind this journey should be renewed for another season. Sometimes it feels like there is already a lot of waste of resources and time on promoting the messages that can be given in one episode rather than prolonging the story and dragging on the same plot. Things must change to make the next season a bit more innovative and interesting. We have read the Secretary General’s Report on Youth, Peace and Security and while it highlights all the progress that young people have made in their peacebuilding journeys, it indicates that there are still many challenges that leave a sense of mistrust between youth and other generations as well as institutional forces (United Nations 2020). The Missing Peace: Independent Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security, which funny enough addresses the problem in its very title, also addresses the cultural, psychological, and structural violence of youth exclusion and sheds light

text: make the next season a bit more innovative and interesting. We have read the Secretary General’s Report on Youth, Peace and Security and while it highlights all the progress that young people have made in their peacebuilding journeys, it indicates that there are still many challenges that leave a sense of mistrust between youth and other generations as well as institutional forces (United Nations 2020). The Missing Peace: Independent Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security, which funny enough addresses the problem in its very title, also addresses the cultural, psychological, and structural violence of youth exclusion and sheds light on its gendered dimensions (Simpson 2018) So, the real questions here are, what does it take for us all to move on to the next season so we can address more intricate challenges that young people face? What do we need to transform the narrative from helping and supporting young people to recognizing their essential contribution and added value to peace and security processes in every part of the world beyond speeches and lip service? Whenever we have some of these answers, let’s start the next season! 20 REFERENCES Simpson, Graeme. 2018. The Missing Peace: Independent Progress Study on Youth, Peace and Security.
