Available at: https://undocs.org/en/S/2020/167 YOUNG PEACEBUILDERS CHALLENGING THE WAR ON DRUGS M o n s e r r a t A n g u l o T he current international drug control THE system is characterised by punitive WAR policies, grounded in the idea that prohibiting drugs will refrain young people ON from accessing and using them. DRUGS, Countries have inevitably failed to achieve a “drug-free world”, considering that the demand or supply of drugs THE has not been reduced (UNODC 2021). Instead, this approach of criminalisation has fuelled violence worldwide WAR and created a profitable illegal market. The upfront battle between the State and drug-trafficking organisations, as ON well as the criminalisation of the production and use of psychoactive substances, have brought high costs to societies that outweigh any possible marginal benefits. US. This is disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations, such as young people. Far from protecting, the war on drugs is actively affecting young people (Transform 2015). The United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) has identified a series of “unintended consequences” of the current drug control system, highlighting that prohibitionist policies have dramatically increased the risks for people who use drugs, producing additional harm that could be entirely avoidable (UNODC 2008). This affects many vulnerable populations, such as young people. The Global South has faced these effects more severely, which are disproportionately impacting populations with marginalised backgrounds (Transform 2015). 22 In general terms, young people who use drugs are often more at risk of facing discrimination or criminalisation than adult users, because they are frequently unaware of their rights, which can result in the inability to access legal aid, abuse from law enforcement authorities, and denial of public health services (Barret, 2015). For example, in schools, random drug testing (Yamaguchi et al. 2003), along with sniffing dogs and strip-searching are common practices that have raised important ethical concerns about rights and privacy (Supreme Court of the United State 2009). Policing undermines the health goals of drug policy and the well-being of those the drug control system supposedly intends to protect. Punitive tactics for dissuading consumption are not only ineffective, but they exacerbate human rights violations. This vicious circle can be reversed with harm reduction interventions and legislative changes that focus

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: from law enforcement authorities, and denial of public health services (Barret, 2015). For example, in schools, random drug testing (Yamaguchi et al. 2003), along with sniffing dogs and strip-searching are common practices that have raised important ethical concerns about rights and privacy (Supreme Court of the United State 2009). Policing undermines the health goals of drug policy and the well-being of those the drug control system supposedly intends to protect. Punitive tactics for dissuading consumption are not only ineffective, but they exacerbate human rights violations. This vicious circle can be reversed with harm reduction interventions and legislative changes that focus on lessening marginalisation, violence and other social structural problems, as well as considering the social and cultural backgrounds (Barret 2015). “IN THEIR BEST INTEREST?”: MISSING YOUTH IN INTERNATIONAL DRUG POLICY LEGAL FRAMEWORK The insistence of governments to send a ‘strong message’ about drugs often overrides con- cerns about youth rights and the well-being of young people (ReverdeSer Colectivo 2020). The criminalisation approach justifies discrimination and social exclusion, high levels of police harassment, and age restrictions or requirements for parental consent for treatment. The absence of legally binding mechanisms on youth and drug control in international law allows countries to continue exercising these practices (Human Rights Watch 2004). The Vienna Convention was the first international treaty to introduce this topic by setting out a “deep concern that children are used in many parts of the world as an illicit drug consumers market and for purposes of illicit production, distribution and trade in narcotic drugs and psychotropic substances, which entails a danger of incalculable gravity” (United Nations 1964). However, the topic is not mentioned in the rest of the international legal system on young people. In addition, the Youth, Peace and Security global agenda has left aside drug policy topics, despite it being widely known that drugs are a peace and security issue (Salifou 2020). The UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC) stands among the few UN human rights treaties in setting out the responsibility of the States to ‘protect young people from drugs’ (United Nations 1990). While it mentions young people as its target, the convention is mostly treating matters relevant to children. By addressing young people solely through a protection lens, the convention actually strips them off of their agency. Article 33 connects the CRC with the three existing drug control conventions adopted in 1961, 1971 and 1988. However, only the 1988 Convention Against Drug Trafficking specifically refers to children or minors, and neither of the only two clauses includes specific measures to address drug use among children or their involvement in the drug trade (United Nations 1988). YOUNG PEACEBUILDERS REVERSING THE PUNITIVE POLICIES ON DRUGS The absence of legally binding mechanisms on youth and drug control in international law is reinforcing the harsh impacts of punitive policies on young people and is providing incentives to governments to continue the criminalisation and securitisation approach, justifying their measures on current public actions that address “children’s best interests”, decreasing youth agency and implementing policies that actually result in human rights violations (Human Rights Watch 2004). The derived policies are not addressing the needs of young people, and punitive strategies have exacerbated the impacts of drug prohibition in regions such as Latin 2233 America (Parker 2018) and South Asia (Coca 2018). The war on drugs is highly ineffective and has had huge impacts on young people. Drug policy is a peace and security issue, and it must be integrated in the Youth, Peace and Security Agenda. Resolution 2250 is the first international agreement of the UN Security Council that recognises

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: youth agency and implementing policies that actually result in human rights violations (Human Rights Watch 2004). The derived policies are not addressing the needs of young people, and punitive strategies have exacerbated the impacts of drug prohibition in regions such as Latin 2233 America (Parker 2018) and South Asia (Coca 2018). The war on drugs is highly ineffective and has had huge impacts on young people. Drug policy is a peace and security issue, and it must be integrated in the Youth, Peace and Security Agenda. Resolution 2250 is the first international agreement of the UN Security Council that recognises the important role of young people in preventing violence as active agent in their communities (United Nations 2015). This, and subsequent resolutions on Youth, Peace and Security have framed the opportunities for action on this topic, on five pillars: participation, protection, prevention, partnership, and disengagement and re-integration. The protection pillar calls on Member States to involve young people in conflict prevention and resolution, and in the promotion of social cohesion. Although this approach has been traditionally based on paternalism, it is evolving towards an approach that, when in synergy with other pillars such as participation, may better fulfil young people’s necessities. The protection and participation pillars can take many forms in drug policy. Protection means reducing the individual, community and social factors that create risks and violence among young people. Participation can be translated as increasing political awareness and community action to respond to the direct, symbolic and structural violence caused by the war on drugs. It’s also an opportunity to identify the harms, repair them and build peace through legislative changes, strategies, programs and services to change the drug consumption approach to be treated as a public health and human rights topic instead of a national security issue. In the following section, a case study of two initiatives that are challenging the current paradigm put in evidence the opportunities to use both pillars, to repair some of the harms caused by prohibitionist policies. Case Study: Young People in Mexico Implementing Community Interventions Using a Public Health And Harm Reduction Approach Mexico has experienced the profound impacts of punitive drug policies, which are still evident 16 years after the declaration of the war on drugs from President Felipe Calderon. The results for young people have been disastrous (Guevara et al. 2018). The use of drugs has not reduced among this population (Martínez 2019), and figures from civil society estimate that around 460,000 young people (without disaggregating if below or over 18 years old) have already been recruited by criminal organizations (Pérez 2020). State institutions have not addressed this topic from human rights, public health and peacebuilding approach. Fortunately, young people in Mexico have developed strategies aimed to change the local policies and the public narrative on drugs by designing health programmes and cultural interventions to share evidence-based information and to build effective communities to confront the stigma and social exclusion related to drug use and criminalization. The Substance Analysis Program (PAS in Spanish) and the Support Don’t Punish Festival are two examples of youth-led initiatives in the YPS prevention and participation pillars that are challenging the war on drugs. The illegality of drug markets has created incentives for producers to adulterate or replace drugs with more potent substances, increasing their profits but posing more risks for users of experiencing overdoses. The Substance Analysis Program is a youth-led project initiated in 2016, that offers a confidential and friendly service to provide information based on scientific evidence, free of biases and moral judgments about psychoactive substances. The data collected as a result

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: (PAS in Spanish) and the Support Don’t Punish Festival are two examples of youth-led initiatives in the YPS prevention and participation pillars that are challenging the war on drugs. The illegality of drug markets has created incentives for producers to adulterate or replace drugs with more potent substances, increasing their profits but posing more risks for users of experiencing overdoses. The Substance Analysis Program is a youth-led project initiated in 2016, that offers a confidential and friendly service to provide information based on scientific evidence, free of biases and moral judgments about psychoactive substances. The data collected as a result of the program interventions is an effort from young people to show the effectiveness of the harm reduction approach in drug policy. It’s also an important resource for policy advocacy 24 and participation in the decision-making process. PAS analyses the composition of substances through chemical tests, to inform about their content and the risks of using them, addressing the risks involved in the illegal drug markets, such as adulteration, substitution and misinformation. The intervention usually takes place at festivals or nightlife spaces, where there is a high prevalence of substance usage. It is a useful mechanism to protect young people without stripping them of their agency, using a peer-to- peer approach that provides information, and a support network, preventing social and health issues, including fatal overdoses. Despite the documented impacts and results of this service in four years, it is not mainly accessible due to the lack of public resources and political willingness (Bencomo & Pineda 2021). Another initiative that is currently being implemented in Mexico is the Support Don’t Punish Festival. This project is a cultural and artistic intervention led by young people collaborating in different civil society organisations. It has been operating since 2018, as part of a global campaign, in response to the International Day Against Drug Abuse and Illicit Trafficking, which is held each year on June 26th by the United Nations. The festival designs activities to increase the availability of harm reduction services and information, in order to promote effective dialogues on the use of drugs and the impacts of prohibitionism among other young people and their communities (Angulo 2021). In four consecutive editions, the festival has promoted honest conversations about drugs through discussions, keynote conferences and workshops in public and virtual spaces. The main result has been the creation of regional and local collaboration networks, which have been used as a mechanism to increase popular education and advocacy activities, and as a venue for strengthening activism in drug policy reform in favour of the generational change. This project is setting an example on how to repair the damages caused by the war on drugs by increasing the participation of young people in the public sphere. It is creating opportunities to build collaborations and partnerships with key actors at the local level, leaving space for young people to provide education and to advocate for less repressive policies and more programmes based on evidence, human rights and social justice. Prevention and building community networks are central keys in this intervention. Leaving no one behind and open spaces for political awareness and dialogue with authorities, nongovernmental organisations, young people and their communities is a way to create collective solutions to attend and manage local conflicts. This is extremely important when we refer to drugs and their policies as a public issue that has affected people in many ways. MOVING FORWARD TO AN INCLUSIVE AND PEACEBUILDING DRUG POLICY REFORM Every violent scenario created as a consequence of the war on drugs is impeding young peacebuilders to address current

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: and social justice. Prevention and building community networks are central keys in this intervention. Leaving no one behind and open spaces for political awareness and dialogue with authorities, nongovernmental organisations, young people and their communities is a way to create collective solutions to attend and manage local conflicts. This is extremely important when we refer to drugs and their policies as a public issue that has affected people in many ways. MOVING FORWARD TO AN INCLUSIVE AND PEACEBUILDING DRUG POLICY REFORM Every violent scenario created as a consequence of the war on drugs is impeding young peacebuilders to address current global issues. While the focus has been on traditional prevention and criminalisation, the international arena should promote the five pillars of the Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) agenda to address the world drug problem. Participation and prevention are two focal points that should be enhanced and intersected to ensure an inclusive drug policy reform. Governments must acknowledge the damages created by prohibitionist policies and explore innovative alternatives based on peacebuilding and human rights, such as the legal regulation of drug markets and harm reduction services and programmes, 25 using a youth, multicultural, and gender approach (GCDP 2018). As it has been evidenced, young people are starting to meet other peers to speak about drugs, forge effective relations, exchange their knowledge and raise interest in the importance of drug policy reform. These efforts must receive the support of stakeholders at the national and international level, from the private sector to UN agencies. Peace is at the end of this war against our psychoactive nature that is harming current and future young generations. 26 REFERENCES Angulo, Monserrat. 2021. Journal of School Health. 73(4):159-164. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1746-1561.2003. tb03596.x 28 UNDERSTANDING THE IMPACT OF ELECTRONIC WASTE FROM A YOUTH PERSPECTIVE E s i h l e M a k i t s h i W THE E-WASTE e, as the youth, are being failed by some governments who CRISIS IS A do not take any or sufficient responsibility for the management of HUMANITARIAN electronic waste. CRISIS. Society’s ignorance of the extensive harm caused by unsustainable electronics can no longer be excused. The role that manufacturers play is untenable. As Ari (2016) states: “the incremental rate of obsolescence and subsequent upgrades of product quality are key psychologically impacting factors factually influencing the consumers’ mindset in contributing to the faster turnaround of the product life cycle”. It is not difficult to see how urgently we need an intervention. In this article, I call for increased awareness of the dangers posed by the transboundary movement of e-waste. Change needs to be enacted at the source of the electronic product lifecycle and due responsibility must be placed on all the actors who interact with it. Governments and regulatory bodies must be held accountable for the safe and sustainable recycling of electronics. This short article will explain the impact of the looming e-waste crisis and how the inaction and negligence of governments affect youthful populations. I aim to focus on nations’ failure to uphold the United Nations Security Council Resolutions (UNSCR) 2250, 2419 and 2535 on youth, peace and security and opportunities to remediate these lapses. These resolutions aim to empower youth by ensuring their 29 participation in the prevention and resolution of conflict. DEFINING ELECTRONIC WASTE E-waste, as defined by Ashok Rathoure, is electrical and electronic equipment that is discarded as waste by consumers. This definition also extends to rejects from the manufacturing process (Rathoure 2019). E-waste contains valuable resources such as gold, silver, palladium, platinum, cobalt, copper, iron, and aluminium that can be extracted through proper handling and treatment processes. It also contains hazardous substances that - if improperly disposed of - may harm the environment and our health. Even in cities with sophisticated waste management systems, e-waste is often discarded alongside other solid waste, ending up in landfills. According to the European Parliament, e-waste is the fastest growing waste stream in the EU, and less than 40% is

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: as waste by consumers. This definition also extends to rejects from the manufacturing process (Rathoure 2019). E-waste contains valuable resources such as gold, silver, palladium, platinum, cobalt, copper, iron, and aluminium that can be extracted through proper handling and treatment processes. It also contains hazardous substances that - if improperly disposed of - may harm the environment and our health. Even in cities with sophisticated waste management systems, e-waste is often discarded alongside other solid waste, ending up in landfills. According to the European Parliament, e-waste is the fastest growing waste stream in the EU, and less than 40% is recycled (European Parliament 2020). This figure is much higher in lower and medium-income countries. HOW THE IMPENDING E-WASTE CRISIS IS ENDANGERING THE YOUTH Resolution 2250, under the prevention pillar, emphasizes the “importance of creating policies for youth that would positively contribute to peacebuilding efforts, including social and economic development” (United Nations Security Council 2015). In low socio-economic areas where they are not adequately accounted for, youth are left to find their own means of putting food on the table. To illustrate the scale of the problem we are dealing with globally, only 17.4% of e-waste produced in 2019 reached formal management or recycling facilities, the rest was illegally dumped to be later recycled by informal workers (Johnson 2021). With zero regulations, informal waste recycles have no support systems and experience deplorable conditions - often without safety measures or the use of any personal protective equipment. Youth make a large component of informal waste recyclers around the world. Entire families thrust this responsibility on youth to support their families or earn their keep in the household. With such a large majority of youth being exploited in the informal waste sector, the industry disproportionally harms younger people from socioeconomically marginalized groups. Without social and economic policies in place, youth who work to support their households often have their education cast aside. Entering the skilled job market thus becomes increasingly difficult, trapping most in cycles of poverty. This problem is most evident in low-income countries with a large population of youth, where impoverished citizens have little or no influence on the waste products being illegally dumped near their communities (du Toit and Bodenstein 2014). States have failed to monitor landfills which have enabled informal waste sorting to thrive, to the detriment of the community and individual health: “informal processing of e-waste extracts valuable metals through open burning, heating or acid leaching reclamation efforts expose children and workers to mercury, lead, cadmium, and other by-products of plastics and metal processing” (Goldizen et al. 2021). Youth, many of whom deal directly with waste, are actively poised. Although the UNSCR 2250 aims to protect youth from armed conflict, its stipulations that aim to build resilient communities can be extended to provide more sustainable and healthier conditions of living for youth who work in landfills across many post-conflict societies. 30 Additionally, young informal waste recyclers face numerous challenges in accessing healthcare services (Kistan J 2020). Due to their socio-economic status, they often do not have the means to make clinic visits. The few who do have access to medical care do not disclose the nature of their work, giving healthcare providers an incomplete picture of what could cause their ailments. Clause 8 of the UNSCR acknowledges the responsibility states have in ensuring the human rights of all individuals and youth are protected. The failure of states to formalise the e-waste industry means that youth in already-precarious situations have to choose between making a daily wage or tending to their health. Without these protections, including occupational health standards

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: their socio-economic status, they often do not have the means to make clinic visits. The few who do have access to medical care do not disclose the nature of their work, giving healthcare providers an incomplete picture of what could cause their ailments. Clause 8 of the UNSCR acknowledges the responsibility states have in ensuring the human rights of all individuals and youth are protected. The failure of states to formalise the e-waste industry means that youth in already-precarious situations have to choose between making a daily wage or tending to their health. Without these protections, including occupational health standards and personal protective equipment, the youth population who engage in this industry suffer great health risks. Debris from discarded devices is overwhelming landfills in low- and middle-income countries (World Health Organization 2021) that receive shiploads of devices and appliances discarded from high-income nations, which often avoid strict domestic rules about device recycling and disposal. This practice is detrimental to the health and well-being of children, youth and women of childbearing age who have no other financial recourse than to work in the growing E-waste dumps in Africa, Asia, and Latin America (Goldizen et al. 2021). HOW COUNTRIES TACKLE E-WASTE AND THE OPPORTUNITIES THEREIN Under the aforementioned prevention pillar, emphasis is placed on creating policies that contribute to peace-building efforts that include “supporting projects designed to grow local economies” (United Nations Security Council 2015). One such initiative can be found in young entrepreneurs. Some e-waste sites in Ghana have developed into entire communities within towns and cities, with distinctive names, such as the Agbogbloshie e-waste site. Around the vast dumpsite dedicated to rudimentary waste scavenging, there are also many workshops and small retail kiosks that refurbish and resell used items and provide skilled apprenticeships to young people aiming to work in the electronics industry (World Health Organization 2021). Formalizing these sectors can be implemented successfully as seen by Pacific Hazardous Waste (PacWaste), which is responsible for improving hazardous waste management across the Pacific region, including e-waste management. PacWaste found that it could collaborate with governments and organizations to implement measures such as developing takeback systems, raising awareness and increasing training opportunities in safe e-waste dismantling and exporting practices (World Health Organization 2021). A glaring opportunity to support youth social and economic development arises in the creation of green jobs with the intent to tap into the growing formalized e-waste industry. A market research analysis of global e-waste management found that its market size was valued at $49,880 million in 2020 and is projected to reach $143,870 million by 2028 (Nair 2021). While these opportunities are ripe for youth involvement, the International Labour Office has found that waste workers face threats of displacement and job losses in favour of large multinational waste management companies (International Labour Office 2014). States must intervene to provision these jobs and provide vocational training to help local communities in staying competitive. One of the most effective tools nation-states can employ is to enforce responsible consumption and production. To do this states assign the responsibility to manufacturers to prevent waste at the source. This strategy promotes product design for the environment and supports the achievement of public recycling. Ever-increasing demand and scarcity of rare metals have been leading to the rapid rise in prices of these metals. These metals need to be recovered from 31 e-waste landfills for reuse in other products. This, in turn, also benefits manufacturers to produce electronic devices with lower costs (Nair 2021). THE ENVIRONMENTAL BURDEN OF E-WASTE When e-waste gets buried at a landfill, it can dissolve in microscopic traces into the runoff

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: To do this states assign the responsibility to manufacturers to prevent waste at the source. This strategy promotes product design for the environment and supports the achievement of public recycling. Ever-increasing demand and scarcity of rare metals have been leading to the rapid rise in prices of these metals. These metals need to be recovered from 31 e-waste landfills for reuse in other products. This, in turn, also benefits manufacturers to produce electronic devices with lower costs (Nair 2021). THE ENVIRONMENTAL BURDEN OF E-WASTE When e-waste gets buried at a landfill, it can dissolve in microscopic traces into the runoff sludge that permeates the site. Eventually, these traces of toxic materials pool into the ground below the landfill. That toxic water under the landfill doesn’t stop below the landfill. It continues to the groundwater and into the area’s freshwater resources. Young Ghanaians around Agbogbloshie face the danger of having to be displaced or fighting for access to clean water. The Korle Lagoon around the site is showing signs of serious contamination (Kushi, et al. 2013). A study has found that climate drivers of water scarcity are associated with an increase in the likelihood of armed conflict (Regan 2020). This is only one example of how states can be in contravention of the youth, peace and security agenda which requires states to provide adequate support to implement violence prevention activities and support social cohesion. This was further supplemented by a resolution in 2018 that reaffirms “the importance of national ownership and leadership in peacebuilding, whereby the responsibility for sustaining peace is broadly shared by the Government and all other national stakeholders” (United Nations Security Council 2018). By offering sustainable solutions - environmentally, economically, and health-wise - through addressing the problem of e-waste, states can ensure sustained peace for many at-risk communities. Ultimately, the role that e-waste plays in the climate change debate cannot be underestimated due to the rate at which electronics are being produced and deprecated. Cambria Khayat argued that “environmental violence that stems from environmental conflict is a form of slow violence as it inhibits individuals from reaching their maximum potential in life” (Khayat 2021). THE WAY FORWARD Policymakers should be enabling and not hindering private individuals to recycle electronic waste. Communities must work together to curb lifestyle patterns that consume an excess of electronic goods out of convenience and ease instead of necessity (Ari 2016). We can reduce the amount of e-waste generated and ensure that e-waste is not discarded as general waste but sent for proper treatment and recycling instead. Municipalities must ensure that waste collectors have quarterly e-waste collection efforts at specific collection points or from homes. We need to legislate programmes to incentivize manufacturers to extend the lifetime of these devices by refurbishing and reusing parts to create a more circular economy. We, as younger generations, need to demand from our governments that the health and safety of e-waste workers and communities are protected. There need to be systems that train and protect workers, and monitor exposure and health outcomes. Governments must incorporate young e-waste workers into the formal economy with decent conditions across the value chain of collection, processing and recycling, and resale by transitioning informal workers to the formal economy. It is also our duty as a youth to raise awareness of e-waste health risks and encourage responsible recycling with policymakers, communities, waste workers and their families. The health sector can provide leadership and advocacy on e-waste initiatives and contribute to multisectoral action by reaching out to other sectors to demand that health concerns be made central to e-waste policies. Chronic exposure to

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: monitor exposure and health outcomes. Governments must incorporate young e-waste workers into the formal economy with decent conditions across the value chain of collection, processing and recycling, and resale by transitioning informal workers to the formal economy. It is also our duty as a youth to raise awareness of e-waste health risks and encourage responsible recycling with policymakers, communities, waste workers and their families. The health sector can provide leadership and advocacy on e-waste initiatives and contribute to multisectoral action by reaching out to other sectors to demand that health concerns be made central to e-waste policies. Chronic exposure to e-waste and its toxic components violates human rights. For many children and youth, lack of access to an appropriate justice system or 32 an effective remedy further violates their human rights (Forti et al. 2021). A Resolution enacted in 2020, UNSCR 2535, reaffirms states’ obligation to respect, promote and protect the human rights of all individuals, including youth, and ensure equal access to justice (United Nations Security Council 2020). The next generation, youth, are burdened with the onus to fix the issues that they have inherited in this world. Climate change is an ongoing crisis that young people are the most vocal about. They are seeing the prospects of their future diminishing rapidly with no real change in sight. The champions of e-waste management are the likes of Alex Lin who in 2004, at 9 years old in Rhode Island, started initiatives to refurbish and recycle computers. His organization called WIN (Westerly Innovations Network) now has a global network that raises awareness about the dangers of e-waste, shares best practices and empowers students to tackle e-waste in their communities. To the youth, advocating for environmental justice is no longer a choice, it needs to be a lifestyle. These types of initiatives are the cornerstone of the youth, peace and security’s pillar of disengagement and reintegration. Clause 17c calls for the support of youth-led and peacebuilding organisations as partners in youth employment and entrepreneurship programs. In this article, I argued that one of the biggest obstacles the youth face is the general lack of awareness of the environmental dangers of e-waste and worse, societal indifference. Even today, the people at the forefront of e-waste activism are teenagers like Justin Shnayder, co- founder of Erasing E-Waste, who uses modern technology such as statistical computing to identify the areas with the most amount of e-waste to be collected. The youth are on the front lines of climate change and are refusing to let their futures slip away (Connect4Climate 2020) With the unbridled growth we have seen in the manufacturing industry and will continue to see, states have a responsibility to deal with the toxic waste it expels. States can do this by also imposing regulations on companies to manage their resources and to recycle essential products. This will ensure that e-waste policies are implemented at the source. Youth who work with e-waste should be empowered to responsibly dispose of the waste and have sufficient education to be aware of the harmful effects of illegal disposal. Governments are accountable for the shipments of waste that endanger the lives of youth populations and the environments they inhabit. No government is above reproach when enabling environmental violence against civilians and youth. Policymakers should ensure that all waste workers including those in the informal industry have occupational regulations in place to protect their rights, health and dignity. Policies must be in place for the creation of green jobs to ensure the socio-economic well-being of the youth whilst attempting to reverse the immense damage already caused by the

THE CHANGING PEACE PERCEPTION OF KURDISH YOUTH IN DIYARBAKIR AFTER THE FAILED PEACE PROCESS M e h m e t İ l h a n l ı & R e v ş e n B e r f i n C o ş k u n T YOUNG he toll of armed violence displaced more than a million Kurdish citizens PEOPLE ARE and killed around 40.000 people, according to estimates (140journos 2017). DISAPPOINTED Over the years, conflict parties signed or BY THE FAILED declared various ceasefire agreements, but could not find a common ground to PEACE PROCESS. transform this violent conflict. THEY HOPE THAT After multiple broken commitments to ceasefire agreements, the leaders of conflict parties entered into a A NEW PEACE closed-door pre-negotiations phase in 2013 (Uzun 2014). Between 2013 and 2015, some positive steps taken for the transformation of the conflict by both sides raised PROCESS WILL hopes for the end of this violent conflict. During this period, the Turkish State adopted a few reform packages to BE INCLUSIVE grant language and cultural rights to the Kurdish minority population (Savran, 2020). However, after a supposedly OF ALL ACTORS, successful pre-negotiation phase between the main parties, the process suddenly fell apart in 2015 (CNN Türk ESPECIALLY 2015). YOUNGER As the peace process was suspended, the violent conflict mutated into new forms. There was an observable shift in the geographical location of the conflict between the GENERATIONS. PKK and the Turkish state, whereby, after a short ceasefire, attacks and armed conflict moved from rural into urban areas, such as the city centres (Konaev & Kadercan 2018). This move sparked a new wave of migration within and 36 between cities and brought an irreversible lack of trust among Kurdish people towards both the Turkish State and the PKK. Young people have been in the middle of this new wave of conflict, both as perpetrators and victims of violence (International Crisis Group 2021). Despite the role of youth have been central to the new phase of conflict, they have been largely left out of political analysis aimed at ending this conflict. With the older population having dominated each of the political, social, cultural and economic processes, young people are stereotyped as being immature, apolitical and/or aggressive to take an active part in these processes. As we argue in this article, one of the most obvious weaknesses of the collapsed peace process between the Turkish State and the PKK was that young people were not invited or allowed to participate in any capacity (Sunca 2016). Even civil society inclusion mechanisms initiatives, which were directly formed by the government to ensure the participation of civil society, did not include any young people, nor did it consult youth groups in any systematic way (Savran 2020). In this study, we provide insights in what youth thinks about the failed peace process that took place between 2013-2015. Our study sheds light on how young people’s personal experiences of the failed peace process influenced their understanding of the current conflicts and how it shaped their imagination of a new peace process. METHODOLOGY This research used a focus group discussion methodology, whereby 10 young Kurdish men and women aged between 18-29 and located in Diyarbakir participated. These participants were chosen because they

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: not include any young people, nor did it consult youth groups in any systematic way (Savran 2020). In this study, we provide insights in what youth thinks about the failed peace process that took place between 2013-2015. Our study sheds light on how young people’s personal experiences of the failed peace process influenced their understanding of the current conflicts and how it shaped their imagination of a new peace process. METHODOLOGY This research used a focus group discussion methodology, whereby 10 young Kurdish men and women aged between 18-29 and located in Diyarbakir participated. These participants were chosen because they live in one of the epicentres of the conflict -arguably the most important political centre for Kurdish political movements-, as well as the largest city predominantly populated by Kurds. The focus group methodology is ‘a carefully planned series of discussions designed to obtain perceptions on a defined area of interest in a permissive, non-threatening environment’ (Krueger & Casey 2014). We particularly chose the focus group discussion as the method of this study, because this method has exploratory potential in terms of better capturing the perspectives and opinions of participants through group interaction or discussion (Bagnoli & Clark 2010). Another reason for the selection of this method is that it enables individuals to express their views within groups and facilitates interaction between the views of participants. In other words, the method utilises the interaction between group participants to collect data for the research (Kitzinger 1994). For this study, we recruited 10 young individuals for two different focus group meetings, which were conducted at two different moments. 50% of the participants were female and the other half were male. Our first focus group discussion was conducted face-to-face in Diyarbakır and had the participation of three female and two male participants. Our second focus group discussion was virtual. In the second focus group, three of the participants were male and two were female. The youngest participant was 20 years old, while the oldest was 28. The age range of the first group was 20-24 and of the second group was 24-28. All selected participants were born in Diyarbakır and spent most of their lives in the city. One of the limitations of this research was that the second focus group meeting, which was initially planned to be face-to-face, took place online. This was due to several of the participants not being able to attend a physical meeting. This hindered some participants from freely sharing their opinions, as they were concerned about the security of the online platform. Another limitation was that we intended to use random sampling. However, due to our time limit in 3377 Diyarbakir, we had to change our recruitment to mini-snowball sampling to gain more time. ANALYSIS OF THEIR PEACE AND IDENTITY PERCEPTIONS AS YOUTH In order to comprehend both the young people’s perception of peace as a concept as well as the Kurdish Issue itself, the participants were asked two questions. Firstly, we asked how they would define peace as a young person. Secondly, we asked what Kurdish Issue means to them. “Peace is the state in which I don’t feel like a second-class citizen.” First of all, most of the participants clearly differentiated peace from violent conflict. For them, peace cannot be reduced to a state of simply the absence of physical violence. Their desire to live in peace means to build a society in which no one is silenced or coerced to face psychological, social or economic violence due to their identities. The young people that participated in our study were well-aware that peace would remain superficial

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: as a young person. Secondly, we asked what Kurdish Issue means to them. “Peace is the state in which I don’t feel like a second-class citizen.” First of all, most of the participants clearly differentiated peace from violent conflict. For them, peace cannot be reduced to a state of simply the absence of physical violence. Their desire to live in peace means to build a society in which no one is silenced or coerced to face psychological, social or economic violence due to their identities. The young people that participated in our study were well-aware that peace would remain superficial if it continues to hide discriminatory practices in society. Beyond that, peace is also understood as a long and difficult process that requires extraordinary efforts that not everyone may be willing to act upon. That is why most of the participants were sceptical of politicians’ promises of peace. From their view, peace promised by politicians has little in common with the way that youth imagine peace. Lastly, the young people challenged the core idea behind the previous attempts of peace processes. The youth based in Diyarbakir see “equal citizenship” as the central point of any peace process, rather than “fraternity of different ones”. They argue that lasting peace can only be possible whenever justice and equal conditions are provided to each citizen. “In the process, only the official language sets created by the ones in power were used. However, ‘fraternity’ is not the correct concept to meet the Kurds’ demands.” From the perspective of the participants, who define themselves as ethnically Kurd, the Kurdish Issue is a multi-layered problem that affects national and regional politics. The solution to the issue requires an inclusive approach at a societal level. As one of the participants put it: “[the] Kurdish Issue cannot be squeezed into only [the] political level. Such a perspective would only narrow the effect of the problem.” Moreover, especially female participants underlined that the people in the region are unable to attribute only one side of their identity. Importantly, the highlight the difference between “being Kurd” and “being a Kurd woman.” They believe that two of the suppressed identities, namely being a woman and belonging to an ethnic minority group, are overlapping and deepening the effect of the conflict on their life experiences. “Being from the region involves more than one identity.” Nearly all participants touched upon their personal experiences by highlighting ethnic differences. Participants underline that they have been politicised from a young age due to being part of an ethnic minority because they were treated differently by their Turkish counterparts, friends, or classmates. Nearly all participants touched upon this early politicisation due to their Kurdish identity. They highlighted the differences in attitude towards them, which went beyond issues relating to the Kurdish conflict. Although none of them heavily interacted with someone from the Turkish ethnic majority at an early age in Diyarbakır, they frequently stated that the way they have been raised is centred around the Kurdish identity. The parents put awareness of the Kurdish ethnic identity at the centre of upbringing. For example, by preparing them for any possible discrimination of the State or by highlighting their anxiety about losing their ethnic identity in the coming generations. 38 The young people from Diyarbakır believe that even though their problems are directly connected to Turkey’s recent political and social climate, being Kurdish enables them to look at those issues from a more comprehensive perspective. “I am studying at Dicle University (based in Diyarbakır) and we have a homogeneous environment. For instance, when I attended a meeting with my

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: The parents put awareness of the Kurdish ethnic identity at the centre of upbringing. For example, by preparing them for any possible discrimination of the State or by highlighting their anxiety about losing their ethnic identity in the coming generations. 38 The young people from Diyarbakır believe that even though their problems are directly connected to Turkey’s recent political and social climate, being Kurdish enables them to look at those issues from a more comprehensive perspective. “I am studying at Dicle University (based in Diyarbakır) and we have a homogeneous environment. For instance, when I attended a meeting with my colleagues around Turkey in İzmir, they had doubts about sharing a post on Instagram in [the] Kurdish language which it is not even questioned in my university. Then, they did not share [it], but I did. Sometimes I feel like the demand for peace is only burdened on us as if the party that constantly seeks for peace should be us. I came to realise that even though our concerns, such as the economic crisis of today, are alike, we are approaching [things] from much more diversified perspectives of the world.” ANALYSIS OF THEIR REFLECTION ON THE COLLAPSED PEACE PROCESS When participants were asked about their views of the failed peace process, nearly all participants started to talk about pleasant memories from their personal experiences. Since the importance of the Newroz celebration for the Kurdish community is obvious, one participant expressed their first feeling by stating that “Newroz in 2014 went very well. There was no available room in hotels in the entire city. It was great in 2015, too, the only problem was the rain.” Another participant reflected their observations from the city compared to the time after the peace process: “In that time, the city was alive. You could run into people who were smiling on the streets. However, as of 2016, Diyarbakır turned into a city with no happiness or hope.” The peace process is seen in the eyes of young people as “a short break to the conflict”. Most of the participants talked about the securitisation of the Kurdish Issue by the Turkish State as a security problem rather than as a political negotiation process. The peace process that went on between 2013 and 2015 brought together high-level politicians from both sides. The process increased touristic activity and economic investment and development in areas populated by Kurds. However, after the abolishment of the peace process and, consequently, the return of violent conflict, political processes transformed into security issues. “Before the peace process, the police forces were everywhere, as if [they wanted to] show their existence and power. Throughout the peace process, we have not seen police forces on the streets of the city that much. However, after it ended, police stops and searches increased, and police forces started to pop up from out of nowhere, not only in the city but also on the roads coming in and out of the city.” ANALYSIS OF YOUTH REFLECTION ON THE POST-PEACE PROCESS PERIOD When asked what the young people think about the failed peace process and how they retrospectively interpret the attitudes of the negotiating parties, all participants expressed their belief that both parties weren’t sincere in resolving the conflict. In other words, the youth based in Diyarbakir lack trust towards decision-makers and they doubt the good intentions of the negotiators of the collapsed peace process. For example, a female participant said: “It was just a story. Both sides were already aware that they would not implement the regulations they had set forth”. Another point they highlighted was

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: ON THE POST-PEACE PROCESS PERIOD When asked what the young people think about the failed peace process and how they retrospectively interpret the attitudes of the negotiating parties, all participants expressed their belief that both parties weren’t sincere in resolving the conflict. In other words, the youth based in Diyarbakir lack trust towards decision-makers and they doubt the good intentions of the negotiators of the collapsed peace process. For example, a female participant said: “It was just a story. Both sides were already aware that they would not implement the regulations they had set forth”. Another point they highlighted was that the process was not as inclusive as they expected. 39 “Women and youth were not at the negotiation table”, two of the participants said, critiquing the Wise People Committee. Consisting of 63 members, the committee only had 12 female members and not a single member under the age of 30. The participants also criticised that the negotiations were only held between the AKP government and HDP/PKK. The participation of other local actors, such as NGOs and other small political parties in the conflict resolution process, were not sufficient. A participant said: “There was an attitude as if the Kurds are represented by a single unity. However, there are many political parties here”. Lastly, a majority of the participants drew attention to the fact that the process did not have a bottom-up approach. “They thought they would resolve this problem with a top-down approach and without reaching to the grassroots, the public, or civil initiatives” said a participant, referring to the exclusion of almost all actors, including youth. Almost all participants think that the process was, despite all the problems, a significant move towards resolving the long-standing conflict. Thus, they are opposed to the demonisation of the process. Some of the participants stated that the reason for the demonisation of this process can be explained by the political decisions taken by parties at the regional level. Overall, there is no doubt that they are extensively disappointed in contemporary regional politics. One of the participants shared his feelings by stating that “After 2015, they turned their backs to us. We were plunged into chaos. All of our rights were revoked. The government denied our democratic rights by appointing trustees to the municipalities and discharging the mayors we had voted for from their offices. We [young people] and our rights are being terrorised”. Another participant said that “People have nothing to lose economically”, referring to the deterioration of the economy in the region. This statement becomes more meaningful when we look at the study conducted in 2020 by the Kurdish Studies Center. According to the research, the Kurdish youth in Kurdish cities have a lower rate of employment compared to their Turkish peers (Kurdish Studies Association et al. 2020). Considering the denial of their political rights and facing severe economic conditions, it could be said that the youth in Diyarbakir has been experiencing economic, social, and psychological pressure. ANALYSIS OF THEIR REFLECTION ON RESOLVING THE VIOLENT KURDISH CONFLICT AND THE ROLE OF YOUTH When we asked the participants how they think this conflict can be resolved and the role that youth can play, they shared that all human rights must be guarded before returning to the negotiation table. “Our basic rights must be guaranteed so we can talk about peace”, replied one of the participants, underlining the importance of rule of law and democracy. Others highlighted: “The conflict can also be transformed by providing concrete rights”. It is quite remarkable that there was a specific emphasis on cultural and political rights, such

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: ON RESOLVING THE VIOLENT KURDISH CONFLICT AND THE ROLE OF YOUTH When we asked the participants how they think this conflict can be resolved and the role that youth can play, they shared that all human rights must be guarded before returning to the negotiation table. “Our basic rights must be guaranteed so we can talk about peace”, replied one of the participants, underlining the importance of rule of law and democracy. Others highlighted: “The conflict can also be transformed by providing concrete rights”. It is quite remarkable that there was a specific emphasis on cultural and political rights, such as the right to mother-tongue education, freedom of speech and the right to vote (referring to the appointment of trustees to municipalities). Additionally, some of the participants pointed towards the Kurdish identity and Kurdish language. One participant stated that “The Kurds must be the subject, not an object (minority)”, while another participant supported this statement by adding that “Local governments should be strengthened”. Moreover, participants criticised that there was no clear roadmap for DDR (Disarmament, Demobilisation and Reintegration). Several participants stated that the DDR process, especially for young soldiers, is an indispensable part of a new peace process. Lastly, the participants envisage a peace process that is inclusive to all actors, including different sexual identities. Through the sessions, we observed that the participants put a lot of weight on the gender dimension of the conflict. “If we want to initiate a new process, we need to destroy the patriarchal 40 mentality and get rid of the grudge and anger-centered mentality”, said one of the participants. Another participant mentioned that “There is literally a genocide of women. It is not meaningful without them”, making a specific reference to the withdrawal from the Istanbul Convention and the exclusion of women from the process. Regarding the role youth can play in the process, we collected an array of answers from the participants. Participants could not imagine a successful future peace process without youth participation. “Young people should take a role now. We are tired of old people and old systems. New young names should come to the fore”, said one of the participants, criticising the system that excludes youth. Another participant drew attention to the stereotype against youth that is widely seen in Turkish and Kurdish societies and said that “Young people are approached with taboo. A person with an age over 50 is considered more knowledgeable than a 30-year-old young person”. Almost all participants underline that these misconceptions and stereotypes must be shattered in order to successfully integrate youth into the process. Beyond that, a participant stressed that “Compared to previous generations, youth are politically less biased and have less political calculations” -meaning that youth can gather around a table more easily than older generations. Moreover, today’s youth engage more with their peers who do not share common lifestyles and opinions, which helps them tear down stereotypes or ideological barriers towards others. “I came to Ankara to study. Now, they [Turkish youth] know me and I know them. This inevitably leads to an interaction between us”, said one of the participants, pointing out increasing ways of connection between young peers. Finally, some participants put an emphasis on the role of technology in youth’s everyday life and its positive effect on youth and their peace imagination. “This conflict can be tackled by youth as they are capable of keeping up with advancements in technology”, replied one of the participants. CONCLUSION Youth in Turkey, alike youth in the rest of the world, mostly are depicted to either be perpetrators of conflict or as victims

source_name: Journal of Youth Peace and Security Second Edition Dec 2023
text: know me and I know them. This inevitably leads to an interaction between us”, said one of the participants, pointing out increasing ways of connection between young peers. Finally, some participants put an emphasis on the role of technology in youth’s everyday life and its positive effect on youth and their peace imagination. “This conflict can be tackled by youth as they are capable of keeping up with advancements in technology”, replied one of the participants. CONCLUSION Youth in Turkey, alike youth in the rest of the world, mostly are depicted to either be perpetrators of conflict or as victims of conflict. In this paper, we discussed how Kurdish youth from Diyarbakir in Turkey perceives the concept of peace and the peace process that lasted between 2013 and 2015. In doing so, we also pointed out how their personal experiences ever since the failed peace process have influenced their understanding of the long-lasting conflict and how it has shaped their imagination of a new peace process. After two focus group meetings, we can say that the Kurdish youth in Diyarbakir has a diverse understanding of peace and identity. Even though they have satisfying memories around the peace process, throughout the years they started to witness the political and social shortcomings of the failed peace process. They are disappointed by the failed peace process. They hope that a new peace process will be inclusive of all actors, especially younger generations. If that is the case, they believe that peace processes and negotiations can be seen as more legitimate and accountable in the eyes of the people. 41 REFERENCES 140journos. 2017. MEET THE AUTHORS YPS RESEARCH NETWORK SHATAAKSHI VERMA Shataakshi is a human rights worker and a peacebuilding activist who has extensively worked in South and East Asia on gender justice, minority rights, humanitarian assistance, policy making and press freedom. She is also the founder of Art of Freedom, a women’s collective based in Kabul that aims to advocate for rights of women and other genders in conflict affected communities around the world through art based mediums. She attended the Harvard Kennedy School’s executive program last year on using public narrative as a tool for storytelling, action and community mobilisation. She is presently working with Reporters without Borders as their Asia Pacific Bureau Program Manager in Taiwan. MONSERRAT ESTEPHANIA Monserrat is a

MONSERRAT ESTEPHANIA Monserrat is a harm reduction, feminism and peacebuilding activist. She studied a bachelor in Sociology at the National Autonomous University of Mexico. She has collaborated with governmental institutions, international organisations and NGOs to implement social projects, and political processes aimed to transform drug policy and promote peacebuilding in Mexico since 2015. Currently, she is leading P.A.S -a harm reduction program- and is part of the Interinstitutional Harm Reduction Committee of Mexico City; a local body responsible for designing and implementing public policies aimed at improving the health and human rights of people who use drugs and other priority groups. MEHMET IHANLI Mehmet İlhanlı holds a B.A. in International Relations from Istanbul Bilgi University. Previously, he worked as project coordinator in a private company. In 2020, İlhanlı co-founded Young Peacebuilders of Turkey. Since then, he has been volunteering for the Young Peacebuilders of Turkey. Currently, he is pursuing a Master’s degree in Political Science at the University of Cologne. 43 REVŞEN BERFIN COŞKUN Berfin Coşkun holds her bachelor degree in Sociology from Marmara University, Turkey. Coşkun, who took junior roles in various non- governmental organizations during her student life, most recently worked as a project coordinator in a project carried out by the United Nations World Food Programme. She still works as a volunteer at the Young Peacebuilders of Turkey Initiative and continues her graduate studies at Ghent University, Department of Conflict and Development. ESIHLE MAKITSHI Esihle is a Safety Project Manager at Meta for WhatsApp Integrity with experience in the tech industry and international organisations such as IATA, INTERPOL and the IAEA. She has served as a One Youth Ambassador for Belgium and her passion is to drive Electronic Waste Recycling initiatives globally. DORSA BABAEI Dorsa Babaei is the Co-Director of PeaceMentors, a young-women-led peacebuilding initiative in Iran. Throughout her years of working on the ground, conducting projects with and for youth of different backgrounds in Iran, India, North Africa and Kazakhstan. Dorsa has developed a great interest in researching youth agency and youth ownership in knowledge creation. She believes unfolding individual narratives reminds us of the importance of inclusion and facilitates more authentic peacebuilding. 44

Moving forward: crucial steps for youth-inclusive peace processes ....................27 References .........................................................................................................................32 4 YOUTH-LED PEACE: The role of youth in peace processes Introduction More than 600 million young people live in fragile and conflict-affected contexts (Wilton Park, 2017). Despite being deeply affected by violence in a myriad of ways, young people’s voices are not heard, nor included in the processes of conflict resolution and peacebuilding. The ‘youth bulge’ is seen as a driver of instability, and young people are typically portrayed as perpetrators of violence or potential ‘spoilers’ who should be protected from radicalisation and extremism (see, for example, OECD, 2011). The active role young people play as peacemakers, mediators and peacebuilders at grassroots and local levels are under-acknowledged and they are often not included in official peace processes. Beyond peacebuilding, young people display ownership, agency and leadership in diverse areas that are of significance to local, regional, national and international peace and security, ranging from climate change to tackling inequalities. Yet, the achievements of young people are hindered due to the absence of adequate recognition, protection, funding and meaningful partnerships. This report highlights the key themes, findings and recommendations of the project Youth-led peace: The role of youth in peace processes led by the University of Glasgow. The objectives of the project were to: • examine the barriers to and strategies for youth-inclusive peace processes • take stock of the progress of the Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) agenda • investigate and emphasise the significance of youth leadership as peacemakers, mediators and peacebuilders at grassroots and local levels • explore the pathways for promoting and investing in youth leadership in peace processes through meaningful partnerships, capacity-building and protection. 5 The project involved two key activities: 1. Consultations with youth-led peacebuilding organisations

project Youth-led peace: The role of youth in peace processes led by the University of Glasgow. The objectives of the project were to: • examine the barriers to and strategies for youth-inclusive peace processes • take stock of the progress of the Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) agenda • investigate and emphasise the significance of youth leadership as peacemakers, mediators and peacebuilders at grassroots and local levels • explore the pathways for promoting and investing in youth leadership in peace processes through meaningful partnerships, capacity-building and protection. 5 The project involved two key activities: 1. Consultations with youth-led peacebuilding organisations in August–September 2020: The consultation process comprised a questionnaire and focus group meeting, with participants from eight youth-led organisations in Afghanistan, Kenya, Liberia, the Philippines, Rwanda, Somalia, South Sudan, and Turkey.1 2. A knowledge exchange workshop held at the University of Glasgow, 29–30 October 2020: The workshop on the role of youth in peace processes brought together representatives from the United Nations (UN) (including the Office of the UN Secretary General’s Envoy on Youth, United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), United Nations Economic, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO), and United Nations Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs (UN DPPA); the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO); conflict- resolution organisations (Beyond Borders Scotland, Search for Common Ground, Interpeace, Initiatives for International Dialogue, Conflictus Conflict Resolution Training); and youth- led peacebuilding organisations (United Network of Young Peacebuilders, Youth without Borders Organisation for Development Yemen, Human Rights Agenda Kenya, Afghans for Progressive Thinking, Generation Peace Youth Network Philippines, Never Again Rwanda, Young-Adult Empowerment Initiative South Sudan, and Young Peacebuilders of Turkey).2 Based on findings and insights from the consultations and the workshop, this report provides an overview of the diverse contributions of youth to peace processes (Part I) and discusses the challenges and barriers young peacebuilders face while working towards peace (Part II). We then explore the multiple dimensions of inclusion in peace processes (Part III) and chart potential avenues for future research, policy and practice in light of lessons learned from youth-led peace initiatives (Part IV). 1. The report reflects the authors’ accounts of the discussions, which do not necessarily reflect the views of the workshop participants. 6 YOUTH-LED PEACE: The role of youth in peace processes I. The roles of youth in peace processes Young people take on active roles before, during and after conflict. They work towards conflict prevention, build peace during conflict, deliver humanitarian support, and participate in post-conflict peace, truth and reconciliation processes. In addition, the work of young people cuts across different levels and spheres of engagement within, around, and even in the absence of, an official peace process. They reach out to and cooperate with individuals, families and communities, their peers, and national and international actors. Box 1: What is a peace process? An inclusive definition An inclusive approach to peace-making requires an inclusive understanding of what constitutes a ‘peace process’. Often the term ‘peace process’ is understood narrowly as an official, national process of conflict resolution between the main parties to an armed conflict – either two or more states, or a state and one or more non-state armed groups. Formal peace negotiations and resultant peace agreements are at the

the absence of, an official peace process. They reach out to and cooperate with individuals, families and communities, their peers, and national and international actors. Box 1: What is a peace process? An inclusive definition An inclusive approach to peace-making requires an inclusive understanding of what constitutes a ‘peace process’. Often the term ‘peace process’ is understood narrowly as an official, national process of conflict resolution between the main parties to an armed conflict – either two or more states, or a state and one or more non-state armed groups. Formal peace negotiations and resultant peace agreements are at the heart of this conceptualisation of a peace process. Broader definitions consider a peace process as ‘a mixture of politics, diplomacy, changing relationships, negotiation, mediation, and dialogue in both official and unofficial arenas’ (Saunders, 2001, p. 483) involving multiple tracks of diplomacy at the local, national and international levels, characterised by non-linearity between phases and tracks of a peace process. Adopting a broad definition is essential to capturing the diversity and complexity of interactions between actors, phases, structures and layers of peace-making and peacebuilding activities. Beyond national peace processes, local peace processes which involve ‘locally based actors’ and ‘address local conflict dynamics within a part of the wider conflict-affected area’ (Wise et al., 2019, p. 2) are also gaining significance. This is particularly the case in protracted conflicts, such as in Afghanistan, Libya, Mali, Syria and Yemen, which have proven resistant to traditional conflict-resolution methods centred around main conflict protagonists. 7 The experiences of the young peacebuilders we consulted attest to the diversity of youth engagement in peace processes. These young peacebuilders are involved in a range of activities to heal their communities from the consequences of violent conflict, to address the root causes of conflict to prevent its relapse, and to build a lasting culture of peace in their societies. The contributions of these young peacebuilders to peace processes in their countries include wide-ranging and varied activities. The following are examples that showcase their work: 1. Initiatives for conflict resolution, peace-making, mediation and dialogue activities, broadly understood as: • working towards building social cohesion for peaceful coexistence between groups that historically have found themselves in conflict • acting as mediators and peacebuilders at grassroots and community levels • setting up spaces where shared visions and understandings are co-constructed • designing initiatives to challenge misconceptions, reduce prejudices, mitigate hate speech and develop respect for others • opening channels of communication and building networks with other youth, including the most marginalised • advocating for human rights and dignity and against violence in any form. The cases of the Anataban Campaign in South Sudan (Box 2) and the youth dialogue initiative in Somaliland (Box 3) are powerful examples of the work young people lead that has a positive impact in their communities and beyond. Box 2: The Anataban Campaign in South Sudan With South Sudanese civil society raising the consciousness of an active citizenry through workshops and information sessions, including in refugee settlements in Uganda, the youth and next generation of South Sudanese are leveraging their collective power to hold leaders to account. This is exemplified by the Anataban Campaign, which has been more politically vocal than other South Sudanese peace organisations. Anataban is an artist collective that utilises the power of arts to raise awareness and foster public discussions on key issues for local peacebuilding such as government accountability and redressing social injustices. The collective also imagines a shared vision of a peaceful South Sudan that society should collectively work towards. For example, the group launched the online campaign #SouthSudanIsWatching to put

This is exemplified by the Anataban Campaign, which has been more politically vocal than other South Sudanese peace organisations. Anataban is an artist collective that utilises the power of arts to raise awareness and foster public discussions on key issues for local peacebuilding such as government accountability and redressing social injustices. The collective also imagines a shared vision of a peaceful South Sudan that society should collectively work towards. For example, the group launched the online campaign #SouthSudanIsWatching to put pressure on leaders to implement the 2018 peace agreement, and the hashtag has been used throughout the world. Young peacebuilders also organise concerts, street theatre, graffiti, poetry readings and other artistic events, with South Sudanese youth re-claiming a public platform to express their grievances and shape a future South Sudan. 8 YOUTH-LED PEACE: The role of youth in peace processes Box 3: Bringing youth representatives from conflicting parties together in Somaliland The Somaliland Youth Development and Voluntary Organization (SOYDAVO) described their experience of bringing together a group of young men and women from warring clans: ‘We opened a seven-day long discussion on prevention of violence and de-escalation of conflicts with 30 young men and women, who had taken part in the latest violent conflict in the district. These young men and women have never gone to school. They were often praised [for] their role in the conflicts by their fellow clansmen and women. Changing this approach requires time and a proper way to present your arguments. [T]his has been possible with the support of the understanding we had in the context and the life examples we have used including destruction of properties, productive communal assets, loss of lives and animals. The young men and women are now peace ambassadors in their villages and they have moved to the urban cities in Somaliland so that they don’t find themselves again engaged in the conflicts.’ 2. Initiatives for healing the traumas of violent conflict and reintegrating survivors and ex-perpetrators: • providing psychosocial support and safe spaces to help youth overcome the traumas developed due to violent conflict they have experienced and its aftermath • supporting the reintegration of ex-perpetrators into society through counselling and developing life skills. Working on healing trauma is a critical step for young people to be able to contribute to and lead peace processes. The case of a youth peacebuilding organisation in Rwanda (see Box 4) showcases such work. Box 4: Dealing with the past, paving the way for the future Never Again Rwanda (NAR) was founded by students who identified the challenges for youth in post-genocide Rwanda. They saw the merits in establishing platforms to engage youth to promote peace and to work on the important issues for youth relating to post-genocide life. NAR focuses on youth engagement within the community, to create spaces for peace, working specifically with youth from survival, ex-perpetrator and returnee backgrounds. For instance, NAR has created safe spaces for youth through closed groups of 20 to 30 individuals who have shared their traumatic experiences and have received necessary support in return, such as psychotherapy. The absence of a platform before this to share views on barriers to safety, inclusion and participation had previously limited youth participation in decision-making. NAR also works to empower young people in Rwanda to become active, critical-thinking citizens. 9 3.
